"Yes, Mademoiselle."

Mademoiselle Bertin seemed satisfied. "Oh, and Madeleine, hide your hair under a hood." Her new boss shooed Madeleine away with a wave of her hand, and a pleasant whiff of rose water reached Madeleine's nostrils. Once again, she curtsied nicely and withdrew. Exhausted, she dragged herself to the outhouse in the back yard, where the kitchen was, but then decided otherwise because she was not really hungry. The excitement of the day, the trip, and the parting had left a lump in her throat, and she knew she would not be able to eat. Funny, she thought, I've been dreaming of food for weeks and now... She sighed. Wearily, she climbed the steps to the attic rooms where the girls who, like her, lived on site. She had to knock on several doors until she found the right room. The girl she shared it with seemed friendly and chattered cheerfully: "I'm Louisa, and you're Madeleine, right?"

Madeleine nodded. They had already seen each other in the fabric store, but there had been too much to do to introduce themselves properly.

"You will like it here, I'm sure," Louisa spoke again. "Bertin is alright, strict, but fair. Did you know that she was appointed fashion minister for the Queen? Oh, and here, this is your bed." Madeleine nodded again and was amazed that employees should sleep in such fashionable beds. She carefully touched the pretty four-poster bed.

"You mean, the fashion minister of Marie-Antoinette?" she asked reverently. Louisa gave her a meaningful affirmative answer.

Oh dear, now I'm going to be even more afraid of doing everything wrong, Madeleine thought glumly. It was true that she had been the most gifted seamstress in her family and had even surpassed her mother in that skill. But mending a few skirts for the domestic needs of an impoverished family of farmers was not the same as working for a tailor's shop, and one which also supplied the royal family. She was beset with apprehension and wondered if her father had been right to entrust her here. How could she hold her own here?

"Have you only just left home?" whispered Louisa, who seemed to sense what Madeleine was going through. Madeleine nodded anxiously. She suddenly felt utterly miserable when she saw the expression of concern on Louisa's face. Only then did the significance of her parents' decision really weigh down on her. Since the evening before, when her mother had taken her and Marianne aside to explain her motives, she had existed as if in an unreal fog, without really understanding. It must have been a kind of protection so as not to cry out loud when Marianne and, finally, she herself had been handed over. And Jeanne? What would become of Jeanne? Had she found shelter in a rich house like Marianne, and would she now be wearing pretty clothes? She sighed and got the feeling that she was the worst off.

Louisa eventually noticed how tired Madeleine was and how monosyllabic she answered, and she graciously ceased to ask questions. She probably knows how one feels when having to settle in again, thought Madeleine, and was grateful to the girl for her consideration. Before the boss called Madeleine over, she had spent half the afternoon between bales of cloth of various types and sizes in a huge warehouse behind the shop, trying to memorize as much as possible of what was being taught to her. The terms were all alien to her, and she found it difficult to distinguish between brocade and damask, organza and tulle. Louisa seemed to be an experienced seamstress for her young age and had given her an encouraging smile, wrinkling her small, freckled nose prettily. "Don't worry, you will soon learn. In a week, you'll know the names and terms by heart," Louisa had whispered to her.

Why should she learn all this by heart? Madeleine longed for her sisters and her parents. She did not like this new life with people who demanded what she felt was absolutely beyond her capacity. For a brief moment, she thought of simply running away. And then what? Had their parents not explained the situation to her and Marianne explicitly?

"My dear girls," Mother had said the night before when Jeanne, the youngest, was already asleep. "We need to talk to you. It won't be easy, but I expect you to be brave." Then the parents told them what they already knew: A few more weeks without food, and the whole family would starve to death.

The two girls knew vaguely about their father's salt smuggling. Marianne's friend, Jacques, had told them about it under the seal of secrecy. He said that many farmers in Brittany rushed into this business to save their families.

That is how her father had found jobs for his daughters: through his connections in Paris. Madeleine assumed that these acquaintances were connected with those dubious deals. Even little Jeanne would be taken care of, the mother had assured both of them, placing her cool fingers soothingly on Madeleine's trembling hands. Marianne had cried but soon calmed down again. They wanted to be strong, for they had promised their mother.

"Oh, Mama, I know you mean well. But what will become of you? Will we ever meet again?" The mother had glanced at the father and then lowered her eyes.

"God alone knows the answer, my child, God alone ..."

***

Chapter 2

Jeanne - Paris, 1782

The first daylight fell through the dirty little skylight, and when Jeanne opened her eyes, she caught a glimpse of a flock of swallows flying past. Jeanne stretched and sat up. She was freezing, but her rumbling stomach urged her to get up.

She shyly opened the creaking door and peered outside cautiously. She heard a loud clatter from the first floor that seemed to come from the living room. Regardless of the cold, Jeanne padded barefoot across the hall. She shivered. The walls of the gloomy stairway were decorated with dusty and crooked paintings, and ragged carpets lay on the floorboards. As she crept down the stairs, the creaky steps made her start. Gathering all her courage, she carefully opened the door to the living room, from where the delicious smell of apples and fried eggs came.

Jeanne immediately saw the terrifying woman from the previous day standing at the stove and baking pancakes while a kitchen maid fiddled with dishes. A plate with the delicious pancakes lay next to the stove. Jeanne stared hungrily at the delicacy that reminded her of her childhood when the family had fared better, and they could afford eggs, flour, and milk.

At the sight of Jeanne, the shopkeeper paused, wrinkled her nose, and gave the maid instructions that Jeanne did not understand. The kitchen helper hurried over to Jeanne, took her by the hand, and led her to a steaming washtub, which Jeanne only noticed then. She immediately braced her feet on the ground, her eyes opened wide in horror. There was no way she would be washed here in the middle of the kitchen-living room.

"Hey, little one," said the maid and leaned down to her without letting go of her. "I'm Lisbeth, and you are Jeanne, is that right?"

She nodded.

"If you want something to eat and a new dress ... you want that, don't you ...?" She nodded again. "... then you have to let me wash you now."

Resigned, Jeanne undressed hurriedly, got into the tub and faced up to the bath, thinking of the delicious pancakes and trying to imagine her new dress. She thought of the distinguished ladies from the day before and of the shop in which Madeleine had been placed.

Jeanne was grateful to Lisbeth for avoiding to expose her to the eyes of the others while she was being bathed.

"Luckily, the master of the house is already left," whispered Lisbeth as she tried to get through Jeanne's disheveled hair with a comb. Without a word, Jeanne endured the agony of combing her hair. After she had been taken out of the tub and rubbed dry, the maid put a well-worn but clean dress on her.

"It's not all that new," Jeanne complained, which Lisbeth acknowledged with a twisted mouth and a nudge on her nose.

"That first has to be made, cheeky little Miss. But don't let the mistress hear that." Lisbeth looked around anxiously, but the old woman had already left the room and taken the pancakes with her. Jeanne's stomach rumbled in protest. 

Instead of the longed-for pancakes with apple sauce, Lisbeth served Jeanne a bowl of gruel, a soggy mass that, despite her disappointment about not getting the pancakes, she greedily tucked in until her stomach began to ache.

She had just heaved herself from her stool when there was a knock on the kitchen door.

"It's the seamstress," said Lisbeth. "Come on, Jeanne, she will want to take your measurements."

The maid had barely finished the sentence when a girl stepped into the kitchen and introduced herself as the seamstress. Her name was Louisa, and Jeanne thought she was very pretty. Her young face with the regular features reminded Jeanne of the angels depicted on the frescoes of her local church. She also smelled wonderfully of fresh flowers and wore a lovely light pink dress with a matching hat. Her waist was tightly laced and her narrow shoulders were adorned with a white scarf that crossed over her bodice and was tied in a bow at the back. That is what I want to look like when I grow up, Jeanne thought dreamily.

The girl, who seemed to be the age of Jeanne's sister, Madeleine, worked very quickly so that she was ready in no time. She wrote something down on a piece of paper and packed her utensils. Jeanne marveled at the dexterity of the young woman and wondered if she would one day be so confident. What can I do? She worried herself, and what do I know?

"I'll be back this afternoon," said the angel, and scurried out through the door, leaving Jeanne agape. Would her dress really be finished by then? Jeanne sighed. She would have liked to talk to the pretty seamstress for a while and ask her questions about her origins. To Jeanne, she looked like a simple girl, but one who knew how to move like a lady. Could one learn that? Jeanne decided that she wanted to be like that too. If her parents and her sisters weren't there anymore and seemed not to care in the least, she might as well make the best of the situation, and stand out. It seemed she would soon get the opportunity to do so because she was called to the shop by her hostess to help. She had to learn the polite phrases, how to smile in an exaggeratedly friendly way and curtsy politely in front of the gentlemen. Jeanne was also initiated into the secrets of setting up the shop, and the shopkeeper entrusted her with setting up the newly arrived crockery set, which was decorated with pretty red poppies. Humming to herself, Jeanne let herself be guided by her inner inspiration and set up the plates, cups, and bowls as she had been told, but she also added a bit of her own creativity. Jeanne learned quickly, and the otherwise disgruntled shopkeeper seemed satisfied and even gave her a piece of candy as a reward at the end of the afternoon.

In the early afternoon, Louisa, the seamstress, came back with the new dress. Jeanne was amazed at the speed with which she had worked. The wonderful little dress was made of a strong, stiff fabric with a floral pattern and was trimmed with ruffles. It also had a matching scarf, which she would cross over her chest like Louisa.

"That's called Fichu," she explained to her. Jeanne put the dress on and, surprisingly, it fitted her like a glove. She immediately felt elegant. If only Madeleine and Marianne could see me like that, she thought, and the idea suddenly made her very sad. "Don't you like it?" asked the pretty seamstress.

"Oh yes, of course," Jeanne stammered. "It's beautiful." The girl smiled in relief and took her leave. Jeanne would have liked to call her back. She seemed to have some connection with her, but she couldn't have said exactly what it was. It’s most probably because she reminds me of my sisters, she thought sadly.

"Come on, now," said the boss. "There's still a lot to do, and we really don't have time to daydream. Now that my husband has given you such a beautiful dress, you also have to prove your worth. Otherwise, it will be deducted from your food and you will have to go to bed hungry."

Jeanne got a fright. Of course, she didn't want to risk that. She had starved for long enough and now that she again felt what it was like to have a full stomach, she didn't want to do without that good feeling. So she put her complaints aside, locked her fears and questions deep in her heart, and made the best of the situation. It didn't help though...

The shop smelled musty but otherwise did not indicate the neglect in the rest of the house for it was kept clean. Jeanne was beginning to have a lot of fun hanging out among the goods and the paraphernalia that she could arrange as she saw fit, under the watchful eyes of the owner. In the course of the day, many customers came: some bought on credit, others paid immediately. But since she did not intend to stay here long, but intended to induce her parents to quickly bring her back home, she watched the goings-on with serenity. Out of sheer curiosity, however, she observed the behavior of the customers and felt secretly amused when she saw the fake smile of the shopkeeper, the corners of her mouth dropping low every time the customers left the shop.

It must be exciting to have your own shop, thought Jeanne. There were many things that she would do a lot better than her boss if she were to follow her own thoughts. She would treat the customers with genuine affection, listen to them, and always try to do the best for them. But despite the unexpected surge of sympathy for the grocer's profession, the next time she saw her mother, she would make it quite clear that she could not stay here. But until then, she decided that she wanted to learn, and made every effort to help the shopkeeper, and learn the many tricks of the trade. Jeanne knew that she was different from other young girls her age, that she had an exceptional perception. She felt and foresaw more than she really knew things. She often overlooked details and had a more general, intuitive view of her surroundings. In this way, Jeanne was able to quickly capture the gist of a situation, understand the individual context, and feel what people really wanted without being able to put it into words. She learned without difficulty, almost instinctively. And when the doorbell rang again that afternoon and a distinguished gentleman stepped into the shop, she wanted to prove it not only to herself but also to her new employer.

"Bonjour monsieur, you are welcome to our modest realm of wonders," Jeanne warbled, not quite sure where she suddenly got this vocabulary from because the shopkeeper would never have expressed herself so volubly. Based purely on her instinct, she guessed that he was a man from the bourgeoisie because he was too plainly dressed for a nobleman, at least as Jeanne imagined the members of this high class. He wore neither a wig nor a walking stick. Besides, an aristocrat would hardly do his own shopping. This insight and the benevolent look of the customer cheered her up.

"Bonjour, bonjour, young mademoiselle," he replied, clearly enthusiastic about this exuberant reception. "What a nice, refreshing way to receive your customers." Jeanne smiled pleasantly and curtsied. The puzzled shopkeeper looked back and forth between the man and Jeanne, and could not decide whether to intervene or not.

"What can I do for you, sir?" Jeanne knew at once that she was in her element, struck a pose, and suddenly felt terribly grown-up.

"I bought a service of plates with blue lilies from you last year and my housekeeper broke half of it again," he said, rolling his eyes to show his displeasure. "Now I wanted to ask if you still had any left?" His gaze wandered from Jeanne to the shopkeeper and back again. The latter was about to start answering when Jeanne got ahead of her.

“Sir, we certainly have a few more pieces in stock, and I'll be happy to check them out for you. However, it could take a while. How about checking out this new service that we have just received? A very special service, because every piece is unique," Jeanne gushed out. Of course, she did not have the faintest idea whether what she was saying was really true, because she had just made it up. She felt like she was back in her childhood playing shop with her sisters by selling small stones, sticks, roots, and flowers on the low wall of her yard. The manager gaped. "Take a look here, this wonderful service with poppies - my personal favorite, by the way - it looks fresh and summery and is sure to bring new life back to your table. Just look at the pretty little petals, how faithfully they have been reproduced. You could almost smell the pollen as if it weren't painted but burned into the porcelain..."

The man studied the service so closely that he actually appeared to be considering a purchase.

"Indeed, it is really lovely, young lady."

It didn't take much more to inspire Jeanne.

"Just consider, dear sir, how pleased your guests would be with this change."

The man nodded thoughtfully, seemed hesitant, and Jeanne ventured a sideways glance at the shopkeeper, who had folded her hands on her breast as if in prayer, as if she were inwardly imploring the Lord in Heaven that Jeanne's boldness would not scare away the good customer.

"We can also give you a small discount if you want to take the complete set, including the bowls and the matching tea set," Jeanne suddenly offered, not knowing how she had come up with the idea. Out of the corner of her eye, she saw the grocer swaying. Jeanne suddenly had doubts. What had gotten into her to venture this far? She suddenly felt hot in the cotton dress Lisbeth had put on her that morning, and she felt her cheeks glow.

"All right, young lady," said the customer suddenly, and a mischievous smile crept on his face as if he were already imagining his wife's enthusiasm. "Please wrap everything up carefully for me. However, I waive the discount. Your boss should pay you that, as a sales bonus, so to speak, because you really have a talent for wrapping a customer around your little finger." They laughed.

"I am extremely pleased that we could inspire you with our new service," the shopkeeper intervened as if she wanted to finally make it clear that she was the owner of the shop. All of a sudden, she seemed to have found her voice again and pushed her way between Jeanne and the man. "You have made a very good decision and you will certainly not regret it, because it is of very exquisite quality, a masterpiece that comes straight from the Meißner factory in Germany." She cast a warning glance at Jeanne, indicating clearly that she should no longer interfere, that there were details that she didn't know. "The royal families from all over Europe get their deliveries from there." Jeanne no longer really listened. She thought that she had done a great job and was mischievously pleased. Even if she still hoped that her parents would pick her up again soon, she still enjoyed the feeling of being important and useful. 

The deal with the customer was concluded in a flash, and the shopkeeper promised the delivery for the same day. When the customer had left the shop, his face glowing with satisfaction, the shopkeeper slowly turned to Jeanne, who was smiling happily at her, as if to say: Well? Did you see how well I did it? She was terribly proud of her first sale. That is why Jeanne was not prepared for it when - she hardly saw it coming - the slap on her face fell so hard that she was almost knocked over. She swayed, holding her smarting cheek.

"Hey," she cried, disregarding all due respect for her employer, "Is that how you thank me?" Tears welled up in her eyes and she felt miserable and small and ... at her mercy again.

"Thanks?!" the shopkeeper shrieked. “You embarrassed me, you brat. Never do that again, understand? Just wait until my Alain comes home, then you’ll get it."

"But ... the customer bought it?!" Jeanne was at a loss.

“Yes, he bought the service that would have been sold either way because it was new and beautiful. But what do I do with the few lily plates that are still rotting away in the store? Eh? How am I supposed to sell them now? Nobody wants to buy a few individual plates, except for a customer who already has this service. It is outrageous to interfere like that."

"You should have given it to him as a present," Jeanne replied snuggly. "The man would have surely refused the present and bought it as well."

"Don't be cheeky," cried the shopkeeper. Jeanne knew she was right, but could neither give a reason nor find the words to explain it; she just felt it was right. But how could she explain it to this mean woman if she herself did not know exactly where this inspiration came from. It was true that she had many years of experience, and yet Jeanne felt she could do better. Was she crazy? Impudent? "You're going to feel the belt tonight as your punishment. Never again, do you hear? Don't ever again behave with such insolence. Go, get your heel of bread from the kitchen, and out of my sight into your room. I don't want to see you anymore." Jeanne left the shop through the back door. "And don't think you can get away with this. Smarter ones have tried before," the angry woman shouted after her. And she mumbled, more to herself: "Just comes here and thinks she knows everything better, this damned brat." Jeanne paused in the hallway and listened. "Huh! Didn't I say right away that it wasn't a good idea?" the old woman went on muttering while appearing to tidy a drawer vigorously. "We should put her in the orphanage if they would take her there. No wonder the parents wanted to get rid of her. One more mouth to feed and cheeky at that. Huh! Alain will take care of that. He's handled more difficult ones before. Huh"

Distraught, Jeanne turned away. She didn't want to listen further, then those terrible words affected her deeply. Did her parents really want to get rid of her? She felt her heart contract painfully. All previous concerns, fears, and questions she had previously repressed surfaced like hungry wolves which had found easy prey and penetrated Jeanne's soul in search of nourishment...

Without a kind word, Jeanne was sent to her room with the usual heel of bread. As she sat alone in this dark hole, all her grief suddenly broke out with such force that she could hardly breathe through her sobs. She held her face in both hands and shed bitter tears. She was overcome by a deep sorrow as she realized that she had been abandoned by her family. Her little heart seemed to shatter into a thousand pieces. But why? Why had her parents done this to her? Why wasn't she allowed to at least stay with her sisters? Exhausted, Jeanne fell asleep at some point without having even touched the moldy piece of bread.

Jeanne awoke, startled, in the middle of the night. Had she heard something? She was miserably cold and reluctantly crawled under the coarse, scratchy, and foul-smelling woolen blanket to warm herself. There! Again, she thought she heard a noise; it sounded ... oh no! There were steps on the creaking stairs, steps that seemed to be coming nearer. Jeanne crawled under the blanket and stared over its edge at the door, which was opened a few moments later.  This time, she saw the man only vaguely because he left the oil lamp on a small, rickety table in the stairway. But she recognized him by his unpleasant smell of sweat. Again he plopped down on the straw bed. He groped for her in the semi-darkness.

"Did you like the dress?" The smell of alcohol suddenly hung in the air, something her father had sometimes smelled like when he came home from a meeting.

Jeanne stiffened.

"Yes sir. Thank you very much."

“And yet you misbehaved?! Just like that? That's how you say thank you?!" It was a statement, not a question. Suddenly he brought out a strap. Jeanne broke out in a cold sweat. She had never been beaten before. As a small child, she had perhaps had a slap on the behind or a small pat on the hand if she dared to taste Mother's food before getting permission.

"But sir, it was a misunderstanding. I only wanted to help."

"Hmm," he mumbled. "But that's not how my wife described it. Are you saying that she is lying?"

"No, of course not. I ask for your forgiveness. I went about it the wrong way. It will never happen again, I promise you."

"I'm glad to hear that, little one, I'm glad to hear that. So, now that we've settled that, please make it easy for us." Jeanne didn't understand and looked at him questioningly. Meanwhile, her eyes had gotten used to the twilight and she could see the determined expression on his face. But there was something else, or was she mistaken? Could she still trust her instinct after it got her into so much trouble?

"What ... what do you mean?"

"Lift your dress and show me your backside," he said. "I promise to make it brief. But if you resist..." he slapped the strap on the palm of his hand, "... then it will get much worse, I can promise you that. Well? What do you choose?" Jeanne was trembling all over. Should she bear it? Just like that? Get it over with? She pressed her lips together and slowly stood up. The man gave an encouraging nod. Jeanne hesitantly turned her backside to him.

"Like this?" she whispered in a trembling voice.

"See? You can do it. Bend forward and lift your dress," he ordered. She obeyed, closed her eyes, and tensed in anticipation of a blow. But it did not come. Instead, she felt a hand on her buttocks and jumped.

"There is another possibility," the man whispered, his voice vibrating strangely. Jeanne spun around.

"And what would it be?" She began to see hope.  Maybe the man wasn't as bad as she had thought; maybe he would spare her.

"Do you remember what I told you yesterday?"

"Yes, sir, I always remember everything I am told."

"Then repeat it to me now, my pretty one."

"I should be nice to you ..."

"Exactly."

"What exactly am I supposed to do, sir?" The man groaned softly as if he were in pain. "Are you not well, sir? Should I get your wife?"

"No, come here, sit next to me. I'll show you how to be nice to me." He had taken her hand and started to rub it against his trousers. Shocked, Jeanne went weak at the knees, but he seemed to enjoy her fear.

She didn't understand what he was doing, but she felt that it wasn't right. Her heart was racing.

"Don't do that, please!" she called, trying to free her hand. But he untied his bib and put her hand into it. Jeanne's eyes widened in shock. She didn't want to touch the old man there. On top of that, he really didn't seem well, because he was breathing harder and harder. Then he reached under her dress. Jeanne screamed. She wanted to tear herself away, but he held her tightly. She screamed again, hoping that the wife of the shop owner would hear her and hurry over, but nothing happened. She thrashed about, but he dropped sideways onto her and blocked her right leg. "Will you keep still, you beast," he grumbled, panting from the exertion. Jeanne felt completely at his mercy. She screamed her head off, but she could not stop him, and no one came to her help...

***

Chapter 3

Marianne - Paris, 1782

"Marianne, do come in, don't be embarrassed," said Madame Necker, the mistress of the house, amused as Marianne paused uncertainly in front of the open double door. "She only just arrived today," she said apologetically to her guests, who were standing with her at the richly spread buffet. "Our Marianne is still a little awkward, but that will pass, won't it, Marianne?" Madame Necker put on an exaggeratedly sweet smile.

"Yes, Madame," Marianne replied politely and curtsied. The guests looked her over like an animal at the fair. She had been given a dress that the daughter of the house had discarded until she had her own wardrobe. Marianne felt like a fish out of water - out of place. An elderly gentleman with an elegant redingote – a long coat buttoned up in the front to the belly – and a white, high-neck scarf placed his pince-nez on his nose so that he could examine her better. There was whispering and giggling, and Marianne felt extremely uncomfortable.

When she arrived, she was given the room next to the daughter of the house, Anne-Louise-Germaine, because Marianne was to become her companion. Madame Necker had explained to her that she was not a companion in the usual sense, – then those were generally educated – but that she had been accepted out of pure kindness. Marianne was supposed to be a playmate for the daughter, who got quickly bored.

But Madame Necker also emphasized that Marianne would also be tutored to make up, at least a little, for her lack of education. She pointed out that persons of her class were not really entitled to these privileges, but because her daughter quickly tires of her companions, and the father feels indebted to an acquaintance who, in turn, owes Marianne's father a favor, she had agreed to give Marianne a try.

"Please sit down with Anne-Louise." Madame Necker pointed to the empty seat at the end of the table, and Marianne obeyed. She wished she were far away, back in the country where she really belonged. She already missed the birdsong and the fresh morning breeze. The fact that they had been starving didn't change the homesickness that raged inside her.  As the eldest of the three sisters, her parents had relied on her not to show her grief during the journey and at the parting. But she had suffered the tortures of hell when she got off the wagon to be swallowed up by the posh house with its thousands of rules and obligations. She not only missed her home terribly, but also Madeleine, and most especially the baby of the family, the refreshingly cheerful little Jeanne with her infectious, melodious laughter; the little sister with whom you could never be angry for long, no matter what mischief she had been up to.

Marianne felt Anne-Louise's curious gaze and looked at her shyly. The daughter of the house seemed to be a bright girl; Marianne liked that and felt some comfort despite her grief. Anne-Louise had intelligent eyes, a pointed nose – which she would have inherited from her father – and a very pale complexion with rosy cheeks. Her dark, curly hair fell down her back in a braid interspersed with flowers, feathers, and threads. Marianne admired this elaborate hairstyle and wondered how long the girl had needed to do her hair.

But Anne-Louise's outfit was nothing compared to that of the other ladies and gentlemen present with their powdered wigs and hats, which reached breathtaking proportions. The ladies wore padded bustles and wide hoop skirts, and each dress appeared to outdo the other in terms of colors, the richness of the ribbons, and pearl trimmings.

"Welcome," whispered Anne-Louise, smiling mischievously at her. Marianne returned the smile shyly. "Don't worry, you'll settle in." Marianne doubted that but she was grateful for the encouraging words of the daughter of the house which indicated that no one was out to gobble her up; even if it didn't help the lump lodged in her stomach, which now and then rumbled softly.

A white, finely embroidered linen tablecloth was laid on a large, oval, mahogany table, richly decorated with flower arrangements and huge candelabras. She was dazed by the fine porcelain plates and the large variety of cutlery. On another just as impressively decorated table were bowls on raised bases with a large selection of foods that spread their wonderful aromas. A farmer's family could live for three months on so much food, Marianne reckoned spontaneously, but she immediately chased away the thought because her stomach suddenly gave a loud, embarrassing growl. Anne-Louise giggled softly.

"It's about to start," Anne-Louise whispered to her. "By the way, we still eat the French way here." When she saw Marianne's questioning face, she had to laugh. "That means everyone helps themselves at the big table." She pointed behind her to the table on which the dishes were lined up. Marianne could scarcely hide the horror that suddenly hit her. Should she really help herself at the buffet in front of everyone present? How unpleasant!" But I'll do it for you today," said Anne-Louise, who seemed to sense Marianne's discomfort. She nodded gratefully to the girl whom she was getting to like more and more. At least that, she thought morosely, at least the daughter seems to be sociable and understanding.

Marianne noticed with relief that the guests had meanwhile resumed their conversations and were no longer paying any attention to her.

"Have you heard the latest about this Prince de Rohan-Guémené, Madame Necker?"

The lady of the house had been watching her daughter from the corner of her eye. She slowly turned to face her guest. "No, what about him, dear Baron?"

"The man went bankrupt, and hundreds of servants, but also traders who had entrusted their money to him, are now facing dire financial trouble."

"Oh, how awful!" A cry of horror went through the room, and an excited discussion ensued among the guests.

"How is that possible, he always seemed so elegant and noble?"

"The poor people!"

"It is said to involve thirty-three million livres"

"How terrible!"

"One should stop trading in money ..."

While some put on horrified expressions, others almost seemed to swoon from indignation, or at least pretended to, Marianne looked around the dining room. On the high ceiling of the room hung an incredibly huge chandelier, which was ostensibly studded with precious stones. The candles brought it to sparkle like an oversized diamond. Huge double doors lined the long window side of the dining room, and heavy red damask curtains highlighted the openings. The parquet floor shone, and a large woven rug and beautiful paintings depicting noble ladies and gentlemen completed the picture.

"Madame Necker, what is the latest from your husband?" To get a better look at the mistress of the house at the other end of the table, a plump lady leaned forward, her ample bosom threatening to jump out of the tight décolletage and spread on her plate.

"Thank you for asking, Countess. Well, he's recovering from the incidents in our Saint-Ouen castle in Switzerland."

"That's good, the Swiss air will do him good after all the excitement with the king."

“Your dear husband worked so hard, Madame Necker. With his reforms and his policies, he was finally a director of the Trésor Royal who also deserved the name," a gentleman next to the countess, who seemed to be her husband, joined in the conversation. "Too bad that our king didn't see it that way."

"I wouldn't say so, but maybe some of the attempts at a reform didn't suit him," another gentleman interjected.

"Oh no," the house mistress waved the napkin in her hand. "After reporting to the king on the financial situation of the treasuries, and the document published caused a considerable commotion, he preferred to leave."

"A regrettable decision. A worthy man, your Jacques," another gentleman, with a particularly big nose, nodded in acknowledgment. "After all, he received the highest prize of the Académie française. How can you let such a man leave?"

"He has even abolished torture."

"Oh, really?" a gentleman with a huge double chin joined in, chewing and smacking his lips. "Wasn't it more about Marie-Antoinette taking a dislike to Jacques? He seemed to thwart her plans regarding a certain Duke of Guînes ..." approving laughter followed.

Marianne felt out of place. She did not understand much about what was being said, her knowledge of such matters being limited. Moreover, she gradually realized that she was in a very important house, where the residents came in and out of Versailles.  But still, she marveled at the freedom of speech of the women, who took part in political conversations without causing a stir.

"It doesn't look good, though. Who knows where all this will lead us to," said the person sitting next to her. There were approving nods from all around the table.

"Well then, to his speedy return to the court," the count ended the conversation solemnly, as if he wanted to avoid anyone getting carried away with inappropriate or even treasonous remarks, and raised his champagne glass.

"To Monsieur Necker! And to our King!" shouted the dinner party in chorus.

Marianne felt dizzy. She raised her glass mechanically, sipped the champagne, trying not to grimace, which made Anne-Louise giggle in amusement.

Impressed, Marianne turned to the dishes that Anne-Louise had served her, but her stomach was not used to large amounts. It wasn't long before she felt like she was going to burst. The hunger suddenly turned into nausea, the rumbling in her stomach into a big air bubble that wanted to force its way out, as if one had pumped gallons of air into her stomach. Anne-Louise also finished her meal and asked her mother's permission to withdraw. The girls curtsied politely in front of the guests and disappeared. Marianne let out a breath of relief, even though she still had a terrible stomach ache.

"I noticed that you couldn't take it any longer," giggled Anne-Louise. "Just let it out."

No sooner had Anne-Louise finished the sentence than Marianne let out a resounding burp. Though embarrassed, she could not help a shy smile of relief.

"Oh, sorry," she stammered when she realized how loud she had just been and covered her mouth with her hand. Anne-Louise giggled and waved it off.

"Come with me, I want to show you something," she said to her new friend, taking Marianne by the hand and dragging her through the corridors to the top floor. The gloomy, long corridors with the numerous doors were just as impressive as the pompous luxury of the large property.

When she got to Anne-Louise's room, Marianne could again only gape in amazement. The room assigned to her already seemed like a queen's chamber, but Anne-Louise's bedroom was beyond her imagination. White floral curtains hung from the tall windows. The curtains of the four-poster bed were made of the same fabric, as were the bedspread and the many pillows. A fire blazed in the chimney, seeming to be waiting for her, and above it hung a massive mirror with a gold rim. Small armchairs and stools stood at a low table that was laden with books and seemed on the verge of collapsing under the weight. Books lay even on the long pile carpet that extended across the entire room.

"Come, sit down," said Anne-Louise, pointing to one of the armchairs. While Marianne was taking a seat, Anne-Louise took a book from under the bed and put on a mysterious look as she ran her hand over its velvety spine.

"Your room is beautiful," exclaimed Marianne, hardly daring to move for fear of breaking something.

"Please, call me Anne-Lou, will you?" Anne-Louise opened her book and showed it to Marianne. The pages were neatly inscribed in delicate handwriting.

"I ... I ... I can't read, Anne-Lou," said Marianne meekly.

"You will learn, very quickly."

"Do you think so?" asked Marianne, surprised, and hopeful at the same time.

"Of course. You are smart and observant. In two months, you will be able to read all the books that are here."

"And what's in this book?"

"I wrote this one." Anne-Louise was clearly proud of it.

"Oh! What do you write?"

"I make up stories. This one is about a princess who falls madly in love with a young trader, an impossible love ... oh, that's so romantic."

Marianne had to giggle.

"Have you ever been in love, Maria? Is it alright if I call you Maria?"

"Yes, you can, and yes ... I was." Marianne looked down in embarrassment, feeling herself blush and hoping that it wouldn't show in the dim candlelight.

"Sorry, I didn't mean to embarrass you, but that sounds so terribly exciting. Love in the country ... tell me about it, please. This is so exciting and it inspires me." Anne-Louise looked excited and genuinely interested.

"Well, there isn't much to tell," said Marianne, thinking of Jacques. What should she say? That he was the most handsome boy in all Cholet? That they had sworn eternal love when they were only eight?

"Every juicy detail is important. Have you already kissed?" Marianne was taken by surprise at the directness of her self-confident hostess, and she felt the blood rush to her cheeks with such force that even the dim light would not come to her rescue. "Hey, Maria, you don't need to be ashamed. This is quite normal. I've already kissed many boys. How else is one to know who is the right one among them? So, tell me."

"Yes, we have already kissed," Marianne admitted, relieved to have said it out loud and that no lightning had flashed from the sky to strike her dead. Her mouth curled up into a mischievous smile, and Anne-Louise grinned knowingly. Satisfaction blazed in her eyes, as well as a craving for more.

"Where? When? Now come on, tell me. Don't make me drag the words out of you!" Anne-Louise became quite jittery.

"We were at a village festival and Jacques took my hand, steered me behind a house, and asked me if I still wanted to be his wife."

"Oh, my God, how romantic," cried Anne-Louise, placing a hand on her lace neckline. "But why 'still'?"

"Because we had already sworn eternal love when we were eight."

"Maria, that's wonderful!" Anne-Louise scribbled something in her book, then looked again expectantly at Marianne, raising her eyebrows.

"Well, and then he leaned over and kissed me," whispered Marianne, fearing that someone might overhear the awkward conversation. But she had to secretly admit that it was wonderful to confide in someone who seemed to understand what it meant to be in love. She felt the pleasant tingling sensation again, exactly as she had felt when Jacques put his lips on hers for the first time, gentle, shy, and so incredibly exciting.

"And? Is he the right one?"

"What do you mean by that?" Marianne was confused.

"They say that if he is the right one, you can feel it here," said Anne-Louise seriously, clutching her stomach. "Did you feel something there?"

Marianne nodded, and they both had to giggle.

"This is so terribly exciting," said Anne-Louise, sighing theatrically. Marianne went on to tell the story of the neighbor's boy, Jacques, whom she would probably never forget in her life. Anne-Louise listened dreamily.

"And his parents didn't want you? How awful. You poor thing. Do you still love him?"

"Oh yes, I do." Marianne's voice carried the whole ardor of her love for the handsome Jacques.

A knock on the door broke their budding intimacy. A servant brought them hot chocolate on a silver platter, set it down in an empty space on the four-poster bed, and left the room.

"And you?" Marianne ventured to ask. "Have you ever been in love?"

"Oh dear, yes, I'm constantly in love," said Anne-Louise and laughed merrily. Marianne smiled. "In love with life, with love itself, and with all good-looking young men. There are so many of them."

"Anne-Louise!" cried Marianne. It sounded more outraged than she intended. Anne-Louise grinned cheekily.

"You sound like my mother when I have been up to something naughty. But can it be stupid to love with every fiber of your being? Is it my fault if the young men my mother invites to her salon to chat about literature, equality, and justice are so outrageously good-looking, and that every charming smile enchants me?" Another long sigh followed. "Can it be stupid to long for that wonderful tingling sensation anew in your bosom?"

"You feel it every time?" Marianne felt inexperienced and backward. "But if you feel it every time, is it still really love?"

As if Marianne had just shaken her awake from a wonderful dream, Anne-Louise let her hands, which she had been holding up in a theatrical gesture, drop heavily.

"Indeed, it never lasts long. As soon as my mother introduces a new young man to her Salon de littérature, it happens to me again." The girls laughed.

"What is a Salon de literature?" Marianne wanted to know and was ashamed of her ignorance. The greenish-blue silk of Anne-Louise's dress rustled as she bent over her cup.

"Oh, of course, sorry, you wouldn't know that. A salon is a place where famous and not so famous artists or art lovers, members of the bourgeoisie, and especially aristocrats meet to debate on books and new ideas, to exchange opinions, and to discuss."

"That sounds very interesting."

"Yes, it is. Above all, you see new faces all the time."

Anne-Louise rolled her eyes meaningfully. Marianne laughed out loud but immediately held a hand over her mouth in embarrassment. Her new friend seemed to know every trick in the book. In the country, she would certainly acquire a terrible reputation and could no longer dare to venture out on the streets. But here in Paris, the free interaction with the other gender seemed to be something quite natural and not a cause for fuss, according to Anne-Louise. But Marianne still had her doubts.

"Our priest would have dismissed all this as the devil's doing," she whispered conspiratorially. She felt a pang at the thought of her home. But she did not want to be ungrateful, because, in the end, she had certainly been lucky with the Neckers.  "Oh, what do I care about a priest," said Anne-Louise. "My mother is a Protestant and comes from Switzerland," she explained. "She is the daughter of a pastor and very religious. To be honest, my mother is very strict. The only thing they allow me to do is debate with the gentlemen because they value my upbringing, good relationships, and politics."

"Politics? What is that?" Marianne felt stupid, like a real country bumpkin.

"Well... hmm... how should I best explain? Politics is the name given to all matters related to the public, a community, and its government. If you understand the one, you also understand the other. It is the art of presenting your ideas to others in order to forward your own interests."

"Oh," said Marianne. "And your mother wants you to learn that? I mean... to speak and debate like a man?" As far as Marianne could remember, it was the men who went to the taverns to discuss the grievances among themselves. She had sometimes heard her mother rant about this or that state of affairs, but the parents had never suggested that the girls should think about it for themselves, or develop their own opinion and express it. It almost seemed wicked to her, but at the same time terribly exciting.

"Yes. She says that society is going through a change and that women have an important role to play in this change, a proper role, at last." Marianne tried to grasp the scope of what was being said. Should women get involved? The thought was absurd ... and yet she liked it. "Take a look at Olympe de Gouge, for example. She recently read her play, which is highly critical of society, to the Marquise de Montesson. It was about the dreadful and unjust treatment of slaves in the colonies. And do you know what? Montesson promptly proposed it for the Comédie-Française, and it was included in the repertoire."

Marianne was amazed that a woman not only wrote plays but was even supported by a marquise; however, Anne-Louise's knowledge impressed her the most. Marianne smiled to herself. How new and unfamiliar this world was to her.

As if she had guessed the sad thoughts of her new companion, Anne-Louise picked up another little book and opened it. "This is my diary," she said solemnly and began to write. Every now and then she looked up and winked at Marianne, who sat quietly next to her, allowing the many new impressions to sink in. Had her sisters also fared as well? What were they doing at this very moment? She was again overcome by grief.

"Here, I'll read out to you what I wrote today: Dear Diary... that's how I always start..." Anne-Louise grinned and Marianne smiled back. "A miracle has happened. My dear mother has finally managed to find, instead of brittle, boring, nosy, snooty and uptight women..." she pulled faces while reading, and Marianne giggled, covering her mouth with her hand "a treasure for me. Marianne is shy and reserved, but she seems to be smart and sincere without any sign of conceit. That immediately appealed to me. She is also really very pretty. See you very soon, Anne-Louise." Marianne blushed again. Anne-Louise laughed and put her diary aside. "You have such a lovely laugh; don't hold your hand over your mouth, Maria." Anne-Louise took a sip of chocolate, which had meanwhile cooled down.

"So, now I have a new task," she said cheerfully. "I'll have to educate you." Marianne liked the idea, even if she secretly resolved not to fall in love all the time.

The girls talked through half the night, and Marianne found it difficult to fall asleep afterward. So many new and exciting things seemed to await her, but she also thought wistfully about her parents and sisters. What would become of Father and Mother? Filled with longing, she thought of her parents' farm, her home, of the simple life that she liked. Would she be able to get used to this noble, aristocratic way of life?

She suddenly realized that she had no way of getting in touch with her sisters. Why had her parents been unwilling to give her the addresses of the sisters? Marianne resolved to do everything possible to reunite her family.

***

Chapter 4

Paris, 1782 - Jeanne

Jeanne screamed and kicked, but the fat man held her firmly. It flashed through her mind how a pitchfork had once slipped out of her father's hand, and the end of the handle had fallen on his crotch. He had hunched over and cursed loudly, which wasn't like her father at all. When she secretly asked her mother about it that evening, she had confided in Jeanne that men were very sensitive down there. So, Jeanne kicked with all her might, took several swings, and rammed her knee into his testicles with such force that he yelled, loosening his grip. He groaned, clutching under his belly. The small victory that Jeanne had won gave her unexpected strength. But to her despair, the shopkeeper did not let go. She looked around feverishly for something to hit him on the head while the man fumbled with her dress again. He had apparently recovered from her kicks, and Jeanne was beginning to weaken. With her left hand, she managed to grab the leg of the little stool near her, lift it with the last of her remaining strength and smash it against the old man's head as hard as she could. With a howl, her tormentor fell to one side, holding the back of his head.

Seizing the moment, she kicked herself free and wanted to run, but the blow had been not been hard enough to knock the shopkeeper out completely. His bulkiness prevented him from following her, but he reached out for her. In a flash and with her pulse racing, she ran down the stairs. By the time the old man was up from the bed of straw, she was already down at the front door. She struggled and jumped to reach the bolt but could not slide it open. Jeanne was losing her lead.

The shopkeeper came down the stairs, groaning and cursing, and when he got to the last step, he reached out for her, his face contorted with fury.

The latch moved. Jeanne opened the door and rushed out into the street.

"Just wait, you beast, I'll catch you!" she heard the shopkeeper roar. The ice-cold air of the night lashed at her like a warning, as if to make her aware of the dangers that lurked in the dark and at every street corner, which might be even worse than the old man. Jeanne ran as if some monster from the tales of old legends were after her. She just wanted to get away from this stinking caveman. "You will never rest ... because I will be breathing down your neck!" The voice of the old man who, only a moment ago, had been lying on top of her echoed through the night. Jeanne wandered aimlessly through the dark alleys with their eerie light. She just wanted to be gone. No time for grief, no time to regret her decision, to just get away!

The houses towered over her like ghosts that might bend down to her at any moment, threatening to devour her. She stepped into mud, then slimy garbage, slipping with her bare feet. Gasping for breath, she had to stop at some point, and sat down in the corner of a backyard, panting. She seemed to have lost him and listened to be quite sure. But she could only hear her own spasmodic breathing, the violent palpitation of her heart, and the rustling in her ears. Now that she felt safe, she began to cry bitterly. Two feral cats fought and screeched close by, and the muddy dirt on her feet was drying and crusting over. There was a smell of rot in the air.

How was she supposed to find her home now? How would she get in touch with her Mama and her Papa? Was she lost now? Marianne, Madeleine, she thought and went on sobbing. Mother must have told her sisters where to find her, and now she had messed up everything by running away like a coward. She was ashamed. Should she have stayed there and endured it? She thought of the shop and what fun it had been to work there. But no! She shook her head. Nobody had the right to touch her there, where the man had put his fingers. That was something Mama had clearly taught her daughters. She believed she could still feel his touch, smell the penetrating stench of his sweat. There was no more hope, it was all over; she would die miserably on the street. No one would ever find her again in this big city. She started as a thought suddenly crossed her mind. Yes, of course, she had to find her sisters! She knew that both were here in the city. She would search the streets, every one of them, and she would recognize the houses, she was sure of that. She felt for her doll, which she had hidden under her dress against her breast before going to sleep. Jeanne could feel herself getting weaker. She was tired, so very tired ... She hugged the doll tighter, leaned her head against the wall, exhausted, and cried herself to sleep.

***

Liese - Cholet, 1782

Liese Cotin tossed and turned in bed. There was a time when it was hunger that would keep her from sleeping, but that night, she yearned for her children.

"Liese, we had to do it, please calm down," whispered Jean-Louis. "They are now sitting in a warm place with their bellies full." Her grief left him feeling powerless.

She stared numbly at the light of the moon reflecting on the wall. She knew that her husband was right and yet ... the house felt empty and desolate, and life had lost its meaning. The images that came to her mind tormented her. Small, sweet-smelling baby bodies that snuggled against her. Laughing, round blue and brown eyes, bouncing curls, smeared mouths, torn clothes, children's laughter ... she also thought ruefully about the punishments she had inflicted on her daughters, such as the occasional spanking. She sobbed.

"Liese, another four weeks, and the children would have died, one after the other. Just think of the neighbor's children." That family had buried three children within just a few days.

"We had agreed that I had to stop smuggling," Jean-Louis tried desperately to justify his actions. It was common for Breton farmers to work as henchmen in the salt smuggling business for an extra income, an extremely dangerous undertaking involving severe punishment. The men secretly transported salt in their barges on the Loire at night. Scouts set traps for the smugglers, and many of them got caught. Most of them were exiled to the French colonies as punishment. They never came back from there, and terrifying stories were told about their whereabouts. Yes, Liese had wanted her husband to stop, she had to admit it, because the meager earnings from the, in fact lucrative, smuggling were not worth the risk of being discovered and punished. Only the middlemen in Paris reaped the big earnings.

Liese sighed. She knew that her girls were safe now, but they were so terribly far away ... Would she ever see her daughters again, would she ever take them in her arms?

They had avoided telling them the whereabouts of the other sisters so that each could come to terms with her situation and settle in quickly. How could she have explained to her daughters why Marianne would grow up in a mansion and Jeanne with a shabby shopkeeper? It seemed too unfair for her to justify.

Marianne was of marriageable age. Sooner or later, she would have left the house, even if it had only been a few kilometers away. And Madeleine, my cheerful Madeleine! Liese struggled to suppress a rising sob again. Madeleine would fend for herself, Liese was sure of that.

"We should at least have kept Jeanne; she is still so small." She again gave way to violent sobbing, her whole body shaking.

"It would have been her death sentence, and you know it."

"Oh dear God, forgive us for what we did. Forgive us for abandoning our children. Please, make them happy." The tears kept streaming down her cheeks, and Liese wondered if she would ever be able to forgive Jean-Louis. But deep down, she knew that he loved his daughters more than anything, and had only acted in their best interest. Not everyone was lucky enough to have so many options. Thanks to his, albeit dubious, business relations in Paris, he had been able to secure employment for his children. However, the pain was too fresh, too strong for Liese to accept this. Again, she could not restrain her sobs.

"Come on then, cry your heart out," whispered Jean-Louis, putting his arm around her. The pent-up grief finally broke out. "Now we have to think about our survival, Liese. It will be difficult enough, believe me ..."

Chapter 5

Alain - Paris, 1782

Alain plodded through the alleys. He had put on a lot of weight and his knees ached from the burden of his massive body. But that was unimportant. He had other worries. How was he supposed to make them understand what had happened? He had been racking his brains to find excuses since last night. He just couldn't think of any.

When a rich customer tried to talk him into employing the little girl - a half-starved peasant brat - he had thought a long time about it. It didn't suit him to have one more mouth to feed. He still had to provide for the old girl, his wedded woman and a good for nothing. She kept a maid as well as a servant whom he also had to feed. Did they think he was swimming in money? He had shaken his head furiously. And now, a shop assistant as well?

Nevertheless, he had agreed with the customer, because he was afraid of losing him otherwise. At the time, Alain, in his fury, had rushed into the restaurant to join his cronies and vent his anger. His drinking companions' eyes had lit up, and they had told him about the countless ways of putting such a boon to advantage. Ultimately, his misfortune could turn into a gold mine and within a few days, he had made the necessary arrangements. First, he wanted to test the little one himself ... An ugly grin spread on his face. But now she had escaped him! He cursed aloud at the thought.

A woman passing by turned to him and frowned. He turned into the next alley and stood in front of the tavern. Alain took a deep breath, braced himself, and opened the door. The smoky air that came out had a calming effect on him. The bellowing men, the barmaids running around, and the raucous noise were like a soothing balm for his fears. Alain let himself fall heavily into his regular place and ordered a pitcher of wine. He drank the first mug in one gulp and belched loudly. His thoughts gradually became clearer.

"Hey, ho, Alain," Johan shouted to him. "Where is our merchandise?" Alain could not help a slight wince and spilled some wine. He broke out in a cold sweat. He saw the man stand up and walk over to him. Damn it all! Johan sat down next to him and gave him a hearty pat on the shoulder. His lustful smile made Alain's fear flare up again, and he wondered how he could talk his way out of this.

"She's gone."

"What do you mean, gone?"

"She ran away."

"You've got to be crazy ..." Johan stared at him in disbelief.

Alain signaled to the passing barmaid for a second mug. He wasn't keen on inviting anyone but he felt guilty today.

"I guess you wanted to test her for yourself and she didn't like that, right?" He stared at him, startled. How did Johan know that? But why deny it? Alain nodded. So what? After all, she was his property.

"You idiot!" shouted Johan, and slapped his hand furiously on the wooden table, making the pitcher bounce. "You should have known that, you bastard! You couldn't wait, could you?" He gave Alain a knock on the head. The maid placed the ordered mug on the table and disappeared.

"When Louis finds out, he will make mincemeat of you."  Alain feared Louis the most. "He had already promised her to Gourdan. She will go into a rage."

"Is he crazy?" Alain was really getting scared. The Petite Comtesse, as the most famous madam was known, might be so enraged as to forbid him entry. And then what? Would he have to give up his privileges because of this brat? "She shouldn't be short of impoverished girls."  He defiantly took a hefty swig. "This one or another ... we will simply find one that looks like her."

"You're completely insane." Johan took a long gulp from the wine. "Do you know many girls with blue eyes? Such young things are in short supply, although some mothers would willingly sell their daughters. Gourdan certainly has the calendar already full for your little dove. The demand for young merchandise is particularly big and she will not be able to afford this loss." Johan shook his head in dismay.

"I'll take care of that.  I'll find her again. Where would such a little one go?"

"Hmm."

"I'll start looking for her as from tomorrow and I swear by God, I'll get this damned bitch back for you."

"You'd better pay the money back though."

A cold shiver ran down Alain's spine. "I can't do that for... for the time being," he stammered in embarrassment. "Don't worry though ... I'll get her ..."

***

Chapter 6

Madeleine - Paris, 1782

"Madeleine, hurry up!" The rumbling voice of the boss resounded from the shop to the store where she was looking for the lace which the waiting customer had asked for. Madeleine's hands were trembling and she felt weak, almost faint.

"Reticella lace, Reticella lace", she murmured feverishly to herself but for the life of her, she could not remember what it looked like. She was assailed by hot flushes of fear. She did not want to fail. After the first difficult night, then an extensive breakfast, she had decided not to question the decision of her parents any longer and to honor them. She wanted to, and simply had to keep this job. It was a perfectly acceptable employment and only few had such an opportunity. After all, it was even said that the boss, Mademoiselle Bertin, would personally look after Queen Marie-Antoinette's wardrobe, be her personal advisor, and go in and out of court as if it were her second home. On top of that, Madeleine got along better and better with the other girls, who all seemed to stick together.

Madeleine looked around, hoping to find her new friend somewhere. But no, how stupid of me, she's making a delivery right now, she thought sadly. I'll have to get out of this mess on my own.

She thought hard. What else did Louisa tell her yesterday? Threads are drawn from a woven fabric and the resulting webs are embroidered with buttonhole stitches ... right, it's the fabric with the holes in it. Madeleine pulled the right bale out of the mass of cloth bales and carried it quickly to the shop, where Mademoiselle Bertin was keeping the client busy with funny anecdotes. When Madeleine put the bale on the counter in front of them, the boss gave her a stern look but immediately turned back to her customer with a friendly smile.

"This wonderful, elegant lace came in only yesterday from Italy, and we only have this one bale in stock. The whole of Paris will go crazy over it."

Madeleine started to raise her hand to correct Rose Bertin, as she had counted at least six of those bales. But when the boss again glared at her angrily and indicated with a movement of her head that she should get out of sight into the storage room, she curtsied and withdrew.

She leaned against the closed door with a sigh of relief. Not finding the material would have been unthinkable. She would urgently have to find a way to distinguish between the many bales. She brooded over this. Since she could neither read nor write, she decided to seek advice from Louise in the evening.

***

Chapter 7

Jeanne - Paris, 1782

"I couldn't just let the poor thing freeze to death! How can you be so inhuman?"

The whispering voice woke Jeanne from her deep sleep. But she still kept her eyes closed. Was she dreaming? There was a strong fragrance of flowers and other smells that she couldn't place. She felt warm and cozy.

"Do you have to burden yourself even more with a brat in your situation? Isn't it hard enough as it is for us to get by?" hissed another female voice. "Are you going to save all Parisian children from freezing to death? Well, good luck then. We could start a regiment of little urchins," she added mockingly.

"I'll find a solution. She will have to work if necessary."

There was a loud snort.

"Don't make me laugh. Are you thinking of making her work the streets? She is still a baby. What man would want such a brat with no tits? Well, there may be a few here and there, but there is not much demand for that." There was a pause. "You could present her as our attraction, as a particularly juicy one."

"Shut up, you goose. Take care of your clients and leave the little one in peace. She will have nothing to do with this, do you hear? This is my concern. Just forget about her."

Jeanne felt a protective hand on her back. She half-opened her eyes.  A young, very heavily made-up woman looked at her lovingly. Jeanne was relieved. The woman had a friendly face.

"Hello there? What have you been up to, for having to lie half-frozen in a corner at night?" Overcome by her emotions and touched by the first nice person she had seen in days, Jeanne sobbed and blurted out her story.

"Well, there you have it!" came the other woman's voice again. "I told you, there are pigs like this ... she might make us rich yet ..."

"Will you be quiet now, you bitch!" hissed Jeanne's savior indignantly. "I'm Claire. And what's your name, ma petite?"

"My name is Jeanne, and this is Emma." Jeanne took out the doll. Claire smiled.

"You seem to come from my area. I know that kind of dress; it comes from the Vendée."

"Yes, I come from a farm near Cholet. And you?"

"I'm from Nantes. You see, I was right, we're neighbors," Claire whispered, winking conspiratorially at her. "That witch back there is called Julie, but don't pay any attention to her. She says a lot of mean things, but you can count on her when you need her."

"The witch is not deaf," came a threatening voice from the opposite, dark corner of the room.

"She's just pretending to be spiteful, but she's alright," said Claire, winking at Jeanne again. Jeanne giggled.

"I have to go to ... er ... work now, little one. Don't leave the room, not on any account, understood?" Jeanne nodded eagerly but was sorry that the kind Claire would be leaving her alone again.

"Here, take this." Claire handed her a piece of pastry that smelled wonderfully sweet.

"And you?"

"I've already eaten."

Before leaving the room, the two women sprayed each other from top to bottom with a liquid they called perfume, which tickled her nose so much that Jeanne had to sneeze. That's where the floral scent comes from, thought Jeanne. Jeanne could still hear fragments of conversation coming from the hallway.

"You have really taken a liking to that child."

"Let that be my worry, Julie."

"What if customers come to the room?"

She let out a disdainful snort.

"I invite whoever I want, when I want, and for as long as I want. If you don't like it, then you know what to do. Not a word more about this, got it?"

Jeanne heard the front door on the first floor slam shut with a loud bang, and for a brief moment, the whole building seemed to shake. And then, they were gone.

Almost starved, Jeanne greedily stuffed the cake into her mouth and almost choked on the dry crumbs that gathered into a lump in her throat. She quickly looked for a jug of water, found it on the little table by the bed, and washed it down.

Jeanne looked around the room. It was very small and sparsely furnished. Colorful dresses with flounces hung on a stand. On closer inspection, she noticed that all the clothes were patched and had often been mended, like her own clothes at home. Home ... Don't think about it, she reminded herself, and kept looking around. There were used plates and mugs on a table with four chairs. In the corner at the back, there was another bed, which was separated from the rest of the room with a curtain. That must be Julie's bed, Jeanne thought. Claire's bed also had a curtain, she now noticed. How nice. That way she could hide from Julie's evil eye.

A line was stretched across the room, on which Jeanne's belongings were hanging to dry. The little dress immediately made her think of the horrible shopkeeper, and she winced. She was glad that she would never have to go there again.

Jeanne considered. She wanted to make herself useful so that the women would want to keep her. She started tidying up the beds and clearing the table, just as her mother had taught her. She found a broom next to the door and as she swept the stone floor, dust kicked up and she began to cough. How had her Mama done it? She took the pitcher from the bedside table and dripped water on the floor. Indeed, the floor was easier to sweep. She looked proudly at the result. Then she wiped the table, the dresser, and the filthy window clean, put the clothes on hangers, made the beds, and dusted the cross that hung over the front door. She looked at the result with satisfaction and hoped that Julie would like her too now.

After she was satisfied that she had done everything, she would have liked to go into the yard to fetch fresh water. But she did not dare to disregard her rescuer's warning. So she snuggled into Claire's bed, drew the curtain, and waited impatiently for her new friend to return. Her thoughts wandered off into another world, into a world where she was independent and needed no one. She imagined what it would be like to travel and find out how other people lived. She also longed to have her own shop, like the shopkeeper. Jeanne was convinced that she would do better than this beastly old hag, much better. She thought of the slap, the injustice, and the abuse by the old man. She shuddered with disgust at the thought that he could have had his way. Did his wife know what her husband was up to?

She didn't want to think about it anymore. She just wanted to look towards the future. And as she lay there all alone, waiting, with no one to confide in, with the constant feeling of fear in her stomach despite the temporary security in which she appeared to be, she swore to herself that one day, she would fare better. Yes, one day, she would be famous, and respected, and loved, and she would never have to be afraid again, never have to run away again. And cradled by these pleasant thoughts, she fell asleep.

***

Chapter 8

Madeleine - Paris, 1782

Hours seemed to have gone by and Madeleine was dreadfully bored. Everyone had left the workshop. The sewing tables stood alone and deserted in the midday sun that shone through the tall windows, and Madeleine could think of no explanation where the girls had gone. She had gone to the warehouse to find another bale of cloth for the boss. She brought it to her in the shop but when she came back, all the dressmakers had disappeared. So, she had decided to wait until her colleagues came back. What else could she do? She did not dare leave the workshop to look for them for fear that she might get fired for that. She had noticed that this could happen very quickly because Mademoiselle Bertin insisted on punctuality, accuracy, and obedience. The smallest faux pas or the smallest cause for complaint could constitute a reason for dismissal. 

Suddenly the back door flew open, causing Madeleine to flinch and jump from her seat. The girls rushed back into the workshop like cackling geese, and as if nothing had happened, they took their seats again and went back to work. When Louise passed Madeleine's table, she paused, puzzled.

"Well, where were you, Mady? We were afraid you'd been fired." Louisa came closer.

"Why didn't you come into the dining room for lunch?" asked Lucienne, a plump redhead.

Madeleine looked at her, dumbfounded.

"Oh no, I ... I didn't know anything about it."

"When the clock over there," Lucienne pointed all-knowingly at the large grandfather clock, "strikes twelve, then you can take a break."

Madeleine's eyes welled up with tears. She seemed to be doing everything wrong. She let herself drop despondently on a stool. Louisa came up to her and whispered: "You can't read the time, right?"

Madeleine shook her head in embarrassment. She couldn't hold back the pent-up tears which ran down her cheeks but she didn't want to be looked upon as a sissy either, so she quickly wiped off the salty tears.

"Louisa," she whispered back, "would you ... can you ... I need help to learn all this." Madeleine pointed at the bales of cloth.

"Don't worry, Mady, I've already thought of something for you. We will practice together tonight, after work."

Rose Bertin burst into the store and clapped her hands loudly. "Get to work, hop, hop, what is there to gossip about? Isn’t the lunch break enough?" There was something almost eerie about the sudden silence. The girls bowed their heads over their workplaces; the seamstresses over the stitches, the cutters over the designs they were working on, and Louisa and Madeleine did the embroidery, something that Madeleine mastered reasonably well. That wasn't enough, and she knew it. But thanks to Louisa, she would make it. She would just have to work a lot more than the others in the first few weeks. Anyway, she could not and did not want to disappoint her parents. And of course, she also knew that there was no going back, unless to face starvation...

***

Chapter 9

Jeanne - Paris, 1782

There was a clatter in the hallway, and a few minutes later Julie was in the doorway.

"Oh, you look like you have been bored, little one." She tossed her bag carelessly on the table. Jeanne was convinced that Julie wasn't a bad person, because otherwise, Claire wouldn't be living with her. Her mother had taught her to always look for the good in people. And Jeanne was just about to start speaking when a man stepped in behind Julie through the door frame into the room. He was so tall that he had to stoop slightly to avoid bumping his head against the door frame.

"Hmm," he muttered sullenly in Jeanne's direction, which was probably meant as a greeting. Jeanne curtsied politely. He looked like just another poor fellow; his patched trousers, the tattered jacket, and the cap full of holes that he wore betrayed his humble origin. 

"Come here, make yourself comfortable," said Julie, assuming a strangely unnatural posture. The man looked around the little room as if he were looking for something.

"How then? Don't you have a room to yourself?"

"Sure, we are alone here. Don't worry about the brat. She's nobody. My roommate took her in. Don't let that embarrass you."

"Should I do it in front of a child?"

"Pah, a child! Just take a look at her, everything has started to grow and bloom. She is not a child any longer!" exclaimed Julie.

"Still, I don't like spectators."

Jeanne sat cross-legged on Claire's bed, praying that Claire would come back at last. Julie was getting too creepy for her. As she tried to look away from what was happening, she wondered if she should just leave the room despite Claire's instructions. What would Claire want her to do? That she should stay here?

Julie walked up to the man, pressed her body against his, rubbing against him, and raised her lips to his ear.

"You're really hot, come on, don't make a fuss. I have a curtain." The man seemed undecided. Julie seemed to have made him waver with her sudden proximity. "Besides, that one here can join in, what do you think? Two for the price of one?" She giggled gleefully.

Jeanne felt the visitor's eyes rest on her, and when she looked back at him, she saw the same glassy expression in his eyes as in those of the shopkeeper she had run away from only yesterday.

"Very well," said the man to Jeanne's horror. "If you assure me that she is no longer a child, then I would like a threesome."

Jeanne caught her breath.

"Come on, little one. You heard. There is work for you."

Jeanne shook her head.

"No, please. Just pretend I'm not here. I'm only waiting for Claire."

"Come on, make it snappy," said Julie angrily. "Do you think you can benefit from our hospitality for free? Times are too hard for that. So move your backside and come over here."

"I'll just go then. I didn't know that I ... that I ..."

"Come on, out with it. You can't say the word, right? Whoring! Yes, little one, what were you thinking, huh?" Julie came up to Jeanne and grabbed her arm roughly. "What else should people like us do in life? Do you have a better idea? No? Sooner or later, you will end up just like us, so it might as well be sooner."

"I don't have all day," the man complained. "Should I look elsewhere?"

"No, everything is all right. The little one surely doesn't want to spoil my business, does she?" She looked at Jeanne angrily, beckoning with her eyebrows. Slowly, she got up and allowed Julie to drag her to the man.

"Come on, get it off," said Julie gruffly to Jeanne while she undressed the man.

Jeanne hesitated. How could she wriggle out of that? She peered at the door; Julie had pushed the bolt. She started to feel sick but slowly untied the straps of her dress, trying to buy time.

The slap on her cheek came unexpectedly. "Come on, the customer doesn't have all day. You heard that, didn't you?"

Jeanne pulled her dress over her head and stood there in her underwear. In the meantime, Julie had also taken off her clothes and led the hesitant man to sit on her bed. Julie started kissing, fondling, and fiddling with him. The man groaned, and his gaze wandered over Julie's shoulders at Jeanne who was still standing in the room, bewildered.

"Come here and bare yourself completely."

Suddenly, the door rattled.

"Hey, Julie? Are you there?" It was Claire.

"I'm busy, come back later."

"I see," Jeanne heard Claire say. "But where is the little one?"

"I'm here, Claire, I'm here," Jeanne called desperately and ran to the door, grabbed the bolt and tried to push it, but Julie got ahead of her, jerked her around, and slapped her once more. 

"Will you do as I say, you beast," the prostitute shrieked.

"Julie, what's going on? Open up immediately."

"Look after your own business, damn it."

"This is my business; the little one is under my protection."

"Nonsense. I also pay rent here and have a right to her, so stay out of this. Take a walk and come back in ten minutes."

"No," Jeanne cried out. "Get me out of here, I beg you, Claire."

"If you touch one hair of the little one, you stinking vermin," Claire's voice had suddenly taken a deep, threatening tone, "then you are going to get it from me, do you hear? I am the main tenant and if I so wish, you will find yourself on the street tonight. So don't do anything rash, Julie, is that clear?"

"I've had enough, now," the customer shouted angrily and got dressed. "What a bloody bunch of useless whores!" He got up, walked to the door, and slid the bolt back. Claire immediately burst into the room.
