"I hope you come to a miserable end, you lousy vermin." With these words, the man disappeared into the dark hallway. Jeanne heard the clatter of footsteps as he raced down the stairway, two steps at a time. Relieved, she fell into Claire's arms, whimpering.

"Honestly," said Julie, as she tied her bodice again, "acting like a fine lady, yet she'll end up like us anyway." She spat on the floor in front of Claire.

"Pack your things and get out of here. I'm fed up with you."

"What? Are you crazy? How am I supposed to find a new place to stay so fast? No way! Besides, I've already paid the rent for this month. So, that's out of the question. I'm staying."

Claire took a leather pouch from under her dress, jerked it open angrily, counted a few coins, and tossed them at Julie's feet.

"Here, I'll pay you the excess, and now get out of here before I forget myself."

"Hey, you must be crazy. The guy didn't even touch her. Such a fuss for nothing."

"Oh yeah? And that's why she's standing there in her underwear? Which part of the sentence 'No, Jeanne will not turn tricks for us' did you not understand?"

"She didn't do that either. I just wanted to initiate her a little bit. The client is king, or have you forgotten that since your maternal instinct took over?"

Julie looked down at Claire contemptuously. "Look around: she has cleaned. Do you really think we can afford a cleaning lady?" Again she spat provocatively on the floor, this time looking at Jeanne as if to tell her that she didn't give a damn about the trouble she had taken.

"Get out of here," Claire said, in a dangerously low voice.

"Come on, Claire. Surely you can see that you can't feed her without her earning something sooner or later.  Right now would have been sooner..." Julie shrugged and smiled foolishly.

"Out, get lost, go on," cried Claire angrily, grabbing the clothes from the hangers that Jeanne had previously hung so neatly, and throwing them on the floor, trampling on them as if to wipe up the spit with them.

"Alright, I'm going!"

Claire crossed her arms and stared at Julie, waiting for the former roommate to finally clear out. Julie quickly gathered everything up, took a shabby suitcase from under her bed, and stuffed her clothes into it. After she had made sure one last time that she hadn't forgotten anything, she bent down again, picked up the coins from the floor, and stopped in front of Claire. Now that Julie realized that Claire was deadly serious, she looked at her with hatred.

"You'll pay for this, you bitch," she hissed, went to the door, stopped briefly, and turned to Jeanne again, who had been hiding behind Claire the whole time, hoping to escape attention.

"And you, little brat, will not be able to avoid it. It will no doubt catch up with you at some point. And then it will be far less pleasant than with a teacher like me, you can believe that. Even you will have to face the music. You think you're something better, eh? But you're not"

"Get out," Claire shrieked, pointing her finger. "Just piss off. Nobody needs your advice."

The door slammed shut and, as before, they heard the clatter on the stairs, another loud bang as Julie slammed the front door behind her, after which the stillness had quite an eerie quality.

Claire let out a deep breath, her shoulders sagging from the exertion. Jeanne timidly came out from behind her.

"Thank you," she whispered and took her rescuer's cold hand in hers.

"Oh, don't worry," Claire said gently.

"I'm so sorry you lost your lodger because of me."

"You don't have to be," Claire replied. "She had been a pain in the neck for a long time."

Exhausted, Claire took off her cape, hung it on a door hook, and sat on a chair.

"Julie is right about one thing though." Jeanne's spirits fell again. What did Claire mean by that? "There are difficult times ahead of us because now, I have no rental income anymore."

"Then rent it out again," suggested Jeanne.

"Oh no, it will always be the same. I just have to work more."

"I can work too," cried Jeanne excitedly.

"Out of the question, do you hear?" Claire said indignantly.

"No, I don't mean like you. I could work in a shop."

Claire shook her head tiredly and took a small package out of the folds of her skirt.

"Here, for you."

Jeanne took it and unwrapped it. It was a fresh loaf of bread. Jeanne broke it in half and held one out to Claire.

"No, you eat. I've already eaten outside."

Jeanne felt instinctively that Claire was lying.

"But I already ate the cake, and I have a very small stomach from going hungry in the country. I can't put much in there. So take it, otherwise, it will dry up, and that would be a shame, wouldn't it?"

Reluctantly, Claire accepted the piece of bread Jeanne held out to her and took a quick bite.

"All right," she mumbled while chewing, "if you insist."

They smiled at each other. Jeanne felt a warm feeling of optimism rise within her. If you had someone to love, to share your sorrows with, to fight for, then everything would be fine.

That evening, Claire went to bed early too, not wanting to leave Jeanne alone again. But even though a bed had become available, they snuggled together and slept.

***

It wasn't long before Jeanne and Claire became perfectly in tune with each other. Jeanne turned out to be the perfect housekeeper, while Claire tried to work harder to make ends meet. She continued to insist that Jeanne should not leave the room, something which was, of course, impossible for Jeanne to observe. It started with fetching water. How else could she have cleaned the house and have enough water to wash and drink? So, she went into the building's courtyard every day and pumped water into the bucket. As the tasks became an easy routine, she started to get bored and at some point, she just couldn't take it anymore. She grabbed one of Claire's shawls, wrapped it around her shoulders, and left the apartment for a short walk.

As always, the streets of Paris were bustling with activity, and Jeanne slipped inconspicuously between people and stalls, hopped over the rubbish lying on the ground, and avoided a group of nasty fellows who seemed to be just waiting to cause trouble. While maneuvering her way around, she bumped into someone and spun around, frightened at having drawn attention to herself.

"Hey, you clumsy ape, can't you pay attention?" The voice belonged to a boy who was holding strange papers in his hands. He was only half a head taller than she was.

"What do you mean with 'clumsy ape'?" Jeanne said indignantly and stretched out her chin. The fact that he had beautiful eyes didn't give him the right to be rude to her. "Clumsy ape yourself."

The crooked grin that appeared on the boy's face was so stunning that Jeanne forgot her anger. "Ouch, you give as good as you get, don't you," he said.

"And now you're making fun of me," Jeanne scolded, crossing her arms over her chest.

"Where do you come from? You look so ... well-groomed ..."

"Well-groomed, me?" She looked down at her dress. The fact that she was constantly mending, darning, and washing could very well create the impression. And when she saw how scruffy the boy was, she got a better understanding of the remark. "Where's your Mama? She must be looking for you."

She glared at him. Who did he think he was to treat her so condescendingly?

"I don't need a Mama to go for a walk," she answered cheekily.

"Oh, sorry, the Mademoiselle is going for a stroll," he said, and laughed, bowing deeply to her and holding up a leaflet.

"What are you doing?" Her curiosity was stronger than her anger. He lowered the leaflet again.

"Why are you interested?"

"I'm looking for work. Are you being paid for doing this?"

He nodded.

"But they don't want girls."

"Really?"

"Yes."

"But why?"

"Because it's too dangerous for them. Do you see them, over there?"

Jeanne looked in the direction he indicated and saw the same group of men whom she had avoided before. "If they get their hands on you, they'll kidnap you and after that, you'll never be seen again."

Jeanne was deeply shocked because this was exactly how she imagined such men.

"Then I'll only work together with you. You will protect me."

The boy laughed again and handed a leaflet to a passer-by, who accepted it with thanks.

"You’re joking. I already have enough problems of my own." She was suddenly overcome by sadness and couldn't quite explain why. Somehow she liked this conceited, blond rascal. There was something decent and lovable about him, and she liked the thought that they might meet every day.

"Are you always here?"

"Why?"

"Because I would like to come back tomorrow."

"Hmm," he replied absently, and Jeanne didn't know whether he meant to say yes or no. "Yes, I'm always here, very close to the market, where it's busiest. But tell me, wouldn't it be better if you were to stay safely at home?"

"What do you care?"

"Why do you want to work? You don't look like you need to." A shadow crossed Jeanne's face and the boy seemed to understand immediately. "It's not the way it seems, is it?" Jeanne nodded and looked shamefully at her bare feet. He followed her gaze and understood.

"Do you know what? Just come whenever you want, if it amuses you," he said suddenly, putting on his delightful smile again. "It's alright with me, you pain in the neck."

This time, Jeanne smiled too. Something in the boy's eyes seemed to tell her that he also liked her - or was it just wishful thinking?

"Great, thanks," Jeanne whispered, hardly believing that she now had a friend who also earned his own money.

"But just between us, what do you actually expect to get from this?"

Jeanne smiled mysteriously.

"I'll tell you next time," she said and backed a few steps away from him. He pushed his cap back and scratched his head, which, Jeanne thought, made him look even cuter.

"Are you going already?" Jeanne nodded. Something told her that it was better to let him fidget a little, not to reveal how much she liked his company. "Oh, until tomorrow then?" He seemed almost disappointed and she detected an underlying invitation.

"Maybe," Jeanne replied, grinning. He grinned back.

"Hey, little one!" he shouted after her, and she turned to him again. "What's your name?"

"Jeanne." "And you?"

"Camille," he said, looking at her for a few seconds with anticipation.

Nice name, Jeanne thought but she didn't say it. Anyway, she thought he was a little conceited. Without another word, she turned around and ran towards the apartment without looking back. But she couldn't get the golden mop of hair, the blue eyes, and the crooked grin out of her mind, and she felt a very strange feeling in her stomach, similar to hunger, almost as painful and yet different ...

***

Chapter 10

Alain - Paris, 1783

At last, he had found her. Delighted, he rubbed his hands together. There she was, the little one, babbling to a street boy.

"Keep on prattling while you can," he muttered, squinting. He felt a load falling off his shoulders. He had been looking for her for weeks, day and night, and in all weathers. And all of a sudden, there she stood before him, in broad daylight.

It was about time, because Louis had given him a dressing-down and threatened to smash his shop to pieces if he didn't get the blue-eyed child. Alain had managed to pacify him for a while, assuring him that such a girl couldn't just disappear overnight and that if necessary, he would deliver goods of the same quality to him. But he had to part with a few livres as a refund, otherwise, he would not have gotten away so lightly. But that had only delayed the approaching disaster because he still owed hundreds of livres that Louis had given him in advance for the girl. Now that he had finally found her, his luck had turned. She would pay for all his trouble. When he saw her standing there so cheekily, he wondered whether he should try her out for himself or wait until it was his turn at Gourdan's? He decided on the former. After all, he was the one who found the merchandise and arranged for her to be brought to Paris, and on top of that, endured all the trouble. The week-long search, the cronies breathing down his neck, and being banned from entry at the Contessa's - he now needed compensation. He chuckled contentedly at the thought that he already had her wriggling on his hook without her knowing it.

It seemed that the street boy was accompanying her for a bit, and Alain kept closely but discreetly on their heels. On no account should he lose her again. A decrepit whore stopped him.

"Some fun, honey?"

The woman gazed at him enticingly as she struck a provocative pose in front of him. She swayed her hips and licked her lower lip lecherously. Alain felt the heat rising. It had been a long time ... but he pulled himself together, remembering his priorities.

"Get out of here, you slut!"

He pushed her aside and resumed the chase.

He breathed a sigh of relief because although the children had gained a head start, they were still in sight. They now turned at a corner. He took off in a flash, although he knew that his fear of losing her now was completely irrational. But the very thought tormented him so much that he dragged himself on, panting and gasping.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

It had now become a habit for Camille to accompany her home.

"And how do you get a job like that?"

Jeanne had been showering him with questions for the last half an hour, and he took it good-naturedly, as always.

They had been meeting secretly for weeks, almost every day. They laughed a lot, shared their experiences and the life they led in Paris. She felt on top of the world in his company. But the strange feeling inside her didn't go away, and she even welcomed its presence in her stomach, which made her feel alive. That brought a nice change to the monotonous housework, during which she reflected on the conversations she had with him or would like to have. So far, she hadn't brought up the subject of work, knowing what he thought about it. But the situation was getting increasingly precarious for Claire and herself, and Jeanne just couldn't bear it anymore. She wanted to contribute, even if it was only a chunk of bread a day.

He had told her how he came to distribute leaflets for a printing company and agreed to give Jeanne a few tips to help her find a job, even if he still thought she wouldn't get one. However, she suspected that he simply enjoyed the feeling of being important. He must be about her age, maybe a year or two older than her, and most certainly found it exciting to impress a girl. When he didn't know the answer to her questions, he always pushed his cap back and scratched his head in embarrassment, a gesture she had grown so fond of that she almost missed it when he didn't do it.

"You have to look for a job that is suitable for you. But this will not be easy, little one, because there are thousands like you here."

"I don't believe that."

"What is it, that you don't you believe? That it's going to be difficult?"

"No - that there are thousands like me. You may not know it yet, but I am unique." 

He laughed, showing his even line of teeth. Sometimes Jeanne suspected that he teased her intentionally so that he could appease her with that smile.

"Would you be willing to teach me a few tricks ... I mean, in case I find a job?"

"Sure, why not?"

"Promise?"

"Promise," he said, laughing mischievously. However, it sounded as if he knew that this would most certainly never happen and that there was no risk of having to fulfill his promise.

All of a sudden, Jeanne felt the hair on the back of her neck bristle and cold shivers ran down her back. She felt as if she was being watched. The horror she had been through since her arrival in this huge city was still fresh in her mind. But when she quickly turned around, all she saw was a shabbily dressed woman who looked like Julie. She was talking to a man she couldn't see clearly. Nonsense, she thought. Who would be watching me? She remembered the shopkeeper's last words when she ran away and shivered. But how should he find her here? She had run a long way and there were so many people here that it would be impossible to find her. She shrugged it off.

When they turned the corner, they were right in front of Claire's house. The boy said goodbye and kept walking. He seemed happy to be rid of a nuisance. Jeanne grinned. She now had enough information to start planning. She would talk to Claire about it as soon as she got home. She hurried up the stairs to the top floor, because the strange feeling just wouldn't go away. The walls creaked, but there was no one in the stairway, and there was no sound coming from the other apartments. The hair on the back of her neck bristled again. She couldn't shake off the feeling of apprehension. What was the matter with her? A loud bang made her jump. Cold sweat ran down her back. Frightened, she pressed herself against the wall and turned around. 

The stairs creaked. She heard footsteps from the first floor and shivered. She felt danger without having an explanation for it. She froze for a few moments, waiting for something to happen. Suddenly a frightened cat whizzed past her. Jeanne breathed a sigh of relief. Just a cat, she thought and laughed at herself.

***

Alain - Paris, 1783

"I found her," Alain bawled proudly across the tavern.

"Where is she?" Louis was suspicious.

"In a safe place. I'll go and get her tomorrow. Today, we're celebrating." He slammed his hand on the table and motioned for the barmaid to bring three mugs.

"From the best, woman, only from the best, today ..."

His cronies joined in his enthusiasm and roared happily. What he withheld from them, however, was the fact that he had lost the little one at the corner of the street. She seemed to have vanished from the face of the earth.

Gone! Again.

He sighed. Pitchers and mugs were brought, followed by comradely shoulder patting.

"To the merchandise," Johan toasted.

"To the merchandise!" shouted the others, laughing.

Alain shook off his doubts. She could only be in the first two, at most three houses from the beginning of the street. He would enquire from the neighbors tomorrow. Such a pretty girl couldn't go unnoticed. He grunted contentedly and raised his mug. "To the merchandise."

He looked around the bar, stared at the girls' backsides, and imagined what he'd like to do with them. His gaze crossed that of a prostitute sitting at the counter who seemed to be eyeing him. He grinned, nodded to her, and motioned for her to join him. She immediately walked over to him, her hips swaying lasciviously. He felt like celebrating today, even though his victory was not yet assured. He lifted his pitcher, emptied it in one gulp, and immediately ordered a new one. He began to feel pleasantly tipsy and elated. The worries of the last few weeks suddenly seemed to be blown away. He would no longer have to listen to his wife's nagging since the brat disappeared.

The prostitute sat down next to him and put her hand on his thigh. Aroused, he grunted with satisfaction.

"Well, what are we celebrating, honey?"

"A great victory," slurred Alain, and grabbed the pitcher that the barmaid was about to put down in front of him. "A wonderful victory." He emptied this pitcher too in one gulp and belched loudly. "But," he whispered so that only the hussy could hear, "there is still a small thing I need to find out to make it complete. But leave that to Alain." He winked and pointed to himself with the mug in his hand.

"Maybe I can help, Alain. I know almost everyone in this town."

"Oh, it's just about a brat," he said, playing it down. "A damn pretty brat who's slipped through my fingers weeks ago. But I got her now." He banged his fist on the wooden table, making the mugs bounce.

"I know every stone, every blade of grass, every brat here. What does she look like?" The woman groped and massaged him provocatively in the places he liked. He grunted lustfully.

"Dark blonde, blue-eyed. Lives on the street parallel to the market."

"You said blue eyes?" The whore narrowed her eyes like a vixen. "What do you want with her?" He stared at her while she kept on massaging him. "Do you fancy brats?"

He grunted in response. The way she grinned made him suspicious and he grabbed her hand.

"Do you know anything about this?"

"What will you give me if I do?"

Alain took a coin out of his pocket and slammed it on the table. He knew that this meant a week's earnings for the prostitute. Indeed, the woman looked at it wide-eyed.

"Is that enough?"

"Yes, sweetheart, that's enough. Is her name Jeanne?"

Alain was dumbfounded. His heart raced. Luck of the devil, it must be his good day. He nodded slowly, almost as if he feared that too abrupt a movement could tear the fragile thread to his lucky streak.

"Then I'll gladly bring you to her. But it should happen during the day, when the mother hen is not guarding her."

"What mother hen?"

"Well, the whore she lives with."

Alain nodded. He would have to wait for the next day so as not to cause a stir. However, to pass the time pleasantly until then, he now had everything he needed. He grinned and grabbed the woman around the hips.

"Is there a place we could go to?"

She smiled wickedly and nodded toward the stairs that led to the upper floor. They stood up.

"What's your name, girl?"

"Julie, but call me Juju, like all my close friends ..."

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

The rain fell in torrents and flooded the streets, making more and more puddles and small watercourses. Night had only just fallen, but the streets seemed deserted. Here and there, you could see someone hurrying into a house or closing the shutters. A drunk came out of the bar across the street and stumbled up the narrow alley, bumped left and right against the house walls, seemingly unaware that the rain was getting heavier. 

Despite the rain, Jeanne bent out of the window and looked out for Claire. She should be back already.

She had wonderful news and she couldn't wait to tell Claire. At the same time, she was also afraid of her reaction. But then, Claire had no right to dictate to her, because she was not her mother, even though she was acting like she was. In any case, Jeanne did not want to stand around idly and watch the situation worsen. Since her friend and protector could no longer afford the rent, she had to work twice as much, and Jeanne knew that she couldn't hold the pace much longer.

There! Jeanne saw Claire turn into the small street.

At last! Claire came down the road, completely drenched. She looked up to the window and saw Jeanne, who was waving at her - still half hanging out of the window. 

"Are you crazy, get inside now!" Claire scolded, raising her fist at Jeanne.

A few minutes later, she came through the door, exhausted, but in a good mood. She hugged Jeanne tenderly. "Never give me such a scare again. It is reckless to hang out of the window like this, you could have fallen!"

"I wouldn't have!" Jeanne pouted. She didn't like being reprimanded.

"Yes, you could have."

Claire took off her wet clothes, rubbed herself dry, and quickly slipped into her nightgown, which Jeanne had already laid out for her, as she did every day.

"You are a little angel, Jeanne, do you know that?"

"Sure. You tell me every day." Jeanne rolled her eyes in playful annoyance. Both laughed.

"I have good news, little one."

"Me too."

"Really? I go first, okay?"

"Well, alright, but only because it's you."

Out of a scrap of paper, Claire unwrapped a piece of bread she got from a client and handed it to Jeanne. She broke it in two halves and held one out to Claire. 

"No thanks, my darling, I've already eaten."

"Liar!"

"No, Jeanne, not this time." Claire smiled. "I have found a new home and a new job."

Jeanne cheered and almost swallowed the bread she was chewing.

"I got a place in a famous Parisian Maison Close. At Madame Gourdan, la Petite Comtesse. We will be in good hands."

***

"What is a Maison Close, Claire?" Jeanne asked a little later. She couldn't stop thinking about it. "Because I wouldn't like to be closed up inside a house."

Claire broke into a hearty laugh. "No, don't worry. Of course, they won't close you in. It is only called like that because, according to the law, this place should always remain closed so that what  happens there remains hidden from the public."

"And what happens there?"

"This is a house where many girls like me work."

"Ahh. And why is it so much better than here?" Jeanne asked suspiciously. She liked being here with Claire and felt safe. Her initial elation turned into doubt.

"That house is called Chateau de Madame Gourdan. The name says it all. We will have our own room there, clothes and enough to eat. That alone, my darling, is wonderful."

Jeanne mumbled. She couldn't deny that she would welcome more food.

"And what else is changing for us?" Jeanne didn't want to back down so quickly.

"There will be a considerable change in the clientele. Instead of the greasy, foul-smelling, and drunken customers from the working class, I will now have to deal with noble lords." Well, Jeanne thought, noble or not, it probably didn't change the awful work she wished Claire could be spared from. But she didn't want to tell her that, to spare her feelings. Instead, she smiled hesitantly because she could already understand that a good-smelling gentleman was more pleasant than ... She thought of the shopkeeper and shuddered with disgust. "I'm also going to make more money, and I won't need to go out on the road in all weathers. The clients come directly to the house."

"In our room?"

"But no, that is only for us, my darling. Special rooms are provided for this purpose. And now you tell, Jeanne. What's your news?"

Jeanne's heart started to race.

"I went for a short walk today - "

"Jeanne?" Claire looked at her, horrified. "Didn't we agree that you would stay in the house?" Claire turned pale.

"Yes, we did. But I just couldn't bear to be locked up anymore." If Claire ever found out that she was going to the market every day! 

"Oh, child, I understand you. But I don't think you know how dangerous the streets are, especially for a pretty girl like you." I have a protector, Jeanne wanted to say, but she didn't.

"Many young girls, sometimes even boys, simply disappear without a trace, and no one knows what becomes of them."

"I'm very careful, Claire, really. But how many years should I live locked up? When does the danger get less? When I'm twelve? Sixteen? Twenty? Should I live like this forever?"

Claire's eyes suddenly brimmed with tears, and she sniffed. "You're right, Jeanne, forgive me. I'm being a real mother hen. It's just ... I lost my child, a little girl, at birth. She would have been your age today."

"Oh... how sad. I'm sorry."

Claire stroked her head lovingly.

'It was so long ago, but it still hurts. And if something were to happen to you, it would be as if –" her voice faltered.

"As if you were losing your little one again."

Claire nodded, visibly struggling with her rising tears.

"But of course, you're right," Claire said, sniffing again and making a weak attempt to laugh. "I should not lock you up because then, I will lose you, won't I?"

Jeanne hesitated and nodded timidly.

"Yes, somehow you would, because then I would wither."

Claire pulled out a cloth handkerchief with a lace rim, dabbed her eyes, and blew her nose.

"Alright, go ahead, tell me.  What did you want to say?"

"I met a boy who was distributing leaflets, and we get along very well."

Claire smiled. She could guess that Jeanne had most probably approached him straight out.

"I asked him if I could also distribute leaflets, like him. He said that all these places had already been allocated, and more importantly, that the masters of the printing businesses would only hire boys." Jeanne paused, took a bite from her bread, chewed with relish, and continued to speak with a full mouth." I asked him what a girl could do when she needed money, and he advised me to ask boutiques if they didn't need people to sell with a hawker's tray."

"Well?"

"Do you know what that is?"

"Many shops have young people advertising for them in the streets; that's their best publicity. Flower shops and lantern sellers like to do this. They hire people to carry around a hawker's tray with bouquets or a stick with lanterns and advertise the business they work for. But Jeanne, you don't really need to work anymore. I will earn enough because we will be looked after by Madame Gourdan. We won't need to spend anything on rent, clothes, laundry, or food, and I only need to give a small part of my income to Madame."

"But I have to think about the future," said Jeanne with a serious face. "I can't spend the rest of my life in your room. What if something happens to you?"

Claire nodded reluctantly. "Besides, my chances of finding my parents and siblings are greater out there on the street."

"You have thought of everything, you rascal."

Jeanne had to grin, and Claire gave in.

"Alright. You win. But wait until we have moved, okay?"

"Agreed. After we have settled in the Chateau, I will start looking for a job as a salesgirl." Of course, Jeanne did not mention that she would still meet Camille, then she couldn't imagine spending even a single day without him.

"Agreed. But on one condition."

Jeanne looked at Claire with anticipation. "You have to promise me not to go with strangers, no matter what they promise you, and to come home before dark. Otherwise, I will worry myself to death. Promise?"

"Yes, promised," Jeanne freely agreed, hugging her friend.

***

Claire's promises were not exaggerated. Their lives changed abruptly and totally. They moved from the shabby backyard room to a "Palace of joys," as the lady of the house called it, on Rue des Deux-Portes. Jeanne had never seen such a magnificent house with so many floors. There was a huge reception room on the lower floor, behind which was the kitchen, the bathrooms, and even a swimming pool. The girls spent most of their time in the salon, played cards, sewed, played the guitar or the harp, sang, embroidered, or read to each other. On the first floor were the mysterious suites where the gentlemen disappeared with the ladies. There were the "special rooms" on the second floor, which were reserved only for the lady clients.

Even Claire was visibly impressed when they visited the establishment, although her first interview had already given her an approximate idea of what to expect.

On the third floor, the girls had their rooms. Her suite, as Claire called it, was equipped with its own fireplace, a dressing table, a dressing room, and a huge four-poster bed. Even a deep pile carpet lay on the floor. They would have to share the large bed, but that didn't bother them at all.

The first day, Jeanne stayed in the room as promised so that Claire could concentrate on her induction. She lay down on the big, cozy bed and began to dream of a better future ...

***

Chapter 11

Madeleine - Paris, 1783

“Jesus Christ, child, you must sew the trimmings on as you were told. Rosettes, Madeleine, Rosettes! Not tassels!" Furiously, Rose Bertin tore everything apart again and tossed the tangled pile of cloth on the table in front of Madeleine.

"We serve royalty, young lady, not riffraff. As a punishment, you won't get lunch today because this dress has to be finished this afternoon!"

Madeleine pressed her lips together and endured the tirade. The boss wasn't completely wrong; nevertheless, Madeleine found the rebuke unfair. How should she learn when she was constantly under pressure and expected to know, after such a short time, what things like tassels, cords, rosettes, and soutaches were? Every night, she studied with Louisa to exhaustion, and it still wasn't enough. Anger and despair came over her.

"Yes, Mademoiselle Bertin," she stammered, ashamed. The other girls kept their heads down and went on working as if nothing had happened. Only Louisa gave her a sympathetic glance.

"I'll stay with you during lunch break. Don't worry, we will manage this," she whispered to Madeleine.

What would become of her without her friend? Relieved, Madeleine continued to work.

"You have to blind stitch here," Louisa explained.

"Blind stitch, blind stitch ..." Madeleine mused. "Oh yes, blind stitching is when the seam remains invisible on one or both sides," she recited proudly.

"And what is staffing?" Louisa persisted.

Madeleine placed the needle and let it slide through the fabric. "When open or folded edges of the fabric are blind stitched."

"And what is it used for?"

"Huh, nothing easier than that," replied Madeleine, playing the smart aleck. "For sewing a blind hem or lining."

Louisa grinned.

Madeleine realized that she was slowly making progress. "I thought a seamstress merely had to know how to pull a needle through the fabric. I never thought that there was so much to learn."

"Don't worry, Mady, you're doing pretty well."

Thanks to Louisa's help, she finished the dress easily during the lunch break, and the girls spent the rest of the time studying. It was not only about learning the correct technical terms and their meanings but also mastering the various sewing techniques, which was the most difficult hurdle for Madeleine. She envied the girls for whom this art seemed to come naturally. 

When the lunch break ended, their colleagues came back and took their seats. Emilie, a young seamstress, unobtrusively dropped a crust of bread into Louisa's lap. Louisa nodded gratefully to her, broke it in two, and handed one piece to Madeleine under the table. It was moments like these that kept Madeleine going, moments when she felt solidarity among the employees. They all held together like glue, even if there was, of course, the inevitable exception.

The afternoon passed very quickly, and towards evening, after Mademoiselle Bertin's usual tea break, a habit she had copied from the English, she came to the girls, which was rather unusual. There was nothing unusual about her stern expression, except that the crease above her nose was deeper than usual, and her eyes seemed to throw sparks. The already quiet working atmosphere became even more tense and nerve-racking for the girls who glanced at each other with furtive, fearful looks. Which of them had made a mistake this time? As much as Madeleine brooded, she could think of nothing. She had stayed here all day and hadn't even eaten lunch. The dress was finished on time and, thanks to Louisa's help, turned out exactly as the boss had imagined. In any case, Mademoiselle Bertin had seemed satisfied, even if she had not gone so far as to praise her. So, what had happened? She feared for the other girls and wondered if Louisa might get into trouble for helping her to finish the dress on time. The thought made her feel sick. She would never forgive herself. She looked around inquiringly, and her gaze fell upon Lucienne. She was the only one who seemed to enjoy the situation. She continued to work with a knowing smile, reeking of deceit.

They all seemed to sense it. A thunderstorm was imminent, and Rose Bertin seemed to deliberately hold it off. Like a prison guard, she crossed the corridors with a malevolent eye and stopped abruptly in front of Emilie. A shock ran through Madeleine from head to toe; she held her breath but did not dare to look.

"You can pack up your things today and leave," said the boss calmly. The girl wanted to ask a question, but Rose Bertin held up a hand to keep silent. "Come to my office, and I'll pay you the month."

Louisa and Madeleine looked at each other questioningly.

Mademoiselle Bertin stepped out of the hall with her head held high. The comrades did not dare to speak. Slowly and without a word, Emilie got up and followed the boss.

The girls worked feverishly and longed for the day to end. Shortly afterward, when the evening meal was finally announced with a gong, they stood up, hastily cleared their work utensils, and put their heads together, whispering.

What had Emilie done wrong? Only Lucienne seemed completely indifferent; as always, she stayed in the background. Louisa nudged Madeleine and pointed out the girl who was standing by the window, grinning. Finally, they followed the others into the kitchen with its appetizing fragrance. Without a word, they agreed: the evil must have come from Lucienne, a girl who was the last to be hired before Madeleine and was aiming for a higher post.

When they reached the kitchen door, the cook suddenly blocked the entrance in front of Madeleine and Louisa with her arms crossed over her corpulent body.

"You are both going to bed with empty stomachs today."

Louisa was stunned. "But we haven't had anything for lunch!"

"Now, don't get rebellious because the boss doesn't see it that way."

They looked questioningly at the cook. Then they remembered: the heel of bread!

"If I were her, I would have kicked you both out. You are lucky that she decided to be lenient."

Madeleine swallowed hard. Tears of rage welled up in her eyes.

"Poor Emilie," she whispered.

Everything became clear to Louisa too.

"Lucienne!" she spat out. "That damn bitch must have seen it all and reported it to Bertin. Now stop wailing and come on!" Louisa pulled her over to the boss's office and knocked.

"Come in!" It sounded like thunder, and Madeleine winced.

Louisa opened the door and stepped in bravely.

"What do you want? Would you perhaps like the same fate as Emilie?"

Madeleine couldn't resist the urge to back away in fear, but Louisa held her tight. Emilie stood motionless in the middle of the room as if she had not yet understood what was happening to her. There was a strong smell of mothballs in the room.

"Madam. I want to say something in Emilie's defense. She ..." 

"There's nothing to defend here. Madeleine was forbidden to eat, and Emilie gave her food against my orders. That was unforgivable."

"Mademoiselle, please, that's not true!" Louisa exclaimed. Emilie turned around and looked at her inquiringly.

"She slipped food to me, not Madeleine."

Rose Bertin was taken aback.

"I wasn't forbidden to eat. I beg your forgiveness if I have misunderstood that.  I stayed with Madeleine voluntarily to help her. So, Emilie didn't act against your will when she slipped me a crust of bread. And I didn't give Madeleine anything. She wasn't hungry anyway because she was too nervous about getting the order ready."

Rose Bertin looked suspiciously from one to the other and leaned back in her chair. She seemed to see through Louisa's ruse, but Madeleine had the feeling that the excuse came in handy. She had heard from the others that the boss hated firing good dressmakers, but understandably, she could not afford to be weak when dealing with more than thirty employees. As a quality inspector, Emilie occupied an important and highly sought-after position.

"I have eyewitnesses," said Mademoiselle Bertin, and stared at the girls as if she could look into their souls.

"Lucienne, I assume." Louisa sounded scornful.

Madeleine's knees had gone weak, and she wished she could vanish on the spot. She admired her new friend for her courage and wondered if she wasn't a little crazy as well. Madeleine already saw herself packing her belongings. Where would she go if Rose Bertin dismissed her too? She could not imagine that.

Mademoiselle Bertin nodded, looking expectantly at Louisa, almost as if she hoped that Louisa's arguments were relevant enough to reverse her decision.

"She is a notorious liar and doesn't get along with any of the girls. Besides, she is surely tired of her lower position and is now happy that such a coveted post has become vacant."

There was a long, uncomfortable pause. Madeleine felt close to fainting. Her knees weakened even more and buckled slightly. The boss took no notice of it.

"Well, alright, girls. It could, indeed, have been a motive. I will have a general hearing. Now go to your rooms, you too, Emilie. And don't you dare to leave them. After the souper, I will question each employee individually and then reach a fair decision."

The girls curtsied and left the office.

"And Louisa," Rose Bertin called out as they were already in the hallway. Louisa came back and poked her head through the doorway. "You are hopefully aware that if you lied, you will pack your bags too. The same goes for Madeleine."

Louisa nodded and disappeared.

***

The waiting was unbearable. Madeleine nervously chewed her fingernails. What would become of her if the boss puts her out on the street?

"Why did you do that?" Madeleine hissed at her friend. "Where would I go?"

"Calm down, Mady, and trust me, please. I know what I’m doing."

"Oh God, it's easy for you to talk. Someone like you always finds solutions. But what will happen to me?"

"We'll definitely stay together."

"Well, then, I'm reassured!" It sounded snottier than she intended. She had slowly gotten used to the new situation and had started to gain a foothold here and see a future for herself. There were still many difficulties to overcome, but she was beginning to feel secure.

"I understand your resentment, I really do. I don’t have a good feeling either."

"For someone who is feeling apprehensive, you seem quite relaxed."

"Maybe I'm more fatalistic than you Mady because I had no choice, do you understand?"

"No choice, no choice," Madeleine grumbled. But she had to accept that Louisa was somehow right. She sighed and let her shoulders sag. She thought of her sisters and that she had always been the most timorous of the three; the one who preferred to stay with her mother at home to sew or run the household while Jeanne romped around in the woods and Marianne went about her involvement in agriculture, even if her father hadn't necessarily endorsed it. "You are right. I'm a coward and an egoist on top of that, just thinking about myself." Madeleine was suddenly ashamed. She would have liked to have some of Louisa's courage; she wished she could also stand up for Emilie.

"Hey Mady, why are you saying that? I do understand you." Louisa sat down next to her on the bed and took her hand between hers. "Believe me, I'm scared too, even if that won't change anything. I have certainly acted rashly. But I hate Lucienne so much... " Madeleine understood. "I would never forgive myself if I were to drag you into a disaster."

"What do you think, will the others speak out? Did they see me eat?"

"I would stake my life on the girls, really. Even if they saw you, I can't imagine them telling on us. The only problem is ..."

"What? What problem? Louisa, why do you say that?" Madeleine's felt her throat tighten.

"Well, since the others do not know what I have confessed, they might deny everything.  And then, they will be blamed for lying." Louisa mused.

"Oh my God," stammered Madeleine, hardly able to bear the tension. Should she be packing her few belongings already?

"However ..." With a pleading look, Madeleine silently wished her friend to say something that would relieve her of the fears torturing her. "I would really be very surprised if Bertin dismissed all three of us."

"I hardly count, in her eyes," said Madeleine. She was overcome by grief. She was painfully aware that she was the weakest link in the chain. It might also happen that the boss punishes only her since the whole situation arose because of her inability to work independently. Those thoughts kindled the flames of fear consuming her within, as if someone had opened a window to feed the fire inside her with the incoming draft. Madeleine gasped for air.

There was a knock on the door, and the two girls froze. Madeleine's heart raced. She thought she was going to faint as a sudden spell of dizziness made her hold on to the bed frame, even though she was sitting.

"Come in," said Louisa with a trembling voice, keeping her eyes on Madeleine. The door opened, and Emilie stuck her bright red-haired head through.

Louisa jumped, and Emilie rushed over to her, throwing herself into her arms. "Oh, Louisa, I'll never forget what you did. Never, do you hear? "

Tears of relief ran down Louisa's cheeks, and Madeleine smiled weakly.

"My God, you have courage, there is no doubt about that," she burst out approvingly.

They found out that all the girls had been behind Louisa, Madeleine, and Emilie, and none of them reported to have seen Madeleine with a bread crust. Some had not grasped the situation and claimed not to have seen anything; others had understood Louisa's strategy and immediately confirmed her version of the story.

"And Lucienne?" she asked as if that name were a swear word.

"She was fired," said Emilie, sounding triumphant.

"Serves her right," said Louisa. "Nothing much will happen to her, though, then she comes from a rich family."

"But the strangest thing was," Emilie suddenly added with a thoughtful expression, "that Rose Bertin helped us ..."

"How?"

"She said: I will question you all one by one. Two different versions have been brought to my attention, and I would like to know which is the correct one. One says that Emilie slipped both girls a crust of bread, the other claims that only Louisa, who was not forbidden to eat, got the bread."

The three girls smiled knowingly and giggled.

Chapter 12

Paris, 1783 - Madame Gourdan

"Contessa!" He bowed elegantly, suggesting a kiss on her hand.

"Marquis!" exclaimed Madame Gourdan, delighted. "What a pleasure to welcome you again." Since she had found out about his return to Paris a few days ago, she had pulled all the strings she could to lure him into her establishment. The competition never slept and had used Madame Gourdan's short prison term to open new, more modern houses. But she wanted to secure this customer with all his special qualities because he paid well and on top of that ... she sighed.

The marquis had also been in prison, and the eleven years of incarceration had left its mark on him. His face was drawn and gray. He had lost his youth. His once blond hair was covered with a greasy wig, and his turquoise eyes looked watery and tired. But his demeanor was still stately, his clothes well-groomed, and she found him as captivating as ever.

With a wave of her hand, Madame Gourdan invited him to sit down. She took a seat on the divan facing him and straightened her wide, extensive skirts. Her bent position caused her breasts, which were still full and handsome despite her age, to swell out of her décolleté. The marquis smiled, swept his laps aside, and sat opposite to her with a stiff back.

"Now that you are with us again, you will not refuse some entertainment, my dear." Her curls bounced playfully around her cheeks.

"Of course, my dear Contessa, and I am sure you have some delicacies in store. Is my favorite chair still there?"

"Of course, Marquis, but it should be a bit rusty by now, then it wasn't used much in your absence ..." Eleven years is a long time, she said to herself, but the look on his face told her that he had guessed her thoughts.

"We'll have to remedy that," said the marquis, deadly serious, as he eyed the incoming prostitute. The girl, dressed in sheer fabrics, set down a tray of refreshments. Following her boss's instructions, she turned her bare buttocks, which shimmered through the fabric, towards the guest, giving him a precise idea of her slim, almost boyish figure. She bent to set the tray down on the little mahogany table, offering him an even more revealing sight.

Madame Gourdan carefully observed the marquis' reaction, who did not fail to notice.

"Too old," he said simply, "too worn out."

The lady of the house frowned. At thirteen years of age, Eleonore was the youngest in the house. It would be difficult to please him.

"During my... uhh... absence, I had time to get an exact idea of what I wanted." He looked around the still tastefully furnished room, took a long sip of wine, put the glass down loudly, and got up. "But I'm sure, dear Contessa," he took her hand, brought it again very slowly to his lips and lingered there longer than usual, "that you can sense my wishes better than anyone else, isn't that so, my little dove?"

She shivered and nodded. He had lost none of his charisma and his power over her. She would give a lot to be young again and catch his eye. The thought of never again being the object of his desire was as painful to her as a knife stab in the guts.

"Yes, Marquis de Sade, I already have an idea ..."

***

Claire - Paris, 1783

Contrary to her expectations, Claire did not start work immediately. A girl named Margot was assigned to introduce her to all the customs of the house.

"We get up at eight in the morning, put on a dressing gown, and come into the dining room for breakfast." She opened a large door to a cozy little drawing-room with low tables and white brocade sofas and armchairs.

"Here is the dressing and hairdressing room. The coiffeurs start their work at nine o'clock, and all girls have to finish and be ready by eleven o'clock."

Claire nodded, aghast. She was speechless as it dawned upon her that her life would now run according to a fixed schedule. She could no longer hang around in bed in the morning as she used to, but neither would she have that lump in her stomach all the time, caused either by worry or hunger.

"In this whole area," Margot pointed to the back, where the kitchen, the small dining room, and the hairdresser's room were located, "the gentlemen have no access." Claire followed the girl, who was dressed in light muslin fabric, into the large hall. Her already freely exposed physical charms became even more visible in the backlight. "Here, we can pursue our activities until after lunch or even receive a special visitor."

Claire frowned. "Who would that be?"

"It could be a gentleman who is particularly fond of you or with whom you have, well, a relationship. This often happens here. It could be a man who falls in love with you, for example."

Claire smiled in disbelief as if the thought were completely absurd.

"But there is a condition. He must not be jealous. If he makes a scene, he will be expelled from the house and will never be allowed back."

Claire marveled at the many rules and prohibitions.

"Can we go out sometimes?"

Margot shook her head. "Too dangerous. Some gentlemen might be lurking out there and just take off with us. Don't forget: We are the elite of the petite Contessa. The establishment is famous beyond Paris, and only the most select gentlemen can enjoy our company. You can imagine the number of men who are excluded." Oh yes, Claire thought knowingly, I could tell a lot about that.

She was then introduced to the other girls. Some were already back from the dressing room and sat in the big salon. Emma, Mathilde, Alexandrine, Adeline, Sarah ... Claire couldn't remember all the names. The girls looked beautiful, well-groomed, happy, and smelled of various wonderful flower fragrances. Claire immediately felt shabby and dirty.

"May I introduce Claire," Margot said to the girls.

"Welcome, Claire. Where do you come from?" the pretty blonde named Emma wanted to know.

"I'm from Nantes." Claire was intimidated.

"And what brings you to Paris?"

"Oh." Claire looked down at her broken boots, embarrassed. She had not been prepared for so much curiosity since no one had ever shown any interest in her. "I had fallen in love with an officer who brought me to Paris and had to leave shortly after."

"Pregnant, I suppose?" Emma suggested.

"Of course, otherwise she wouldn't be here with a brat, silly," Margot said.

The other girls giggled.

"She's not my daughter, she's ... my sister," Claire lied. The idea that Jeanne was her younger sister did not come as unpleasant to her. She had to negotiate hard with the matrone so that Jeanne could be freed from work.

"It's a pity," the elegant lady with the elaborate hair-do and powdered hair had said. She had smelled of expensive perfume and wore a beautiful and wide ball gown. "I would have had interested parties for her as well."

Suddenly, a bell rang. The girls turned to Emma and winked at her.

"Well, who could that be?" they teased the beautiful blonde. Emma rose in playful grace and haughtiness. "I don't know what you're talking about, Mesdemoiselles." She winked and disappeared from the salon, swinging her hips. Her flawless physique was highlighted by her transparent muslin dress and long stockings. No one seemed to value underwear here, Claire thought, although she herself – for hygienic reasons – did not wear any. However, she had never worn any see-through clothes. She would have to get used to that and to the strict rules.

Claire spent the rest of the day learning the house rules, bathing, dressing up, and visiting the rooms on the first and second floors. She learned that men were received on the first floor and on the second – to her astonishment – ladies could satisfy their lust with female whores.

She also learned that each customer had a booklet at his disposal to choose 'specialties' from. The rooms were equipped with cords having different colored tassels. These cords led to the basement and were connected to bells. Each girl thus had her own bell.

"What has Madame planned for you, Claire?"

"I'm supposed to replace a girl who left last week."

"Ah well, then you have the green bell. That is for oral pleasures."

Claire sighed in relief; there were things she loathed more than others.

"No, uh ... penetration?" she wanted to know.

"Everything the customer likes, Claire, but your specialty is oral intercourse. If he wants more, he has to pay for it. We'll talk about payment later."

When Claire returned to her room in the late afternoon, her head was spinning. She now understood the meaning of the induction phase.

"And?" Jeanne asked excitedly.

"A great deal to learn, little one." Claire stroked her cheek.

"You smell so good and look so pretty." Jeanne was thrilled. "You could be taken for a 'Comtesse'."

Claire sighed. She had a hollow feeling in her stomach, but this time, not from hunger. She had eaten so much that she feared she might get too fat for the job in a few weeks. She had also managed to bring Jeanne something to eat, which she gobbled up hungrily.  Claire became thoughtful. Until then, she had been her own master, and now she had to get used to a regulated life. But then she thought about all the advantages and decided that she should be grateful.

For Jeanne as well, this environment was better than the run-down, unheated room they had left.

A warm fire was already crackling in the fireplace. Claire and Jeanne took the back stairs to go for a bath. Then she spent the evening making Jeanne a new dress from an outdoor dress that the girls had discarded, with the help of two girls who happened to have the evening free. It felt a bit like a family, and she immediately sensed that the girls stuck together. She particularly liked that.

Late in the evening, exhausted but happy, they went to bed.

"Come on, tell me, Claire, what did you see?"

Claire turned to Jeanne, propped herself up on her elbow, and rested her head on her hand.

"It's so much that I don't know where to start. There's a swimming pool, a bathhouse, a hairdressing salon, and - imagine - even a dressing room with the craziest clothes." 

What she kept from Jeanne were the incredible utensils she had seen, such as whips, special chairs, and dildos for women. The 'Petite Comtesse' even seemed to be trading in those articles. A child of Jeanne's age did not need to know such things. In Claire's opinion, she already knew too much about this sinful life.

As if Jeanne had read her mind, she blurted out: "I've seen women come as customers. That's strange, isn't it?"

Claire cleared her throat, embarrassed. "You're still too young for that, Jeanne."

"Oh, nonsense. If you knew what one learns in the country from the boys in the stable!" Jeanne made a circle with her left hand's index finger and thumb and stuck her right index finger into it. Giggling, she covered her mouth and hid her head in the pillow.

Claire was genuinely appalled, even though she felt that she was being hypocritical at the same time.

"Jeanne!" she scolded indignantly.

"Come off it." Jeanne was offended. "You can't take a child to a house like this if you want it to remain unspoiled," she added precociously. Claire's mouth was agape. Yet, she had to admit that Jeanne was right. "Well, that's also true. What else do you know?"

"What do you mean? In the country, we all live in confined spaces, and the walls are thin. For as long as I can remember, I've heard how my parents ... well ... I also know that women come here because they like women, but I don't understand how it works." She made the obscene gesture with her fingers again. Claire shook her head. How could such a young thing know so much? On the other hand, the more she knew, the better she could avoid certain dangers.

"Well, there are devices for that," Claire tried to explain, embarrassed. She was rather uncomfortable with the subject because she also had some difficulty with what women did with each other. She sincerely hoped that something like this would never be asked of her.

She stared at the canopy of the four-poster bed. She couldn't help thinking of one thing. She shook herself.

"Jeanne, you must promise me something."

Jeanne propped her face on her hands and waited to hear what Claire wanted to say to her.

"If, for any reason, you should find yourself alone in a room with a man, never sit on one of the chairs. Do you swear?"

"But why?"

"Just swear it."

"Yes, Claire, I swear. Now tell me why."

"There are chairs a girl had better not sit on, especially in this house ..." Claire yawned.

Before Jeanne could ask any further questions, Claire blew out the candles, and both went to sleep - with the soft reddish glow of light and the pleasant sound of the still crackling embers of the fireplace - closely embraced as usual.

Chapter 13

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

Jeanne proudly strolled through the alleys with her tray.

"Come to Madame Besson: Coffee, tobacco, Eau de Vie, everything your heart desires!" she exclaimed for the hundredth time that day. She had picked up this and similar slogans from the other vendors. 

Jeanne was so curious and observant that she picked up a lot very quickly. Her big blue eyes helped a little too, of course. The customers were charmed, and the other vendors admired the little girl with so much willpower. She had earned their respect and could always count on their support.

Since she and Claire moved into the 'Château' of the 'Petite Comtesse', they were doing really well. There were rumors that Madame was very ill, but she always seemed to be in good spirits when they met her in the drawing-room. Claire also told her that the mistress had been through a lot. The king had passed laws that made it more and more difficult for such establishments to exist. The 'Petite Comtesse' had even been in prison for a year, it was said. Competitors had used her stay in prison to open even better houses and lure the clientele away from her with their luxury. When Madame came back from prison, she reopened her house, and some of the customers came back, but the competition made life difficult for her.

Such stories made Jeanne thoughtful. She didn't want to have to depend on other people her whole life. She had plenty of ideas but didn't know how to realize them. Not yet.

When she got to the little shop in downtown Paris, Madame Besson, her boss, was enjoying her coffee.

"Well, Jeanne, did you sell well today?"

"Yes, Madame." Jeanne curtsied, and Madame Besson had to smile. Jeanne knew that Madame Besson was very fond of her, although she thought she was a tomboy. At first, she had been a bit reluctant to hire Jeanne because she considered the competitors to be disloyal for hiring vendors with hawker's trays, despising them for it and because Jeanne was a girl. But Jeanne had such an outspoken way of expressing herself that she hadn't been able to resist her for long.

"My lady," she had said. "That will be the new fashion. If you ignore this, you will fall behind your competitors, and you may even lose everything." Jeanne's arguments had been convincing and coherent and had given Madame Besson something to think about.

Jeanne noticed that the old lady was taking the bait. "But a girl can't take such a difficult job, and certainly not at your age. You would have to be on the streets in all weathers."

"You're a girl too, and you run a much bigger business than a tray!" Jeanne had exclaimed defiantly. "And should I starve to death just because I'm a girl? Also, I am certainly not too young for that."

Madame Besson had to smile at the skillful compliment because she was already at an age when it was pleasant to be called a girl; Jeanne had made clever use of it. And even if Jeanne knew that Madame Besson was aware of that, the lady had been moved and had smiled at her. She had taken the decision of giving Jeanne a chance and didn't seem to regret it.

Not only did Jeanne bring considerable sales to her shop - but she also sold well with her vendor's tray. Many new customers found their way to the store, as Jeanne praised the business and her boss to the skies. Besides, Jeanne felt that she brought vitality to the lady's lonely existence, which is why she loved this work all the more.

Jeanne felt that her new boss also enjoyed their moments of togetherness. That was when there were no customers in the shop, and Jeanne came to fill her tray. On top of that, Jeanne had lots of good ideas for new goods, and Madame Besson began to trust her childlike instinct.

"Come, sit down and tell me." Madame Besson put a cup of hot chocolate in front of her, which the girl liked very much.

"When I grow up, I'll be like you," Jeanne mused. "I will buy my own shop."

"Oh, my girl, that has its drawbacks too, you know..."

"Oh, yes? What kind of drawbacks?" Jeanne's curiosity was aroused.

"You can get very lonely, you will have all kinds of worries, you have to keep up with the competition, and you don't sleep well at night. You have to be constantly afraid of bad months or of losing your business and livelihood altogether. Then there are the many taxes, and you never get to go out for a breath of fresh air."

Jeanne became thoughtful.

"Why should you be lonely? Don't you have a fiancé?"

Madame Besson had to smile. "No, my child. I once had a husband. But he fell in the war against Austria."

"Oh, I'm sorry," Jeanne whispered meekly. That explained a lot. Jeanne found Madame Besson very beautiful with her light red hair, the light, almost transparent complexion, the freckles, and the pale blue eyes. She did not understand how such a beautiful woman could be alone.

"It doesn't matter, Jeanne. Besides, I actually feel good without a husband, you know."

They smiled at each other. But Jeanne wasn't so easily distracted from her line of thought. "A mobile shop would be ideal so that you can go out for some fresh air ..."

Madame Besson laughed out loud. "Then you have to become a camp follower, Jeanne."

"What's that?"

"These are women who travel with the armies through the country and supply them with all kinds of goods and food. They are also called Vivandières. They usually help out in the canteens."

Jeanne was amazed. That sounded like her dream job.

"And how do you become a camp follower?"

"You need a horse, baskets and a folding table, the goods, as well as good, warm clothes and boots. You also need a permit. Most are married to a soldier and are therefore more easily accepted."

"Ah," Jeanne said. "And from whom could I get this permit?"

"From a Commander-in-Chief, a Commissioner, or a General."

The shop bell rang. Madame Besson got up abruptly and almost spilled her coffee. She served the customer while Jeanne loaded her tray anew with tobacco.

When the customer left, Madame Besson came back to Jeanne from the counter.

"I'm sorry, Madame, I don't want to bore you with my questions."

"But you don't, my little Jeanne. And you still have time till then."

"Yes ... I have time to save enough to buy a horse, baskets, and goods." Jeanne grinned mischievously.

"But I will be sad when you leave me."

"Oh, well. I will be too old for hawker's trays by then, and you will certainly have grown tired of me." Jeanne giggled, and Madame Besson laughed heartily.

***

"Child, come in." Madame Gourdan waved an inviting hand. Jeanne stepped shyly into the semi-darkness of the room and curtsied politely. The lady looked so distinguished and elegant that Jeanne felt small and insignificant. When she returned from Madame Besson, a girl had intercepted her at the entrance and told her that Claire wanted to speak to her; she should go to Madame Gourdan. She hoped nothing had happened to Claire because she wasn't in the room.

"I was told that Claire wanted to speak to me. Where is she?"

"Don't worry, your sister is coming soon. She's serving a customer right now." The lady of the house smiled.

Jeanne let out a sigh of relief.

"Come, sit next to me." Madame tapped her hand lightly on the red and gold sofa next to her.

Jeanne obeyed. She felt uncomfortable. Why was she called? Would she be thrown out again?

"Until Claire comes, let's chat a little ..."

Jeanne nodded. Why not? Chatting couldn't hurt.

"May I offer you something to drink?"

Jeanne shook her head. The sooner she gets out of here, the better.

"Well, as you know, this is a brothel." She emphasized her words with an all-encompassing wave of her hand.  A whiff of floral scent swept across to Jeanne. She nodded. "And as you know, everyone here has a job to do."

Jeanne nodded again, trying to guess what the lady was getting at. So, she would be thrown out after all. Her heart began to beat faster. Where should she go at such short notice?

"May I be granted some time, madame?"

Madame Gourdan seemed to sniff out something, and suddenly Jeanne realized that she had made a mistake.

"Well, all right." She patted her updo with her ringed fingers.  The glitter on her wig sparkled. "But I will ask for a small thing in return."

Jeanne backed away, startled.

"Don't be afraid." Madame Gourdan laughed, waving her hand. "No, nothing like that. I just want you to keep a longtime friend of mine company. Would you do that for me?"

Jeanne noticed the sparkle in the woman's eyes. She wondered if it reflected the glitter on her wig. "Well, if it's just keeping company, I'll be happy to. But I'd like to talk to Claire about it first."

"Of course you can, she's coming soon. She agreed to this." Jeanne still remained suspicious and looked around her. The other half of the room was dark, and it smelled unusually musty for this house as if these chambers weren't used very often.

"It's late, my dear. I have to see to the girls. You just stay here and wait for Claire."

Jeanne nodded, relieved. It was somehow harder to breathe in the presence of the lady. She looked forward to being on her own.

Madame Gourdan left promptly, her skirts billowing after her, and pulled the bolt from outside. Why did she do that? Jeanne began to feel uneasy. Was it for her protection? Most probably, she thought, and calmed down again.

Jeanne got up from the divan and walked around the room. A huge mirror hung on the wall. She looked at herself in it. Her dark blond hair fell softly over her shoulders. Her lightly tanned complexion made her look healthy. It made her blue eyes stand out even more. Working outside in the fresh air did her good.

A noise made her start. She heard someone breathing, or was she just imagining it? Jeanne's heartbeat quickened again, and her ears started to buzz. She turned around slowly. The sound had come from the dark part of the room. She shuddered. Where was Claire?

She tried hard to hear something, but the buzzing in her ears got so loud that she gave up. She stumbled back to the divan, feeling a lump rising in her throat, trying to pull herself together and not to cry. She was about to sit down again when she heard a match cracking, and the light fell on a dark figure in the corner of the room.

Jeanne cried out in horror, and her hands went to her throat. She looked at the door but remembered that it was locked. Her first thought was about the shopkeeper. So, he had found her again. But she immediately recognized her mistake, because the man was tall and looked quite elegant. She was almost relieved.

"Don't be afraid, girl."

The man lit an oil lamp and gently blew out the match. "Just to keep me company, do you remember?" Jeanne pulled herself together and calmed down again. She nodded hesitantly. "Come, don’t be shy. Sit down here next to me," he said softly, pointing to the chair in the corner.

Jeanne sighed with relief. She could now see his face, and he seemed to be a well-educated middle-aged man. She came closer. 

"I'm waiting for my ... sister," she stammered, feeling stupid.

"Then we'll wait together. In the meantime, tell me about yourself. I want to know everything about you, about your homeland, your parents..." He looked at her invitingly. His friendly smile dispelled Jeanne's last doubts. There seemed to be a magical power coming from him. He pointed again to the chair in the corner. Jeanne pulled herself together. This man did not look like one of the ordinary customers. He looked different. More distinguished and noble.

She walked over to the chair. She had never seen such a chair before. It seemed to come from another century. The man encouraged Jeanne with a nod, and she sat down.

Chapter 14

Marianne - Paris, 1783

"I am starting an unprecedented endeavor, one which is unique and can be replicated by no one. I want to show the world a man in his wholly true nature, and this man will be myself." Anne-Louise took a break, looked theatrically out of the window, and pondered. A sudden downpour brought her out of her reverie. She read extracts from the works of Jean-Jacques Rousseau, whom she idolized, every day to Marianne.

Anne-Louise read to her daily to make her familiar with the text until she could read it herself. She saw it as her duty 'to lead the ignorant lamb to the fruit of wisdom,' as she called it. Especially since she seemed to like Marianne more and more and would like to take her to the salons. But without literary knowledge, it would be an unpleasant experience for her friend.

"I alone. I know how to read in my heart, and I know mankind. My nature is different from that of all I have seen; I even dare to believe that I have not been created like any other human being. Even if I'm not better, I'm still different... ” Anne-Louise sighed.

"How do you like his texts, Maria?" The leather armchair creaked as she shifted her weight.

"Interesting," Marianne replied cautiously. In fact, she didn't know what to make of it. But she didn't dare to say so, because she sensed how important reading was for Anne-Louise. Even more than reading, Anne-Louise's passion was writing. One seemed to go hand in hand with the other.

"I don't always understand what the author is trying to say."

"Oh, it'll come in time, don't worry." Anne-Louise put the book aside and popped a cupcake in her mouth.

There was a brief knock on the door, and Anne-Louise's mother, Madame Necker, came in.

"Child, I have to talk to you." As usual, she treated Marianne as if she weren't there, and sat down on the armchair opposite Anne-Louise's. There was more creaking as she sat.

"Yes, Mother?"

"We spoke about it before. I have found a wonderful English gentleman to be your fiancé ..."

"Mother!" exclaimed Anne-Louise indignantly.

"He's an excellent match, child. William Pitt, a parliamentarian who ..."

"I refuse."

"Anne-Louise-Germaine Necker, please pull yourself together. Marianne, please leave us."

Marianne got up quickly, curtsied, and left the room. As soon as she closed the door behind her, the screaming started. Marianne stood hesitantly in the hallway. A maid scurried past, her arms laden with freshly folded linen, and disappeared into the closet next to Anne-Louise's room.

Marianne heard only fragments of the conversation through the door. "Ungrateful" ... "Never" ... "You can't do that to me!"

When the maid came out of the closet, Marianne snuck in. She could hear better from there.

Anne-Louise was laughing hysterically. "But I've been promised to him since I was a child."

"That's right, but he's 17 years older than you. Mister Pitt, on the other hand, is young and a handsome man."

"I don't want to live in England, Maman, and that's the end of it!" cried Anne-Louise. Marianne put her hand over her mouth, startled. She would never have dared to speak so disrespectfully to her parents.

"Oh, Anne-Louise, a wedding is based on sensible reasons, not on love."

"And do you think I love the old de Staël?" countered Anne-Louise. "He's the Swedish ambassador, and he's wealthy, and I can live in my hometown, Paris!"

Marianne's confusion increased. How can such a romantic person not want to marry for true love?

"He won't stop me from loving whoever I want!"

"That is improper. I'll have Mister Pitt come, and then we'll see."

"You can save yourself the trouble. I'll be sick the day Monsieur Pitt sets foot over the threshold of this house," Anne-Louise replied impudently.

Marianne heard steps and guessed that Monsieur Necker would have entered the room. Marianne held her breath. Now, this would surely come to a head. What if Anne-Louise really did get married? Would they send her back home? Even if she was slowly getting used to the luxury and comfort, she longed for her parents, though not for the hunger.

"What is going on here? You can be heard all over the house. What is it, Minette?" As always, he was completely calm.

"She wants to marry me off to a Mister Pitt so that I can be deported to England," sobbed Anne-Louise.

"There, there, nobody will send you away from here."

"I only wanted to ..."

"Leave it, Suzanne, leave us alone now."

Marianne hurried out of the closet. She couldn't bear to think that Madame Necker might catch her listening.

Later, when her father had left, Marianne ventured back into Anne-Louise's room, afraid of the mood in which she would find her friend.

Anne-Louise was trying on a new pair of gloves. As Marianne entered the room, Anne-Louise turned around and grinned mischievously at her.

"I should be an actress, don't you think?"

Marianne smiled, impressed but uneasy. She couldn't explain it, but Anne-Louise's talent for manipulation unsettled her. Anne-Louise took off her gloves and dropped them carelessly on the floor.
