Chapter 15

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

"So, your home is the beautiful Vendée." The man paced up and down the room. Jeanne admired his smooth manner. He was dressed in black, had a sword on his side, and used a walking stick, which he didn't seem to need. A white wig adorned his head, and he exuded a pleasant scent of wood. He leaned against the fireplace and pulled a little lever.

"Yes, sir, in Brittany."

"Don't call me sir, Jeanne. I want to be 'Marquis' to you. Your Marquis ..."

It wasn't unpleasant to talk to him, but Jeanne couldn't help glancing at the door. When would Claire finally come? Again, the marquis went to the fireplace and shook the lever. Jeanne heard a slight click. Where did that come from? She looked at the chair beneath her.

She started to feel uncomfortable.

"Did the Contessa tell you what this was about?" He pronounced the word Contessa with a heavy Italian accent.

"You were in the room when she spoke to me," Jeanne replied pertly. She was still indignant that he had been hiding in the dark.

"Well, that's right." He looked her up and down thoughtfully and frowned. "You will have to learn obedience," he muttered barely audibly.

"I beg your pardon, sir?"

"Nothing, little one ..." He turned his head, raised his cane to the fireplace, and tapped it lightly on the lever as if to remove rust. Something seemed to have disconcerted him. Jeanne heard the soft click again, and a slight vibration under her made her flinch. She thought hard. What was she missing?

"Now, child, tell me more about yourself."

Jeanne felt hot, and her pulse quickened. She sensed danger but couldn't quite explain why. The gentleman seemed quite harmless and gentle. He didn't get too close to her, either. And yet, something screamed in her subconscious. She had overlooked something, forgotten something. She felt it so strongly that her stomach contracted. She tried desperately to remember.

"Sir, not that I don't feel comfortable in your presence," lied Jeanne, "but I have work to do."

The marquis froze. He held the lever and shook it.  It clicked and vibrated under Jeanne. The chair trembled slightly, but nothing happened. The marquis cursed.

Jeanne suddenly froze, her eyes popping out of her head. Chair, it suddenly dawned on her. She felt a wave of heat go through her. "Never sit on a chair here," Claire had drummed into her. But now, she sat there as if paralyzed. What would happen if she got up?

The marquis fiddled feverishly with the mechanism of the fireplace and seemed to ignore her. He cursed. Jeanne looked around the room furtively. She looked desperately for a way to escape. Even if there had been windows, they were on the third floor. The door was locked. She was near desperation. The man cursed again, and Jeanne could see that he was losing his composure. Her initial admiration turned into disgust. Her eyes suddenly fell on a cord with a tassel hanging from it. The chair cracked violently, and the vibrations grew stronger. Jeanne jumped up in horror.

Nothing happened. The marquis had turned his back and was still fiddling around the fireplace. Jeanne crept slowly to the cord. She pulled on it. First gently, then again firmly, and finally several times in a row.

The marquis turned around and looked at her, horrified. "What did you do, child?" He looked at her in amazement.

Jeanne tried to hide her trembling.

"I ... I have to go now," Jeanne stuttered.

The marquis remained surprisingly calm. He tried to regain his composure as if he had suddenly become aware of his unworthy demeanor by the fireplace. He cleared his throat and straightened his black silk redingote.

"I have an arrangement with your mistress that you should keep me company, so please sit back in the chair."

"She's not my mistress, and I prefer to stand." Jeanne sounded bolder than she felt. Her anger gave her strength.

"You little toad," hissed the marquis, looking more menacing than if he had yelled at her. Jeanne winced. "Will you obey, now? You don't know who you're dealing with!"

A thousand possibilities shot through her head. She would be put out on the street. Claire would be cross. What should she do? "All right, sir, what should I do?"

Satisfied, the marquis straightened up, squared his shoulders, and put his chin up.

"Well, that's better."

He motioned for her to sit down again. Jeanne reluctantly went to the chair and took a closer look. Only now did she notice the many chains and rings that were hidden inside. She gulped. She felt hot. She slowly turned around and sat down on the chair. Nothing happened.

The marquis went back to the lever. His eyes shone with excitement.

"Now," he exclaimed, pulling the lever down.

The click of the latch on the front door gave both Jeanne and the marquis a start. Jeanne jumped up. The door opened, and Margot appeared.

"You rang for me, sir?" Margot was dressed in black and was wearing a mask.

"Jeanne?" she exclaimed in surprise. "What are you doing here?"

Jeanne sobbed and ran to her. The marquis dropped his arms in resignation.

"Lord, this child doesn't work here," she exclaimed indignantly. "Jeanne, go to your room. I'll take care of the guest."

At that moment, there was a clang and rattle from the corner. Large iron rings snapped shut over the chair, where Jeanne's legs had been just a few minutes ago, and another ring closed at the level of the stomach. Jeanne looked in horror at the monstrosity as the seat slowly parted in the middle. Cold shivers ran down her back, and her knees went weak. Margot supported her. The marquis looked at her with a cruel smile. Jeanne pulled herself together, curtsied quickly, and left the room. As the door closed, she heard bits of conversation.

"This is my specialty, sir. What do you want?"

"The chair doesn't work," she heard the marquis whine.

"That is normal, Maître. It is rusty and has not been used for so long. We will find other means." Jeanne could hear the rattling of chains as she hurried off.

***

"Hou - se, Hor - se," spelled Jeanne.

"I'm genuinely impressed, Jeanne." Madame Besson nodded encouragingly.

Jeanne had gradually recovered from her experience with the creepy man. She learned from Claire that he had made an ugly scene in front of Gourdan. Since then, Claire was even more afraid and wanted to lock Jeanne in the room. But Jeanne resisted. Madame Besson had been giving her reading and writing lessons every day after the shop closed for the last two months. She also taught her bookkeeping. Her boss had said that she was gifted in the field of commerce. Jeanne had learned arithmetic very fast and understood the relationship between incoming goods, expenses, and taxes, fixed and variable costs.

Madame Besson was proud of her little pupil, and Jeanne absorbed everything enthusiastically.

"As a camp follower, it is important that you should be able to read, write, and count so that you can't be ripped off," she had said, giving Jeanne a good reason to learn.

"What does ripped off mean?"

As so often, Madame Besson could not suppress a smile. "When someone tries to cheat on you. People could try to pay you less or have you sign documents that put you at a disadvantage," she explained to her.

Jeanne had hopped excitedly when Madame Besson told her she wanted to teach her to read and write. She could hardly wait to go through whole books, but she yet too slow.

"A pie - ce ... piece of ... bre - ad ... on the ta - ble," Jeanne read on.

"The wa - gon, the do ... door."

"Another week and you'll be able to read and write fluently." There was pride in Madame Besson's voice. Jeanne beamed.

"We're breaking up for today. It will soon be dark, Jeanne. Go home quickly." Madame Besson was uneasy, knowing the surroundings in which Jeanne lived. Jeanne knew that the woman was deeply concerned about her living in a brothel.

Jeanne thanked her and grabbed her coat.

"See you tomorrow, Madame, have a nice evening." Jeanne hurried away. Every day, she felt that she was getting a little closer to her goal. The streets were still bustling with people. She recognized Camille at a street corner, and her heart missed a beat at the sight of him.

"Well, little one? There you are. How are you?" He seemed relieved to see her.

"Great, Camille. I can't complain."

Jeanne saw that Camille was now also carrying a box of sweets in front of him in addition to the leaflets. He caught her look, pushed back his cap, scratched his head, and grinned.

"Yes, I also sell sweets. Here." He put one in her mouth. A heavenly mint taste spread in her mouth. Jeanne opened her eyes wide in amazement. "Suck on it, Jeanne, don't swallow it, okay?" He smiled.

"Oh, it tastes so good." It was her first candy, and she loved it. "I'm learning to read and write at the moment, Camille," she told him proudly. The candy bulged in her cheek.

Camille had to grin. "Great, Jeanne. You're very fortunate."

Jeanne nodded and went on sucking with relish.

"I'm glad you're well."

"Why? I mean, why shouldn't I be well?"

"Didn't you hear about the scandal?"

To her, Camille seemed to speak in riddles.

"No, what scandal?" She sounded bored. "And what has it got to do with me?"

"Well, because it has to do with a girl who lived in this ... well, in the same house as you."

Oh, God! She was shocked. Claire?

"Well, tell me, don't keep me hanging." At that moment, she had no idea how appropriately she had chosen the expression and did not understand why Camille was looking at her so strangely.

"It's a scandal about the infamous girl molester. The marquis is back behind bars, and the citizens are demanding his head."

Jeanne felt dizzy.

"Tell me, what is this about?"

"The marquis was imprisoned for molesting girls years ago. As soon as he was free, he started again. The girl was found half-dead on the bank of the Seine. It's a miracle she survived. The poor thing went through agony. She was one from your house."

Everything was spinning in Jeanne's head. She leaned on Camille, then everything went black before her eyes.

***

Chapter 16

Madeleine - Paris, 1783

Only the whirring sound of the scissors slicing through the fabric could be heard. The girls sat at the long worktable behind the store, kept their heads down, and sewed. The dressmakers cut out the templates, and Louisa designed models. She had finally been able to get her ideas through. Thanks to Anne-Louise-Germaine Necker.

At first, Rose Bertin hadn't taken her seriously. "Prove yourself first. Work your way up. Then maybe you will catch my attention, and who knows, I might give you a chance," the boss had said to Louisa.

Disappointed, Louisa had stowed away her notebook with the fashion drawings back on a shelf and sat back at the sewing table. Since then, the notebook had laid there, forgotten. Then one day, while Madame Bertin was looking for something on the shelf, it slipped out and fell open at Anne-Louise-Germaine Necker's feet. 

Louisa had rushed to pick up the sketches, but the customer had let out a cry of delight.

"I want this dress, Madame Bertin, this one and no other!" She had smelled of roses and looked enchanting in her yellow silk dress. Her dark curls hung out from under a straw hat adorned with light, silk flowers.

"Are you sure, Mademoiselle Necker?"

"Oh yes, Madame Bertin, quite sure. It is the dress that I have always secretly dreamed of as a present for my companion. Can it be finished by Friday? We are hosting a ball."

"Of course, Mademoiselle, we will do our best. The dress will be delivered around noon on Friday."

"Oh, fine." She had clapped her gloved hands lightly, and her curls had bobbed gaily.

"It should be a surprise for my friend Maria. Please deliver the finished dress to the usual address."

That was when Louisa was allowed to realize her first design. She worked feverishly for days, hardly eating and without a break, for fear that it would not be finished on time.

Madeleine looked up from her needlework and watched her friend admiringly. Her hair, which was otherwise neatly pushed under the hood, seemed to rebel, and single strands peeked out wildly.

The clock struck noon.

The seamstresses put their work aside, took off their overalls, and went into the kitchen, chatting. Madeleine remained seated.

"Aren't you going to eat?" asked Louisa.

"Only if you go too."

"This is blackmail." Louisa smiled. "Alright, get us something from the kitchen, okay?"

"Isn't that forbidden?"

"Yes, it is forbidden to eat over the fabrics. But if we eat in front of the door, nobody can say anything, and it saves me the waiting time."

Madeleine went out to get her friend's meal. There was a lovely smell of quince in the kitchen.

The cook understood immediately. She served Madeleine two pancakes with delicious jam.

After they had eaten, Louisa took up her work again. Madeleine sat by the window and stared at the table in front of her.

Sometimes, when she sat very still, she was overcome by intense melancholy, just like now. She had been forced into leading this life, away from her family. And yet, although with a bad conscience, she had to admit that it hurt less and less. She loved this work, the environment, and also her colleagues, especially Louisa.  Her family was here now. But sometimes, there was a pang in her heart when she wondered how little Jeanne was doing and whether Marianne was happy.

She missed her parents too. Why hadn't Father come by to see how she was doing in the past few months? She sighed, fiddling with a piece of used felt. Lost in thought, she took a quill from the table and held it to the fabric. Then she took ribbons and pinned them together with the feather to the felt. She twisted and turned it around. It had a strange shape. She found the rest of a lace border on the table and wrapped it around the strange structure. That looked hilarious.

She grinned and put the felt down again. Yes, it was better not to brood but to keep busy. As soon she had something to do with her hands, the gloomy thoughts disappeared and gave way to a kind of contentment she had not known before.

She went to her seat and resumed sewing.

The next day, late in the morning, the dress was ready. Rose Bertin came to the sewing room, especially to appraise it. She put her pince-nez on her nose to better assess the small seams, frills, and pearls. Satisfied, she took it off again. "Very good, Louisa. A wonderful job and bravo, girls, for the good work."

Everyone had shared the thrill with Louisa, and now, the girls looked at each other with relief and cheered. Exhausted, she sank into her chair. Praise from the boss was as rare as a shooting star in winter.

"Madeleine, would you please wrap the dress in tissue paper and deliver it to this address."

Madeleine nodded, but her hands began to tremble. She was seized with panic. She didn't dare tell Rose Bertin that she couldn't read. But Madame Bertin, the old vixen, couldn't be fooled, and she motioned for Madeleine to come with her. In her light yellow silk dress with a hoop skirt and high collar, Rose Bertin strutted ahead of her with her head held high, her ringlets bobbing at every step.

Madeleine followed her meekly, wondering what would come next.

"You can't read, is that right?" Oh, dear! Now she will sack me.

"No, Mademoiselle, I apologize," Madeleine whispered.

"You can't help that. Would you like to learn?"

"Yes, Mademoiselle, very much."

"I'll take care of that." Mademoiselle Bertin drew a plan on a piece of paper with distinctive signs. A cross for a church, a croissant for a bakery ...

Madeleine smiled gratefully as she took the piece of paper.

"Thank you very much, Mademoiselle," she stammered.

"Now, be quick and deliver the dress."

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

"Everything your heart desires! Come to Madame Besson, the best goods, the best quality!" It was as if she had known these slogans all her life.

Jeanne strolled through the alleys that she now knew by heart, greeting shopkeepers, shoeshiners, and passers-by. They all knew Jeanne by sight. She covered increasingly large areas around Madame Besson's business. She had agreed with her boss not to move further away until she knew the area well. She often mistook certain girls for her sisters and ran to them. But hope turned into disappointment each time they turned around.

Camille came around the corner, raised his cap in greeting, and grinned broadly. "Hey there, little one. All going well?"

"Yes, Camille. And what about you?"

"Very good."

If I ever marry, Jeanne thought, it would be to a boy like him. Camille must have been around 13 years old. He was tall, had medium-length, blond, straight hair, an angular chin, and the most beautiful smile in the world. He was always in a good mood and always had a joke to tell. She liked people with a positive attitude and saw him as her role model. She had recovered from the encounter with the marquis and told Camille the story. He had been shocked but also fascinated that she had seen that monster up close. Since then, a close friendship had developed between them.

They walked together for a while, and he again gave her a piece of candy. Jeanne put the sweet stone, as she called it, into her mouth and rolled her eyes in delight.

Camille laughed heartily. "It is so easy to make you happy, my dear Jeanne." He kissed her cheek and said goodbye. Jeanne stopped short and touched her glowing cheek. I'll never wash again, she thought and strolled on dreamily, stopped in front of a fashion store, and looked at the large sign over the door. It seemed strangely familiar.

"The great Mo-g-ol," she read softly.

She looked carefully at the shop window and wondered if, as a future sutler, she might also be able to sell thread and sewing needles. Could soldiers sew? Probably not. She grinned.

The shop door opened, and Jeanne shrank back. A Mademoiselle in a simple dress and a bonnet came out with her head bowed over a note. She turned around and called out something to someone in the shop.

Jeanne admired the long, blond braid that peeked out from under the hood. Just like my sister, Jeanne thought wistfully and waited eagerly for the girl to turn around. But the blonde with the hood went in the opposite direction. Driven by a powerful urge, Jeanne wanted to follow her. Every time a girl looked even remotely like one of her sisters, she needed to make sure. Jeanne was convinced that this was the only way she might be lucky enough to find Madeleine and Marianne in this huge city.

"Stop, stay there," called a voice, and Jeanne turned around. An old woman held out a coin. "Give me two of the small soaps, please."

Jeanne cursed inwardly, took the bars of soap out of the box, and held them out to the woman.

"Come to Madame Besson's, they have great goods at exceptional prices, high quality, the best in all Paris," she droned on as usual and tried to keep the blond girl in sight, but she had already walked around the corner.

Jeanne turned away sadly and sighed. It was surely just her imagination again, as it had so often been lately.

***

Madeleine - Paris, 1783

The streets were all similar, but thanks to the map drawn by Madame Bertin, Madeleine found her way around.

After a twenty minutes' walk, she came to the building that Madame Bertin had drawn as a castle. Madeleine realized that the comparison wasn't far-fetched. She rang the bell on the outer gate. After a few moments, a page appeared. Madeleine had a feeling of déjà-vu but couldn't explain why.

She was led into the marble-floored entrance hall and asked to wait. She heard two girls joking with each other upstairs.

"No, you."

"No, you."

"But it's your dress."

"You chose it for me."

"But I'm tired."

"And I have no shoes on."

"I'm your boss."

"No, that doesn't count; that's unfair."

The girls cackled happily.

Madeleine looked around in awe. Precious, gold-framed paintings hung on the walls of the hall. Tables and chests of drawers decorated with brightly colored flowers stood between the huge double doors. Housemaids, kitchen maids, and other servants were bustling about in expensive, black dresses with white aprons.

"Ungrateful beast. I'll go and get the dress, then. We can't keep the delivery girl waiting."

Anne-Louise-Germaine Necker hopped down the stairs, humming gaily. She wore a simple afternoon dress made of light blue taffeta.

"Thank you, Mademoiselle." She took the package from Madeleine, pulled off the tissue paper, and spread it out.

"A dream," she breathed in awe. A bit louder so that her friend could hear, she added: "Much too nice for a good-for-nothing like you." She winked at Madeleine.

A chuckle came from upstairs.

"Come here and have a look at it, Maria," begged Anne-Louise.

"No. I'm not presentable, and you know that," came the playfully priggish reply from the upper floor.

Madeleine curtsied and turned to go. Something in her guts bothered her.

"Just a moment," said Anne-Louise, putting the dress aside. She rummaged in a drawer and gave her a Louis d'or. "For your trouble."

"Oh, thank you, Mademoiselle Necker." Madeleine curtsied, overwhelmed. It was a lot of money. The page led her back to the gate. Before she stepped out in the street, she paused and turned around thoughtfully. She just couldn't explain the strange feeling in her stomach. On the way back, she tried to shake off this strange sensation and concentrate on not losing her way. Rose Bertin was sure to be waiting for her, and she definitely didn't want to be scolded.

When she finally got to the shop, a whole committee was waiting for her. The girls stood around Mademoiselle Bertin and looked blankly at Madeleine. No one spoke a word. One could have heard a pin drop. Rose Bertin was holding something in her hand. On closer inspection, Madeleine recognized the piece of felt she had decorated in a moment of thoughtful boredom. Rose Bertin stared at Madeleine expectantly. "Did you do that?"

Oh, dear, thought Madeleine, what have I done?

"Yes, Madame ..." Madeleine knew that denial wouldn't do any good. The atmosphere was tense. The girls stared at her. Looking for help, Madeleine looked around for Louisa, but she was nowhere to be seen. She had a bad foreboding.

"I did it in my spare time and only used leftovers," she tried to defend herself meekly and looked down in shame. What would happen now? Would she lose everything again? It was so horrible to live with this constant fear gnawing at her.

"It's just fantastic!" Rose Bertin put the felt on her head and looked at herself in the mirror.

Madeleine was speechless. The girls grinned at her.

"You mean ... you like it?" Madeleine was dumbfounded. She couldn't believe what was happening. Her silly creation was actually sitting on her boss's elegant head.

"My goodness, I certainly do. You are very talented. If you can do something like this out of leftovers in five minutes, what will you create when you have good material and time?"

The girls giggled at the joke.

"That was done unconsciously, Madame. I just wanted to keep my hands busy ..."

"I appreciate your modesty, Madeleine. Then I hope you'll soon want to keep your hands very busy." There was general laughter, and the tension eased into happy babbling, congratulations, and many questions from the girls. Rose Bertin tugged at her new headwear. "I'll teach you. Hats are my specialty. But of course, I can't be everywhere."

Madeleine still couldn't believe it. A dream had come true. The boss herself would be her teacher, designing the clothes with Louisa and the matching hats with her. It was hard to believe that the most elegant women in Europe would soon be wearing their creations. The significance of the idea made her dizzy. Now it was time to think about the future. While her colleagues shuffled around her, the queasy feeling in Madeleine's stomach did not let up, even if she no longer knew where it had come from; an inexplicable feeling of having overlooked something important...

***

Chapter 17

Marianne - Paris, 1783

"You look ravishing!" enthused Anne-Louise, delighted when she saw Marianne in her new dress. "Turn around!"

The light flounces fluttered as Marianne turned around.

"I've never worn such a beautiful and expensive dress."

"You're not just wearing it, Maria. It's yours."

Marianne beamed; she was speechless.

Madame Necker was having a party tonight, and many important personalities would be there. Anne-Louise was to be introduced to society, and Marianne was allowed to come along. She was overjoyed. Which girl had not dreamed of a ball with beautiful clothes, dancing, and admirers?

"I'm almost ashamed to be so happy, Anne-Louise."

"Have I already told you that my father wants me to be called by my second name from now on, especially in public?"

"Louise?"

"No."

"Do you have another name?"

"Yes."

"Well?"

"Promise me not to laugh."

"Of course." Marianne tried to look serious.

"Germaine," Anne-Louise burst out, and both began to laugh heartily.

"Now, tell me, have you ever heard such a terrible name?"

"Oh, go on, it's alright." Marianne tried to stay serious. But when she saw Anne-Louise's face, she snorted again with contained laughter.

"From today onwards, you must call me Germaine; otherwise, my dear father will be cross."

Anne-Louise's father was a former banker who, until recently, had been France's director of the Tresor Royal. Anne-Louise had spent a whole morning explaining to Marianne the connections at the royal court and in politics. But Marianne wasn't sure if she really understood everything. Her father would soon return, Anne-Louise had declared, whatever that meant.

Marianne had changed a great deal in the last few months. She had learned to read and write, took dancing, singing, guitar, stitching, Latin, geography, and history lessons with Anne-Louise, or Germaine as she was now called.

"Mademoiselle Germaine Necker?" Marianne now teased her. The girls laughed so much they had to hold their stomachs. Marianne knew that Anne-Louise saw her as a true friend, a soulmate, even though they did not come from the same social class. And she felt the same.

Anne-Louise was suddenly earnest as she said: "Soon there will be a revolution, Father says, and then we will all be the same anyway."

"A revolution?" Marianne didn't understand.

"The people will rise up against the monarchy and abolish it. They will demand freedom and equality." Anne-Louise swirled around as if performing an act depicting justice, spinning and dancing gracefully, pretending to reach out for something invisible, for a promise which cannot be held.

Suddenly Marianne thought about her parents and friends in the country, who had been starving for years, and a shadow crossed her face. Anne-Louise paused in her joyful, theatrical dance and looked at her with concern.

"Marianne? Are you all right?" whispered Anne-Louise.

"It's nothing. I was only thinking of my home in the country," Marianne replied absently. This is not the time, she thought to herself. She should not yield to melancholy.

"You miss your family, don't you?" Marianne shrugged, not wanting to hurt her dear friend, especially not on this important and special day. "Speak up. Do you think I don't already know?" Marianne searched Anne-Louise's gaze for reproof but finding nothing but understanding, she nodded timidly.

"I don't want you to think that I don't like it here, but I do worry about my parents."

"And Jacques?"

Marianne nodded again, even though she knew how silly it was.

"I know it must seem stupid. He is surely already married to a wealthy farmer's daughter and has already forgotten all about me."

"Oh, Marianne, I can't imagine that. One doesn't forget a girl like you. Have you ever really looked at yourself? Your beauty is breathtaking. As beautiful as the morning dew that settles on the leaves, pearly, fresh and irresistible ... stop, wait, I have to write that down." Anne-Louise dived for her diary under the bed and quickly wrote something in it.

Me? Beautiful? Marianne studied herself in the mirror that hung over the fireplace. True. In this beautiful ball gown, she looked like a princess straight out of Anne-Louise's fantasy world. But she had never thought of herself as being beautiful.

Satisfied, Anne-Louise closed the book again. Reading the doubts on Marianne's face, she stood behind her, gently took her shoulders, and looked at her reflection in the mirror.

"Just look at those lovely, slightly slanted, brown doe eyes, those beautifully curved full lips, those pink cheeks, and high cheekbones, and that perfect nose. Not to mention your beautiful, dark brown, straight hair. I would give anything to have even one of these things!"

"What are you talking about? You are beautiful, Anne-Louise."

"Germaine," corrected her friend.

"Sorry... Germaine. Just see how many admirers you already have." It was true. Not a day went by without a handsome young man paying her friend a courtesy visit.

"They love my position or my alert mind, but not my looks."

"But isn't it much more important to be loved for your soul than for your looks?"

"Oh, Marianne, you are being philosophical! How wonderful." They both giggled. "You're certainly right. But that doesn't change the fact that I would also love to have some of your beauty."

"As always, you are exaggerating, Germaine Necker. You look wonderful, and we only need to show up on the streets to prove it. Everyone looks only at you."

"That is solely due to your 'don't touch me' body language, dear Maria, because once you put that aside, they will have eyes for you only."

Marianne chuckled at this preposterous notion. It was true that she exercised restraint. On the one hand, because it suited her nature and her upbringing, and on the other hand, because she knew exactly where her place was: she was an employee, even though the family had come to like her, and she reciprocated. She would never dare to go overboard and act like a princess or a woman of the world.

As always, Anne-Louise had managed to distract her from her grief.

"In any case, you can be sure that a revolution will also benefit your parents ... and Jacques."

Marianne nodded. If that were true, then she was all for this revolution.

"But," she suddenly whispered, "Isn’t it treason to talk about overturning the monarchy?"

"Of course," said Anne-Louise, putting on a solemn face, but then she broke into a giggle and whispered: "That's exactly what makes it exciting, don't you think?"

Marianne wasn't so sure about that, but she still smiled. Anne-Louise was in such a good mood that she couldn't help but believe in it too.

"In the salons of the gentry all over Paris, this is all they speak about, my dear. The king would have to lock all his subjects in prison."

"But why would the upper class want a revolution? Wouldn't it be dangerous?"

"Certainly," said Anne-Louise, and became thoughtful. "It's in the Air du Temps. It is the time for thinkers, and philosophers, and enlightenment. The injustice is so obvious that it is almost better if the revolt comes from our ranks than just from the people." Marianne began to understand. An action controlled by those above seemed more desirable than a brutal outbreak from those below, especially for the bourgeoisie.

"But let's talk about the upcoming ball now. We'll have time for politics later."

***

The music played, and the gentlemen asked the ladies to dance. Marianne had taken dance lessons but was still rather clumsy. Her new dress with the deep décolleté and the sweeping hoop skirt gave her a grace that her dance steps belied. Not a dance was left out, and the young gentlemen did not seem to mind her lack of sophistication. On the contrary. It was the first time that Marianne noticed the penetrating glances of the opposite sex.

As she danced by, she heard Madame Necker say to another lady: "She just lacks the innate grace of the aristocracy. You don't learn something like that, voilà, proof."

Embarrassed, Marianne turned away, seething with rage, but she still tried to concentrate on the dance steps. She was annoyed at her own awkwardness but even more annoyed at this mean remark.

Her dance partner now seemed relieved to walk her back after the dance and turned to her neighbor, a native baroness with auburn curls adorned with gold threads and pearls. Shortly afterward, the woman was gliding loftily across the dance floor, sleek and elegant.

Marianne was deeply hurt. Her feet ached in her dancing shoes, and she wished she could run away. She was out of place here. Confused, she reached for a glass of wine from the tray of a servant passing by. What was supposed to be her introduction to society seemed to be turning into a disaster. She gulped down the acidulous drink in a few sips.

"Maria, my dear, are you enjoying yourself?" Anne-Louise came to stand beside her, gasping for breath. Her ocher-colored dress, embroidered with pink roses, was one of the most beautiful of the ball and brought out her golden complexion superbly.

"Yes, Germaine, of course."

Anne-Louise's father was not far away, and the girls were careful to follow his instructions by the letter and only call Anne-Louise by her second name.

"But you look sad, Mari!"

"You don't make a princess out of a frog," Marianne quoted from a fairy tale that Anne-Louise had recently told her. "I can't dance, and I guess I'll never learn. And on top of that, I am making a fool of myself. But don't worry about that. Don't let me spoil this wonderful evening for you."

"Come on." Anne-Louise held out her hand. Marianne didn't understand right away but let herself be dragged along without resistance. Her friend led her to the dance floor and gently began to dance with her. Marianne was stiff and clumsy, but gradually she relaxed as her friend laughed in her face. Marianne suddenly forgot where they were. She surrendered to the music, rocked her head to the rhythm, and forgot herself in the process; the wine, which she had drunk a little too hastily shortly before, did the rest.

Marianne's tension eased, and the dance steps came naturally. The awkward duckling turned into a beautiful swan that glided elegantly, almost provocatively, over the parquet. Her brown eyes sparkled, and her wavy hair, studded with flowers and pearls, swayed gently to the rhythm of the music.

The girls did not go unnoticed. A general outrage spread among the crowd. The older women fanned themselves, piqued, and called for their smelling salts. The young women watched, torn between spite and jealousy, while they bad-mouthed this unladylike performance behind their hands.

The gentlemen, however, enjoyed watching a spectacle which seemed to fuel their fantasies. When the dance was over, the bachelors came rushing with requests. The girls became the sensation of the evening. Madame Necker shook her head, and gave her daughter a disapproving look.

Marianne could hardly believe that the young men were suddenly fighting to hold her, the awkward peasant girl, in their arms for a dance. Anne-Louise winked conspiratorially at her.

"Mademoiselle Germaine, may I?" A young soldier held out his arm.

"Of course." Anne-Louise gave Marianne a meaningful look. Marianne had to pull herself together to avoid giggling foolishly.

"Mademoiselle?" A pleasantly calm voice made Marianne turn around. A dark-haired, blue-eyed soldier bowed and extended his arm to her. "First Lieutenant Gaspar, at your service," he introduced himself, his Adam's apple bobbing up and down. Marianne took his arm and walked to the dance floor, spellbound.

His eyes met hers, and the world around her blurred. How long did they dance? She couldn't have said. A minute or an hour, was that important? She floated along, drowned in the deep blue of his eyes, as in a glacial lake from which there seemed to be no rescue. Her heart was pounding so violently in her chest she thought it would burst, jump out and scream with happiness. For him, too, there seemed to be only her, and the feeling was just amazing. She suddenly felt beautiful, desired, and alive. Why is he looking at me so intensely? Marianne wondered. If he was trying to throw her off balance, he had succeeded.

At some point, they paused at the end of a dance, their cheeks glowing warmly, and he invited her to take his arm.

"May I get you some refreshment or take you into the garden, dearest Mademoiselle?"

"Oh yes!" replied Marianne, feeling shaky and hot. "Some fresh air would be nice."

She didn't know if this was appropriate, but before she had time to think, they were already stepping out through the huge double doors. Marianne closed her eyes for a moment as the fresh air caressed and soothed her overheated skin.

"With all due respect, may I ask for your name?"

"Oh, I’m sorry. Marianne, Marianne Cotin."

"It's a beautiful name for the most beautiful of the ball," he whispered and kissed her hand. Marianne felt a pleasant shiver go through her. The soldier smiled brightly. "Please call me Pascal." Marianne blushed. The embarrassing pause which followed was cut short as they started to stroll toward a gazebo.

"Marianne?"

"Yes... Pascal?"

They sat on the cold stone bench, and he took her gloved hand in his.

"May I court you?"

"Yes, of course, Pascal." Marianne wasn't sure what that meant exactly. But it sounded nice.

"Monsieur Pascal, I have to tell you that I am of humble origin." She had gathered all her courage to make this confession to him.

"Me too." He grinned.

"Oh, really?"

"Yes, I come from the lower nobility."

"I'm not aristocratic at all. I come from a family of farmers."

"Is that an obstacle?" He kissed her hand and looked deeply and pleadingly into her eyes.

"N ... no. Not for me."

"Not for me either, Marianne. I fell in love with you at first sight. There is something special about you, something that you don't find in the other society ladies."

"Oh..." Marianne didn't know what to say.

"Something pure, unspoiled," he added in a whisper when he noticed that Marianne was uneasy.

"Don't all young girls have that?" Marianne didn't understand.

"Oh, no! The aristocratic ladies are ... well, often a bit arrogant and conceited." He imitated the stiff mannerisms at the court. Marianne giggled gleefully.

"Whereby you, Marianne, exude a natural grace. I am deeply devoted to you."

Marianne had never felt this sudden prickling in the belly, so violent that she had to gasp for air. She never wanted to leave this alcove again. Never again. They sat there silently and looked into each other's eyes. What she saw in his eyes was pure love.

"Do you love me a little too?" He suddenly broke the silence that had felt like being on a lonely island in a distant ocean. If that pleasant tingling in her stomach was love, yes, then she loved him dearly.

"I don't really know you yet," she replied cautiously instead. She didn't want to hurt him or appear improper. "But, yes, I am attracted to you."

"You make me happy, dearest. As from today, I will court you and ask for your hand as soon as it is appropriate, and my situation allows me."

She had to think spontaneously about her first great love and couldn't avoid the comparison. It had been different with her and Jacques.

They had known each other from an early age and had lived through a lot together. They had become engaged against their parents' wishes and promised to wait for each other no matter what. Marianne was hesitant. Was Jacques still thinking about her? What had become of him?

Pascal felt the change in her.

"Did I say something wrong, Marianne? I beg your forgiveness if that is the case... if I am overwhelming."

"No, not at all." Marianne came out of her reflections. Perhaps Jacques had long forgotten her or was even married.

"I can wait, dearest. I can wait a long time until you are ready to favor an unfortunate lover."

Marianne smiled. Which girl would not be charmed by those words?

"Please be assured, my most beautiful flower, you have robbed me of my heart, and every minute without you will be sheer agony. I will think about you day and night."

His gaze glided over her face, to her neck, to her bare shoulders, her décolleté, and finally back to her eyes. Her breath was taken away, and without realizing what was happening to her, Marianne slightly parted her lips. He seemed to regard it as what it might well have been, an invitation, and bowed to her, looking yearningly at her lips.

"May I kiss you once, please?" he pleaded in a whisper.

"Yes," Marianne murmured, trembling inwardly. His soft lips lay on hers, and as if struck by lightning, an unprecedented, violent emotion shook Marianne to the core. She wished she would never have to leave this alcove, never ever.

Chapter 18

Alain - Paris, 1783

"There's a little girl coming in and out and who fits your description," whispered Johan.

Alain looked around. He wanted to make sure his old woman wasn't within hearing distance. He had lost everything and was now living with his brother-in-law in the poor district. The good life was over.

Sometime before that, he had followed the whore named Julie, who had shown him the door to the house. But when he pounded on all the doors and questioned the neighbors, it turned out that the little beast had moved out only a few hours earlier. A few hours! Could you believe it? A couple of hours that had changed his life forever. Just a couple of stupid hours!

When Louis realized that Alain didn't have the little one and wouldn't be able to find her so quickly either, he had flown into a rage and brutally beaten him up for making him lose his reputation as a reliable supplier of fresh goods for Gourdan. He had urged Alain to get the hundreds of livres he owed Madame Gourdan as quickly as possible; otherwise, he could forget about his shop. Alain had never been in such a fix before, then he knew that Louis never made empty promises. And sure enough, less than a week later, his worst nightmare came true. Louis had come with a group of men who had demolished his shop. He could have rebuilt it, but he knew that it was no use because Louis would come back. Since then, he and his old woman had been hiding in a sloppy dwelling, a filthy hole in the poor district where he endured her incessant nagging. He had lost weight and felt weak. His trousers hung on his body, and he only fed on gruel and porridge. There was no more wine, and no prospect of whoring either.

Louis was known all over Paris, and nobody wanted to help Alain for fear of getting into trouble with Louis and his wild gang.

The hatred Alain had for this peasant tramp was immeasurable. If he ever got his hands on her, he would strangle her. No, tear her to pieces. Better still, desecrate, torment, strangle and then tear her to pieces.

Johan was the only one who still secretly kept in touch with him. Not often, because it was too dangerous. Louis could consider this as a betrayal and take revenge on Johan as well. Alain wouldn't be surprised if he were already spying on him.

"Are you sure?"

"She fits your description exactly. Pretty, blue-eyed and with long, dark blond hair."

"Yes, then Gourdan has her after all!" Alain was indignant. But Johan shook his head. "No, the little one doesn't work for her. I've been making inquiries. She only lives there with a whore who is supposed to be her sister."

Alain scratched his testicles. Damn lice, he thought. "Is that so? Her sister?!"

"How long are you going to stand at the door, you stupid good-for-nothing?" snapped his wife from inside the house.

Johan grimaced in sympathy, which tormented Alain even more. Now he even had to bear the pitying looks of others.

"I don't want you to get into trouble with your old woman as well."

"She can go to hell. When I finally get hold of the little one, she'll see who is the boss."

Johan grinned and walked away. Alain also hurried back into the house.

Revenge, he thought.

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

"A well-run bookkeeping consists of the main ledger and the subsidiary ledgers." Jeanne pondered briefly and continued to quote: "These serve to explain the general ledger. They include the stock ledger, the payroll book, the banking book, and the current account book -"

She paused as someone pounded on the shop door.

"It's been closed for an hour," said Madame Besson, getting up to look. Jeanne went back to her reading, with which she was making good progress. If she ever really did realize her dream of owning her own shop or even becoming a sutler, she was well prepared.

"Go away, we're closed," Jeanne heard her boss say.

"Who is it?" Jeanne asked curiously as Mrs. Besson was blocking her view.

"Oh, one of those young, scantily clad things. She surely wants to beg. She looked at me so pleadingly that it made my heart sore. But I don't give alms; we hardly have anything ourselves," said Madame Besson, genuinely sorry. But shaking her head, she indicated to the woman that she would not open the door. "There's no doubt about what she is trading in. I'm tolerant, you know that Jeanne, but I still don't want one of those in my shop." The person was insistent, inducing Madame Besson to indicate with her hands that it was closed.

"I want to see Jeanne, please, it's urgent. Is she still there?" Jeanne heard the familiar voice call through the locked door. She jumped up from the stool. Claire?

"This is Claire," Jeanne exclaimed. "Please, do let her in."

Madame Besson reluctantly opened the door, and Claire dashed into the store, panting.

"Has something happened?" asked Madame Besson, lifting a hand to her throat in shock.

Claire nodded.

"Claire!" Jeanne rushed to her friend. "Was has happened?

My little one, Madame Gourdan ... You must come home with me," Claire stammered, stroking her curls, and gave Madame Besson a pleading look of apology.

Alain - Paris, 1783

Alain was making plans. He would have to explain everything to Gourdan. After all, he was responsible for the brat who had been personally entrusted to him by her old man. The Comtesse would then explain everything to Louis, and Alain could lead a normal life again. Well, halfway normal, because he had surely lost his customers and would have to start everything from scratch, not to mention the shattered shop he would first have to rebuild.

He looked down at himself and was ashamed. His pants were stiff from the dirt, wrinkled and riddled with holes. He leaned dejectedly against the wall of a house and scratched between his legs. Could he show himself to the refined madam in this state? He was in limbo. Would they even recognize him and let him in, dressed as he was? He shook off his doubts. He had no choice. It was the only chance to get his life back on track and break out of this misery.

He kept walking. Heavenly smells of roast and wine wafted towards him from a tavern. He felt his stomach cramp. The hunger was already unbearable before, but now he could have killed for a piece of meat. He ran, stumbled, got back to his feet, and kept walking. She would have to listen to him. Everything would be alright again. He would go back to his former way of life with all its advantages and pleasures. He could even feel something stirring in his loins again. He grinned blissfully. At last. Only a few meters between him and his new beginning. Alain turned around the corner and stopped dead in his tracks. Armed soldiers were standing in front of the house.

What had happened here? Has another whore been beaten to death? This happened often. Shit, he thought. That didn't suit him at all. He would have to be patient now. Sighing, he leaned against the moldy wall of a house and waited.

Jeanne - Paris, 1783

"Dead? But she was fine this morning!" Jeanne was dumbfounded. How could someone just die like that?

"She was ailing since her - well," Claire cleared her throat, "incarceration, but she knew how to cover it up."

Claire felt Madame Besson's stern, critical gaze, and instinctively adjusted her dress.

"I know what you're doing for Jeanne, and I'm very grateful to you. Unfortunately, she has to leave earlier today, because the gentlemen from the police have come to close down the house and we have to find accommodation for ourselves quickly."

"You can take tomorrow off, Jeanne, so you can have time to do the needful. Let me know as soon as you can ... and ... if you have a problem, come to me, okay?

Jeanne nodded. "Thank you, Madame Besson."

Jeanne and Claire said goodbye and ran to the Château. When they got to the reception room, the other girls were running around like frightened hens, jabbering in confusion. Some sat, dejected, on the red velvet armchairs in the large drawing-room; others wept. Jeanne thought feverishly. She had heard of a Palais Royal from one of her comrades. He had said: "Over there, the whores are traders, and the traders become whores, you can't tell them apart." Surely this piece of information could turn out to be of good use.

A small man stood in the middle of the room with his legs wide apart. His fat belly seemed about to burst the seams of his coat, and his powdered wig, which was decorated with rolls, sat crooked on his fat head. The dusty smell of flour that emanated from him reminded Jeanne of a bakery. His gaze roamed lasciviously over the half-dressed women. He cleared his throat and finally assumed an exaggeratedly stern expression. "Mesdemoiselles, your attention, please!" He waited until the chatter had ceased completely. Emma was still chatting when she noticed the sudden silence, and turned around in amazement. Ashamed, she wrapped her silk bathrobe over her naked body and legs with stockings only reaching up to her knees.

"Well then," the man growled. "I am inspector Marais. As you all probably already know, Madame Marguerite Gourdan, née Stock, passed away today. The establishment must therefore be closed."

There was chaos as the prostitutes all started to speak at once.

"But Monsieur, is there no one to take it over?"

"Quiet!" Inspector Marais thundered through the room, making the girls jump. "That is not my problem. In the meantime, it definitely has to be closed. I'm sorry, ladies. You have a week to move out of here. After that, you will be expelled by force."

Some of the girls were relieved that they still had one week left, while others seemed shaken. The older women were particularly concerned because it would not be easy to get a place in another brothel at their age. Madame Gourdan had kept them because they had served her faithfully for decades.

Jeanne ventured to walk up to the inspector.

"Dear Sir, excuse me. How can one find the Palais Royal, please?"

The inspector looked at her suspiciously, but Jeanne's clear gaze seemed to fascinate him. He cleared his throat again.

"Aren't you too young to work in such a house?" he asked, dismayed.

"I don't work here, I'm a trader, and I live here with my sister."

"Is that so? A trader. And what do you trade in?"

"In petty wares, sir. Tobacco, eau de vie, soap, and many other things." She wished she hadn't left her hawker's tray at Madame Besson, but how could she have known that it would come in handy today?

"Hmm," the inspector muttered. "Do you have any savings?"

"Yes, Sir. Why?" said Jeanne truthfully. She did not have much, but the fact that they lived for free and were fed at Gourdan's had allowed her to put some money aside, however, with a very specific purpose in mind.

"Well, if you have enough money, I can assign you a shop in the gallery in the Palais Royal. One has just become vacant.

"How much, Inspector Sir?"

"Well, let's say 40 Louis d'or for allocation and five per month."

Jeanne reckoned. "20 Louis d'or and 7 Louis d'or a month," she suggested.

The inspector stopped short and had to smile.

"25 and 6, and that's my last word." He held out his hand, and Jeanne struck into it, as she had learned from her comrades.

"Done." Jeanne was proud of herself. She knew that a shop in the Palais Royal was something special. The shops usually included a warehouse and an apartment.

"Pay me out now, and tomorrow you can come to the Palais," the inspector said.

"Sir, I don't buy blindly. I want to see the shop first, then I'll give you the money."

"Child, how old are you?" the police officer asked, impressed.

"Fourteen," Jeanne lied, "but I'm small for my age."

"Well, I'm not worried about you. You'll go far, girl."

"Yes, Sir, in the true sense of the word."

Without hesitation, he gave her an appointment for the next day.

Satisfied with her new achievement, Jeanne went to Claire, who was having an agitated discussion with Emma and the other girls.

"Where are we supposed to go?" Emma exclaimed anxiously. "Oh, dear, oh, dear ..." Margot shook her head. She was lost. Jeanne plucked lightly at Claire's dress.

"Not now, Jeanne."

"Claire," Jeanne insisted.

"Not now, please." Nevertheless, her friend turned around. She hesitated when she saw the joy in Jeanne's eyes. "What is it, my darling? What have you been up to again?"

"Nothing. I have just saved our lives."

Chapter 19

Alain - Paris, 1787

The pitcher flew past his head and crashed against the wall. "And don't you dare come back without bringing your earnings, you miserable scoundrel!" 

Alain had ducked just in time. He grabbed his worn-out vest and dashed out of the house. Once on the street, he turned around several times, but his wife was not following him.

"The old woman is becoming increasingly unbearable," he muttered angrily. They had been starving for weeks, so he had started stealing. At first, he was ashamed to have sunk so low. But sitting in the tavern and drinking his first jug of wine, he had no regrets.

He had come to attend events such as executions, fairs, theatre performances, and meetings to pursue his new activity. It was easier than he thought to cut off the wallets from a distracted audience. He always took only what was enough to treat himself to a bit of pleasure.

But now, the old woman had found out because he had once again hit the bottle too hard. She had beaten the truth out of him, and since then, she had turned life into hell for him because she wanted to have her share of the spoil.

He furiously kicked a moldy wooden chair standing in front of a door, which fell apart in a clatter. Alain shrugged and plodded on. He would have to steal more to bring something home and still afford his small pleasures. He thought hard about how he could do it.

A thought crossed his mind. He grinned. Then he looked down at himself and raised his eyebrows doubtfully. He would need better clothes than those he was wearing to be admitted there. "Alright," he said aloud, "I will buy new clothes from the next loot, and then the palace shall be mine!"

Jeanne - Paris, 1787

"The performance begins in a few moments, Mesdames, Messieurs," the announcement echoed through the palace. White-gloved pages waved their hands invitingly to encourage the guests standing in front of the theater to enter.

Finely dressed ladies and gentlemen chatted while strolling to the entrance of the theatre. Jeanne admired the variety of beautiful dresses and colors. A heavy smell of perfume and powder hung in the air, as it always did every time an event took place. Ladies spun their umbrellas like tops over their shoulders.

She noticed that many noble ladies followed court fashion with their pompous hoop skirts and oversized wigs. Others wore bustles, a kind of stuffed hind part.

Jeanne folded her hawker's tray shut and walked quickly to the business galleries. It had taken her months to find her way around the oversized halls and feel comfortable in them. Her dearest friend, Camille, had greatly contributed to that. A few months later, he had followed her to the Palais Royal, which was a considerable competition for all the other shops in Paris.

"Bonjour, Jeanne. Where are you running to so fast?"

"Oh, Camille, I was just thinking about you." Jeanne's cheeks flushed pink. Why did she say that?

"Thought of me? I'm flattered," Camille said and grinned to tease her. At seventeen, he was already a young man.

"Oh, you ..." Jeanne gave him a friendly slap on the arm. She liked his ways, his smile, and his proximity as much as on the first day.

"Did you sell much today?" he asked.

"It's not bad." Jeanne had to admit that she had hoped for better business at the Palais Royal. But the customers here had higher demands than those in the alleys of Paris. Claire also had great difficulties. Almost every boutique in the gallery also offered girls for prostitution. For the last four years, they had not earned more than they needed to live, pay rent, and taxes. They had no money for new clothes, coats, shoes, and even less for jewelry or perfume.

Claire, in her worn clothes, was in competition with the prostitutes who could afford expensive fabrics and perfumes. That made Jeanne furious. She pondered over solutions every day. How she would love to get Claire away from this job.

Even she wasn't doing much better. She had used the money she had saved in the boutique and even borrowed from Claire to pay the inspector. A horse and a future as a sutler seemed to slip further away from reach.

Jeanne's thoughts were interrupted by the sight of a passing lady wearing a tulip arrangement with feathers tied together by a large white bow on top of her hat. Camille and Jeanne grinned at the grotesque structure, and as always, when she saw Camille's teeth flash, her heart leaped.

"And you, Camille? Did you sell well?"

"No, as always." He was also resigned.

They strolled together for a while without a word. Jeanne enjoyed this shared silence because she was never uncomfortable with Camille, and she enjoyed every second of his company.

"Get out of here, you ruffian! Who do you think you are? We don't tolerate such practices!"

A shopkeeper was hitting a half-naked elderly man with her closed parasol, who quickly took off.

Jeanne and Camille looked at each other again and snorted with laughter. "At least, you never get bored here," said Jeanne.

"That's true," Camille admitted. "There is also more security here than where we used to be."

"Also true," said Jeanne, and they laughed again. Whenever he was around, the sun seemed to shine, problems seemed to take a back seat and solutions there for the taking.

They sat down in a corner, and Jeanne served her friend a glass of eau de vie, which he gratefully accepted and knocked down in one gulp.

"Hey, that's not water!"

"Yes, it is. Water of Life," Camille laughed at his own joke and gasped as the hard drink burned its way down his throat.

They sat for a while, watching the passers-by, who turned away superciliously when their eyes fell on the two of them.

"You know, Jeanne, I think I'll get out of here soon."

"No!" Jeanne couldn't help blurting out spontaneously.

"Well, not right away." Camille looked at her in surprise. He must know how much I like him, Jeanne thought sadly. But she also knew that he considered her as a younger sister. That was why he was surprised. Jeanne sighed.

"I want to join the army."

"Then I'll come with you," said Jeanne truculently.

As Camille laughed with his handsome, boyish face, Jeanne felt a stab in her heart.

"But ma jolie, what will you do in the army?"

"You'll see."

"You must understand, Jeanne. Shall I stay a hawker all my life?" She understood all too well but refused to admit it and crossed her arms defiantly, becoming increasingly restless. "In the army, I can work my way up through good performance."

"Yes, or die," she replied angrily. "Besides, the army is not for common people like us. You will be sent to the front as an easy target for the enemy; every child knows that..."

He seemed puzzled as if he were wondering how Jeanne knew things like that. "One gets snippets of conversation here and there, you know," she said apologetically.

Two girls of Camille's age, very elegantly dressed in sheer fabrics, strolled past them. Camille raised his cap and gave them a big smile. The girls giggled.

Alain - Paris, 1787

He took a long swig of wine and set the mug down. The café was crowded, as always at this time. But he was careful not to go about his work in closed spaces. There were too many eyes that could have caught him doing it.

Alain followed her at a safe distance through the halls of the palace. She looked lovely, he thought. Even better. That would make his plan even more fun. And this time, he wasn't going to let anything spoil it. He grunted with anticipation. He made up his mind not to rush anything. He would plan everything carefully. He didn't want to leave the time, the place, and how he would do it again to luck. Not this time. He followed her at a reasonable distance and saw her disappear into a shop. Marianne - Paris, 1787

Balls, dance evenings, theater, and salons were the order of the day. Since her first ball, when she met and kissed her admirer, Pascal, one event followed the other. Pascal often came to Madame Necker's salon and participated in literary meetings.

Marianne was very proud of him. He courted her, as he said he would, took her for walks in the garden, kissed her as often as possible, and wrote her sentimental love letters. A few months ago, he received his transfer order to the Rhine Army. He had been heartbroken ever since.

He wrote to her almost daily and swore eternal fidelity. Marianne enjoyed his letters, affection, and attention, but keeping up with her benefactor's unbridled rhythm of life gradually caused the memory of Pascal to fade.

She liked to stroll in elegant attires on the arm of Madame Germaine de Staël – formerly Anne-Louise Necker – who had gotten her way and married the Swedish ambassador. They would walk along the galleries of the Palais Royal and marvel at the variety of shops and well-attended cafés every time they passed that way.

Germaine had married Baron Erik Magnus Staël von Holstein last year, just as her father had planned since her earliest childhood. Her husband had introduced her to the court, which had impressed Marianne to no end. Even if Germaine enjoyed the reputation of a married woman and had already given birth to her first child, for which Marianne envied her fervently, she did not seem really happy. Something seemed to be eating her. Actually, Germaine no longer needed a companion, but the girls had grown so fond of each other that Germaine insisted on keeping Marianne with her. Marianne did not accompany her to the court, but she often went to the theater, the cafés, and above all, the now-famous salon of Madame de Staël, her former Anne-Louise.

Her friend had started very early to do the same as her mother and hold a salon. She secretly read Voltaire and harbored revolutionary thoughts that Marianne did not always understand and even found dangerous. The more she took part in such events, the more she began to understand the connections since she was one of the few who had experienced the true extent of poverty first hand. But she did not express herself as fluently as Germaine, who had been used to political, literary, and philosophical debates in her mother's salon from early childhood, mastered Latin, English, and music, and even wrote her own books.

Marianne had been a diligent student and listened carefully to the lessons from her friend. She had understood how lucky she was to grow up in this society, and that knowledge also meant power over her own life and thoughts.

Today, she and Anne-Louise would go again to the theater. As always at the entrance, the pages called out to encourage the theatergoers to come in and not miss the beginning of the play. As the two young women quickened their pace, they bumped into the tray of a vendor so that the goods fell out of her box, clattering on the marble floor.

"Can't you be careful?" Germaine scolded angrily. However, Marianne started to bend down to help the girl, but Germaine dragged her on testily.

"Please, Maria, do not degrade yourself by helping a street girl."

Marianne shook her head at her own absent-mindedness. Germaine was right. How stupid of her. She would have to shake off the peasant girl in her. But did she really want that? Come to think of it, why did Germaine make so many speeches about freedom and equality if she was not going to stick to those principles herself? Marianne was suddenly vexed.

"Why didn't you let me help her? Aren't we all equal?" Marianne dared to question. It was the first time that she dared to hold her own. Germaine looked around in surprise as if she had just heard an enormity.

"What does equality, at the level we speak, have to do with a dirty street brat?"

"Everything," exclaimed Marianne, frowning indignantly.

"Oh, Maria, you're confusing everything. When we speak of these ideals, we are talking about globality, equal rights for all, equal laws for all, enough food for all," Germaine corrected her. "But that doesn't mean that I have to sit down with the poor on the street or eat their horrible groats!"

Marianne disagreed.

"Still, that doesn't mean that one should not respect them, right?"

"But of course. We do respect them, which is why we want to change things. Now come on. Besides, the brat should have been careful. She gets in our way, and we should be the ones to apologize?" Germaine was now getting angry. "Pah! That's out of the question, do you hear?" Marianne gave in and nodded silently. She looked around once more, but she could no longer see anything through the dense crowd. She thought she heard her name coming from a great distance, as if the ghosts of the past were coming to life, as if something dear to her was somewhere far away, trying to reach out to her. It must be an illusion. Her fantasies were beginning to play tricks on her. Was she becoming sick? Sick in the head? The idea gave her a huge shock. Perhaps that was why she was being sensitive about such a petty matter as the vendor's box.  She sighed. Still, she was left with a strange feeling...

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1787

Jeanne put the goods back in her box. She was annoyed at herself. What was going through her mind? Camille, of course. She was still mad at him. Suddenly she stopped and looked back on the accident. The girl who had bumped into her and wanted to help her... Marianne! My God! Her eyes nearly popped out of her head. Was it possible? She dropped the box again, her goods scattering once more on the floor, and ran off, hoping to find the girl among the passers-by.

"Marianne," she called desperately. "Marianne!" She ran, pushed, hopped, and looked in all directions. A few steps away from her and just before the entrance to the theater, Jeanne saw the girl again. She was just turning around, and Jeanne was now absolutely sure. That was her sister.

"Marianne!" she cried as loudly as she could. People turned around. Even Marianne stopped.

"Hey, you rude brat!" an elderly gentleman scolded as she bumped into him. She could see Marianne turning away again. Jeanne wanted to tear herself away, but the gentleman held her tight.

"Well, who do we have here?"

Jeanne looked at him in amazement. She was shocked to see the shopkeeper she had once run away from. He looked so completely different, she almost didn't recognize him. She went weak in the knees. My God! What did he want from her after all these years?

"Come on, daughter, I forbade you to wander around here." He licked his lips and pulled her along with him.

Jeanne screamed, but nobody took any notice. She bit his hand. The shopkeeper snarled with pain, but he did not loosen his grip. Jeanne's throat tightened, and old memories came back to her. How could he possibly be here? She braced her feet against the floor, but her shoes slipped on the marble. She screamed again at the top of her voice, this time in mortal fear. Passers-by turned around. Was it a father who was chastising his daughter?

"Help me, please," screamed Jeanne. "This man is not my father; he is a child molester." Some people paused briefly, eyeing Jeanne curiously, but she read uncertainty in their eyes. Of course, why should any of these fine gentlemen dirty their hands for her? Hadn't she already seen for herself earlier on, when the ladies hadn't even bothered to help pick up her box again?

"Just keep whining, you brat. This time you won't get around your mother's punishment." People shook their heads and decided not to intervene. Who knew if it wasn't really the father? Why should the man make up something like that? Jeanne kept screaming, kicking out, scratching, biting ... but no help came...
