Chapter 20

Madeleine - Paris, 1787

Thick bales of fabrics were piled up on the long counter, but the customer just couldn't make up her mind. As is often the case, it was very busy at Grand Mogol, and Rose Bertin was abroad on a business trip. The girls had to cope on their own, which was rather tricky with spoiled customers like this one. Madeleine, who would have preferred to work on her creations, picked up a new apricot-colored organza fabric and rolled it out.

"I assure you, Madame Pelletier, this fabric is currently the most popular." She tried to convince the customer without showing her nervousness.

The shop bell rang again, and an opulent gentleman in his thirties was let in by the page. He took off his elegant hat, adorned with feathers, and made a perfect bow.

"Mesdames, Mesdemoiselles, Monsieur," he greeted everyone. His gaze wandered around the shop and stopped at Madeleine. The girls curtsied, and the ladies bowed slightly, returning the greeting.

"This is Talleyrand," Madame Pelletier whispered and nodded meaningfully as if it were the king himself. The name meant nothing to Madeleine, and she didn't really care about the man either. She just wanted to get rid of her customer.

Mademoiselle Véchard was the first saleswoman to approach the gentleman.

"How can I be of service to you, Monsieur?" But the distinguished gentleman continued to look unabashedly at Madeleine. He cocked his head and screwed up his eyes, seeming to consider something. As if to free himself from some thought, he shook himself and turned to Mademoiselle Véchard.

"I would like to be served by this demoiselle, if I may." He waved his hat slightly in Madeleine's direction. The fine hairs of the feathers danced in the breeze; some were set loose and floated to the ground.

"As you wish, Sir. I will have her released." Madeleine was inwardly jubilant to be rescued from the clutches of this tough customer. The saleswoman replaced her, and Madeleine strode towards Talleyrand, putting on her loveliest smile.

"Mon Dieu," he said, and again bowed gallantly. "An angel."

Madeleine looked down, embarrassed. Men liked to look her in the eye, but unlike them, flattery from this gentleman with the half-closed blue eyes and the slight smile was pleasant. She looked at him again. He was neither handsome nor ugly, but he exuded a magical attraction with a mixture of fatherly confidence, manly arrogance, with an almost boyish, cheeky smile. He wore elegant yellow brocade clothes, and his white wig went well with his cream colored lace scarf and white stockings.

"I'm looking for silk shirts, belle petite."

Madeleine led him to a sales counter. Suddenly, she was acutely aware of her swaying backside, which was emphasized by the bustle. Her blond braid, which was supposed to sit strictly under the hood, hung seductively over her back. Madeleine took advantage of the boss's absence to allow herself a few liberties, as the girls continued to stick together like glue since Lucienne's dismissal. She offered him a seat in one of the pink armchairs.

Limping and leaning on his cane, he walked over to the seat and sat down at her bidding, his bad foot stretched out.

"May I offer you some refreshment?"

"You may do anything." A slight smile played on his lips. "A brandy, please."

Madeleine instructed a girl to bring him the drink he wanted. She got four rolls of cloth and spread them on the table in front of him.

"Here, you can feel how fine our newest silk is and how smooth it feels on the skin."

The corners of Talleyrand's mouth twitched as if he might have added: "And how smooth does your skin feel?"

Madeleine ignored his gaze, but his presence kindled a fire in her. The brandy was served, and Monsieur Talleyrand sipped it. Over the rim of his glass, he looked Madeleine straight in the eye and, as she thought, into her heart. She wondered if he knew how she felt.

He smiled knowingly as if he had already experienced this situation a hundred times, as if he knew precisely what effect it had on young, innocent girls and budding women.

"I'll take three shirts from each color." He set the glass down on the table.

Madeleine beamed with delight. It was the best deal in days, or rather, weeks.

"Would you like anything else?"

"Nothing more for today. Anyway, nothing that would be for sale." He winked at her. "May I just sit here a little longer in this comfortable chair, young lady?" In a whisper, he added: "Then I can watch my angel a little longer."

Madeleine smiled and felt a hot flush rushing to her face. She wished she could fan herself.

She curtsied, stammered a farewell, and disappeared into the store. Once there, she leaned against a wall and tried to collect herself. She was breathless, and her heart was beating wildly.

She had never felt so attracted to a man before. She was in turmoil as she thought of his words, his penetrating gaze, and his gentle, almost ironic smile.

The doorbell rang again, and Madeleine heard her name called. She straightened up, put on her salesgirl's smile, and went back into the shop. A customer wanted to buy new silk, velvet, and lace veils. Madeleine advised her, but her mind was elsewhere. More precisely, her thoughts revolved around the gentleman who was sitting in an armchair a few meters away, drinking another brandy, watching and scrutinizing her with delight, as if he wanted to devour her.

Madeleine could very well sense that he knew her plight. Every time she glanced in his direction, and their eyes met, she smiled sweetly at him. She was sure that the customers and saleswomen present also felt the magical attraction between her and Talleyrand. The heat suddenly became oppressive, and she wondered if it had anything to do with the summer-like temperatures.

After a while, Talleyrand got up, put down his glass, said goodbye to the company, and limped out of the shop.

He turned back to Madeleine at the entrance, put on his hat, tapped its edge with his forefinger, and nodded to her. Madeleine was on cloud nine. That meant "See you soon," didn't it?

***

Marianne - Paris, 1787

Dazed, Marianne sat down on the velvet armchair. She actually loved going to the theater. But she was not feeling well. There was a strong smell of rice powder and all kinds of odors. She held the tightly laced bodice on her stomach. She felt a hot flush and wondered if she had closed it too tightly?

Germaine was not paying attention to her. She was waving left and right whenever she saw a face she knew.

"Oh, do you see this gorgeous man over there?" Germaine raised her opera glasses to her eyes. "I've never seen him in our salon."

Marianne did not react. She had heard her name clearly earlier on in the hall of the Palais Royal.  She could have sworn that one of her sisters had called her.

"Maria?" Germaine nudged her and handed her the opera glasses. Marianne raised it limply to her eyes and watched the gentleman. "Hmm," she said.

"Is that all?" Germaine's indignation seemed real. Then she waved it off. "You and your Pascal! You will learn soon enough that there is more than just one great love, you dreamer."

Marianne smiled, although in a daze. If only she could think in peace without having her friend's constant chatter in her ears. She wanted to go over the previous scene again in her mind because she instinctively sensed that she had overlooked something important.

"Look, it's starting, Maria!"

The lights were dimmed, and the curtains raised. But Marianne's thoughts were far away. Where though? She had lost the thread after a while.  But the poor girl with the hawker's tray was certainly part of her brooding.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1787

A punch landed squarely on his nose with a sickening crunch, and Camille stood angrily in front of him.

"Get out of here, or I'll kill you!" he hissed furiously, holding the shopkeeper by the collar. The man snatched his hat off the floor with one hand and held his nose with the other.

"You'll hear from me, you brat!" he roared through the gurgling as blood ran into his mouth, but when he saw Camille's threatening look, he quickly got up and left.

Camille turned to Jeanne.

"I just saw my sister when this horrible creep of a man tried to pull me away."

Camille nodded.

"Is everything alright? Did he hurt you?"

Jeanne shook her head. The way Camille worried about her was downright touching. He had saved her. Jeanne didn't want to think what would have happened if Camille hadn't found her at that very moment. But now that the man was gone, her nerves failed her, and her whole body started to shake. Camille put his arm around her.

"Where is she?" he asked softly.

"Who?" Jeanne was momentarily confused.

"Well, your sister!"

Bewildered, Jeanne turned around, but Marianne had disappeared into the crowd. Dejected, she let her shoulders sag.

"Thanks for your help. I’ll be fine."

"Are you sure?"

"Yes. You can leave. I'll manage on my own now."

"Is it because you're still mad at me?"

She shook her head and had to smile.

"Of course not, silly."

"Then, I would like to stay."

"And your sales?"

"I was going to call it a day anyway, so come on. Where's your tray?"

Only now did Jeanne remember that she had left it lying on the floor in her haste to follow Marianne.

"Somewhere there, if someone hasn't already stolen it."

Camille took Jeanne's hand and pulled her along, guiding her through the crowd against the stream of people still walking towards the theater. But as much as Jeanne searched, the ladies with their hoop skirts, feather hats, wigs, umbrellas, and bonnets all looked the same. She kept looking for a while and turned away with a sigh as the gates of the theater closed. In her dejection, Jeanne wondered if finding Marianne again was not a hopeless pursuit. She recognized Marianne; she was sure of that. But too late. But why hadn't she recognized her sister immediately? She decided to come back as soon as the performance was over.

From a distance, they saw a bunch of boys picking up the goods from her box lying on the floor. They took as many soaps, tobacco, and eau de vie as they could carry with them and ran away as they saw the two of them coming. Camille started running, but it was already too late.

"Hey, you there!" Jeanne rushed to the pathetic remains of her goods. "If I ever lay my hands on you, you worthless rabble!" She cursed angrily at them. The box was still there, but the few products that had not been stolen by the hooligans had been trampled on the floor. Together, they picked up everything that could still be saved.

***

"Huh, what's the matter with you today?" Claire asked.

"Oh, nothing." Jeanne sat down grumpily on her bed.

They had a small apartment behind the shop with a kitchenette, beds, and a laundry room.

"Do you have any problems?"

"No," lied Jeanne. She didn't want to scare Claire with the fact that the shopkeeper had turned up. Her friend was worried enough as it was about her. The idea that this scoundrel now knew where to find her, scared Jeanne. But after his confrontation with Camille, he probably wouldn't dare bother her anymore, or so she hoped.

Claire left it at that and tried to doll herself up with what she had left of make-up and powder.

"The theater has only just started, so you still have time before the first clients come out."

Claire paused and looked at her. "What's the matter, Jeanne? Don't you want to confide in me?"

"I saw my sister."

"Oh! That is wonderful."

"But I recognized her too late. She had already disappeared into the crowd.

"Maybe it wasn't her? As you so often ..."

"Oh yes, Claire, it was her this time. I'm sure." Jeanne sighed.

"Well, if she goes to the theater often, you'll see her again," her friend tried to comfort her.

"She was dressed so elegantly. Like a princess," said Jeanne sadly.

"You and your dreams of princesses. You are a princess too. My little salesgirl princess."

Jeanne smiled half-heartedly.

"Claire?"

"Yes, my darling?"

"Have you ever been in love?"

Claire smiled.

"Yes, I was, with the officer I followed to Paris. Very much so, I would say."

"And is it normal for it to hurt?"

"That what hurts?" Claire's eyes opened wide.

"Well, love. It hurts here." Jeanne pointed to her stomach. Claire looked relieved.

"Yes, this can happen. If you don't know whether the other person loves you too, or if you are jealous, or if you miss him ..."

"Hmm," said Jeanne, lying down and staring at the ceiling.

"Don't you want to tell me what's bothering you?"

"I love a boy who is older than me, who looks at beautiful prostitutes and sees me as his little sister." Tears ran down Jeanne's cheeks. She wiped them off, angry at herself for being so weak. "Besides, he wants to go away. To the army. And then?" Sobbing, she threw herself on her pillow.

"Does he know that you love him?"

"Now, he does."

"How did he react?"

"Not at all, he was too stunned ..." Now she had to laugh despite her tears. Claire laughed too but sounded concerned. Jeanne knew that Claire couldn't stand to see her protégé so sad. At fifteen, she thought Jeanne was too young to think about falling in love, and Jeanne knew it. Claire would have preferred Jeanne to remain a child forever. But things didn't work that way.

"Jeanne, it makes my heart sore to know that you are so sad," said Claire and sat down next to her on the bed. "It's true. Love can really hurt. But in time, it will pass."

"Oh, yes?" Jeanne sniffed. "I doubt it. I will surely die from it," she said as she shook helplessly from another fit of crying.

"If that were true, my darling, then I'd be dead a long time," said Claire. Astonished, Jeanne paused and looked at her friend, who was staring at her hands in embarrassment. How selfish I am, thought Jeanne. Claire never complained, never whined or hinted in any way at her worries, be it with a sigh. And yet she pursued the most difficult job in the world and endured it all because women like her – with no education, without a family or a husband – didn’t have many options. For years, she had looked after Jeanne without complaining, shielded her from all misery, and preferred to starve rather than see her protégé go without. She would never have come this near to having her own shop without Claire, and also, without Madame Besson. And what was she doing? She was crying her heart out for a seducer. She sniffed. But still, she couldn't bring herself to get really cross. What a sweet seducer he was. Jeanne was doubly stricken: out of grief over an unrequited love and because she felt so deeply sorry for Claire.

"Forgive me," stammered Jeanne, throwing herself into Claire's arms. "I'm just thinking of myself." Claire rocked her like a child, kissed her forehead, and hummed soothingly.

"Time will heal a broken heart. At one time or other, it will pass, believe me. What remains are just a few nice memories that you cherish and keep in your heart like precious stones. Every now and then, you take them out, look at them, and tell yourself that you have been lucky to feel and experience something like this. Then it doesn't hurt anymore. You might feel a slight tweak, but that isn't even unpleasant because it also reminds you that you are alive, breathing, and feeling."

"That sounds beautiful, Claire. I am so happy to be your protégé, so infinitely grateful."

They hugged each other. Even Claire had tears in her eyes.

"Oh, Jeanne, sometimes I wonder which one of us is the protégé," she said and laughed softly. Jeanne had to smile; she liked the thought. She had never seen it that way before. At that moment, her heartache over Camille faded into the background. She had someone she could count on and who could count on her. Was there anything more important? "After all, it's you who we owe this boutique to, and that I'm independent. Even if times are tough and I can’t be picky with customers, I am better off here than in any Maison Close. It's all thanks to you."

"Now, don't overdo it. I owe you my life." They looked at each other with a smile.

"And besides, my little Jeanne, there is still hope," Claire said unexpectedly, sounding confident.

"Do you think so?"

"Sure, give him some time. Let him writhe."

"But what if he does go away? Or take someone else?" Jeanne's eyes opened wide. She couldn't stand the thought of losing him or seeing him with other girls. She felt everything inside her cramp up again.

"Maybe he was just saying it lightly. It's not that easy to join the military. And military schools are expensive, I know that from ..." Claire fell silent.

"You still love him, don't you?" asked Jeanne.

"Yes. But he's been married a long time and has forgotten about me. And he wouldn't get involved with a whore anyway."

"He already did," said Jeanne.

"Back then, I was what you would call a decent girl from a good family."

"Why didn't you go back home?"

"Out of shame. Out of spite. I didn't want to give in and give my parents the satisfaction of having been right."

Jeanne's thoughts wandered back to Camille. His laugh, his mouth ... His kisses felt so good ... He smelled so good ... How could she stop Camille from leaving? How?

"He hasn't been selling well since he gave up the sweets. That's surely why he wants to go," Jeanne said, more to herself. There seemed to be a message between the lines of this sentence. But what was it? Jeanne thought hard.

"I can understand him, somehow. There he has a wage, food, and a place to sleep. For a young man with no prospects, that idea doesn't seem so bad," Claire pointed out.

They both sat, lost in their own thoughts.

After some time, Claire got up and went to the old mirror that hung on the wall. She pinched her cheeks listlessly and frowned at what she saw in it.

"Of course," Jeanne suddenly exclaimed, causing Claire to jump. Jeanne slapped her forehead.

"What is it now?"

"Why didn't I think of it before?" Jeanne stared at her friend with wide eyes. "I just got the idea of the century!"

Chapter 21

Claire - Paris, 1787

Claire strolled up and down in front of the palace. The freezing autumn wind went right to her bones, as she was so slim. Many young, well-dressed, made-up, and powdered prostitutes strolled up and down in front of the entrance, shivering from the cold. Distinguished gentlemen engaged the pretty girls in conversation; others were approached by the prostitutes themselves. They laughed out loud at jokes, batting their eyelashes flirtatiously. Some disappeared on the arm of a gentleman and came back after a while to entice new customers.

Only Claire was left out.

She lacked the desire and the necessary self-confidence to behave as artificially as the other prostitutes. Her dress, which had been patched several times, and shabby stole, did nothing to improve her situation. She sincerely hoped that Jeanne's idea could be realized. It was high time because Claire was tired of this existence. She didn't want to spend the rest of her life serving men and hoping, in this freezing cold, that a bad-smelling creep would take pity and spend a couple of Louis d'or on her. When she looked down at her outfit and then compared herself to the fresh, sparely clothed beauties smelling of perfume, she couldn't even blame the gentlemen for not coming to her. She couldn't keep her teeth from chattering.

A man came down the street, and in the twilight, Claire could only see his silhouette at first. He seemed hesitant, almost shy. He went up to the loitering prostitutes, who turned away one after the other, shaking their heads.

Claire watched him curiously. He was a young soldier, rather small and slender. His uniform looked unkempt and even torn, and his long, greasy hair hung over the collar of his coat. He walked away but came back to make a fresh attempt. He was again turned down by the girls. His gaze fell on Claire, and he came closer timidly as if expecting her to turn him down too.

But Claire couldn't afford that. No matter how scruffy he was, she needed the money.

"You seem to be freezing. How can you stand it here in the draughty hallways?" he started the conversation.

"It is hope that helps me, monsieur."

"You are so petite and weak. Aren't you tired of this trade?"

"Certainly, sir, but I have to make a living." Claire frowned. She had never been asked such questions before.

"Right." He pondered on that.

"Is there no other trade that would be more suitable for your health?" His face radiated genuine concern. His serious gray eyes seemed to pierce through her.

"Yes, maybe, except that I haven't learned anything."

"May I invite you to my humble home for a cup of hot cocoa?"

There was something almost touching about this awkward way of starting a conversation with a prostitute, and if she hadn't been so cold, she would have giggled.

"Yes, Monsieur. With pleasure," she said instead, doubting that the gentleman really only wanted to have cocoa. Claire hooked up with him, and they walked away in the freezing wind that whipped through the dark alleys.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1787

Occasional passers-by strolled by, but towards the evening, it became much quieter. Jeanne had been sitting on the stool behind the counter for the last hour, pondering on her idea. She looked at herself in the glass pane of the showcase and straightened her hair.

"Hello there, ma jolie."

Jeanne started.

The door of the shop was cracked open, and the tip of Camille's nose appeared. "May I come in?"

Jeanne looked at her friend in astonishment. He had never come to the shop before. Her heart skipped a beat.

"All clear?" he said, pretending to be afraid and looking around everywhere before coming in.

"Idiot," replied Jeanne, smiling.

"Ahh, it's so good to see you smile again."

"I have good news for you."

"For me? Let me guess. You are, in reality, a rich princess waiting for her prince charming?"

Jeanne snorted with laughter.

"No? What is it then?"

"Our lives will change very soon. And you won't have to leave here."

Camille pushed back his cap, scratched his blond shock of hair, and grinned expectantly at her. "Well, I wonder what you're up to again."

"It's actually obvious. It seems so simple that it's almost stupid."

"Come out with it, now, or do I have to kiss you first?"

Jeanne gave a startled laugh and stared at him.

"You don’t need to feel obliged. Just forget about it," she said, avoiding him and nervously putting away a few cigar boxes under the sales counter.

"What if I don't want to forget about it?"

"Then, that's your bad luck. I've already forgotten about it," she said, grinning.

"You little beast." He came around the counter and stood close to her, his blue eyes looking intensely into hers.

"No, you didn't," he said softly.

"Conceited as always, our dear Camille," said Jeanne, trying to appear calm despite the wild beating of her heart.

"Stop fooling around with me," Camille whispered.

"Said the wolf and ate the sheep?!" countered Jeanne, standing her ground. If only he could look inside my mind, she thought. A mighty storm had broken out in her. Her hands grew damp, and her throat went dry.

Camille pushed back his cap and scratched his head, a gesture that was sure to melt Jeanne's heart completely. He closed in on her, disturbingly near, and ran a finger over her arm, tender and yet so full of meaning.

"Don't! That tickles," Jeanne said, playing down the situation.

"Oh yeah, that tickles? Should I show you what tickles?" he asked with an amused grin and went on to tickle her in earnest.

Jeanne dodged him nimbly, ran into the back room, and tried to lock the door. But Camille got ahead of her and put his foot in the door. Jeanne gave a mirthful squeak, ran to the bed, and hid under the covers.

"Now I've got you," said Camille theatrically, playing the hungry predator, and slowly closed the door.

"Well, where could she be?" For the sake of suspense, he pretended to search under the table, behind the dresser, and in the kitchen. Jeanne's excited giggles were muffled by the covers.

"Ah, I think I heard something. Oh no, that was nothing. I'm going to lie down on the bed for a rest.  All the searching has exhausted me."

He flopped down on the bed.

"Oh, what's that?" He felt for Jeanne and found a hand.

"What is this? Who does it belong to?" He groped further.

Jeanne's heart was beating so hard she felt it might jump out of her throat. She pulled the covers off her head to catch some air, showing her flushed face.

"Oh, how lovely," said Camille.

"Not as beautiful as the others."

"More beautiful."

"Why?"

"Because it's you, Jeanne."

"Your sister?!"

"No, not since that kiss." He took her hand and played with her fingers, embarrassed.

"Oh yeah? Why?"

"Because you have stolen my heart. I have feelings for you, more than I would have thought possible."

"Like a brother for his sister?"

"No, damn it, Jeanne, the way a man loves a pretty girl."

"Why?" She didn't know why she was asking such stupid questions, but her doubts would not subside.

"Jeanne!" he groaned in annoyance and let his head fall on the pillow. "Because you kissed me."

"So what?"

"And I liked it very much."

"So what?"

"You, pain in the neck!"

"Pain in the neck yourself."

"Did you like it too?"

"Yes," she had to admit unwillingly, conscious that she was blushing.

"Do you want to do it again?"

"Hmm, maybe."

He turned to face her, hugged her tenderly, and hesitated briefly. Jeanne could hardly stand it anymore. She wanted him to finally do it; on the other hand, she didn't want to give in. But damn it all, why doesn't he just do it?! He leaned forward, and his lips touched hers. Overwhelmed, Jeanne closed her eyes. Tumultuous sensations blazed inside her, like fireworks being set off. He said he loves me; the thought flashed through her mind. Me, the little Jeanne. There was something unreal about it.

"Should I show you something, Jeanne?"

"What?"

He gently pushed his tongue into her mouth and let it circle slowly around hers. Jeanne melted away. It felt wonderful, so close to him, so intimate.

"Do you like it?" he asked in a whisper.

"Yes, that is very nice. Where did you learn that?"

"From watching."

"You, peeping tom!"

"Don't get cheeky," he said, tickling her.

Jeanne squeaked with laughter.

They kissed again, this time more fervently. Jeanne wished it could last forever.

"I've never felt so good in my whole life, Jeanne."

"Me neither. I could spend the whole night in your arms."

"Me too."

"Camille, I've always loved you."

"Even as a baby?"

"I wasn't a baby."

"Yes, you were."

Camille kissed her so quickly that she couldn't reply, and Jeanne giggled when she understood his scam. He grinned at her cheekily. She never wanted to stop kissing him, never ever.

"Jeanne?"

"Yes."

"Didn't you want to tell me something?"

"Oh, yes." Jeanne suddenly let go of him and straightened up. He grinned again. "My Jeanne is back."

They laughed heartily.

"Why did you stop selling candy?" she asked him straight out. Camille frowned in surprise, wondering what she was getting at.

"Because I wanted to be here, in the palace. Where it's warm. Where you are." He kissed her neck while he said this, sending shivers through her. But still, Jeanne was not distracted.

"Are there any shops here that sell candy?"

"I don't think so. Why?" He sat up, thinking. Jeanne smiled meaningfully. "Ahaa! I get it! Sapristi! You know every trick in the book, don't you, my girl? You want to start a candy business. But how should we go about it? We don't even know the recipes. Besides, we don't have the equipment or the raw material ..."

"We don't need it either!"

"We don't? Tell me how. I can hardly wait to hear it."

"We go to your former employer and offer to open a branch for him."

"A ... what? What's that?"

"A sideline which he stocks up with goods and bears his name. I learned that from Madame Besson." She thought back wistfully about her benefactress, whom she, unfortunately, could not visit as often as she would like. "Having a branch in Palais Royal is pure gold."

Camille was simply amazed.

"And does that work? I mean, will he agree to that? "

"As sure as Jeanne Cotin is my name, and going through the worst swamp has not stopped me," she replied with her head held high, "of course he will." Camille laughed out loud. "We buy the goods from him in bulk, so he doesn't run any risks. And on top of it, we pay him a percentage after the sale. In return, he gives us low prices so that we can sustain the business."

Camille thought feverishly while Jeanne got up, went into the shop, closed the shutters, and bolted the door.

"Jeanne?"

She sat down again next to him. "Yes?"

"You are a genius!"

He took her in his arms, looked at her tenderly, and hugged her tightly.

"Stay with me tonight," whispered Jeanne, and Camille moved closer, pressing against her.

"But what will Claire say?" he asked anxiously. "I don't want to find myself with my head cut off tomorrow." Jeanne giggled at the thought of Camille being afraid of Claire. "I don't want to cross the mother dragon."

"Why? We're not doing anything wrong."

"Alright. Agreed. I wouldn't be able to leave now anyway," he said, pulling her close and kissed her again passionately. Jeanne had never felt like this before. She discovered completely new emotions that had nothing to do with the languorous tingling in her body. It was like a deep longing that took hold of her whole body and asking for more, even if she couldn’t fathom what it was exactly. Camille seemed to feel the same way because his sighs and groans became more intense with each kiss as if he were in agony from having to hold himself back. And Jeanne couldn't deny that she felt a certain satisfaction in holding him back and feeling so intensely desired...

***

Jeanne was awoken abruptly by her friend coming home late at night. "Where have you been for so long?" Jeanne mumbled sleepily.

Claire collapsed tiredly on the bed, and Jeanne carefully let go of Camille. They had fallen asleep at some point, snuggled close together. Camille mumbled in his sleep.

Claire pricked up her ears. "What was that?"

"Camille."

"What is he doing here?" It sounded like a pathetic attempt to be indignant, but Jeanne could tell that Claire was too worn out to be upset.

"Shh, be quiet, or you'll wake him up!"

"You didn't ...?" whispered Claire, horrified.

"What are you thinking? And even if - how does that concern you?"

"I feel responsible for you, Jeanne. You're still too young ... you still have plenty of time for that. Hold him off, Jeanne, because men are often in a hurry to run off once they have what they want. Believe me."

"Not all men, surely."

"I haven't met one yet who was different. In the worst case, they run away immediately; at best, they play around a little with you. Or should I say it the other way round?" Claire whispered. "If they leave immediately, your heart may suffer less because you know straight away where you stand. If you give in too early, they will lose interest, and you will be impure in their eyes.

"Impure?" What was Claire babbling about? Was it her exhaustion that was getting to her?

"Yes, when it comes to marriage, they want a girl that has kept her virginity. Then how does a man know that he was the only one when you were ready to give yourself to him..."

"That's nonsense," Jeanne hissed indignantly. "Camille knows very well that I love only him. He'd never think of me like that." She wanted to listen to Claire's advice, but what she said had to make sense before she could take the advice.

"Of course. I just want to save you from any heartache, sweetheart." Claire sighed and wriggled her feet out of her shoes. "Maybe you are right, and Camille is not like the others. Still, take care of yourself, Jeanne, will you?"

"Yes, yes, alright. We only kissed and promised each other eternal love, that's all."

"Swear."

"Claire!" scolded Jeanne. "You'd better tell me where you've been. I was worried. That's also why Camille stayed with me. We looked for you, but you weren't in your usual place."

This was true. They had waited a long time in the cold for Claire and then returned to the room, where they had kissed over and over again and then fallen asleep.

"I was with a gentleman, a soldier, to be precise."

"Was he nice?"

"Hmm, yes. He was bizarre. A strange little man."

"Why?"

"Well, he talked a lot and seemed educated, and yet he behaved like a ... a peasant. Not like an officer. I've had many officers, but I've never had one so clumsy, so uncouth. With bad manners and badly dressed."

"And?"

"Nothing much. We drank cocoa, and he offered to find another job for me. He said that I shouldn't do this job anymore."

"He is right. I already like the man."

"Oh, Jeanne, do you think I like doing it?"

"No, but you're not doing anything to get out of it either. That will change, though."

Claire yawned. "So be it."

"And why did it take so long?"

"Because we talked a lot. He spoke about the people, about history repeating itself, about famine ... about Alexander the great, his idol..."

Jeanne giggled. She could picture the scene: Claire, yawning, and having to listen to hours of lectures about Alexander's expeditions.

"And otherwise?"

Claire started to giggle. "He was still, well ... he had never ... he was very awkward and finished very quickly."

Jeanne snorted with suppressed laughter and Camille grunted again in his sleep. This brought Jeanne to wonder if he had already  ... She didn't want to complete the thought for fear of the eventual pain.

"Was he young?"

"Not that young. Maybe twenty ..."

Jeanne lay back, relieved. Then she still had time.

"But that is late; it usually happens earlier," said Claire, amused. "He is Corsican."

"What's that?"

"He comes from an island called Corsica, in the Mediterranean. It used to be Italian."

Now it was Jeanne who yawned unrestrained. What did she care about an island in the Mediterranean?

"And he had a very complicated name."

"What is it?" asked Jeanne sleepily.

"Buona ... something, and his first name ..." Claire tried to remember.

"Oh yes, now I recall. His name was Napoleon."

"Napoleon," repeated Jeanne. "Napoleon Buonaparte," she mumbled sleepily, "remember it."

"I beg your pardon?" Claire didn't understand.

"Oh, nothing," muttered Jeanne and went back to sleep, hugging Camille very closely.

***

Chapter 22

Madeleine - Paris, 1787

My angel, since I set eyes on your enchanting beauty, I can no longer sleep at night.

How can such a creature exist?

How can words express the deep feelings which overcame me during our brief meeting? I am looking for such words, but they all seem too trivial, too common to describe this burning flame.

What should I say?

I love you, Madeleine.

Plain and simple, I do.

I need you.

My intentions are honorable, even if the urge to hold you becomes unbearable.

I missed your presence from the moment I left the Grand Mogol. How else could it be with soul mates, if I may be bold enough to presume that you have feelings for me? The brief moment that we spent together was like going through the upheaval of a devastating storm that left its indelible mark behind.

Your face is etched in my heart.

I beg you to meet me again.

Should you have a free afternoon for a trip to the countryside, where you, the most beautiful of all flowers, would be among her own kind, kindly let me know through the waiting messenger.

You would make me a happy man and provide me the opportunity to show you the worthiness of my affection.

On the day and at the time of your choice, a carriage will be waiting for you on the other side of the palace.

Awaiting your reply,

I remain your devoted friend and admirer,

CMT

"Charles-Maurice Talleyrand," Madeleine whispered and held the perfumed letter against her breast. "Yes. Oh, yes, I want to see you again."

She went back into the shop, her cheeks burning hot, and delivered her answer to the messenger. "Tell him: Sunday at ten o'clock in the morning would be appropriate," she whispered so that the bystanders wouldn't hear. But Rose Bertin, who had meanwhile returned from her business trip and seemed to have her eyes everywhere at the same time, was not to be deceived.

The messenger bowed and quickly left the shop as if he sensed disaster approaching and wanted to be out of the way as fast as possible. When Madeleine skipped elatedly back into the workshop, Louisa looked up from her work and grinned. She was working on a sketch for the Belgian royal family.

"There, you see. I told you he was coming." Louisa seemed genuinely happy for her friend.

"Who is coming?" came Mademoiselle Bertin's stern voice from the door.

The girls winced.

"I ... eh ... it's ... a customer wanted ..." Madeleine stammered, desperately looking for a way out. She paused, hung her head, and took a deep breath before answering honestly: "I have a date with a gentleman." She knew that lying wouldn't do her any good. Rose Bertin was everywhere and knew everything. Why couldn't she go traveling again?

"Young lady, we are not in a cathouse here. As long as you live under my roof, it is entirely out of the question that you should meet a gentleman and a customer at that. Who is it anyway? "

"Monsieur Talleyrand, he is highly regarded in the ..."

"For God's sake, child. Are you out of your mind?" Mademoiselle Bertin interrupted, made the sign of the cross, and sat down. The girls looked at each other in surprise.

"Not only is he a rabble-rouser, but he is also a notorious womanizer. No woman is safe from him." Her ringlets, twisted with a curling iron, bounced with every jolt of her head.

Madeleine gulped, and her stomach cramped.

"But Mademoiselle Bertin, please, his intentions are honorable."

"That is what they all say." Rose Bertin laughed bitterly. "But once you reach my age, you will also think like me. Believe an experienced woman; you will be grateful to me later." She shook her head and closed her eyes as if the things that suddenly went through her mind were too terrible to see. "And the consequences, my God, Madeleine, just think of the consequences, for your reputation, for the business and ... " She put her hand on her belly and nodded in the direction of Madeleine's, "and for you, dear Madeleine, for you. Unthinkable..." With these words, the boss left the workshop, indicating that the subject was closed. All color had drained from Madeleine's face. Deep down, she knew that Rose Bertin was right. And yet ...

The girls sat pensively, each lost in her own thoughts.

Louisa put down her feather.

"What will you do?" she whispered.

A wave of defiance rose in Madeleine. "I still have enough time to be old and experienced ... but now, I want to live!"

"You're going anyway?" Louisa grinned conspiratorially.

"No, Louisa, I'm not just going." she laughed back mischievously. "I'll fly there ..."

***

After taking one last look in the mirror, Madeleine snuck out the back door. A delivery of white fox and hare skins for the winter collection was just being unloaded. Taken up with the past few days' exertions, Rose Bertin completely forgot about spying on Madeleine. She had other worries, then she feared for her business with the royal family. The increasing uprisings of the Parisian citizens against the king also frightened Madeleine, but they came at just the right time for her Sunday meeting.

As promised, the open carriage stood in the shade on the other side of the Palais Royal. Despite the early morning, it was already quite warm. Most of the time, the city's air became so stuffy around noon that you could hardly breathe.

Madeleine wore a pale yellow linen dress with white dots. She had plaited her hair in a loose braid, from which several rebellious strands peeked out, which Rose Bertin would undoubtedly have described as inappropriate.

"This is seductive," Louisa had remarked. The braid fell forward over her right shoulder, and she had designed the bonnet to match the dress, a small hat with white ruffles and ribbons.

She crossed the street, her heart pounding.

Talleyrand was standing at the carriage with his bad leg stretched out casually. He smiled and put a hand to his heart when he saw her.

He bowed to her, his hat tucked under his arm, and lifted her delicate hand to his lips to indicate a gentle kiss.

"I would have wished to die had you not come," he whispered, looking into her eyes. Madeleine - blushing as usual - wasn’t sure what to answer, so she gave him a friendly smile and said nothing. He intimidated her terribly, especially since her boss had passed on the gossip about him. And yet, she couldn't turn him down. She longed to know more about him and feel the desire in his words and his gaze again.

The carriage rocked as they got on and sat down. On its floor was a basket covered with a red and white checkered blanket.

"A picnic basket," said Talleyrand, grinning smugly at this good idea. "Do you like picnics, my angel?"

"What is a picnic?"

Talleyrand laughed heartily as if she'd made a good joke. "A picnic is a little gourmet event that takes place in natural surroundings."

She smiled. "Oh, that's such a good idea," she exclaimed enthusiastically, clapping her gloved hands. "We used to do that at home, before ..."

"Before?" Talleyrand's attention was awakened. Without taking his eyes off her, he motioned to the driver, and the carriage began to move. Madeleine enjoyed the gentle motion of the carriage.

"Before the famine, when we still had enough to eat at home, we would often sit in the meadow with Maman and Papa in summer and eat there. We called it casse-croûte."

"It's still called that today. Picnic is just the more modern expression and means taking a lot of time, basking in the sun, playing games, and just having fun. Casse-croûte is more of a hastily eaten meal."

"Well, that was the case, because in the country we never had much time to ... play ... and the picnics were short-lived."

Madeleine turned her head in embarrassment to the little carriage window as she thought of her parents.

"Do you miss your parents?" Talleyrand asked.

Madeleine felt a lump rise in her throat and nodded. "I'm deeply absorbed in my work, but now that we're going to the countryside and I am thinking about them, I have to admit that I do miss them terribly. I don't even know how they are doing. And then my sisters..."

"If you wish, my sunshine, I can try to make inquiries about your parents."

"Would you do that for me?" Madeleine beamed with excitement.

"That, and much more." Talleyrand smiled benevolently at her. What a good thing that I came, thought Madeleine happily.

"And my sisters? They live in Paris, but we've lost touch."

"Of course, I can also ask about them. What are their names?"

"Marianne and Jeanne," replied Madeleine, her voice trembling as she uttered their names again after such a long time.

"Are there any leads that you can give me? A description? A place?"

He really meant it! He wasn't just saying it.

"Unfortunately, I know very little. Marianne should look very much like me, only that she has brown hair and brown eyes. She is a year older than me and was taken in by a family living in an elegant house. The name was Becker, or Pletter, or something like that. Do you know any family of that name?" She was so excited that she had to fan herself for fresh air. Talleyrand shook his head thoughtfully.

"No, my sweetheart, but I'll do all I can to find out. And the other one?"

"Jeanne - she should be fifteen by now - is dark blond and has wonderful, big, blue eyes." At the thought of the baby of the family, her throat suddenly tightened. Embarrassed, she turned away again, clearing her throat.

"Eyes as beautiful as yours?"

"Oh, no. Much more beautiful."

"With all due respect, young lady, that is not possible." Madeleine smiled.

"I don't even know where she has been staying," she said pensively.

"I will do everything in my power to help you find your beloved sisters. I give you my word of honor as a gentleman," he whispered, taking her hand in his again and breathing a bolder kiss on it.

"You are so kind to me," Madeleine whispered back, moved.

"Not good enough for you, my dear," he said, putting his hand over his heart. "I swear that my life would have been worthless if I hadn't finally met you."

During the journey, Talleyrand wanted to know about her. While they were trundling through the beautiful landscape, Madeleine told him about her childhood, sisters, and life with Mademoiselle Bertin. She also confided to him the remark Rose Bertin had made about him.

"Did she say that? A womanizer? Me? Incredible!" Talleyrand shook his head in disbelief. He gave a signal and

"Hoo," the coachman called immediately, stopping the horses.

"We're here, my dearest." Talleyrand, still very agile despite his handicap, gallantly helped Madeleine out of the carriage. He grabbed the basket, and the driver drove off to halt some distance away under the shade of a tree.

The sun felt pleasantly warm. They strolled dreamily across the meadow among the colorful flowers down to the stream. Madeleine found it so beautiful; she could have wept. She picked wildflowers, tied them in a bouquet, and placed them on her head like a crown.

Talleyrand watched her with glee and seemed enraptured by her childlike nature, brought out even more by the rural surroundings. He took off his red velvet redingote, and elegantly laid it on the grass under a tree for Madeleine. Madeleine's eyes shone, and her cheeks flushed with excitement and happiness. She put down her hat and sat on the jacket that was lying ready for her.

Talleyrand took off his vest and sat facing her, dressed only in a cream silk shirt. He also unwrapped the tightly tied scarf from his neck, all the while never letting Madeleine out of his sight.

"Are you happy?" He asked with his hoarse voice, of which she had grown so fond.

"Oh yes, Monsieur, what a wonderful idea you had to organize a ... pique-nique."

"I already found you adorable before, but now, glowing and sparkling with happiness, it becomes almost unbearable ..."

Madeleine felt a sharp stitch in her belly when he said this. The longing to be kissed by him increased with every second. As if to distract himself from the temptation to do something stupid that he would later regret, Talleyrand pulled the cloth from the basket, spread it out, and brought out one treat after the other.

Madeleine was amazed at all the trouble he had taken. He laid out a large loaf of bread, eggs, grilled meat on small skewers, apples, strawberries, chicken, pies, and cheese on the blanket.

He had even thought of including a carafe of red wine and two glasses.

Talleyrand poured the wine and handed her a glass. "To your happiness, Madeleine. May I be granted the privilege of bringing you the happiness that will last forever?" They toasted and drank to that. The sudden bliss that came over her was such that it scared Madeleine. She couldn't make out why she was so scared of losing that happiness. Madeleine didn't want to think about how empty life could be after such an experience.

"Now, it's your turn. Tell me more about yourself," she asked Talleyrand, emboldened by the wine.

"Oh, dear child, there isn't much to tell." He considered where to begin and knocked an egg against the rim of a ceramic bowl.

"I come from a humble background like you, Madeleine." He saw her smile of relief. "As a child, I fell off my wet nurse's changing table, hence my disability ..." He nodded his head in the direction of his orthopedic shoe. "I used to be called clubfoot."

Madeleine nodded sympathetically and took another sip of wine. "As a young man, I would have liked to join the army, but I was turned down because of my disability. Then I began studying at the Sorbonne, after which I was ordained as a priest." He handed her the peeled egg and offered her a piece of bread. Madeleine gratefully accepted both.

"Oh," she exclaimed. She was perplexed. A priest? "You don't look like a churchman," she burst out spontaneously.

"No." He laughed. "Quite right! I'm not one anymore. Well, actually, this is only partly true. I am about to disengage myself, although the Pope is against it. Soon I'll be a simple citizen again. Great things are about to happen in this country. There will be better times for your people as well. Bon appetit, my angel." Talleyrand took a hearty bite from his bread, topped with cheese.

They ate copiously and enjoyed the silence, the air, the warming sun, and the gurgling of the brook. Now and then, they looked at each other and knew intuitively what the other was feeling.

"Please call me Maurice, will you?"

Madeleine nodded. "Agreed, Maurice."

They held up their glasses for a toast and smiled at each other before sipping the wine.

After the meal, Maurice lay down on his back and told her about the coming revolution, his ideas and ideals, and what he hoped to achieve. He explained how he imagined civil rights and that everyone should be on an equal footing before the law.

Madeleine, thrilled by the sound of his voice, listened to him and drank his words like the wine that was already making her tipsy.

"If you've had enough of this, my dear girl, tell me, and we'll talk about other things."

"Oh no, Maurice, I'm so happy to listen to you ... even if you were to talk about creepy things, it would still sound wonderful." She giggled and realized that the wine was clearly going to her head. What a liberating feeling to be so easy and carefree and not have to think about tomorrow!

Maurice straightened up and stroked her face, pulled back a strand of hair, and looked at her tenderly. Madeleine quivered at his touch, which was as soft as his velvet jacket. She couldn't hold back anymore. Forgetting her shyness, she bent down to him, and their lips touched.

"My angel," he whispered, distraught, "are you crazy? How am I supposed to remain honorable if you kiss me?"

"Never mind the honor. I want to live ... here and now; then who knows what tomorrow will be?" She swung the glass, and some wine spilled over. He took the glass out of her hand, set it aside, and smiled indulgently.

"How true," he said, pulling her toward him, and taught her how to kiss.
