Chapter 23

Marianne - Paris, 11. June 1789

"The king has dismissed Father again!" Germaine said indignantly as she rushed into the drawing-room. Her pink chintz dress swayed around her. Marianne looked up from her book.

"That does not bode well, does it?"

Madame Necker followed her daughter into the drawing-room, desperately looking for her smelling salts. Despite the rice powder she had dabbed her face with, her flush of excitement showed.

Her husband had been summoned back to the court a few months ago, this time as finance minister, to bring the increasingly intricate financial situation under control. He was the only hope, so it was said. The states-general he convened saw him as the savior of France. He had proposed solutions, devised reforms, but from what Marianne had picked up at the evening table talks, the king ignored the well-intentioned advice and almost always made the wrong decisions. Necker resisted the reworking of his plans to no avail. In protest, he did not attend the Séance Royale of the 23rd June 1789 and declared that he was resigning.

On that day, he came home early, totally dejected. Germaine, who arrived shortly after him, had opened the windows and shouted: "Listen, Father, listen!" The crowd in the streets was raving and calling his name. The whole of Paris seemed about to set up barricades.

"They think you've been fired," Germaine declared, not without pride. "It is even said that angry crowds came up to Versailles to protest."

Necker had been surprised by the events. And indeed, that same evening, he was called to the castle by the royal couple and requested to withdraw his resignation. This bit of news also spread quickly, and the day of the 24th of June became a personal triumph for Necker.

From that day on, Marianne listened to the daily reports he gave to his daughter and wife, which Marianne found more and more exciting as she gradually began to understand the context. For example, he said that the crown – that is how Necker called the king – could no longer rely on his troops. They are said to have left their barracks and fraternized with the people. Louis XVI sometimes made concessions, only to reduce them to a minimum afterward. It looked like he wanted to buy time in this way. But time for what? Germaine's father had suspected, and there were also rumors, that the king had requested German and Swiss mercenary troops to be stationed around Paris. Since then, Necker had come home almost every day with new stories, shaken by the king's recklessness, who did not seem to take the situation seriously, believing that he was untouchable as the sovereign.

Marianne always felt dizzy at the thought that it could be otherwise, but those who went in and out of the house and belonged to Madame Necker's literary circle certainly seemed to share Necker's opinion. They looked at the events partly with fascination and partly with fear for their own existence as if they suspected that their privileged life was coming to an end. No one seemed to be sure whether the long-awaited change would be so desirable after all, especially if, instead of being discussed in drawing-rooms, it would be put into practice by the people. This could have dire consequences. For days, the atmosphere had been electric and fluctuated between excitement and fear.

One evening, Necker spoke about the thousands of unemployed people who were invading Paris from the neighboring provinces in the hope of getting work and food. Marianne shuddered and thought of her parents.

"700,000!" Necker had shouted indignantly. "Can you imagine such a crowd and what it would mean if the food supply came to a standstill?"

"That would be a disaster," Germaine had replied, and her father had merely nodded, disheartened.

"The king is playing with fire. On the one hand, he is clearly undecided, and on the other hand, he gets foreign troops to deploy, tries to buy time, and plays one side against the other." One could hear the dismay in Necker's voice. "This enormous mass of people represents a huge army of misery," he had added.

"Where could it be?" Madame Necker broke Marianne out of her brooding as she pulled open the drawers of her mahogany chest. She was, however, too overwrought to find what she was looking for.

Marianne jumped up. She knew that Madame Necker was looking in the wrong chest of drawers. "Sit on the divan, Madame, and I will bring it to you." With a finger in the closed book to keep the page, Marianne hurried to the little chest of drawers by the double door and returned a few moments later with the bottle of smelling salts.

"Thank you, my child." Madame Necker was as white as the wall.

"Dismissed again, how terrible," wailed the lady of the house.

Political intrigues had driven Necker in exile for several years to Switzerland before he was personally recalled by the king a year ago.

Where is all this going to lead us? Dismissed, then called back again ..."

"To a revolution," Germaine said calmly. She sat composedly in the armchair by the fireplace and adjusted her dress. Her curly brown hair was braided in a long plait that hung over her shoulder. Marianne knew that she hated wigs.

"The people will no longer be quiet, mother, that's for sure." At these words, Madame Necker reached for her smelling salts again, fanning herself. "The people are rioting. The king was utterly stupid to dismiss Father. Today is Sunday, but the sparrows are already singing it from every rooftop. People fear that the king will now move Parliament from Versailles to a remote provincial town." As always, Germaine was well informed. "They believe it's a royal ploy, and I think they're right. The king's next move will probably be the dissolution of parliament." Madame Necker took in a sharp breath at the thought.

"He won't dare to do that, will he, child?"

"What's going to happen now?" asked Marianne, finally closing the book she had been reading. Despite all she had learned, she still did not trust herself to draw her own conclusions.

"The fear of the impending national bankruptcy and famine will lead to the worst. People are already gathering in Palais Royal and listening to revolutionary speeches."

"I think my girl is right," sniveled Madame Necker. Color was slowly returning to her face.

"People won't put up with this any longer. The situation will escalate. I can feel that."

"Yes, mother, the time has come. The people will emancipate."

Marianne wasn't sure whether it was good or bad news...

Monsieur Necker came through the entrance hall into the drawing-room. Outwardly, he seemed calm, but whoever knew him could see how agitated he was.

He immediately asked for a cognac, gulped it down, slammed the glass on the mantelpiece, and paced up and down in the drawing-room, hands clasped behind his back and frowning. His light blue silk ensemble with the high, white stockings, wig, and buckled shoes made a big impression on Marianne. Although she had been with this family for the last seven years, she was still fascinated by their way of life. The women looked at Necker expectantly and glanced at each other questioningly.

At last, Necker stopped, and everything seemed to come to a halt; even the grains of dust seemed to remain motionless in the air.

"This time, he went too far."

A shocked silence followed.

"I'm afraid the king has made another huge mistake, which will be the last."

"Oh, my God. That's how I feel too, Jacques," Madame Necker agreed.

"What happened?" Germaine wanted to know.

"Marie-Antoinette was able to prevail. I've been a thorn in her side for a long time."

"Have you perhaps not gone too far yourself, my dear husband?"

"My conscience would not allow me to see human rights being trampled upon."

"And now?"

"We can't do anything right now except wait and see. The people are seething. France will soon implode..."

"The French will at last claim their rights," added Germaine with satisfaction. Her father poured himself another cognac and swallowed it in one gulp.

"Yes, Germaine, they will ... and I won't be there to see it ..." They all looked at each other in dismay. The women held their breath as the unspoken question hung in the air: why? "I was requested in writing by His Majesty the King to leave France at once." And more to himself, he added, shaking his head and turning to the window: "How could I have been so naive and not seen the Queen's intrigue coming?"

***

Madeleine - Paris, 1789

"Dear me," moaned Rose Bertin when she saw the turmoil in the streets of Paris. The unrest that had been simmering for weeks did not bode well.

She heard the news from a customer earlier this morning. Appalled, she sat down on the stool behind the counter. One of her girls brought her a glass of eau de vie. Actually, she never drank, especially in the morning, but the strong drink would help her face the impending horror.

The news was on everyone's lips. Young boys handed out leaflets and shouted them out loud. The streets seemed busier than usual; people seemed to hurry, almost rushing as if everyone wanted to do something quickly before disaster struck.

Rose Bertin touched her forehead. It felt hot. Although it was early morning in July, it was already very stuffy in the shop. Or was it because of the excitement about the events?

Madeleine and Louisa noticed the tension in their boss and shrugged at each other. They didn't understand what it was about. It was perhaps some political crisis, as it had often been before. Anyway, nothing would change. So, why put up such a show? They knew that Mademoiselle Bertin went in and out of the royal palace and was close friends with Queen Marie-Antoinette. She enjoyed an excellent reputation there. That is why she was more affected than others by the events at the royal court. Madeleine had been designing headgear for almost six years now. There was hardly a Parisian lady who did not wear her creations. This gave her, just like Louisa with her designs, a special position in the workshop.

In the meantime, they were assigned their own room with plenty of daylight, in which they could let their imagination run free. Madeleine had christened the room Le Petit Atelier, which was generally adopted, even by the boss.

Since Rose Bertin was famous all over Europe and her fashion was in demand with several royal families, Madeleine's hats and bonnets, as well as Louisa's dresses, were worn in numerous countries. The girls were especially proud of that, although they couldn't quite picture the extent of their success.

An excited customer suddenly burst into the shop.

"Mon Dieu, mon Dieu, where is all of this supposed to take us?" the girls heard her exclaim.

"I don't understand all this fuss," Madeleine whispered. "Necker has been dismissed. So? It wasn't the first time, was it?"

"It doesn't make any difference to us, but I heard from the baker that the people were going to revolt. And that can have unpleasant consequences for nobles and anyone having anything to do with them," Louisa presumed.

Madeleine shrugged. She didn't quite understand the state of affairs, but she clearly felt the tension everywhere.

"They say that it is now worse than ever for the farmers."

"Are you thinking about your parents?"

"Yes. They were already hungry back then, but if it gets worse now ... I dare not even think about it," she said, feeling powerless and miserable. "I wish I could send them some money, now that I am earning well.  But I don't know where to, and how. I don't even know if they're still alive." Madeleine looked sadly out the window.

Louisa nodded anxiously and put her pen aside.

"Ideas elude me today," she said with a sigh. "I feel the excitement that has gotten into everyone. There are irritated, aggressive people everywhere, as if we were sitting on a bomb." She stood up, came around the table, and put a comforting hand on Madeleine's arm.

"Maybe Maurice will have some news soon."

Yes, Maurice! Madeleine's heart warmed up at the thought. She wondered if he also felt the unrest. She longed for him so much. Every day without seeing him was torture. Rose Bertin should not find out. Even Maurice wanted it to be kept a secret, to protect Madeleine's reputation, he said. But it was also because he was still not officially released from all other obligations...

***

Jeanne - Paris, 12. July 1789

They sat on one of the marble benches under the huge colonnades of the avenues that enclosed the Palais Royal garden, watched the goings-on in the Café de Foy opposite, each sucking on a peppermint gumdrop. People sat with a jug of wine in the shade of the plane trees, engrossed in lively discussions. Even in broad daylight, girls freely offered their services to the gentlemen passing by. A flower seller trilled a song to attract customers. Women strolled through the shaded corridor with baskets on their arms, and the men stood debating here and there. 

Jeanne wore a pretty but simple white silk dress with ornate edges on the wrap. She had bought her hat – a red bonnet with a white rim – from the Grand Mogol. They fared well since running the candy business.

"How excited the high society ladies and gentlemen are today," she said. There reigned an inexplicably tense atmosphere.

Camille nodded, and a shadow crossed his face. Jeanne knew him too well to ignore it.

"Do you know what's going on?"

Camille looked around. "I think something is brewing," he whispered. "And when it starts, I'll be there." He grinned at a passing customer as if he were talking about the weather.

"When what starts?"

"The war." Camille looked mysterious.

"You're crazy. What war?"

Passers-by turned to look at them.

"Shh, will you be quiet?" Camille looked at her sternly and put his hand over her mouth. She mumbled something incomprehensible.

"What? I didn't understand you." He grinned, gave her a quick kiss on the cheek before letting go of her.

"It is rumored that the king would bring troops to the Champ de Mars," whispered Camille. "If that is true, it would be a shameful provocation because it would violate the agreements."

"Why would the king want to do such a reckless thing? You're crazy, my darling," said Jeanne, shaking her head and laughing.

"Not at all, you will see, Jeanne. You have a good sense for business, and I have a good sense for that sort of thing. And I tell you, it will start soon. The starving people will no longer play along." Jeanne nodded and sighed. The misery of the people seemed to get worse from day to day. Children die in large numbers from hunger and disease. Why should it be so? Why should only the rich have a right to a comfortable life? She remembered her parents, who had accepted this as God's will. Maybe it was really time for a change and for the people to demand their share.

But as good as it sounded, she didn't really believe it. She preferred to rely on herself. That was safer. She wanted to concentrate on concrete, tangible things, like her business, which had been extremely successful for the last two years.

Claire took care of the counter as always, but she didn't have to walk the streets in the evening anymore. Meanwhile, she also had a new suitor, another officer. Jeanne had so much success with her shop that the queue extended to the theater on some days.

Camille got up, dropped a kiss on her head, and went to work.

"Gumdrops, glazed chestnuts, the best in Paris, only available at Chez Jeanne ..." He winked at her, smiling, and disappeared into the crowd. Jeanne's feelings for him hadn't changed, or rather, they had become deeper. Since confessing their love to each other two years ago, they had been inseparable. But she felt that he was getting more pressing and wanted to move on. Jeanne was afraid. Not of love itself, because they had already tried out many things together. She was afraid of the consequences. What if she became pregnant?  She didn't dare to ask Claire if and how something like that could be prevented because Claire would only get upset and maybe even want to lock her up.

But she didn't want to lose Camille either. He was nineteen now and the most handsome young man in the whole gallery. She didn't think that just because she was in love with him. She was quite objective about him being handsome. Too handsome, Jeanne sometimes thought. His new clothes showed off his blond hair and bright eyes even more. His masculinity and his strong charisma were especially attractive to women. His irresistible smile could charm any woman, young or old. That helped the business, but it was painful to Jeanne whenever she witnessed it, even though she trusted him. How long would he, or could he resist the many temptations that lurked around every street corner if Jeanne continued to refuse him?

Sometimes, when she looked for him in the gallery, all she had to do was look for a crowd of women. He was sure to be right in the middle. He flirted and laughed, and each time Jeanne was overcome with jealousy. But if she dared to speak to him about it, he would make fun of her and tease her, saying how sweet that she should be jealous, but that he just wanted to increase sales by being charming. Jeanne wanted to believe him but was also scared because she did not give him what he so dearly wanted. She was afraid that others would. She still had Claire's words in mind and knew that she was right. A girl who gave in to lust without marriage was a lost girl in the eyes of society.

But she also knew that he loved her and that they were united by a powerful bond. But would that be enough to resist his youthful urge in the face of all the temptation?

Jeanne was not so sure about that.

"Citizen, citizen! Listen to me," a deep voice suddenly echoed through the garden. People stopped and looked in the direction from which the call had come. As if swept away by a wave, the galleries' visitors moved curiously towards the call coming from the Café de Foy. Jeanne could see the strain on their faces, a mixture of excitement, anticipation, and ... fear. She suddenly realized that Camille's earlier remark had not been said lightly.

Her curiosity aroused, Jeanne followed the crowd. What was so special about this troublemaker who seemed to attract everyone? As she made her way through the crowd, which appeared to be getting denser as she moved forward, and finally got to the café, Jeanne saw the speaker who had climbed onto a table. The brown-haired man was around thirty, appeared to belong to the upper class and yet did not look like a nobleman. He studied the crowd around him with dark, serious, almost angry eyes.

"I've just come from Versailles," he announced and paused for a moment. "Necker has been fired!" A murmur went through the crowd. "Citizens, this is a sign. The sign of Saint Barthélemy for patriots!"

Despite the heat, an icy mood settled over the bystanders. People looked at each other, bewildered. Nobody dared to speak. Jeanne shivered. What was all that about? The night of St. Bartholomew had been a terrible event some two hundred years ago, during which Protestants were massacred. The atrocities had been so terrible that even today, people talked about these incidents behind closed doors.

"German and Swiss battalions will come from the Champ de Mars to cut our throats."

People jumped up in indignation, shouting insults that were not aimed at the speaker but at the king, who seemed to have abused their trust. Jeanne began to feel queasy. Could what the man was saying be true? Had the king ordered the assassination of his own people?  She shivered. War - Camille's voice echoed in her head.

"We only have one resource ... weapons! Take up arms, take up arms!" yelled the man, his voice already hoarse. "Let all of Paris take up arms!"

A riot broke out. The crowd roared, newcomers joined in enthusiastically in the war cry.

"Friends," the speaker called, raising his arms for further attention. "We know our enemy, but we also have to identify ourselves to one another." As if looking for something, his gaze wandered around and stopped on the tree that stood behind him and its branches, which stretched far above him. He plucked a green leaf and held it up. "All proud citizens who want to fight should hang a green cockade on themselves," he shouted, his voice cracking with eagerness. "Green! Green, like the color of hope."

Next to Jeanne stood a pretty young woman. Her face beaming, she was gazing starry-eyed at the agitator. Like the others, she turned to the tree closest to her, tore off a leaf, and held it up as a token of her approval. She gave Jeanne a bright smile.

"Isn't he fantastic?!" she whispered to Jeanne. Jeanne nodded, not sure what to think of it all. Of course, it was exciting to imagine a nation revolting against those in power. Perhaps she had already suffered too many changes in her life to be able to trust these new events, now that her situation had finally somewhat stabilized.

"Who is that?" asked Jeanne timidly, afraid she might offend the woman.

"This is Camille," the young woman breathed enthusiastically, "Camille Desmoulins, the lawyer and politician, my fiancé."

Oh, thought Jeanne and understood the adoring glow on the blond woman's face.

"That will be our symbol, our uniform, our victory," Desmoulins shouted, shaking his curls. Roaring applause exploded through the garden of the Palais Royal. Whether big or small, young or old, ordinary citizens or ladies and gentlemen of the bourgeoisie, people hopped around under the trees to catch a leaf. They held up their leaves, waving them as they cheered. Distraught and eager to get away, Jeanne turned to make her way out of the turmoil when policemen in black robes and tricorns suddenly appeared.

"What's going on here?" one of them shouted. Boos erupted, and the crowd turned like a single block to face the policemen, who turned pale at the unexpected situation. One of them pointed to Desmoulins.

"Yes," he cried hoarsely, "I am the one who is calling my brothers to freedom! You won't get me alive." As soon as he had finished the sentence, he pulled out two pistols from under his redingote and waved them about wildly. "I would rather die as a hero than see the land of our fathers, our beloved France, bound in slavery!"

"Vive la liberté!" people cheered and applauded uproariously. "Vive la France!" The euphoric cries echoed like a single roaring outcry through the corridors of the Palais Royal, carried by the wind over the roofs of Paris and spread through the whole of the bustling capital.

***

Marianne - Paris, 1789

You should get married. Without her sisters, without her parents. Marianne was deeply disturbed. She had never expected that her life would be like this. As a young girl, she had imagined herself getting married in a traditional country dress. She had imagined a decorated village and a big party with neighbors and friends as well as a dance evening, according to the ancient, rural rituals. She had wanted to run her own farm with Jacques. But what would she have here?

She didn't want to be ungrateful. She was well aware that she had been rescued from misery and that she had been fortunate. She also knew that life with Jacques, as she had imagined, could never be because poverty, hunger, and illness made life so difficult that there was no room left for romance.

So, what bothered her? She had met a man who doted on her, and she got on so well with her benefactor, who kept her in her service even though she didn't really need her anymore. She also appreciated how lucky she was to be treated like a family member by the Neckers, although she was, in fact, just a servant. She had Germaine's wild temperament to thank for this. The mother was so averse to conflicts with her daughter that she always gave in to her, thus avoiding eventual confrontations with her husband.

Since Marianne was the only female companion whom Germaine had ever really accepted, Madame Necker had no other choice than to treat her like an adopted daughter. Unfortunately, it also meant that Marianne had to get married as is right and proper.

And now, it was Germaine who also expressly wished that Marianne should marry.

Marianne was almost certain that Germaine just wanted to keep her closer to herself because her future husband was a professional soldier and would spend more time at the front than at home.

But then, she really did want this relationship with Pascal. She had grown very fond of him and would be a good wife to him, she knew that. She sighed.

He wrote to her that he was coming home on leave very soon and had something important to say to her. He also contacted the Neckers and requested an interview. And yet, something kept her from an unreserved feeling of happiness. She tugged at her stole and went to the window. The sky was covered with dark clouds, and the mood outside was as depressing as hers when she thought of a wedding without her family.

***

Liese - Cholet, 1789

The surrounding fields had been lying fallow for months. Liese stood by the window, stroking her belly and lost in thought. She was expecting another child, the third since her girls left seven years ago. She shook her head sullenly and went back to the stove. The disgusting smell of the bubbling grit made Liese nauseous. For weeks, they had been eating nothing but porridge and herbs from the forest.

"Still better than nothing," Jean-Louis would say to her. Liese sighed and sat listlessly at the kitchen table. Jean-Louis was once again at a meeting. They were getting more and more frequent, and he stank of brandy every time he returned home.

"Something is finally happening, you will see, Liese, there will be better times," he recently declared.

"Then we can bring the girls back to us," she said hopefully.

Jean-Louis looked at her blankly when she spoke of their three elder children. To him, they were dead and buried. She bore a grudge towards her husband for making her pregnant the first time after the 'forced placement' of her girls, as Liese called it. As soon as her daughters left, she begged him to restrain himself in bed, to no avail. He could not and would not let her be. Within a few years, they had had a boy – Papa's pride and joy – and another girl.

Liese loved her children, but she sometimes found herself railing on them in frustration about her other three, the grown-up girls. This never lasted long because she knew, of course, that there was nothing the poor children could do about it.

Jean-Louis even forbade her from talking about the girls.

"Neither at home nor to the neighbors," he thundered angrily before rushing off to a meeting. How heartless a man could be!

She would never forget her daughters. Secretly, she nursed the memory of her girls. She kept small carved toys, dresses, and hairbands in a box, like a treasure, hidden in the clothes chest, where Jean-Louis would never find them.

Were they happy? How were they faring?

These questions bore into her like stab wounds. She prayed daily for her daughters and dreamed of a trip to Paris. However, with the increasing number of children and poverty, this became more and more unlikely. Her only remaining hope was that the girls would find their way home to their mother at some point. It was all she hoped for and what meant the most to her. Nothing else mattered as much anymore ...

***

Chapter 24

Jeanne - Paris, 14. July 1789

"Please, Jeanne," Camille whispered. They lay half-naked on his bed and kissed, as they often did in the early mornings when Claire thought she was out with her tray. Jeanne's petticoats lay carelessly over a chair, and her bodice untied. His small room was sparsely furnished but comfortable and clean. Pretty curtains hung on the window, lace doilies lay on the tables, and a bouquet of flowers stood in a vase. Camille showed a pronounced domestic penchant for a bachelor, and Jeanne wondered if he had a housekeeper.

Since business had improved, he moved from his old slum-dwelling closer to the Palais Royal.

As he lay on top of her, Jeanne was stirred by his hardness pressing against her body, demanding, trembling, and ready. She was also tempted to finally give in to his urge because she too felt the need to have him inside her at last, to feel herself melt with him into one.

The night before, she had made up her mind to surrender to him the next morning. She wanted it so much, and yet she suddenly had her doubts.

"Jeanne, I love you. And I've been waiting for you for so long. I'll be careful. Please." Shivers ran through her body as he caressed her neck and kissed her breasts. "Jeanne, my flower, my love, my life ..."

She surrendered at that.

"Very well," she whispered.

He looked at her in disbelief. "Are you sure?"

"Yes."

"Oh, Jeanne!" He pushed up her petticoats and caressed the insides of her legs tenderly.

"It'll hurt a little at first."

"I know that. We've talked about it a hundred times ..." They got completely undressed. Camille slipped under the covers and admired her body before spreading the blanket over her again. His hands slid over her body as if they wanted to explore every curve, every hollow. Jeanne gasped under his caresses, pleasant shivers spreading over her whole body. He smelled wonderfully of mint. They quivered in anticipation as they huddled together, and Camille kissed her, at first tenderly, and then becoming more and more intense and demanding. Burning waves of passion seemed to engulf him, and Jeanne was carried away into the depths of overwhelming ecstasy. The time had now come for her to become his woman.

"Do you like it, Jeanne?" he whispered.

"Yes, it's wonderful, go on."

They had petted so often before, but this time they lay completely naked against each other, and it was even more wonderful than usual, more exciting, more real. The prospect of going further with the love game today and allowing him into her most intimate place brought her excitement to unexplored heights.

The point came when Camille couldn't take it any longer, and sensing that she was ready too, he carefully pushed himself up against her and kissed her firm, small breasts. Jeanne pressed herself against his manhood, driving Camille almost mad with desire. She then surrendered completely to him.

***

"I'm sorry," he stammered.

"What are you sorry about?"

"That it happened so quickly. I was too aroused. It'll be better next time." He smiled, took her head between his hands, and kissed her tenderly. "It was wonderful for me. And for you?" he asked sheepishly.

"For me too. To feel you inside me was a wonderful sensation."

"We had melted into one."

"Yes."

"I love you, Jeanne."

"I love you too, Camille."

"Do you have any regrets?"

"No, how could I?"

Camille smiled and dropped back into the pillow.

Jeanne's heart swelled with emotion. The extent of her love for him could not be expressed in words. She didn't regret anything. After all, at eighteen, she was already a woman. Now, she was his woman.

Camille suddenly listened up.

"What is it?" whispered Jeanne.

They heard a commotion in the street below the window. The room was on the second floor. Camille got up in a hurry, quickly put on his shirt and trousers, and went to the window to push the shutters open.

Jeanne followed suit, getting dressed in a hurry, and came to the window. Large crowds were moving towards the city center. Something unusual was going on.

"Look, Jeanne, they're armed. The time has come!" he exclaimed excitedly. "The Bastille is under siege since Necker's dismissal. There will be an onslaught today." Camille seemed well informed, and Jeanne suddenly wondered if she really knew him as well as she thought. She looked down at the street again. She shuddered, but this time, not with joy. It looked dangerous, very dangerous. Suddenly, the storm bells in almost every church in Paris started to ring at the same time.

Whichever way Jeanne looked, people were streaming in. Some were armed with muskets, some with spears and axes, and others with pitchforks. People seemed to be rolling in from all parts of the city, shouting their slogans hysterically.

"Where did they all come from?" Jeanne wondered. Turning to Camille, she saw a fanatical glow on his face. His fighting spirit showed through his sparkling eyes and his glowing cheeks.

"The people are rising against their king," he said, fascinated. Jeanne had to admit that the thought was as exciting as it was frightening. She felt cold shivers running down her back.

"Down with the tyrants!" they heard someone shout.

"To the Bastille!"

The crowds cheered.

Camille's eyes shone.

"Come on, Jeanne! Come on, we're going with them!" he urged, his eyes gleaming feverishly with a passion that bordered on folly. He took her by the hand, but Jeanne resisted. She didn't want to let the mob on the street ruin the most romantic moment of her life.

"Can't we go later?"

"Jeanne! History is unfolding here. I can't miss that." He kissed her, grabbed his cloak and a broomstick.

"Come on, let's go!" He held out an inviting hand.

Jeanne shook her head.

In no time, he was up and away.

Jeanne stared at the door, stunned. She suddenly remembered Claire's words:  In the worst scenario, they run away immediately, and at best, they play around a little with you. Or should I say it the other way around? If they leave immediately, the heartache may be less because you know straight away where you stand ...

No, Jeanne thought. No, Camille is not like that. I know it ...

***

Madeleine - Paris, 14. July 1789

"Close the shop immediately!" Rose Bertin ordered her employees. "Lock all windows and put bars on the doors."

The girls jumped, and abruptly left their work stations. They ran  frenzied from window to window locking and securing them. They all seemed to sense that something out of the ordinary was happening. The restless anxiety they had sustained for days due to the rumors and gossip was now taking shape.

Madeleine's heart was racing. Already of a fearful nature, she was now confronted with something really monstrous and beyond imagination.

Hundreds, thousands of people stormed past the shops in droves, shouting, singing, and cursing the king.

"To battle!"

They suddenly seemed to crawl like little rats out of every crack and hole in the capital, rising from the catacombs of the Parisian underground. Like flocks of ants attracted to a blob of honey, they all streamed in the same direction. Madeleine thought it looked like free bread distribution, but she knew better.

"Long live freedom!"

"Bread for all!"

The calls got louder and more pressing. For the last two days, one could sense that something was imminent. Everyone knew or suspected it; some even hoped it would happen. And I, Madeleine wondered, what can I hope to expect from this? She couldn't be against it because that would mean denying her origins, her family. Hunger had been her daily companion for many years; how could she have forgotten? But she couldn't be for it either, because that would mean being against the system that had fed her so well up to now, that allowed her to develop. And then there was Maurice, dear Maurice, who was associated with Versailles. Madeleine sighed.

The forty girls who worked for Rose Bertin were clearly insecure as they stood whispering nervously in the back yard.

"Mesdemoiselles, may I have your attention, please?" Rose clapped her hands. Her peach-colored silk dress rustled softly as she walked over to them.

"Something terrible seems to be going on in town today, so we're closing. But work goes on. Keep calm. Please go back to your workbenches and pay no attention to what is happening on the streets. This will pass ... when the ruffians have tired themselves out."

Disappointed, the girls went back to their sewing tables. For once, Madeleine and Louisa joined them because the day's unusual happenings made them feel lonely in their studio. They all spent the day sewing and peering outside repeatedly, curious but also afraid.

There was an unexpected loud hammering on the front door. Startled, the seamstresses jumped up from their seats and gathered at the door leading to the shop to see who it was.

"Please let me in," pleaded a female voice from outside. Rose Bertin hurried to the door, pushed back the bolt and grid, and let Madame Necker in. A terrible crash followed as the windowpanes of the neighboring shop shattered. Rose quickly pushed the bars forward again and bolted the door as the shop across the street was looted. The girls looked at each other in horror, relieved that Rose had the good idea of closing the shop in time. There was no telling what would happen if they fell into the hands of the bullies.

A bloodcurdling scream rang abruptly through the streets, and there was another crash. Clouds of smoke blew past the shop window.

"My goodness, what's going on?"

"They have stormed the Bastille, Mademoiselle Bertin. Just imagine that!" Madame Necker was completely exhausted.

"Do sit down, Madame." Rose led her to an armchair. Louisa hurried over with a bottle of smelling salts, and Madeleine brought her a cup of tea.

"Can you tell me what's going on? Who has stormed the Bastille?"

"The rebels, hundreds, or thousands of them. You'd think there's a nest somewhere that they've been hiding in until today. I never thought there were so many people in Paris! Apparently, many have been killed and injured ... hundreds ... and the killing and looting continue. Mon Dieu, mon Dieu... What will become of us?"

"You poor thing, you are shivering. Lisbeth, please get a blanket."

The girl obeyed.

"Why were you on the street during the commotion?"

"Oh dear, I wanted to see you and order a dress. Our Marianne is getting married. I heard screams and gunshots, but in the distance. But then they came back from the Bastille to plunder."

Madame Necker slowly began to calm down. "I didn't have a choice. There was only one escape for me, and that was forward ... in the hope that you would be there." Madame Necker sobbed and dabbed at her nose with a lace handkerchief.

"Calm down, it's alright. It'll soon be over," Rose Bertin tried to appease her.

Voices rose again, shots rang out, people wrangled in the street, others ran past. Madeleine and Louisa looked at each other, terror-stricken.

"Long live freedom!" they heard someone call.

"Vive le peuple!"

"Pain pour le peuple!"

"Vive la France!"

Madame Necker sat up indignantly.

"Soon be over, did you say?" She frowned. "On the contrary, Madame Bertin, on the contrary. What you see out there is just the puny beginning of something that is way beyond our imagination."

Jeanne - Paris, 1789

"Monsieur is making history, huh!" Jeanne answered irritably when Claire asked why Camille no longer came to the store.

He hadn't come back since the day she'd given up her virginity to him, on the day the great revolution began. Jeanne was deeply hurt. At first, she felt only anger, which had gradually turned into sadness. Claire looked down, embarrassed.

"Just say it," said Jeanne sadly to her. "Say that you were right." At that moment, Jeanne was afraid she had said too much.

"What do you mean?" Claire frowned, and then coughed.

"What now? The flu in summer?" Jeanne avoided the question.

"Oh no," said Claire. "I merely choked on something. Don't change the subject."

The last thing she needed was for Claire to interfere.

"You said that men get bored easily, or something ..."

"Yes, once they have what they so badly want, which is to get you into bed. Jeanne, do you have something to tell me?"

Jeanne rolled her eyes, but deep down, she felt a sharp pang. Had Camille now gotten what he wanted? Had it been so disappointing that he no longer wanted her?

"I know exactly what you are referring to. When should I find the time for that, huh? We work all day, and I'm always here in the evenings, aren't I?"

Claire eyed her suspiciously as if she wanted to wordlessly convey to her that where there is a will, there is also a way, that she was not stupid and knew exactly to what follies the desire for a loved one could drive a person. How right you are, Claire, Jeanne thought dejectedly.

"Now, don't look at me like that," Jeanne scolded instead. "Don't we have other worries?"

Claire nodded, cleared her throat, and suppressed a cough.  It was true.

"Yes, Jeanne, forgive my lack of trust. I just want Camille to respect you, that's all. Just look at the state of things in the city; he is sure to be right in the middle. Don't worry. He'll come back to you."

Yes, that was true. For days, people had been talking about nothing else but the revolution. The Palais Royal was in turmoil; the cafés – where wild debates were being held – were bursting with people. Armed citizens, with the three-colored cockades of the revolution tied around their hats - which had replaced the green leaf of hope of Camille Desmoulins - ran excitedly through the halls, exchanging news and moving on together.

"Good afternoon." A pretty demoiselle stood at the entrance of the shop. She was neatly dressed, had beautiful hazel eyes with long, dark lashes, freckles on her snub nose, and a full mouth.

"Good afternoon, Mademoiselle," Claire replied amicably. Jeanne got up and was about to say goodbye to Claire.

At that moment, the stranger said: "I'm looking for Jeanne."

Jeanne turned around abruptly.

"For me?"

"Are you Jeanne?"

"Yes, that's me."

"I have news for you. Camille was injured in the storming of the Bastille."

Marianne - Paris, 1789

"Marianne, dearest." Pascal bowed to her, placing a kiss on her hand, and put his left hand around her waist. He held his gloves and hat in the other. "I missed you so much."

Marianne allowed him to kiss her. He stepped back, took off his sword belt awkwardly, and laid everything down on the table by the window.

The Necker family, including Germaine, received him that morning and gave them permission to spend a few moments alone. He had to give his word of honor as a French soldier to uphold all the rules of decency, which he would have done anyway, as Marianne knew. He was very honorable, even a little too honorable, according to Germaine, but Marianne didn't quite understand that. How could one have too much of a virtue?

Pascal suddenly got down on his right knee and took her hand. Marianne trembled. Now, the time had come.

"Marianne, dearest. I have been given permission to officially ask for your hand." He paused and tugged at his recently grown mustache. It made him look more mature and manly, Marianne thought.

"Will you be my wife, Marianne?" he asked uncertainly since Marianne just stood there and said nothing, as if petrified, seeming to wait for something. But for what? Jacques? That was over. Her life had changed drastically, and she would probably never see her home in the country, her parents, and dear Jacques again. Grow up, she admonished herself. Should she remain dependent on the Neckers forever? No, life had to take its course, and Jacques certainly saw it that way too. A handsome guy like him wouldn't stay long without a bride, and his parents needed a good marriage that would end all their worries. That was all over, once and for all. She would have to turn this page over and write a new one, as Germaine did every day. Furthermore, she really loved Pascal. Not as deeply as Jacques, but enough to imagine a life by his side. All right, thought Marianne, and shook off her doubts.

"Yes, dearest, I will," she finally answered in a tear-choked voice.

Pascal got up and pulled her close, touched by her tears, which he thought were tears of joy. Marianne gave a little sob but then pulled herself together. He handed her his handkerchief, and she blew her nose in it, wiping away her tears.

"You have made me the happiest man in the world," he said softly.

"Oh, Pascal, I'm very happy too. I'll be a good wife to you, I promise."

"I don't doubt it for a moment," he said gently, pulling her close again and kissing her tenderly.

Jeanne - Paris, 1789

"What?" With one leap, Jeanne was next to the girl. "Is it bad? Where is he?"

"He's in his room, and he's not looking very well. He is running a high fever and keeps calling for you."

Jeanne grabbed her cloak, made a sign of the hand to Claire, and disappeared. The girl could barely keep up with her.

"I don't understand," said Adelaide, concerned. "Are you moving away? He didn't tell me about that."

"No, damn it!" Jeanne cried out angrily. "I was his lover!"

Chapter 25

Pascal - Rhine Army, 26. August 1789

Pascal looked at the cards he was holding in his hand, and furtively observed the other players. The new pack of cards had a Jacobin cap instead of a crown and a sun instead of the lily, the symbol of the French monarchy. The jack counted more than the king and the queen. For Pascal and his cronies, playing cards was a pleasant change from the tough everyday life of a soldier, and he didn't really care whether he was playing with revolutionary or royalist cards and rules; what counted was the outcome. He grinned to himself because he had been ripping them all off for the last hour. He leaned back contentedly. The evening air was mild, and a screech owl trilled in the twilight.

"Hey, guys, have you heard?" a comrade from the neighboring tent called over to them. They looked up. "The courier has just delivered the message: Today, the National Assembly declared the human and civil rights," he said solemnly.

The men nodded.

"Well then, they might improve the conditions for us soon," muttered Charles, a card player who sat next to Pascal and kept losing. "That also falls under human rights, doesn't it?"

The people sitting around laughed timidly.

"You are probably right there; the conditions in the army are inhumane," agreed Pascal. He would never want Marianne to know in what dire conditions he had to live and fight; he was ashamed of it. "Who are we now reporting to? The King or the Assemblée? Does anyone know?"

"The one who pays us," muttered Charles, and got a few more laughs. "I don't care a fig. They are all alike. You’ll see. The people's saviors and liberators of today will crack down tomorrow once they understand how difficult it is to govern. And in no time, we will have a king, an emperor, or some other tyrant." Charles had been the longest in the army, and everyone took his word for it, for he was known to have a nose for such things.

While the others sorted their cards, Pascal's mind wandered. Revolution or not, ruled by the king or the people, he was lucky. He would never have dreamed of marrying a girl like Marianne. Her beauty and virtue made him the happiest man in France.

When he first saw her at the Neckers' ball, how innocently, almost clumsily, she tried to adapt to this stilted society, he had already fallen for her. Coming from a modest background, he had already given up the idea of finding a suitable bride. There was no way he would want to marry one of those snooty women, such as Germaine de Staël. The very thought gave him unpleasant shivers. Yes, he was a lucky man.

Marianne wanted to have children and live in the country. He knew nothing about farm work and had no intention of learning, but he was careful not to mention that to her. He could still talk his way out of it after the wedding.

"Here! Lady of diamonds." Sure to win, his buddy, François, laughed at him.

"Ha, better here! King of diamonds!" Charles triumphed.

"Jack of hearts, I won," countered Pascal calmly and gathered the money.

His comrades threw their cards on the pile in resignation.

"I give up. Good night, guys." Charles rose, picked up his musket leaning against the tree trunk, headed for the tent, then turned around again.

"And Pascal?"

"Hmm?" Pascal counted the coins he had won.

"You know what they say, don't you?"

"Well, what?" He went on counting calmly.

"Lucky at cards ..."

Marianne - Paris, 27. August 1789

The salon was stuffy and crowded. The whole of Paris seemed to be coming and going through there. In every corner, people were talking, drinking, flirting, and dancing. Marianne stood by the window, in the company of a charming young man who was drooling over her.

"I am in favor of the revolution!" she heard Germaine shout, which was received with enthusiastic applause from all sides.

Germaine was in her element.

Civil and human rights had been declared the day before, and there was no lack of talking points.

"What do you think about it, Marianne?" said her companion, bending slightly to her, his crippled foot forcing him into an uncomfortable position.

"Personally, I just listen and don't really think anything," she admitted. He winked, seeming to like her openness.

"Oh, a rough diamond in the midst of all this luxury." With the hand in which he held his glass, he pointed to those present with an elegant, circling, and all-encompassing movement.

"But I think it may be good for the low-class people to finally get justice," she ventured.

"Truly, truly," her companion said, seeming amused.

"Are you making fun of me?"

"Absolutely not, Marianne. But with me, you don't have to pretend. I know where you come from, who you are, and how you feel among this crowd here," he said, making a head movement towards the loudly chatting society, "how you must be feeling." He took a sip from his glass and looked at her roguishly. She smiled.

The way he looked at her reminded of a hunter watching his prey, just before he brings his rifle to his shoulder, estimating the shot, already a little hungry and yet patient, as if he had no doubt that he would hit his target.

A shrill laugh came from the group conversing around Germaine, who, as usual, stood close to the window to show herself better in daylight. Marianne watched as she leaned boldly against a young man in a lively discussion.

"Seeing that you obviously know pretty much everything about me, may I perhaps know how you got this information and why you are interested in it?"

Her interlocutor laughed uproariously.

"What charming innocence," he said, leaning toward her. "My name is Talleyrand, Charles-Maurice Talleyrand, the first member of the Comités de Constitution. It's my job to know everything about everyone, young lady."

Marianne blushed, feeling queasy. She knew that Germaine frequented important people, but she had not expected such a noble gentleman to address her or even consider it necessary to make inquiries about her. She tugged nervously at her hairdo. Noticing her dismay, Talleyrand immediately became serious.

"Don't worry, my dear child. If I know everything about you, and I doubt if I really do," he smiled again and winked at her sleazily, "it is because my new friend Germaine, your benefactress, asked me to take care of you. A task that delights me, I have to admit." He bowed gallantly.

"To take care of me?"

"Well, yes. I admit that it must sound a bit strange. But your friend thinks that you need to be trained in the art of letting yourself go. Dancing, chatting, laughing, and enjoying life."

"I don't think I need a teacher for that." If I lay my hands on you, Germaine, Marianne thought indignantly. She knew that Marianne was engaged.

"Please. You have misunderstood me, dear Marianne. Not a teacher. Shall we say, a companion?"

Marianne quickly emptied her glass.

"And what's that supposed to do for me?" she asked, feeling that the drink was giving her the courage to question. "Germaine must have failed to inform you that I am engaged, dear Mr. Talleyrand."

He raised a fist to his mouth and giggled behind it.

"Forgive me." He made a face, seeming to force himself to be serious. "But you're really too cute, dearest."

The man irritated and annoyed her at the same time. What was going through his mind? Had he not been such a high-ranking personality, she would have put him in his place a long time ago. But she preferred to deal with the situation gracefully.

"What good is it to me if, let's say, I allow you to be my companion?" she asked, fluttering her eyelashes coquettishly.

He giggled again, looking at her in fascination.

"I love it when people play with me. Well, what would you say if I were to promise you that I would find your sisters again?"

"Oh, my God!" Marianne said, swaying slightly on her feet.

"No, Talleyrand," he joked. "God may not be very well disposed toward me right now ..." he said, clearing his throat.

Marianne's thoughts were in a whirl, her breathing heavy.

"What do you know about my sisters?"

"Nothing," he said, a little too hastily to seem natural, calling Marianne's attention." But I'm sure I'll find out something soon if you give me enough time to learn the things I don't know about you yet." He moved his eyebrows up and down in a grotesque dance to indicate a double meaning.

Marianne felt herself blushing. So that was it! She understood and hesitated ... but not for long.

What did Germaine have in mind? The urgent desire to find out more about her sisters was still so painful and present after all these years that she could hardly refuse, and so her anger at her friend subsided. To get over the shock, she grabbed another glass of sparkling wine from the tray of a passing servant and took a long sip.

"All right, let's start the interrogation whenever you want." She smiled at him suggestively, feeling terribly wicked. But to her surprise, she felt neither disgust nor a guilty conscience. On the contrary, a pleasant and expectant, tingling sensation gradually took hold of her. Talleyrand raised his glass and toasted to her.

"Long live the companionship ..."

Napoleon - Auxonne, 1789

"Brother, it is a first step in the right direction ..." Napoleon paused while writing and pondered. The thought of the revolution sent a surge of emotions boiling in him. His eyes glowed with contained excitement. Although he was indifferent to the common people, the National Assembly's resolution of August 4, 1789, represented a significant step to him: no more privileges for the fine aristocrats. He gave a satisfied grunt and leaned again over the paper, dipped his quill into the ink, and went on writing: "Prepare everything for my arrival. I have been granted leave, and I will arrive in Corsica in September. The fatherland needs us now, brother."

Napoleon leaned back in the creaky chair and closed his eyes. Images of his homeland appeared in his mind. His future sphere of activity should be concentrated on his beloved island. He smiled - he could hear the chirping of the crickets. Was there a smell of eucalyptus? He could also clearly imagine the smell of thyme and almonds, a little girl running across a lavender field to meet him, her hair blowing in the wind. His childhood playmate ... He sighed and opened his eyes. Then another picture came to his mind: that of a young girl at the well. He wondered how she had fared since then. He was never been able to forget those big blue eyes. He pushed the thought out of his mind. Women! They were just as attractive as they were inscrutable. He hadn't known many until now, but enough for him to believe with certainty that there was no perfect woman for him. Especially since he still did not hold a position that would allow him to set up a Foyer. But even if the situation allowed, he doubted that the woman of his dreams could really exist: charming, virtuous, quiet, loving, and only concerned about him. He sighed. It was the reason why he was convinced that he would have to do great things. Why else would fate have given him so much intelligence and yet so little charm - and still less talent to court the female gender?

Only a few months ago, he had seen no meaning to his life. Even if he had never really thought of suicide, his useless existence had been a burden to him. He dragged himself through the world without a goal and suffered from his inner struggle: the contradiction between his methodical mind and the passion that filled him with longing.

But now, important challenges awaited him.

He would free Corsica from its bondage to France.

She particularly liked the free shoulders and bulging breasts. What a shame that she couldn't bewitch her loved one with it. She smiled. Pascal was so well mannered. He would most probably blush and look away.

Germaine had suggested that she should come here, and Marianne actually enjoyed the silence and solitude whereby she could indulge in her dreams. Very often, she wasn't sure if she was really doing the right thing. Without the support of her family, she often felt lonely and abandoned. Even if she loved Germaine, they were very different personalities. Things that her friend took so lightly seemed forbidden and decadent to her. She felt old-fashioned and uptight, and yet something was seething inside her, something that longed to be explored.

If only she could finally marry Pascal, she would feel like a wife and find fulfillment and a purpose. But would he also wish for the life she dreamed of? Would he lose his shyness and touch her the way her body longed for? She sighed and opened her book.

A noise made her sit up. She thought she heard footsteps on the gravel path. She tried to catch sight of the intruder through the purple flowers, but the sun blinded her. The steps she heard were uneven. A light, refreshing breeze played with her curls.

"Dear Mademoiselle Marianne," she heard a voice say.

She stiffened.

Talleyrand!

"Oh, Monsieur, what a pleasure," she stammered in surprise.

"May I join you?"

"But of course." She shifted a little to one side.

He blew a gallant kiss on her hand, waved his hat artfully, and sat down beside her with his leg stretched out stiffly.

"To what do I owe the honor?" Marianne had collected herself again. After all, he was a high-ranking gentleman of the Parisian society.

"I just had to see you."

"Oh," said Marianne, bewildered. She felt a warm flush.

"Does that surprise you?"

Marianne nodded.

"You must have noticed my affection for you?"

"Well," said Marianne, pulling herself together and tucking a curl behind her ear, "you can find someone nice without necessarily thinking about them," she replied. He exudes so much manliness and  self-confidence, she thought. How could such a man desire her? As Germaine said, no woman could resist him. What did he want from an inexperienced country bumpkin like her?

"So, you didn't think about me anymore?"

Marianne wondered if she should lie.

"Yes, I did," she said honestly and smiled. Despite the shade of the gazebo, she felt increasingly warm.

Talleyrand grinned.

"How did you think of me? As a friend? As a man?"

"As a man who will hopefully bring me news of my family soon," she dodged cleverly. He laughed.

"Indeed, I will. Give me a little more time because I'm following a good lead right now."

"Seriously?" Marianne beamed.

"Oh my dear, if you could see yourself now. The glow that emanates from you is simply irresistible. You'll break my heart if you make me wait any longer."

Marianne turned to him. Talleyrand seemed to find her highly amusing. "Well? Am I entitled to an answer? As a friend or as a man?"

"Well, maybe both, what's the difference?" she said, grinning cheekily.

"If you don't know, dearest Marianne, then it is high time a man let you in on this secret." His coaxing voice sent her into a trance.

Marianne was startled. It surprised her that her body reacted so violently. She turned slightly away from him to hide the blush spreading on her cheeks.

"Marianne?"

"Yes."

"Admit it. You are secretly dreaming of a real man, with no obligations, no consequences ..."

She gasped in indignation and straightened up, but her racing heart betrayed her. She turned even further around and was tempted to get up. Talleyrand leaned slightly over her exposed shoulder and breathed a kiss on it.

She shuddered and closed her eyes. A gust of wind caressed her hot skin. What had gotten into her? She loved Pascal with all her heart, and yet she longed for this man.

Talleyrand, who seemed to sense what she was going through, caressed her arm so gently that she could have mistaken it for another breeze. Pleasant goosebumps ran over her body. Talleyrand moaned lustfully against her ear as he continued to blow gentle kisses on her shoulder.

"You dream of a man who will touch you all over and caress you," he whispered tenderly.

She trembled with delight at his words and was ashamed for it. "Yes," she breathed softly.

"You want a man who will make your most secret dreams come true, Marianne. I am that man; you know that. Admit it."

"Yes," she moaned louder now and turned to him.

He grabbed her, pulled her close, and pressed his lips to hers. Her arms were bent between them as if in defense, her hands braced against his breast. Marianne gasped for breath. Her body seemed to be dissolving into nothingness, her knees went weak, and even if she wanted to, she couldn't fight back. Did she want that? No, a voice screamed within her. No, I want to be loved at last. She wrapped her arms around his neck and pressed her body against him.

Talleyrand groaned with pleasure and thrust his tongue into her mouth, which she readily opened. All she felt was the desire and fire within her, and she willingly revealed to him all her unfulfilled, most guarded, and precious secrets.

***

Chapter 28

Madeleine - Paris, June 1791

Flower pots, jugs, tools, goblets, and other utensils stood on the sidewalks in front of the boutiques. People meandered through the narrow streets like ants in an anthill while the vendors advertised their wares.

Madeleine was delivering a parcel to Mrs. Pelletier, and she welcomed the change it brought her. She was so occupied with her work that she hardly ever had a free minute to herself, let alone go out. And when she did, she stole away with Maurice. So the walk gave her the time and space to think about him undisturbed.

She was in high spirits as she strolled past the people, dreaming of her lover, who would soon make a respectable woman of her, of that she was sure. She looked apprehensively at the dark, ominous clouds forming in the sky above. She would see him again in two days and hoped that the weather would have improved by then because Madeleine loved the excursions in the countryside so much, much more than the meetings in some back rooms of dubious diners. Why such a respected member of society had to hide in such a way was a mystery to her. Maurice had explained that it was about things that still had to be settled with the Pope. He was afraid that his love for her would be misinterpreted, leading to the refusal of his request. So it was better, he said, to keep their relationship a secret for the time being. On no account did Madeleine want to anger the Pope. She loved Maurice so dearly that she would wait for him all her life if necessary.

She became introspective as her thoughts wandered to her sisters. Could Talleyrand really help her to find them? At first, she hadn't dared to mention it again, and he didn't said a word about it either. But recently, she had summoned up all her courage and spoken to him about it again. He said that he had come across some leads but had nothing concrete yet. She resolved to speak to him about it again next time.

She loved her work, but since meeting Charles-Maurice Talleyrand, new opportunities had opened up for her. Her former day to day existence with no plans for the future had brightened up with new perspectives.

"Maurice, Maurice, Maurice," she warbled to herself, her skirts bobbing with every springy step.

An old woman suddenly grabbed her arm and held her tight. Her greasy hands clutched the fabric of Madeleine's beautiful dress.

She got a fright. What did this woman want from her?

"A small donation, Miss, and I'll read your future."

Madeleine considered; she was very tempted. How dearly she would like to know what the future was like. But she shook her head. "I'm sorry, but unfortunately, I have no money with me."

The wrinkled old woman's evil eyes bored through her. Madeleine shuddered. She had often heard about fortune tellers but had never met one.

"That will bring you bad luck," said the old woman angrily, and let go of her. Madeleine stumbled on, horrified. "You will not get what you long for, and great disaster awaits you ... I see suffering and a dark cell ..."

Madeleine's throat tightened, and she ran on, panting. Far away from here. She didn't want to hear the horrible voice anymore. Out of breath, she leaned against a wall. The woman was nowhere to be seen, but the words echoed in her ears. She took a deep breath and slowly calmed down. Fortune telling is nonsense, she told herself. Superstition. She should in no way start believing in it. But her initially good mood had fizzled out, and even if she did everything to stop thinking about it, she was left with a constant feeling of unease.

When she arrived at Madame Pelletier's, she struck on the door with the huge door knocker. Soon after, she was admitted and taken to the lady of the house.

"Oh, my child, how nice of you to come," she said effusively when she saw her. Madeleine curtsied and put the package on the table.

"May I offer you some refreshment?"

"No, thank you, Madame Pelletier. I have to go back immediately."

The lady rolled out the paper and took out the new dress.

"Oh, my dear girl, splendid as always," she exclaimed happily. "Please wait until I've tried it on so you can make any changes, if necessary."

"Of course, Madame."

A servant brought a silver platter with a letter.

"Thank you, Hugo." The lady tore open the envelope, read it, reread it, shook her head, and let the letter fall, her face as pale as a sheet. "Oh, my God," she uttered, raising a hand to her throat.

"Can I help you, Madame Pelletier?"

"No, you may go," her customer replied gruffly, fanning herself. She seemed close to fainting.

Puzzled, Madeleine curtsied and said goodbye. What could have been written there? She suspected a message about the death of a relative.

Madeleine left the house and strolled back the same way she had come. On the side of the road, she heard the call of a newsboy and approached curiously.

"The king's escape thwarted. The king is charged with treason. The royal family stopped in Varennes ... Read all about the king's escape!"

Madeleine was startled. She had already guessed something of the sort. Mademoiselle Bertin had ordered the making of a gray and black traveling costume in secrecy; it was to be as inconspicuous as possible and yet elegant enough to befit a queen. Rose Bertin had made such a fuss about the secrecy that it couldn't go unnoticed. In any case, Louisa and Madeleine had drawn their own conclusions about it. The late-night personal delivery to the court had also been highly suspicious. But the escape had failed. Madeleine felt sorry for the poor royal family. Even if she understood the revolutionary ideas and even partly supported them, it was still about the king, the country's ruler willed by God.

Maurice had explained to her that England had both a king and a parliament. Could it not be the same in France? Madeleine quickened her pace because she felt the ominous restlessness that hung subliminally in the air as if the news had announced a new era. The streets through which she had recently strolled in a reverie now seemed forbidding and unsettling; people looked worried and lost. Whereas some cheered the news, others frowned anxiously and seemed to want to get away as quickly as possible. Get away? Where to? Was there an exit from this never-ending morass? This is how Madeleine felt about the unrest that had shaken Parisian life for the past two years since the storming of the Bastille. There was hardly a day on which some news did not stir up the uncertainty that had crept into people's hearts. Madeleine lowered her head and hurried back to the security of the shop.

When she arrived, the shop was deserted and the counter unoccupied, which was very unusual. Amazed, she went through the storerooms into the dining room and found Louisa and Mademoiselle Bertin, who was standing in tears by the fireplace and wailing loudly.

Louisa quickly pulled Madeleine into the store.

"You already know?" asked Louisa in a whisper.

"Yes, I found out from a leaflet distributor who was trumpeting the news in the streets."

"The poor queen," stammered Louisa, and Madeleine nodded.

The old woman with her prophecy had already slipped from her mind ...

***

Napoleon - Corsica, 1791

Napoleon's attitude toward France had changed. He no longer thought about Corsica's independence but was proud to belong to the enlightened and powerful French nation. He proudly wore the three-colored cockade on his hat. The unrest on his beloved island and the resentment of the Corsican revolutionary and resistance fighter Pasquale Paoli, whom he admired, affected him very much.

Since Napoleon had joined the Jacobin Club Society of Friends of the Constitution in Auxonne and had publicly committed himself to the revolution, Paoli's party had rifted. Paoli's supporters continued to demand Corsica's independence, but Napoleon's followers wanted to belong to the Grande Nation.

Napoleon got up and walked to the window of the little room in his parents' house. In the distance, he could see the mountains and the sea spread out before him. His hour was approaching; he sensed it. Many royalist-minded officers had quit their duties and left France as a precaution. The army was glad for everyone who stayed with the flag.

Napoleon had extended his last home leave without official permission. However, this did not lead to any consequences for him, much to his astonishment, but was perhaps not so surprising after all. It clearly showed the mood that prevailed in the army and proved his assumption that non-nobles were now being shortlisted as candidates for posts as officers.

It was now time for a man like him, despite his low status, to finally get a move on and prove his skills.

He thought hard about his options. Wouldn't it be better to leave Corsica and try his luck on the continent? Wasn't he wasting his time here? He picked up the writings of Rousseau.

"Oh, Rousseau, why did you only live sixty years?" he quoted from his own essay. "In the interests of virtue, you should have been immortal."

His gaze wandered dreamily over the juniper bushes to the mountains. No, he wasn't finished here yet, but the moment would come. He could feel it, as true as his name was Napoleon Buonaparte...

***

Alain - Paris, 1791

Alain grumbled as he pushed his cart in front of him. His old woman had finally died. It was about time because he couldn't stand her anymore.

The revolution had changed a lot in his life. Louis had died in the storming of the Bastille, which had suited him well. Alain grinned maliciously. There was a god, after all.

He then wanted to rebuild his shop, but he didn't have the means. No bank would lend him money; his reputation was ruined. But he was lucky in his misfortune. An old Jew had been interested in his business, and he had been able to sell it to him for a reasonably good price so that he finally had some money again. His first steps had, of course, taken him to the tavern for a delicious meal. But to his horror, he discovered that nothing of the sort could be found anymore, not even in restaurants. Food was scarce and almost unaffordable. That gave him an idea. He didn't want to open a store anymore. That was too much work and too risky. It was more like a millstone around the neck and had never prospered. No, he wanted to be free, keep away from getting into debt, and still benefit from the grim situation prevailing.

***

Because if that inn had difficulty getting food, other bars and shops must be going through the same problem. It was with this in mind that he founded his new company.

He decided to invest his money to buy food outside of the capital and auction it off to the highest bidder. In this way, he gradually gained new customers and was soon financially back on track. At first, it was mainly Jews who did business with him. They, at least, didn't care about his bad reputation. He rented a small room and from time to time, he indulged in a whore from the Palais Royal to satisfy his needs. He became more thrifty though, and stuck to the cheap whores to avoid getting into financial difficulties again. All in all, he recovered, and his whole life had changed. Only one thing remained engraved in his brain, hot and sore like an ugly brand. That was the thought of the bitch that was to blame for all the opportunities that had so often slipped through his fingers. He kept an eye open in the hope that one day, he would get hold of her again. And even if it's the last thing I do, I will have her! It sounded like a promise, but he regarded it more as a prophecy.

He hardly dared to go near her shop because the young fellow hung around there all the time. Alain therefore kept his distance and watched her unobtrusively as often as he could.

At night, he dreamed of her so realistically that it became an obsession. Most of the time, he imagined her to be submissive. In his dreams, he held her like a maidservant, always docile and willing. Sometimes he imagined her rebellious and cheeky and had to be locked in the cellar. He couldn't think of anything else. If it were the last thing he would do in his life, he would take her and then kill her!

***

Chapter 29

Jeanne - Paris, August 1792

"I'll be back in an hour, Claire," Jeanne called into the shop and disappeared. She had to go to her supplier to get more sweets, chestnuts, lozenges and rum cakes, the famous Babas.

She had been postponing this for several days because the situation in the city had worsened. She heard wild rumors of sieges, looting, and even murders. Was her imagination playing tricks on her, or was she actually feeling the tension in the city? The few passers-by scurried past her, looking around anxiously. The locked bars in front of the otherwise busy shops gave them a ghostly appearance.

She felt the hair on the back of her neck stand up as if someone was watching her.

She looked around, but there was no one. Shall I never feel free from this monster, she asked herself. It had been so many years ago, and so much had happened in the meantime. She could hardly imagine that the shopkeeper was still thinking of her. Like everyone else, he must have other worries.

The day in August was still warm, although summer was slowly drawing to a close. Jeanne got hot while running, and beads of sweat formed on her forehead.

She looked up at the beautiful townhouses, but here too, life seemed to have slipped away. It shot through her mind that rats would leave a sinking ship. She quickened her pace, feeling more and more uncomfortable.

She wondered where everyone was. How can so many shops be closed in the middle of the week?

As she turned the corner into the Rue Montorgueil, she saw from a distance that the Confiserie Stohrer was open. She breathed a sigh of relief and walked quickly to the shop.

"Bonjour, Jeanne," greeted an employee. "We've been expecting you for days."

"I didn't dare."

"You're not the only one." He nodded understandingly. "We will soon have to close too because the customers stay away. Most businesses have received threats, and so have we. But the boss is stubborn. He won't allow himself to be intimidated."

"Luckily." Jeanne grinned. "Otherwise, I will soon run out of goods." That's one thing I don't need, she thought. Business has been slow enough since the revolution.

She thought that the riots would only last a few weeks, but there was something new every day, and no one felt safe anymore.

The young man began to put together an assortment for her. Jeanne loved this fashionable shop with its wonderful aromas. The colorful candies, chocolates, and small cakes raised her spirits. The store also reminded her of Camille, her sweet Camille, at the time when he was still cute, innocent, and loyal. She missed him terribly and often wondered if she had made a mistake in rejecting him so harshly. But she quickly shook off this thought because it was he who had been at fault, not her. And every time she thought about that episode with Adelaide, especially about the confession that followed, she felt the pain again.

Nicolas Stohrer, the founder and owner of Confiserie, came out of the warehouse.

"Jeanne, what a pleasure to see you. How is Camille? Do you have got any news of him?"

That felt like a stab piercing through her. Jeanne shook her head regretfully. No, she had no news. Did she even still want any?

"Well, what's the matter with the boy? Surely he isn't lost or perhaps in jail? Oh, sorry." Nicolas noticed Jeanne's unease.

The thought that Camille might be in jail hadn't occurred to her. That would explain a lot. The thought made her dizzy.

"I'm a fool. It was thoughtless of me to talk like that. Please sit down, Mademoiselle," he said, indicating a brocade armchair.

"No, thanks. I’ll be fine."

Nicolas nervously twirled his mustache with his fingers.

"It is unforgivable that I should discourage the few customers who still come to me in these terrible times with my careless chatter. Where will all this take us to?"

Jeanne reassured him with a smile.

"What else is new, Nicolas?" she asked, trying to change the subject.

He leaned on the counter. "There will be problems again. The city is restless. This time, it's not just the peasants and petty bourgeoisie who will rise. Even business owners are now about to join in." He nodded importantly to confirm his words.

Jeanne was surprised that as the owner of a shop, she didn't know anything about this. When she asked Nicolas why, he shook his head in amazement and stuck out his lower lip in thought.

"Do you take part in events and meetings of the various groups?"

She didn't. She took care of her business and Claire, who was getting paler and was often ailing. Jeanne was delighted that Claire no longer had to work on the streets and instead helped her out in the shop.

"Then that will be the reason," he said smugly.

"And you, Nicolas, will you take part?"

"In the second revolution? No, that's too dangerous for me in my old days. Just take care of yourself, child. You should be on the streets as little as possible. You never know ..."

Jeanne thanked him, collected her goods, paid, and said goodbye. Once she was back on the street, she took a deep breath. As always, it stank of feces, and she thought wistfully of life in the country, where it smelled of earth, forest, meadows, and dung. The air was nothing like the foul stench of the city. It was now ten years since she had last breathed fresh country air. Only on rainy days was the city air a bit more bearable.

The supplier's words came back to her mind and she thought of Camille. Her anger was over, and she missed him very much ... his smile, his cap, the scratching of his head. Why had she reacted so violently? She sighed. She loved only him and didn't want any other man.

When Jeanne looked around, she realized that she had lost her way. She tried to recognize the streets. Where was she?

She heard strange noises, a frightening rumble that seemed to swell. She fled into a narrow alley and suddenly, all hell broke out.

In retrospect, she could not have described how exactly she got to the Tuileries' great square, for she was swept away by the crowd that gushed from every street. She could either keep running or get trampled down, so she had to follow. The angry horde rushed into the square, screaming and screeching, rallying to attack.

Armed men and women of all walks of life shouted, brandishing their guns, axes, and pitchforks. The King's Swiss Guard and the National Guardsmen had positioned themselves on the other side.
