More and more people flocked to the square, demanding the king's abdication and singing the new anthem of the revolution. Jeanne had heard the freedom song many times. It came from the poet André Chénier, one of her most loyal customers:

"La République nous appelle,

sachons vaincre, ou sachons périr.

Un Français doit vivre pour elle;

Pour elle un Français doit mourir."

"The Republic is calling us,

we can win, we can perish with her;

A Frenchman should live for her;

He shall die for her!"

The singing was overwhelming and gave Jeanne goosebumps. Had Camille died for the republic?

She was only just able to take refuge on the stairs of a manor house and climbed up the steps to the elegant building. From there, she had an overview of what was happening. The box with the sweets was getting too heavy for her, so she set it down on the steps.

A threatening hush suddenly settled over the square, as if they had all followed an order to be silent. Jeanne adjusted her hood and looked around in amazement. How could so many people be so quiet? Here and there, she heard a cough, mumbling within individual groups, or the clanging of a lance. Pigeons flew up and fluttered noisily overhead. Time seemed to stand still.

Then chaos broke out.

The crowd stormed the National Guardsmen. But instead of fighting them, the soldiers switched sides and stormed with them! Jeanne saw a man wedged in the crowd suddenly struggle free and jump up next to her to reach safety from the jostling mob. She only gave him a glance. He wore an outdated uniform, had the three-colored cockade tied around his waist, and his matted bicorn was crooked on his head.

Jeanne pressed her hand over her mouth in horror when she realized that the Swiss Guards were now alone facing the thousands of people ready to kill.

"Coward," ranted the man next to her. "Where is the king? He should show himself; that would calm down the crowd."

Jeanne looked at him furtively. The din broke out again. This time shots rang out, and swords and axes clashed with muskets. Hundreds of Swiss Guardsmen were wiped out by the bloodthirsty people before Jeanne's eyes. A stampede broke out and turned into a carnage with shouting and trampling. Jeanne wanted to turn away, but she was petrified and under shock. She pressed her hands over her open mouth.

The mob spread out like a roaring monster and gradually overflowed up to the stairs of the surrounding houses, rolling towards them like thick silt, ready to pull them down into its depths. Obviously alarmed and in a panic, the man pushed the front door open, grabbed Jeanne and pulled her inside.

He had recognized her.

***

Marianne - Paris, 1792

"Marianne!" Germaine lilted through the house. "You have a visitor!"

Startled, Marianne slid a bookmark between the pages, closed her book, got up, and straightened out her clothes.

A visitor? For her? She was beset by fear and hope at the same time. She glanced in the mirror and smiled. Her hair looked gorgeous, pinned up and decorated with flowers. A few wisps of hair fell casually on her face. Germaine had shown her how to do her hair beautifully in a few simple steps and still look natural. Her light blue summer dress with the white frilled border on the neckline suited her fair complexion and dark eyes.

She hastily gathered her skirts and went down the stairs. And there was Pascal, looking at her expectantly.

"Pascal!" she exclaimed happily after she had overcome the initial, brief disappointment.

"Marianne, my heart." He bowed and kissed her hand.

"May I offer you some refreshment?" Germaine interrupted.

"Oh, thank you very much, Madame de Staël, very kind of you, but I wanted to speak to Marianne first."

"Of course, just go into the garden. I'll follow you at a distance" Germaine winked.

Marianne hooked up with him, and they left the hall, crossed the salon, which was still empty at this time, and went into the garden. True to her word, Germaine sauntered after them out of earshot, book in hand. The garden was in full bloom, and the smell of rose petals that had already fallen off, covering the ground, was alluring. The avenues were enhanced by a line of small fountains with stone statuettes. Summer was drawing to a close and would soon be replaced by autumn with its warm colors.

"Dearest, you get more beautiful every time we meet. I seriously wonder if I deserve you at all."

Marianne knew that he meant it. Everyone agreed that Marianne had developed into a real beauty since she had gained more self-confidence. Pascal, on the other hand, looked inconspicuous at her side. But Marianne loved him as he was.

"You flatterer," she chaffed. "You want to talk your way out."

"Oh, by no means, Marianne, and you know that too, you teaser. You are probably wondering why I came here so unexpectedly?"

"Yes. I hope you will soon tell me."

"I was promoted to lieutenant and transferred."

"Oh, how lovely," she exclaimed, clapping her hands in delight. He looked around for Germaine, who had disappeared behind a tree for the sake of discretion. Pascal seized this moment to steal a kiss from his beloved, which was easy because she had the same thing in mind.

"My dear Marianne, how sorely I have missed you."

Marianne leaned her head on his shoulder, and he hugged her.

"I can hardly wait for us to finally say our wedding vows, Pascal."

"I almost regret being in the army, you sweet darling." He brushed a strand of her hair aside and kissed the back of her neck. "I wish I could always be with you. To come home to you and our children after a day's work. How wonderful that would be."

Marianne closed her eyes and imagined this shared happiness. The splashing of the little fountain next to her stirred her fantasy.

"They say that some women accompany their men to the front..."

"For heaven's sake, Marianne!" He drew her away from him so that he could look at her. "Despite all the joy to have you close to me, I would never allow such a thing. You have no idea..." He seemed genuinely shaken.

"Of what?"

"Of the horrors on the front. That’s no place for women. And I think that men who expect such a thing from their wives are egoistic."

They sat on the bench in their favorite arbor, where they had first kissed. Pascal pushed his saber aside. She preferred to avoid the other arbor with its memories, although she regretted nothing. But it didn’t change her plans in any way.

"Is it that bad?"

"Worse!" He plucked a hair off his uniform.

"We often go hungry, the men have diseases, often lice too and, well,..." he searched for the right words, "... it's not always refined and decent."

Marianne understood. She sat at an angle to him with her hands folded in her lap, feeling that she was not being taken seriously.

"The constant bombardment would be nerve-racking. A girl as pretty as you would not only be out of place but would also be in great danger. Aside from the dangers of battle fire, the diseases, and the rude guys, you could also fall into enemy hands. You have no idea - and it is better that way - what would happen to you there."

Marianne nodded. She felt honored by his solicitude, but she was no longer the innocent girl he thought she was.

"I would like to take over my parents' farm later," she ventured. "Would that be fine with you?"

"I would be fine with anything."

"Anything?"

"Anything that makes you happy would be fine with me. Moreover, remember that even if I come from the aristocracy, I am from the land aristocracy, and I love the country."

"Loving the land and working on it is not the same thing."

He smiled. You are right about that. But at least when I retire and draw a pension, we shouldn't have to toil. Apart from that, even mucking out stables by your side would be a pleasure for me."

Marianne laughed gaily at the thought that Pascal might enjoy mucking out stables.

"One can tell you've never held a pitchfork; otherwise, you wouldn't be talking so carelessly."

He grinned mischievously. Looking into his deep blue eyes, she saw how much he loved her, and she loved him dearly in return for this unconditional affection.

Embarrassed, they looked around for Germaine, but she was still out of sight. Pascal leaned forward and kissed her more wildly than usual, an intimation that it really was time for them to get married. Marianne longed to be touched and loved by him and finally, to experience what Germaine had known for a long time: What it was like to be a mother.

***

Chapter 30

Jean-Louis - Cholet, 1792

Jean-Louis shuddered as the piercing scream rang out. He cursed himself. Why couldn't he let her be? There were willing whores everywhere who were ready to do anything for a piece of bread. If only he had listened to her and simply left her alone.

Liese's scream rang through the house again. This had been going on for hours. He had called for the neighbor, Madame Chabot, Jacque's mother. They couldn't afford a midwife.

He cursed and wished he could run away. His frightened children looked at him.

"Why is Mama screaming like that?" asked his five-year-old.

"The baby won't come out, little one." Jean-Louis lifted the child onto his lap and hugged her.

"Then we have to sing so that it feels like coming over to us." The father had to smile, and the nine-year-old brother rolled his eyes.

The little two-year-old began to whine. "Mama, Mama, Baba, Baba." Jean-Louis went to her cradle and picked her up. My God, he thought, how am I supposed to deal with all this if... He hardly dared to complete the thought. Mothers died in childbed every day. But Liese is strong, he thought and calmed down.

Another piercing scream came from the room. The child in his arms began to cry. Jean-Louis broke out in a sweat. Where was the porridge? He went to the fireplace, but it was cold.

An unusual silence fell over the house. His heartbeat grew faster. The sound of a crying baby came from the room. Relieved, he turned to his children and ruffled their hair one by one.

Well, there you go.

He took two mugs and poured himself and his son the rest of the homemade booze.

"Come on, you're a big boy now. We're celebrating today." He poured the drink down his throat in one gulp and put the mug down with a contented "Aah."

The room's door opened, and an exhausted Madame Chabot stepped out with the screaming baby in her arms. She wiped her sweaty forehead dry with her bloody apron and looked grimly at Jean-Louis. He frowned questioningly.

The neighbor shook her head regretfully.

***

Camille - Lyon, 1792

Camille's feet ached, and he was struggling to walk on. He had crossed half of France to join his unit on the Mediterranean. He lacked the necessary funds to pay for a carriage. His despair and the prospect of doing something better with his life had motivated Camille to take this step.

Since the incident with Adelaide, Jeanne ignored him. He found her reaction exaggerated, even if he could understand her. But was it his fault? And didn't the love he harbored in his heart for Jeanne count more than a brief fling with some woman? Besides, what was she thinking? Did she really believe that a young stallion in his prime could curb his nature and remain celibate for so many years? How simple-minded! He snorted.

When it got too much for him, he left angrily, feeling like the fifth wheel on the wagon.

But the more he marched, the more he missed her, and deep remorse gripped his heart. He yearned to achieve something and return gloriously to his homeland and to Jeanne. As a hero. Her hero. He wanted her to be proud of him. Besides, he imagined she would be regretting it too. Oh, yes! She should be aware of what she had lost. Did she already miss him? Was she sorry?

He stopped at a crumbling street sign that read "Marseille." So the direction was still right.

He dropped his bundle and sat on a milestone.

When he volunteered, the Colonel had looked at him suspiciously from head to toe and then shaken his head.

"You won't last long," he had grumbled but signed him up anyway. Camille had been waiting for an explanation, but it hadn't come.

"Why?"

"Don't ask stupid questions, lad. You'll see. In times when the king was there, you would have been deferred, but nowadays, one can no longer be picky. Come on, sign here." The Colonel had held out a sheet of paper, and Camille had put a cross on it.

"You have two weeks to join your unit in the south. Now, buzz off!"

Camille had expected more enthusiasm and turned to leave, disappointed.

When the Colonel thought that Camille was out of earshot, he had called out: "Next one. Jeez, another greenhorn ... Well, that'll be some kind of army in the south!"

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1792

Jeanne freed herself roughly from his hold. "My crate!" She wanted to go outside again.

"Child, it's not just your crate you are risking here, but your life. What do you think will happen when there is no one left out there to tear to pieces?"

Jeanne stared at him. It took her a few seconds to collect herself.

"Napoleon Buonaparte?!" she uttered in surprise. Napoleon stood with his hands outspread, unable to react as she threw her arms around his neck in relief.

"Now, now..." He gently stroked her back and slowly pushed her away from him.

"I see you remember me too!" Napoleon smiled in amusement.

"You remembered me?" asked Jeanne eagerly. She almost forgot what was happening outside the door.

"Jeanne, isn't it?"

"Yes, Jeanne. How come you still remember my name?" she asked, astonished.

"How could I forget such eyes?"

Jeanne bowed her head, embarrassed.

Napoleon lifted her chin with his right hand. "Never be ashamed of them, do you hear? Look at people proudly and with a steadfast gaze."

Jeanne looked at him.

"One look from you is like the lash of a whip," he muttered and let go of her.

Only now did she look around the building. It seemed deserted. There were white patches where pictures used to hang. Cobwebs hung from the ceiling, and there was thick dust on the few remaining pieces of furniture.

"Where are the residents?"

"They have fled, like so many others. Hundreds of these houses are empty." Napoleon looked around.

"We have to go upstairs in case those out there," he jerked his head toward the front door, "should come up with the idea of breaking into the houses and looting."

Jeanne hesitated. To be alone in a doorway with a man was already more than daring. Going upstairs with him was almost an invitation.

Napoleon did not seem ready to hold or even tolerate long debates with her about propriety and decency. He hurried ahead of her taking two steps at a time. The sword rattled by his side, and he held it tight.

A thud on the front door made Jeanne flinch. She decided it was better to follow Napoleon. If he had been inclined to harm her, it would have been possible down here too.

As she reached the first floor, the entrance below was stormed. Loud roars echoed off the walls. Napoleon took her by the hand and fled with her to the second floor, where he searched for a trapdoor to the attic.

He found it in one of the dusty rooms and pushed Jeanne up the steps of the lowered ladder. When he, too, had climbed to safety, he pulled up the ladder. Jeanne couldn't repress a sneeze and immediately put her hand over her mouth, startled.

"Sorry," she whispered, dismayed.

"Hold it down, girl. If they find us here, we're done for."

Jeanne was slightly irritated and asked herself if his directness was his idea of being charming. But she saw the good intention behind it because if he was right, he was just about to save her life.

Napoleon moved over to the skylight and peered down. What he saw was shocking. Of the two thousand Swiss Guards, about two hundred were still alive. They had surrendered. The other soldiers lay dismembered in the square.

"What is it?" Jeanne wanted to know.

"Don't look," he told her.

Jeanne obeyed because what she had already seen was enough for the rest of her life.

***

Alain - Paris, 1792

Alain had seen her in the tumult of the crowd. Just as he made to go for her, she disappeared as if swallowed up by the earth. Once again, she got away from him.

Alain swore.

There was only one way for him to escape the turmoil, and that was to push forward and join in the fight. So he grabbed a scythe lying on the ground that had been dropped by an injured militant and plunged into the crowd. The euphoria of the masses infected him, and he let out his years of pent-up hatred and frustration on the Swiss Guards. How he hated that girl! He struck out, and a head rolled. And how he had hated his wife, who had tormented him for years. In his obsession, he grabbed a pitchfork and stabbed the next guard in the chest, who went down on his knees with eyes wide open, looked down at his wound, and fell over.

The satisfaction did not last long, and the irresistible compulsion to murder again welled up in him. Here, he could finally let off steam, unseen and with impunity.

His eyes gleaming with fanatism, Alain went on shedding blood. He was surprised at the strength that he could still muster at his age. A woman was fighting in front of him. His eyes suddenly blurred, and she became the picture of his late wife.

"No!" His scream was drowned in the general din. The woman turned around and yelled at him. He stabbed her and speared her on his pitchfork. He yanked the weapon out again, and blood spurted in a high jet from the wounds. The contorted face of the dying woman looked at him in amazement.

He saw Jeanne's blue eyes and stabbed again. He would slaughter the whore. Another woman stopped next to him and yelled something at him. He shrank back. What did she want?

He looked at the dying woman and, realizing that it was neither Jeanne nor his wife, he dropped the pitchfork and scythe in horror as if they were red-hot.

He slid sideways through the crowd. He panicked as people pushed, trod on his feet, and yelled at him to march forward, not sideways. He wasn't sorry for the woman. Women were all the same. But if anyone saw what he had done, he wouldn't have long to live. He had killed one of them, a revolutionary!

"She deserved it," stammered Alain, squeezing himself further through the crowd. He was close to fainting.

His hatred for Jeanne flared up again. It was her fault if he was stuck here. Nothing was it should be since she entered his life. He had to put an end to this once and for all.

When he finally freed himself from the crowd, he ran until the hustle and bustle subsided, and the streets were deserted. He stopped at one corner, panting, and leaned against a wall. He looked down at his clothes, which were in rags and caked with dried blood. He made a final decision.

He wasn't just going to defile her. She would have to die so that he could finally be free. She would suffer first, then die. He raised his bloody fist and swore it to himself.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1792

"We have plenty of time," said Napoleon after another furtive look out of the window.

Only now did Jeanne notice that her dress was torn. She gulped. She became acutely aware of the danger they were in.

They heard furniture being knocked over on the lower floors and the trampling of steps in the stairway. Then there was silence.

"What have you done with your life in the last few years, Jeanne?" asked Napoleon in a whisper.

Jeanne sat there thoughtfully and looked back over those few years. Yes! What have I done?

"Survived!" she replied simply.

Napoleon made a low sound that might have been a short laugh.

"You did quite well, it seems."

Now it was Jeanne's turn to smile.

"Well, let me think," she said, tapping her lower lip with her forefinger, looking at the roof beams and pretending to brood. "I was given away by my parents, abused by a shopkeeper, and then taken in by a hustler; then I ended up in the largest whore house in Paris - mind you, not as a prostitute, but in the care of my protector - where we were again kicked out because the owner passed away. I bought a boutique in the Palais Royal, and here I am." While she droned out the events of the last ten years in a few words, Napoleon's expression changed from surprise to indignation, from disgust to appreciation. Then he nodded and pulled the corners of his mouth down, expressing his admiration.  Jeanne also put on a somewhat crooked smile.

"I think you've experienced more at your young age than many an old man," said Napoleon, staring out of the window. "You have a gift for business, that's good. I have a gift for war strategies." He told her about his homeland, Corsica,  and about his parents and siblings. He would soon return to the island because a struggle for freedom was due to happen there as well. His gray eyes glowed with passion. Jeanne listened to him eagerly. She could literally see his home in her head and even imagined she could smell the eucalyptus and the maquis. Jeanne understood his concern.

"And you? Are you also going back home?" He wiped the dust off his knees.

Jeanne shook her head vehemently. "No, my life is here now, in Paris. There are only two things that I dearly wish for..."

"Which would be?"

Jeanne smiled sheepishly and played with a strand of unruly hair.

"Go ahead. Tell me." He seemed genuinely interested.

"I want to find my sisters."

"And?"

"Travel. I want to travel as ... a sutler."

Napoleon seemed genuinely surprised.

He fumbled with the worn bicorn while thinking.

"Freedom is not about doing what you want, but rather not having to do what you don't want."

Jeanne tried to understand the sentence and nodded thoughtfully.

Napoleon grinned. He looks really charming when he smiles, thought Jeanne. Why does he do it so rarely?

"Rousseau," said Napoleon. "Ever heard of him?"

Jeanne shook her head. "Who is that?"

"A writer. One of my favorite authors. Do you read?"

"Rarely," Jeanne admitted. She didn’t have time for that.

"You should."

She nodded.

"I'll come and get you when the time is right," he said resolutely.

Jeanne didn't understand.

Napoleon said nothing for a long time.

He played nervously with the brim of his hat, then spun it like a top.

"I've just been made captain. There will certainly be war. France is in a difficult position and needs capable men, especially since most of the officers from the nobility have fled. The chances of advancement are better than ever." He seemed to be saying the last sentence more to himself. "And should I be entrusted to command an army - and I will be - I'll take you as a sutler."

Jeanne laughed. She didn't know whether he was crazy or if he was serious.

"It's a deal," she held out her open hand.

Napoleon looked at her earnestly and took her hand in acceptance.

"It's a deal," he replied. His handshake was firm.

Jeanne had learned that people with such a handshake keep their word.

***

Chapter 31

Madeleine - Paris, September 1792

"What's the matter, Madeleine?"

Madeleine had not been feeling well for days. She hardly ate anything.

"Is it because of your loved one?"

Madeleine smiled weakly.

"Oh no, everything's fine with Maurice," she said. Louisa was relieved. She knew how important Talleyrand – Maurice – had become for Madeleine. Since her secret Sunday meetings with him, she had opened out like a butterfly, and everyone told her that she was even more beautiful than before.

But still, Madeleine kept the details of their relationship to herself. She only rarely spoke about an incident or a funny anecdote. She laughed a lot with Maurice, and the love between them grew. But even without any details, Louisa certainly knew how her girlfriend felt because Madeleine's face spoke volumes. It was all fine with Louisa, as long as Madeleine remained her friend, continued to work, and Rose Bertin did not find out about the relationship. What was Madeleine doing wrong anyway?

Besides, Madeleine was even more inspired for her hat creations. A few months ago, she finished her newest design: a hat piled with everlasting flowers. She called it "Picnic by the Stream," and only she and Louisa knew what exactly was behind the name. Her latest creations were called "Calash-Outing," "Sofa in Nature," "Love in the Forest," and "Madame T.," which Louisa found uncomfortably close to being a giveaway, but her fears remained unfounded. Rose Bertin was too busy with her own life to look for a meaning to it.

The Queen's failed attempt to escape last year had made her situation increasingly precarious. Many shops were closed or banned by the government. Rose assumed that the Grand Mogol was only spared because it employed more than thirty workers. But sales were falling, and layoffs would soon be necessary. What then? Would Rose still be safe then? Madeleine's love life remained the least of the boss's worries, and that was a good thing.

The only thing that irritated Madeleine were the rumors she picked up from customers who were only too happy to pass on the latest gossip. Madame Pelletier - the lady who had noticed Talleyrand's attraction to her at the time - reported to Louisa that Talleyrand was a man of the most wicked kind. That is supposedly why he is not yet married and probably would not consider doing so either.

She sometimes heard even worse rumors from other customers, such as that Talleyrand would always use his handicap to soften the girls' hearts.

Who would be wary of a cripple, said the ladies. But all the same, Louisa hoped that Madeleine's good spirits and the regularity of their meetings were a positive sign for this love story's future.

Madeleine choked again.

"Should I call a doctor?" asked Louisa, eyeing Madeleine with concern.

"No, that will pass. I feel sick every morning, and it's gone after an hour or so..." Madeleine did not finish the sentence but put her hand over her mouth in horror. She stared wide-eyed at Louisa.

"Are you overdue ... well, you know ... mother nature and all..." She pointed to Madeleine's belly.

Madeleine reckoned. Indeed, it was a long time since her last menses. How could she have missed that?

Madeleine nodded. "That means I'm expecting a child."

Louisa looked like she was going to faint. She propped herself against the wall and gasped. "My God, what are you going to do? How could you..." Louisa's reproving tone annoyed Madeleine.

"Well, do you think you can limit yourself to holding hands for months with a man his age?" Madeleine hissed angrily. "Besides, I wanted to," she added defiantly.

"Will he marry you?"

"Of course he will. We'll see each other again on Sunday, and I'll tell him about our good luck." She touched her still flat stomach and smiled blissfully.

***

Marianne - Paris, 1792

The six-horse carriage lurched through the streets. While Germaine read Goethe's poems aloud, Marianne tried to find a deeper meaning in its words. Two servants sat with them in the carriage, keeping themselves busy embroidering.

Storm bells rang from a distance. Germaine lowered the book onto her lap with a frown and looked out of the small, smeared window.

"What can it be?" one of the servants anxiously asked.

"Oh, another head will fall, or some royalist is being prosecuted." Germaine let out a bored yawn.

In reality, she was scared like never before in her life but didn't want to show it, and Marianne knew that. Weakness did not suit the eloquent and strong debater that Madame de Stael was.

The situation was escalating further. Her father, who had been living in his Swiss castle in Coppet since his final dismissal three years ago, had sent his personal courier to her with an urgent message the day before. She was told to leave Paris and France as soon as possible because she was in mortal danger. Germaine had been previously warned several times of the threat. But if even her father, who did not scare easily, now intervened, he must really have a serious reason to do so.

Germaine cleared her throat and started reading again. The mood was so tense that no one dared to breathe freely. The bells could be heard more and more clearly. The servants looked around anxiously, trembling with fear.

Crowds passed by, and they could hear loud shouts. Germaine paused again and closed the silk curtains to shut out the evil glances of the passing citizens. The carriage was slowing down, or did she just imagine it? Marianne felt hot, and her hands became damp. She noticed the suddenly tense atmosphere but tried to keep calm.

"Germaine, when do we arrive in Switzerland?"

Germaine seemed grateful for this change of subject and was about to respond when the carriage stopped with a jolt, shaking all the occupants. Germaine cursed.

Perturbed, she knocked against the inside of the carriage with her closed fan.

"Hey, why are you stopping? Go ahead, hurry up..."

The door was opened, and countless distorted and hateful faces, men and women armed with pistols, dung forks, axes, and other weapons, roared out the wildest insults. The maids screamed, and one of them fainted.

"Stop them. They are taking away the gold of the nation!" the women in the street shouted, holding the horses.

Fearsome men escorted the carriage at a walking pace.

"On to the Assemblée," one man bawled out, and the others repeated the exclamation in unison.

Marianne lost her self control. After taking care of the unconscious maid, who was now lying whimpering in the arms of the other servant, trembling like a leaf, panic began to take hold of her. Fearing for her life, hundreds of thoughts shot through her head. Would she ever see her husband again? Would she be able to give birth to the life growing in her?

After her wedding to Pascal, he took a few days off. She thought back spontaneously to the first night with him. He had been awkward and not to be compared to Talleyrand, who had ripped her clothes off her. But it had gotten better, and they had even started joking about it. A pleasant intimacy had set in between them. But she had not become pregnant. When she bled two weeks after his departure, she was bitterly disappointed. How could she ever conceive if her husband was never there? But her husband had soon returned, and she had been all over him, hungry for love and with a belly yearning to receive life.

Pascal had become suspicious. What had happened to his timid Marianne? But when she confided her fears that at this rate, she might never conceive, he finally conceded the good sense of taking appropriate action as often as possible, whether considered unseemly or not.

And now she really was expecting, and he didn't even know. Would he ever know? She wanted to write to him, but changed her mind and postponed the letter until they reached Switzerland. She was planning to tell him both news at the same time. Maybe he would never find out now. She struggled with her inner conflict. And what about her parents and sisters? She started sobbing and shaking violently.

"Maria, my goodness, control yourself. I've dealt with worse situations before. If they notice that we are afraid, we are lost."

Marianne nodded, mechanically brushed her dark curls from her face, and straightened her back.

She vowed that if she ever got out of this situation alive, she would leave Germaine as soon as possible to look for her parents.

This is how the queen must have felt when her plan to escape failed, she thought.

***

Madeleine - Paris, 1792

Madeleine snuck out the back door as usual. He would be waiting for her now, and she was terribly excited about this rendezvous. She would tell him about their child today. He'd also promised her a surprise about her sisters when they last met. She could hardly wait; the week didn't seem to end.

"Madeleine," she heard Rose's stern voice.

Madeleine froze. She turned around slowly. "Yes, Mademoiselle Bertin?"

"Don't go out without your stole. You might catch a cold. The air is quite cool in September."

"Oh yes, of course. How stupid of me. Thank you," stammered Madeleine. Relieved, she took the shawl Rose handed to her.

"Have a nice Sunday, Madeleine." With that, Rose left. Madeleine breathed a sigh of relief.

When she turned the corner, she saw from afar that the carriage was covered, and her heart leaped. Maurice, too, had thought of the cool air. How considerate of her lover.

Talleyrand was not standing in front of the vehicle as usual to receive her. He was often in the mood for jokes and was certainly hiding behind the little windows' curtains. To thwart him, she changed direction and approached the vehicle from the front. The rustling of her dress made the horses whinny as if in a greeting. With her index finger on her mouth, she urged the driver to be silent, jumped to the window, and called: "Boo!"

But the carriage was empty. She looked around inquiringly. Unsmiling, the driver leaned down and handed her an envelope.

She tore it open expectantly. What did he have in store for her again? He had no shortage of ideas. She looked forward to the trip on the Loire, when they would pass by a castle which a friend had temporarily left to him. She wondered if he would invite her in? That would be dangerous. They had agreed that for the time being,  secrecy would be in the interest of them both. Although that would soon no longer be necessary but he couldn't know that yet ...

My angel,

I am quickly writing a few lines that won't reach you until I'm already abroad. Yes, my sweet sugar flower, I had to leave the country for business reasons, but I'll be back very soon, rest assured. How could I exist without you? My life would be like a meadow without flowers.

Unfortunately, the urgency of my journey did not allow me to hold you into my arms again. This makes my heart bleed. On my last visit to the Grand Mogol, you said you wanted to tell me something. You promised me some "wonderful news."

I look forward to learning about it on my return.

My darling, I will miss you so much. I'm happy for you and with you, and whatever it is you wish to tell me, I grant it to you. Wait for me. I'll come to you soon, and then I will keep my promise for a surprise.

Dearest Madeleine, my heart belongs to you.

Forever yours,

With my deepest love,

Your friend

MT

Madeleine let the paper drop, held her stomach, in which she carried his life, and leaned against the carriage. The coachman looked at her with compassion.

"Bad news?"

Madeleine nodded, adjusted her stole, and said goodbye.

I grant it to you, he had written. My God, she sincerely hoped he would be back soon. She was still slim, but she wouldn't be able to hide her condition for long. What if he didn't come? What if he had fled like so many since the storming of the Tuileries?

But why would he? He was a Republican, a respected man with a good position in the government. She shook her head uncomprehendingly. It made no sense to her. No, he would surely be back soon, just as he had written, and then everything would be fine.

***

Germaine - Paris, 1792

The carriage stopped. Germaine stared out the window and took a deep breath. After several hours of driving, escorted by the mob, they finally arrived at the Assemblée of their district on Place de Grève. She instinctively knew that she was going to face a most critical moment.

Under no circumstances should she show weakness. The door of the carriage was thrown open, and the running board roughly let down. Insults broke out again loudly.

"Come on, you whore, now you're going to lose your life!"

"Take the traitor to the guillotine!"

Germaine straightened up proudly, showing off her heavily pregnant belly. She was also carrying another child - Narbonne's child.

The crowd around them seemed ready to spring. At that moment, Germaine bitterly regretted not having listened earlier to the numerous warnings. She had believed she was safe being a staunch supporter of the revolution. Surely people knew that!

She had helped many aristocratic friends, especially her beloved Narbonne, to escape. Since the violent upheaval raged and countless heads had rolled, her enthusiasm for the revolutionaries had been somewhat subdued. How could she approve of the indiscriminate slaughter of people she had known for years? People lived in fear and terror, and everyone suspected everyone else of treason, conspiracy, or double espionage.

Shots rang out in the distance, and shouts could be heard. Germaine shook inwardly but remained outwardly stolid. Before getting out of the carriage, she persuaded all the carriage occupants, especially the maidservants, to pull themselves together, or they would all be doomed.

Her gendarme stood protectively in front of her as they got out. They moved cautiously and pressed close together through the crowd armed with pikes. They were pushed up the staircase lined with spear bearers. Germaine could smell the fear in her own sweat.

A man bumped into Germaine, and her gendarme readied his sword. She felt that if she were to hesitate now, they would be lost.

The bloodthirsty people groaned aloud. But they reached the hall of the municipality alive, and the door was closed behind them. A tall man dressed in black approached them. His wig with the tight curls and his high, white cravat gave his already pale, powdered, and sweaty face a fearsome expression. He made a slight bow.

When she recognized Robespierre, she was not sure whether to be relieved or scared.

"Citizen Robespierre," Germaine greeted the member of parliament and main responsible for the terror.

"Citizen de Staël. It seems that you have been caught trying to escape?" A cynical smile was drawn on his lips.

"Not at all, citizen." Germaine used all her talent and experience in the art of rhetoric to find the correct arguments. "How can a traveling Swedish diplomat be called a fugitive? As you surely know, my parents live in Switzerland. I am heavily pregnant and wish to bring my child safely into the world in my parents' vicinity. Does that seem suspicious to you?"

Robespierre looked at her intently, as if he wanted to look into her soul. Germaine's head was hot and her face crimson, as it often was when she led heated debates, except that her very head was now at stake.

Robespierre led Germaine and Marianne into his office.

"Please wait here." And the door was closed. They heard the key turn twice in the lock. Robespierre's footsteps echoed long after his every step as he walked away.

The two women went through hell in fear. There were moments when their fear gave way to indignation, alternating with desperation and hunger. But fear soon prevailed again. They waited for hours. They sat petrified on old, hard chairs in the sparsely furnished office that smelled of musty files.

When Marianne couldn't stand it any longer, she went to the window and saw how the incensed people plundered the carriage. A bickering woman spotted her at the window and called out threats to her.

"Have you gone mad?" Germaine pulled her away from the window.

"They're looting the carriage," whimpered Marianne.

"That is not important, Maria; it's no longer important." Marianne did not follow and looked at her friend questioningly.

"Don't you understand, Maria?" whispered Germaine. "They want my head. If I ever get out of here alive, then those few pieces of clothing are insignificant ...

Marianne stared wide-eyed at her. "Your head?" she moaned in disbelief, looking around in despair. "Then they want mine too!"

"For heaven's sake, please pull yourself together," hissed Germaine.

At that moment, the door to the office was thrown open. Robespierre stood in front of them with his legs apart and arms folded.

"Citizen de Staël, you can continue your journey."

A rivulet of blood ran down Marianne's leg.

***

Chapter 32

Rose Bertin - Paris, 1792

Rose Bertin's hands were shaking, but not from the cold. She hardly dared to look at herself in the mirror that hung over the fireplace. Her cheeks were sunken, and her skin was pallid. It was an elderly woman who looked back at her from the mirror; she didn't recognize herself. The tension of the past few months had left dark circles under her eyes.

She took one ledger after another and put them in the blazing fire. She had to destroy any evidence that could prove the queen's debts, which would result in further charges against her. 

The poor queen was accused of debauchery, fornication, and high treason. And they wanted to incriminate her with much worse, unimaginable things that no one could believe; the queen allegedly  molested her own son sexually. Troubled by these hideous rumors that defiled their poor ruler and which were already being whispered at every street corner, Rose put the next ledger into the flames. Nothing coming out of her house should add to Marie-Antoinette's worries. Her beloved Marie-Antoinette, who had appointed Rose Bertin Minister of Fashion, would soon be brought to court. Rose felt terribly sorry for the queen. The poor queen and her children had been held in confinement since August and, to make things worse, her son was ill.

Rose touched her cheeks, flushed red from the blazing fire. She had done what she could for Marie-Antoinette. She continued to supply her with clothes, lace scarves, hoods, and gloves and was well aware of the danger involved.

In agreement with the royal hairdresser, she smuggled messages sewn into hats and hair ornaments back and forth. In this way, her queen had maintained contact with her brother Leopold II, who was ready to undertake military action to save the royal family.

But Leopold II died in March, and his successor was not prepared to take this risk. That had been the last hope, and the worst was now to be expected.

And now, Rose also had to start thinking about herself. Many of the nobility - or those who helped them - had been arrested or executed without trial. It was enough if one citizen reported another for the latter to be in great danger.

At the beginning of this terror, she expected interrogation and her own arrest any day. But nothing happened. Then she understood why. The sole reason why her business was still going on was that its closure would be economically disadvantageous for the state. Many aristocrats had been less fortunate than her and had been forced to close. Rose employed so many girls, suppliers, and producers of raw materials that she was one of the few entrepreneurs spared for the time being. How long that would last, nobody knew.

The situation worsened for her in June when she was suspected of wanting to emigrate. It was only with great effort and with the help of well-situated friends that she had been able to convince the police superintendent that her trips were business trips, which she had always undertaken, as she supplied almost all European royal courts.

She now feverishly tore pages out of a sketchbook containing her drafts for the queen. She sobbed and put a handkerchief to her nose. She didn't even notice the tears running down her cheeks. Madeleine and Louisa stood by her and helped her in the painful task of destroying her life's work.

"Madeleine, please get me the demonstration dummies as well."

"The dummies too?" exclaimed Louisa, horrified. For years, Louisa's job was to make the small show dolls with the miniature dresses worn by the French royal family and other European courts. She had put a lot of effort into it and couldn't believe that the dolls should also be destroyed.

"They're the last mementos of our queen," Louisa whimpered.

Madeleine gently stroked her hair. "Yes, but Mademoiselle Bertin is right, Louisa."

"Please do it quickly." Rose Bertin's hand shook wildly.

Madeleine went to a small cupboard, took out a bottle of schnapps, put some of it in a mug, and handed it to her. Rose drank it up in one gulp and gradually calmed down. She thought of the many friends and acquaintances who had lost their heads under the guillotine.

She had been warned this afternoon and told that her name had been mentioned. She had no choice now, but she just didn't want to flee.

"I'm sure one of the girls betrayed you," Madeleine said thoughtfully as she threw the next account book from 1777 into the fire. The book caught fire, and the flames leaped brightly.

"Yes, maybe the girl I had to fire a few years ago. What was her name?"

"Lucienne," Marianne replied mechanically and continued to stare into the fire blazing in the fireplace. "I never liked Madame know-all, but could she really have denounced you? Out of jealousy? Or pure malice?"

"Out of a sense of republican duty," said Rose.

"Yes, that's right. She has always been a keen Republican. But she wasn't the only one in your business."

"Again, that's true." Rose nodded. Talking was doing her good. She even managed to give Madeleine a grateful smile.

"What are you going to do now?"

"I don't know, my child."

She now also stared thoughtfully at the fire and put the next book in delicately, as if she wanted to express a farewell.

Louisa came back looking pale, pushing a large box in front of her. She took each doll out one by one, stroked the golden or silver flowery robe, kissed it, and could not help her sobs as she let it slide into the fire.

Madeleine took the next doll from her friend's hand. "Go and put some water on for a nice hot tea, will you?"

It was September, and despite the fireplace, you could feel the humidity of autumn. Yes, Rose thought dejectedly, a tea will definitely do us good now, then it may be the last one ...

***

Marianne - Coppet, Switzerland

"No, no, no, no!" yelled Germaine. She was seriously perturbed. Narbonne had stopped writing to her. For weeks, she wrote to him while he was in exile in England but received no reply from him. "Is he sick? Doesn't he love me anymore? Have his letters been intercepted, or does he already have a new lover? How could he do this to me?

Marianne sat next to her, stunned, witnessing her friend's temperamental, hysterical outbursts. She thought she knew Germaine but was beginning to doubt if she really did. They had survived the dangers of the journey and had finally arrived safely in the Swiss castle of the Neckers in Coppet. Marianne had lost her child, and she could hardly bear the pain. She still wept every night, mourning her immeasurable loss. She, who felt destined to be a mother whose purpose in life was family, had lost her child. She couldn't get over it. Every time she thought about it - and she did so every day - her heart was sore, and she had to pull herself together not to burst into tears. She felt an empty lump in her belly where the baby should have grown. Would she ever be with child again?

Her husband's rare home holidays made the undertaking extremely difficult, and her hope of motherhood faded day by day.  She sometimes even wondered if she would be able to have children at all. She had often heard about women, especially in the country, who did not get pregnant after miscarriages. They, however, saw that as a stroke of luck because they then had fewer mouths to feed. Would the same happen to her?

Marianne's throat tightened, and she shuddered. But Germaine didn't seem to notice her distress at all. She was too busy with herself and hadn't said a word of sympathy for Marianne's loss, either during the trip or since arriving in Switzerland. Marianne wondered if she misjudged her friend. Was it out of consideration that Germaine didn't bring up the subject? Did Marianne's usual composure in public mislead Germaine to believe that she had overcome the loss of her child? Or did she assume that Marianne would cope with the loss of an unborn child as easily as she had dealt with the loss of her two-year-old daughter? Did she take it for granted that a modern woman should be happy about not having so many children? This could well have been Germaine's line of thought. As so often, Marianne wanted to see the good in everything and everyone.

Perhaps Germaine felt guilty about flaunting her own pregnancy while Marianne had miscarried? But that would be expecting too much of a good thing, thought Marianne. Despite her tears, she had to smile at this absurd thought.

Germaine was about to give birth, was surrounded by friends and her parents, and didn't seem grateful in the least. Selfish as she was, everything revolved around her state of mind, and Marianne felt the gap and lack of understanding between them growing even more.

"Germaine, please calm down. You'll see him again soon, then you can talk to him."

"If I'm still alive," Germaine sniveled theatrically. "Because what is my life without my love, my passion? "You can't understand this. You never found out what it is like..."

No, maybe not, thought Marianne. But she missed her husband very much, especially after the loss of her child. She had decided it was preferable to tell him nothing about it.

"Why shouldn't you be alive anymore? You have survived previous births," said Marianne, trying to appease her.

"How stupid you can be at times," Germaine sighed derisively. "Because I'll kill myself if he doesn't write to me soon," she said laconically, staring straight ahead. “I'm sitting here, fat and useless while the gentleman doesn't deem it necessary to send me a message. All my pleading and my complaining are of no use; he has forgotten me."

Again she sobbed loudly - her pain seemed unbearable. Whereas Marianne was deeply grateful to her friend for many things, Germaine now revealed a side of her character which repelled her. She could hardly blame Narbonne for wanting to break away from such possessive outbursts. He was a man of the world, and threats would certainly not encourage his return. Could her otherwise intelligent friend not see that she was about to lose his respect with her embarrassing letters and destroy her self-esteem at the same time?

A servant came into the room. "Mail," he said simply, looking apologetic for his intrusion.

Contrary to her usual composure, Germaine jumped up and rushed to snatch the letter from him. As she read the return address, her face lit up into sheer euphoric joy.

"Maria, he has written, he has written! It is a letter from my life, my heart, my beloved."

Marianne was a little apprehensive of the news that might be in the delicate envelope.

***

Chapter 33

Jeanne - Paris, 1792

The container fell to the floor with a clatter, and the candies spread across the parquet. Jeanne stared wide-eyed at the marquis. Her pulse quickened, and sweat ran down her back for fear. She thought she would never see him again. She slowly backed away.

"Well, well, dearest Jeanne. That's your name, isn't it?" He lifted his hat in greeting. "What a lucky coincidence." He leaned on his cane, relaxed, and was obviously pleased with her reaction.

"What are you doing here? Get out of here!" Jeanne hissed.

"I just happened to pass by, little one. I love candy, especially these here." He picked up a blood-red candy. It shone like a ruby as he held it to the light to look at it. "Red is my favorite color," he said and smiled mockingly.

Jeanne struggled to regain her composure. Oh God, why did Claire have to be out with her lover today of all days? Even the shopkeeper didn't scare her as much.

"I'll call the National Guards; they come here often," she stammered, conscious how unconvinced she sounded.

His smile broadened. He put the red candy in his mouth and closed his eyes for a moment, relishing it. "You have excellent wares here, my little Jeanne. Simply delicious," he mumbled through his full mouth. He had grown fatter since their last meeting, Jeanne noted and was annoyed by such a stupid thought. What should she do? Scream? Run for it?

She tried to escape around the counter through the shop door, but he grabbed her wrist.

"Let go of me, or I'll scream."

***

Marianne - Coppet, Switzerland, 1792

"Maariiiaa, where are you?" Germaine ran nervously up and down her room, packing a scarf and then a robe into her suitcase.

"Here I am." Marianne stepped into the room.

"Is that how you are going to dress?" Germaine said disapprovingly. "We're going to England, not to the peasants." She chuckled at her joke. "You will see that the ladies and gentlemen are very elegant there."

It was Christmas, and Germaine had told her parents that they were going to Geneva. Since her parents strongly disapproved of her going to England and pursuing her extramarital relationship with Narbonne, Germaine made up the story of going to Geneva so they could get away without a fuss. Germaine would leave her newborn son in the care of her grandparents. She just had to see Narbonne. His letters had been disturbingly short and evasive, and she felt that he may be slipping away from her.

"Are you going to England?"  Marianne asked.

Germaine laughed flippantly and went on packing.

"What do you think of this dress? Maybe a little daring for the stiff English?" she said snootily, imitating the English accent.

"Come on, Maria, what's the matter? Don't be so glum. The situation is precarious but not hopeless. They're not going to cut our heads off." Germaine said, laughing hysterically.

She associated Marianne's hesitation to her fear of the Neckers' reaction if they found out that their daughter was in England against their express wishes. But that was not Marianne's worry, and deep down, Germaine knew it. But as always, she had trouble letting go of people.

In the last few weeks, it had become increasingly clear to Marianne that her place was no longer here, no matter how comfortable life might be. Germaine's threats and warnings no longer frightened her.

She just felt that the time had come to find her family and live her own life.

"I am not coming with you this time."

"You must! I'll be bored without you, dear."

"I have to go, Germaine."

"After everything we've done for you?" Germaine was beside herself. "How can you do this to me?"

Marianne tried to stay calm. "How can you do this to me? I have always been there for you, followed your whims, and didn't look for my parents and sisters as you wished. I stayed with you when you got married and had your first child. I even came to Switzerland with you at the risk of my life."

"You could never have had such a wonderful life," said Germaine arrogantly, "if I hadn't..."

"Yes? If you hadn't done what?"

Germaine remained stubbornly silent and sighed.

"I don't want to lose you, Maria. You are my muse, my confidante, my friend..."

And your henchman, Marianne wanted to say. Yes, she had to admit it to herself.

"Germaine," said Marianne on a friendlier note, "you are holding me like a prisoner. Of course, I am allowed to go to the theater, attend salons and go for walks. But am I not allowed to have a life of my own? In your salons, you defend the equality and freedom of every person. And yet, you won't grant it to me. All I ask of you is your help to find my parents and sisters. Is that asking for too much?" Disheartened, she sat down in the nearest armchair.

"But why now?" Germaine turned so abruptly to Marianne that her sapphire-studded earrings rocked wildly.

"I don't know. Maybe it's the general mood of the upheaval... the general excitement. I feel it has to be now."

"You're making your own revolution, so to speak?" Germaine sneered.

Marianne thought for a moment and shrugged.

"Yes, you could call it that." She wasn't angry with her friend. She understood and even shared her worries. But the pain that came over her when she thought of her parents was more gripping.

"You won't come back then?!"

"Why shouldn't I?" Marianne was earnestly surprised. "Unless you don't want me anymore?"

"You will stay in the country, I'm sure of that."

"Would you rather lock me up and keep me to yourself?"

"No, of course not. You're right. What do you need, Maria?" Marianne was relieved because she had come to fear that, like Germaine's lover, she was only one of her whims. Having taken her decision, Marianne had already prepared everything in the morning and exchanged her elegant clothes, jewelry, and hats for a simple dark red day dress with a white apron, and a frilled cloth crossed over the breast - a so-called fichu - which common citizens usually wear.

"A little money so that I can afford a restaurant on my trip and not have to sleep on the street. I also need to have a cloak made for me." 

Germaine went to her desk and took out a thick purse full of coins.

"Here, take it all - you never know." Then she took one of her ermine cloaks and handed it to her.

Marianne shook her head.

"Too posh."

"Oh, you’re right. You shouldn't be dressed too expensively these days; that arouses suspicion." She tossed the cloak sadly onto an armchair.

"Thank you for everything, Anne-Lou," Marianne whispered softly.

The two young women smiled at each other, remembering old times.

"Unfortunately, that Anne-Lou no longer exists. She is now married to an old man and banished from her beloved country..."

"Of course, she is still there," protested Marianne with a smile. "She is the same one who took a lover, continues to run her salon and publish her books."

They fell into each other's arms, weeping.

"You will come back, Maria, my Maria, say you are coming back."

"Of course I'll be back. But please understand..."

"Shh, of course, I understand you. I was a silly goose, et puis voilà, c'est tout!," she exclaimed, kissed Marianne on the cheek, and left the room sniffing.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1792

"I just want to buy sweets, Jeanne. Is that a crime?" he said calmly, letting go of her wrist.

"I don't want you here, Monsieur le Marquis," Jeanne hissed at him. "Get lost!”

"I have retired and paid for my crimes," he said, bit through the candy with a loud crunch, and chewed it calmly.

"I don't care. I still don't want you here."

"You have nothing more to fear from me, little one. You're too old now, too worn out, too ugly..." Jeanne stared at him in disgust. He seemed to be enjoying the situation and laughed scornfully. "No, really. I've calmed down. People are too immature to understand my creation and my work." he said and sighed facetiously.

"Before you completely lose your temper, please give me a bag of the red ones."

Jeanne recovered from her stupor, opened a bag, filled it mechanically, and handed it to him.

"A little more, please, child," he said, deliberately prolonging the moment to annoy and gain power over her.

Jeanne continued to hold the bag out to him impassively. He needed to leave. He slammed a Louis on the counter.

"Still as rebellious as before, I see. I should have driven that out of you back then."

"Go away. Get Out!" shouted Jeanne, beside herself.

He laughed uproariously.

He looked at her sardonically, deliberately took the bag from her hand, and whispered: "What a shame the chair didn't work. How I would have loved to let you feel my whip. Another person had to suffer greatly for your misbehavior..." He bowed again and was gone.

Jeanne choked. She locked the shop, stumbled into the back room, and threw up. Would those old demons persecute her forever? She felt an irresistible desire to flee. Away from here, from these monsters of the big city. But that would ruin any chance of ever finding Camille or her sisters again. She sobbed. Oh Camille, how I miss you ...

Unlike her usual self, Jeanne threw herself on her bed, doubled over in her grief, and wept. She imagined Camille's face, his blue eyes, his smile, his cap ... She had never really grieved over losing Camille, and now the sadness took control of her. It took over her mind and tormented her.

And Marianne, where could Marianne be? And Madeleine? Would she ever see her sisters again? Why did her life have to be so full of twists? Why didn’t things ever happen her way? She cried rivers and surrendered entirely to self-pity.

***

Camille - Marseille, 1792

Some soldiers lay lazily on the ground; others sat idly and stared in the distance. Some cleaned their bayonets; others mended their stockings as well as they could. Each tent had its own campfire, and there was usually a pot with a boiling broth of roots hanging over it.

Camille reached his unit a few weeks before. It did not seem to bother anyone that he arrived late. After crossing the whole of France, the south was a surprise to him. He would never have imagined how mild the climate was here. He was also impressed by the sea, which was sometimes deep blue and sometimes like golden oil, stretching majestically to the horizon.

Camille quickly found a friend in Bernard, an Alsatian who served in the Army of the South under General Carteaux for many months. He also volunteered in the hope of improving his miserable existence. However, Camille realized very quickly how small the prospects for advancement were in the army. Finally, this way of life did not quite meet his expectations. "That is how it is with us all," Bernard once said, and they tried to make the best of their plight.

"Hey guys, there's something yummy for us tonight," said a comrade named Daniel, raising his eyebrows meaningfully.

Bernard laughed and grinned at Camille. "You are coming with, aren't you?"

Camille didn't understand.

"New prostitutes are arriving at the camp tonight. It is about time ..."

"Ah," said Camille, but he couldn't share the thrill about this news.

"Do you prefer boys?" His buddies hooted with laughter.

"Absolutely not." Camille shook his head and waved them off.

"A loved one," suggested Bernard.

Camille nodded wistfully. He didn't even know whether Jeanne had already forgotten him or damned him forever. Pretty as she was, she would ... Camille didn't dare to finish the thought. He was so heartbroken.

"Oh, come on," cried Daniel, "she'll never find out. What else is left for us in this hell? The food will definitely not make us happy here."

The men cheered in agreement.

"And who knows whether we will still be around tomorrow..."

***

Chapter 34

Marianne - Paris, 1793

Marianne was freezing. She spent the New Year celebrations in a stagecoach to Paris, a  journey which turned out to be complicated. They were held up by a roadblock every hour and asked for their reasons of travel and their destination. She often had to show her papers. A distinguished fellow passenger shook his head in exasperation when they were stopped for the fifth time.

"Look," he snapped impatiently at the controller, "at this rate, I'll be late for my performance in Paris."

"Your name?" muttered the Republican officer.

"André-Ernest-Modeste Grétry."

The officer hesitated. "The André Grétry?"

"Yes, exactly. Now do let us move on, officer."

"Of course, I beg your pardon," said the officer with a slight bow. He gave a signal, and they were allowed to continue their journey.

Marianne could hardly believe that she was traveling with the famous writer of the opera Richard the Lionheart.

But she didn't dare speak to the gentleman as he seemed quite upset.

After a long silence, the famous artist fished a small box out of his pocket. He opened it and held the contents out to Marianne. She was delighted to smell the aroma of mint candy, which she loved.

"Oh, thank you very much, Monsieur Grétry." Marianne grabbed a piece of candy and put it in her mouth with relish.

"Most welcome, young lady. I rarely have the pleasure of traveling with people who are as reserved as you are."

She smiled with a bulging cheek.

Grétry also put a piece of candy in his mouth and closed the box, grinning. Probably because he also had a bulging cheek.

When they drove into Paris, the last traces of the September massacre could still be seen in some places. The city looked dark and dilapidated. Marianne read about it in a Swiss newspaper and shuddered at the thought of the terrible events from which she and Germaine escaped, just in time.

The papers spoke about the massacre was instigated by a certain Danton. When she read the terrible things that happened in the name of the revolution, she understood how lucky they were to have been spared by Robespierre.

As the carriage finally arrived at their destination, the gentleman handed her a ticket for his new opera.

"Dear Mr. Grétry, thank you very much. Please don't be offended, but I'm only passing through, and unfortunately, I won't be staying in Paris long enough to attend cultural events." Marianne wanted to give him back the ticket.

"What a shame. Then you can give the ticket away." He bowed and was gone. Marianne put the ticket in her pocket and got up.

After an hour's walk through the pitiful streets of Paris, she stood shivering in front of the Palais Royal, whose name had changed in the meantime. The roads were empty, and snowflakes fell silently onto the cobblestones. An eerie atmosphere hung over the city. It shouldn't be very far to the Grand Mogol, the shop which Germaine had recommended to her before she left.

"Go there and say you were sent by me. Rose Bertin will give you a good cloak and a muff," her friend told her insistently. She needed to hurry because night would soon fall, and she had no place to stay yet.

***

Madeleine - Paris, 1793

For weeks the smell of putrefaction hung over the otherwise foul-smelling city. When it didn't rain for a long time, it stank unbearably of urine, feces, and rubbish. Madeleine had adapted well to her new life over the years, but she would never get used to the smells. She thought wistfully of the excursions into the countryside with Maurice. Madeleine sighed and spontaneously caressed her belly, which was already bulging.

The fine gentlemen who still dared to come into the shop smelled mostly of heavy perfumes. But these customers could be counted on one hand. The clientele had changed radically, and business was not as good as before the revolution, which was partly explained by the aristocracy's exodus.

In addition to the modern and elegant clothes for the European royal courts, she created clothes mainly for the bourgeoisie. White-blue-red cockade ties for the waist were in great demand because people wanted to openly display their affiliation. Small, round tricolor cockades for hats and lapels, which had replaced the green leaves, were now also very fashionable. Anyone who did not wear a cockade was looked upon with suspicion.

The clothes also became simpler. Flowing muslin fabrics enveloping the attractive figures of women from all walks of life gave more than just an idea of their shapeliness. Foremost was the famous society lady Thérésa Tallien, who was renowned not only for her exquisite beauty but also for her disreputability.

Louisa took advantage of this new higher society's zest for life and designed dresses in the Roman-Greek style that were laced under the bosom and fell freely over the body.

She called this fashion "Mademoiselle M." and was very successful with it. The long scarf that went with it completed the image of a Greek goddess.

The new fashion came in handy for Madeleine because it kept her belly hidden for as long as possible. She lived in the hope that the child's father would at least turn up before the birth and save her from the shame of having an illegitimate child.

The mood in the shop had changed noticeably since the ledgers were burned. Rose Bertin was more withdrawn, though not unfriendly. She lived in a kind of feverish state. A nervous twitch had set in on her right eyelid and didn't seem to go away. She jumped at any unusual noise and had visibly lost weight.

The queen's trial was nearing. At the same time, the cruelest stories of denunciations were spreading. People reported their neighbors as being royalist or hostile to the state, simply because of their anger over a previous dispute or envy for their success. Hate, jealousy, and resentment were enough reason to get rid of someone.

Anyone who was charged with or simply suspected of high treason did not come out of it alive. People sentenced to death were shot or executed on the scaffold every day in the city. Fear had a smell, and it crept out of every nook and cranny. The mistrust increased with every day. Everyone suspected everyone else.

Madeleine and Louisa thought of Lucienne. They had every reason to want revenge but never heard from her again.

Madeleine admired her boss's courage. Not only did she stay in France despite the pending danger, but she also continued to deliver laundry to the prison for the "poor queen," as she called the former ruler.

Louisa pulled the bars closed. A newsboy passed by, waving his paper back and forth in front of him.

"The new iron suitcase scandal – exclusive – Talleyrand accused of treason – the new scandal!" he called out.

"Hey, boy, over here, please." Louisa pulled the bars open again.

She unfolded the newspaper with trembling hands. It was written on the first page, in black and white and capital letters: CHARLES MAURICE TALLEYRAND ACCUSED OF TREASON. It read: Evidence of Talleyrand's collusion with the king has been found in Louis XVI's suitcase.  He is now considered an enemy of the state, deserter, guilty of high treason, an emigrant, and no longer allowed to enter France. Shaken, Louisa lowered the newspaper, went to Madeleine's room, and handed it to her without a word.

Madeleine sat down. "Oh, the poor man," she whispered when she saw the title.

"My God!" Louisa scolded. "The poor man? Don't you know what that means for you?"

But Madeleine didn't want to hear of it. She didn't want to lose hope. Sharp anguish, but also a feeling of great relief rose in her.

"At least now I know why he's not coming back to me," she whispered and sobbed. He loved her but was forced to stay away.

As dusk fell, Rose came into the room and prepared a package for Marie-Antoinette with four fichus, bonnets, and gloves and tied it up with wrapping paper and a cord.

"One last order," she said gravely.

Suddenly, someone hammered on the shop door and rattled the bars. Rose winced, and Madeleine and Louisa looked at each other, startled.

"Did you push the bars closed?" whispered Rose.

"Yes, Rose," said Louisa.

"My God, they're coming for us now," cried Madeleine, on the verge of fainting. "They have heard about Maurice and me, and now they're looking for me because they can't find him."

"Be quiet," Louisa warned her.

"You're not talking about Maurice Talleyrand, are you?" Rose hissed at her. Madeleine got a fright. How stupid of her. She had actually given herself away in her excitement.

"I ... uh, I can explain everything to you, Rose," Madeleine tried to talk her way out of it.

"Not necessary, Madeleine. I am very disappointed. I warned you and forbade you to have a relationship with him. And you chose to ignore my warning and disregard my instructions. Pooh! Shame on you."

"It's not what you think, Rose. We're in love; we're going to get married." Madeleine whimpered.

"My dear child!" Rose said quietly with controlled anger, clapping her hands together. "Did he actually tell you that? Talleyrand? Getting married? Never, my girl; you poor..." Rose shook her head. Madeleine wanted to scream at her. But her voice failed her, and she trembled all over as the bars rattled loudly again.

Mademoiselle Bertin crept to the connecting door, peered carefully into the darkened shop, and froze.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

Jeanne quietly opened the door to the back room so as not to wake up Claire. Her friend was still sick. Her condition worsened over the winter. Claire just couldn't stop coughing. Jeanne called in a doctor who diagnosed tuberculosis. Jeanne was devastated, then she knew that the disease was considered incurable.

Claire was the only family she had left. Jeanne had actually stopped longing for her parents because it hurt too much. Besides, she was angry with them. As she got older, she had less and less understanding for people who abandoned their children. Jeanne had no wish for parents, children, or a husband. That only meant trouble. She wanted Claire, her Claire, who had always been there for her in times of need. What would life be without her? She knew that she should prepare for the worst, but how could anyone admit such a thing?

She discovered an unopened envelope on the small desk next to her bed. She turned the oil lamp up and looked at the handwriting, which she didn't recognize.

To Jeanne

Palais Royal

Paris

Palais Royal had been crossed out and replaced by Palais de l’Egalité.

She turned the envelope over her heart missed a beat.

From Camille Duchamps,

Sergeant

Besieging Army, Toulon

Furious, she tossed the unopened letter into the trash can, put out the lamp, and fell back on her bed.

Claire coughed.

Jeanne closed her eyes in despair. But sleep would not come to her. How come Camille wrote to her? So he ended up in the army. She should have guessed it. She was curious, but her pride prevented her from opening the envelope.

"Maybe tomorrow," she mumbled languidly and dozed off. Claire's terrible croak woke her up abruptly. This time it was a violent attack. Jeanne got up immediately, lit the lamp again, and helped her friend to sit up. The coughs got worse day after day.

Claire held a handkerchief to her mouth. After a few minutes, she calmed down a little and fell back exhausted on the pillow. Jeanne handed her a cup of water, which she drank thirstily.

"Did you see that you have a letter?" Claire asked hoarsely.

"Shh, don't speak."

"Jeanne," Claire insisted laboriously, "I can see how you've been tormenting yourself for months. Read the letter. What did you learn to read for?"

Jeanne got up, pulled the letter out of the bin, and tore it open. She persuaded herself that she only did it for her friend's sake. The envelope unfolded into a large sheet.

My beloved Jeanne,

You know that I can hardly write. Even if I was lucky enough to learn to read from you, it is not enough to write letters. A comrade has offered to write these lines for me because he saw how unhappy I am.

We are camping here near Toulon and live like animals. It is cold and humid, and many of us fall sick with dysentery or tuberculosis. My leg was injured  last month, but don't worry, I'm already better, and the wound has healed well.

I don't even really know if you care about me. I'm sure you still hold a grudge against me. I can understand that because I was a conceited cad who only thought of himself. I ask for your forgiveness. As I know you, my little Jeanne, that's not enough. I'm just relieved to have spoken out to you.

The war and the fighting, the dying of men, the hunger, the cold, and the sickness make you see things from a different perspective. You grow up quickly. I now understand who I am and what I want.

My Jeanne, a life without you is a life without hope, without joy, and without a future. You are the woman of my life, the love of my life, even if you no longer want to know anything about me. I'll soon be on home leave, and then I'll come to you.

With my deep love,

Camille

Jeanne's eyes were swimming with tears as she put the letter down.

Camille. She was touched, and yet that didn't change anything. It might change her attitude towards him if they ever met again, but her heart had turned to stone. There was no way she would surrender and open her heart to him once more. She wouldn't be able to trust him again. Mechanically, she folded the letter, put it on the table, and turned down the lamp.

"And?" Claire asked in the dark.

"Tomorrow, my dear, try to sleep now." Jeanne kissed her friend on the forehead and let herself fall on her bed.

That night, she lay awake for a long time.

***

Rose Bertin - Paris, 1793

What was such a shabbily dressed person doing here? Rose Bertin shivered. Since the king's execution a month ago, she understood that nothing was impossible and that the revolutionaries wouldn't stop at anything or anyone. She stood with her back to the wall, leaned her head back, and tried to get her breath back. With such cheap clothes, it could only be a Republican woman. And then, so late in the evening. A spy, perhaps?

She pulled herself together, threw on a dressing gown to look like she had just got out of bed, stepped into the store, and headed for the door.

How can I help you?

"I come from Madame de Staël. I urgently need a cloak."

"Come back tomorrow, I don't know any such Madame..." Rose lied, frightened.

"But I'm sure you know Anne-Lou, pardon, Germaine. She only buys her clothes from you and swears by your seamstress, Louisa."

Rose was taken aback. Only a few knew Madame de Staël's real first name. Only someone close to her could know that.

Mademoiselle Bertin unlocked the door, looked anxiously in all directions, and let the stranger in. She quickly barred the door again and led the young woman into the shop.

"What do you want at such a late hour?"

"Forgive me, Madame. I am Marianne, Madame de Staël's companion. I need a ... well, inconspicuous cloak against the cold. But it should be something simple."

"I understand. So you too? You are also on the run?"

"To be precise, Germaine already fled to Switzerland last year. She is now going to England, and I will not be following her this time. I am of low status and have nothing to worry about. But I did not think it wise to travel with elegant clothes."

"You are right there, Marianne. Please come with us into the living room. We're making a fire, and tea will be ready soon."

Since the nightly burning activity, she had become more intimate with her girls, particularly Madeleine and Louisa, who were now her accomplices. Rose had planned to flee that night, but she kept that to herself. This time, she had been urgently advised to do so.

Only Louisa was in on the secret as she was supposed to run the business during her absence. Louisa had taken the news as calmly as possible.

Marianne followed Rose into the living room and was grateful for the warmth of the fire. Her hands in the thin leather gloves were cold as ice, and it had been foolish to wander through the night without a cloak. She sat down on one of the velvet armchairs by the fireplace and warmed herself by the crackling embers.

Rose sat down next to her with a stiff back and clasped hands while Marianne looked around the living room.

"You can warm up and relax. Louisa will be here in a moment with the tea, and she will find something for you to put on."

Marianne smiled gratefully at her hostess. She was a little intimidated in the presence of the queen's friend and former fashion minister. It's hard to believe that she was now sitting with her by the fireplace and having tea.

*

Madeleine followed Louisa into the kitchen because she was worried about her. For the past few days, her friend had been nervous and anxious, which was very unusual for her. After all, Louisa was the one who always encouraged everyone, never lost hope, and always talked Madeleine into doing the right thing. It was almost as if the ground was being pulled from under Madeleine's feet because Louisa was her sole support.

"Tell me, what is it with you?" Madeleine insisted, annoyed at the fear in her voice.

"I'm fine now. I can still serve the tea."

"Nonsense," replied Madeleine. She took the already prepared tray and carried it into the room.

"Madeleine, where is Louisa? Is she alright?" asked Rose, worried.

"Yes, she seems to be better...she..." Madeleine paused and stared at the visitor, on whose face the fire was casting ghostly shadows. But yes ... it was her. Rose Bertin looked at Madeleine with concern.

"Are you not feeling well, Madeleine?"

Marianne turned around and looked straight into the face of her younger sister, standing in front of her, petrified.

"May I introduce you to Marianne, the companion of ..." Rose was interrupted by a loud clatter.

The tray had fallen on the floor, and the expensive china service shattered into thousand pieces, while the hot tea spilled over the carpet and some books.

"Oh, sorry, oh, sorry," Madeleine stammered.

Marianne jumped up and ran to grab her. The two girls fell into each other's arms, weeping for joy.
