"My sister, my little sister."

"Louisa, did you know ...?"

"No, madame. I knew about a sister, but not that I already knew and even saw her very often."

The girls let go of each other and stared questioningly at Louisa, who was so touched that she had tears in her eyes.

"Well, don't you know?" she exclaimed excitedly.

The sisters shook their heads vigorously, unable to get a word out of their throats, tightened by intense emotion.

"I sewed her clothes. All her clothes..." Louisa said to Marianne.

"Yes, you are Louisa, I know that ..." Marianne said, thinking back.

"And Mady delivered them." Louisa was quite shaken. "At least in the beginning, then she was given other tasks."

Madeleine thought about her first delivery. "Were you the Maria who didn't want to come down to try Louisa's new creation?"

"Yes. I wasn't presentable." The girls stared at each other.

"My God! Were you Mady, the delivery girl?"

"Yes," cheeped Madeleine, her eyes swimming in tears.

"What a waste," said Louisa, wringing her hands. "You've been so close all these years without knowing it." Louisa shook her head in dismay, forgetting her own grief.

The girls nodded and fell into each other's arms again, sobbing. They mourned the lost time and, at the same time, were beside themselves with joy at having finally found each other.

"So," said Rose, more severely than intended, "I'm sorry, but I have to take your leave now." She got up. "Please give your sister a sensible cloak."

Sniffing and blinking, Madeleine broke away from her sister.

"Oh, forgive me, Rose. You are quite right. You're staying with us today, aren't you?"

Marianne nodded nervously. What else could she answer? Her mind was racing.

"You can sleep with us in our bedroom, but first, we will find you a cloak."

*

When the girls said goodbye to go to bed, Rose was still cleaning up.

Exhausted, the girls climbed the steps to their room, dressed in their nightgowns, and prepared a place to sleep for Marianne, who then snuggled in with her sister.

"I am going to our parents in Cholet."

"Oh," said Madeleine in awe.

"Do you want to come with me?"

Madeleine hesitated. "I can't just leave Louisa alone ... and besides ..." She took Marianne's hand and put it on the bulge of her belly.

"A child?" breathed Marianne, not wanting to take her hand off her sister's round, firm body. Wordlessly, she stared from Madeleine's face to her stomach and back.

"I am going to be an aunt? How wonderful ..." Again, tears came to Marianne's eyes as she spontaneously thought of her lost child.

"Yes, now I understand why you can't get away. Where is the happy daddy?"

"He's currently on the run," Madeleine whispered as if the walls might have ears.

"It's Charles-Maurice Talleyrand," Louisa burst out.

"Talleyrand? The Talleyrand?" Marianne stared at Louisa in disbelief.

"Yes, Marianne. We love each other. He is going to marry me, and ..." Madeleine jutted out her chin.

"Sister, he's a churchman and a well-known ..." Marianne swallowed her words.

"A what?"

"He's a member of the church," said Marianne, avoiding a possible blunder.

"I don't want to interfere, but I think it would be better if you were honest with your sister," Louisa said, suspecting what Marianne was avoiding to mention. She had heard the gossip often enough and was glad she would not be the one to tell her friend.

"Do you know him?" Madeleine asked her sister.

"Yes, I already had the pleasure."

Louisa and Marianne exchanged a furtive, knowing look.

"Madeleine, I know him from Germaine's salon."

Marianne told her about her life with Germaine, the hundreds of salons she had attended, the parties and debauchery at those meetings, and ... yes, about Talleyrand, his advances, and his corrupt reputation.

"Oh, that's why ..." Madeleine muttered. "That's why he didn't tell me before where you were?"

"I beg your pardon?" Marianne didn't understand.

"Oh, nothing. Leave it; it is no longer important. I'm so happy to have found you." Madeleine took the news with composure. Her joy of being reunited with her sister overrode everything else. Besides, the memory of her lover had faded lately, and she was already contemplating life without him. The newspaper report had only confirmed her suspicions, even if she didn’t really want to admit that to Louisa.

Learning about his infidelity had been a hard blow, but she was not totally surprised because the morality of the salons and the lords of higher status was well known. But deep in her soul, when she thought of their rendezvous and excursions, of his words and how he gazed at her, she doubted her sister's account and clung to her hope that this love had been genuine; that he had cared at least a little bit about her...

"You think you know a person well, and then you learn that he is hiding thousands of secrets." Madeleine shook her head uneasily.

"My little sister, I am terribly sorry to have caused you such sorrow, but perhaps everything about the rumors is not quite true."

Madeleine smiled sarcastically. "No, maybe he is as innocent as a baby, and the rest of the world is made up of liars."

Marianne had to smile despite the gravity of the situation. Louisa breathed out a sigh of relief.

"At least we are now together and can find solutions," Marianne said with determination.

"But I have to go to our parents, Madeleine."

"I understand."

"Do you know anything about Jeanne?"

"No, and you?"

Marianne said no. "I don't understand our parents," she said.

"Hmm ..." Madeleine pulled up the covers.

"I mean, how can you just drop your children off and never come back?" The question lingered in the room like a ghost that won't go away and that couldn't be driven away either.

Marianne looked at Madeleine in the moonlight. Her sister.

She couldn't believe it. Overcome by emotion, they hugged each other again, sobbing. They still had so much to tell and ask each other. But exhaustion got the better of them.

"He seemed so true, so ... sincere", whispered Madeleine before falling asleep on her sister's shoulder.

At daybreak, the grille of the shop entrance was rattled so loudly that the girls in the garret were startled by the din. They looked at each other in horror.

"In the name of the Republic, open the door!" someone shouted from below.

"Open the door, or we'll ram it in!"

Madeleine threw on a dressing gown and hurried down the stairway with her nightgown flying. Where could Rose be? She looked into her room on the first floor, but the bed was neatly made. Was she perhaps already in the kitchen? Again the grille was jolted, this time so violently that it began to bend. With trembling hands, Madeleine quickly grabbed the keys to open the door and the half-bent grille. Despite the cold, she broke out in a sweat.

Republican secret police soldiers stormed the shop.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

"Claire, Claire!" Jeanne cried desperately. Her friend had passed out after a violent coughing fit. Claire slowly recovered consciousness as Jeanne shook her, trying to wake her up. She had not left her bed for days. Claire grabbed Jeanne's wrist.

"Jeanne," she said weakly and smiled.

"Talking is too much effort, Claire." Jeanne put her hand gently on Claire's mouth. Claire took her hand away.

"Jeanne, it's important. I have to tell you something."

"No, you ..."

"Jeanne! "It's coming to an end; I can feel it ... "

"Oh, come on! What are you talking about? We can handle it. You must fight, and not let the illness defeat you." Upset, Jeanne wanted to get up, but Claire held her back with her last remaining strength. Claire had never been a fighter. Tears came to Jeanne's eyes.

"Please, dear, don't lie to yourself. You just have to accept it. This fight is lost. The disease cannot be defeated." Finally, Jeanne nodded reluctantly.

"But what will become of me then?" she asked, choking with her tears. Her heart ached. Not Claire. Not Claire too.

Everyone had left her. Her parents, her sisters, Camille, and now Claire as well.

It can't be.

“As I know you, my dear Jeanne, you'll get through superbly. I was just a millstone around your neck."

"You're talking nonsense!" Jeanne shouted angrily. "Without you, who knows what would have become of me?" Through a haze of tears, she saw Claire's pale, emaciated face.

"Yes, but now you've been dragging me along for years and your decisions depended on me. Try to see the good side. Once I'm gone, you will be free."

Jeanne laid her head on her friend's chest, sobbing.

"To be free is not to be able to do what one wants. To be free is not to have to do what one does not want," Jeanne said, quoting Rousseau, whose books she had devoured since meeting Napoleon. Claire smiled.

"Where do you pick up such grandiose sayings?"

Jeanne grinned through her tears.

"Please don't say that I would be free without you."

"It's okay." Claire stroked her little one's hair.

"Jeanne?"

"Hmm?"

"I often think of the scruffy man with the weird name and greasy hair."

"Why?"

"Because he warned me not to catch my death in the cold and damp." She continued to stroke Jeanne's hair. "What was his name again?"

Jeanne looked at Claire, amused. Then they both laughed heartily, which ended with another coughing fit for Claire. When she took the handkerchief from her mouth, there was blood in it. Jeanne pressed her lips together.

"Please come here," Claire croaked with the last of her remaining strength.

Jeanne snuggled up against her friend, who hugged her tightly and seemed to fall asleep, exhausted.

After a few minutes, her grip eased. Jeanne raised her head and looked at her friend, whose mouth and eyes were open.

"Claire? Claire? No, no, no!"

With a bloodcurdling scream, Jeanne collapsed in front of the bed, from which the arm of her long-time friend and companion hung limply.

"Napoleon was his name, Claire, Napoleon," whimpered Jeanne.

***

Madeleine - Paris, 1793

"We're looking for Mademoiselle Rose Bertin. Are you Rose Bertin?" Madeleine shook her head vigorously. The man gave a signal to the others.

"Where is she?"

"Probably still in her room," Madeleine managed to reply in a weak voice. She held on to the counter as her knees started to tremble, threatening to give way.

The soldiers had already gone past her and were searching the house. They gave off a smell of freshly polished shoes and damp leather.

"She needs to come with me for interrogation."

"But why?"

"No questions, Mademoiselle, or you'll come with me too."

Marianne and Louisa rushed down the stairs in their dressing gowns and with disheveled hair to join Madeleine, who was shaking like a leaf.

When the soldiers had searched the house, the sergeant went to the general and shook his head, indicating that they found nothing.

"So, you don't know where this lady is?"

Frightened to death, Louisa and Madeleine promptly shook their heads.

"She was here last night. I have no idea what that means," Louisa said, and her surprise sounded genuine.

The gentleman turned to Marianne. "And you, young lady?"

"I'm just visiting here," she stammered, recalling the images of the September massacre from which she and Germaine had just escaped.

"Ah, visiting, hmm." The officer scratched his head and wrote something in his notebook.

"Your name?"

"Marianne Cotin."

"For the time being," he thundered, "none of you are leaving this house. You must be available for eventual hearings. Anyone who escapes is guilty of treason, and you know how that ends up." He grinned broadly and indicated a slash of the throat with his hand. "Like our dear king, don't you forget that, right?"

The girls nodded, terrified, and resisted the urge to back away.

"With respect, officer," Marianne ventured forward but immediately regretted it. "I, well ... I am on my way to Cholet. I would like to visit my parents there. Could you please tell me when I can continue my journey?"

"You will know in due time. You'll stay here for now. If you have accommodation problems, there is surely a place in prison." He laughed, and the soldiers grinned wickedly.

When the soldiers were out of sight, Louisa sat heavily on the stairs.

"What a mess!"

"Where is she? Where is she hiding, Louisa?" Madeleine racked her brains. "In the attic? In the kitchen?"

"She is gone."

"Gone?" Madeleine couldn't believe it. "But where to?"

"She fled to England. Last night."

"And did you know about it?"

"Yes."

Madeleine flew into a rage.

"Do you know that they can kill you for that?"

Louisa nodded.

"And now, I am also into the secret."

"Madeleine." Marianne tried to appease her sister. "Calm down. Come, let's make some tea and think about it quietly."

Madeleine glared at Louisa. "There isn't much to think about. Louisa, you should have told the men. Now you have become involved. You know what they do with traitors; you've seen it often enough in the neighborhood." Madeleine was aghast.

Louisa started to sob. "I couldn't rat on Rose, please understand ..."

"Rose, Rose," Madeleine replied sarcastically. "Does she worry about us? Is she worth landing on the scaffold for, tell me, Louisa? Is she worth it?" Madeleine exclaimed hysterically, shaking her friend.

"We'll find a solution," said Marianne firmly. "Now, come on, girls, let's go to the kitchen." She pulled the completely distraught Madeleine away from Louisa, who just stared ahead in silence, weeping.

After a soothing tea and honey sandwich, Madeleine became more amenable. She didn't look at Louisa anymore, but she didn't scold either.

"What did Rose ask you to do, Louisa?" asked Marianne soberly, twisting her long, dark hair into a bun that held up by itself.

"I'm supposed to keep the business going until she comes back. She said it could take years ... I didn't think anything of it. She often travels through Europe."

"Then everything is alright. Rose is just traveling again as usual, and that's all you know."

"And what will you do now?" Madeleine asked her sister.

"I will have no choice but to stay with you. How awful." She poked Madeleine in the side, who was looking sullenly at Louisa but then had to grin. The girls laughed in relief. Madeleine felt something warm running down her legs and stared at Marianne in horror.

"I think the time has come."

Chapter 35

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

"Hear, hear," yelled the officer through the halls of the palace. "According to today's decision of the National Committee, the halls of the Palais de l’Egalité will be confiscated and closed until further notice. You have five days to clear the gallery."

A commotion broke out in the halls of the palace. The shop owners cursed with raised fists. Since the hall owner was arrested a few days ago, there had been rumors of a possible closure. And now it was official.

Jeanne let her shoulders hang in resignation. Anyway, ever since Claire had been brought to the Paris Catacombs, avenue René-Coty and lowered into her grave, she'd had enough of the shop. Everything reminded her of people who no longer existed in her life. A fresh start would be the most sensible but an uncertain future scared her. So far, she had always been brave and constantly fighting for someone. Would she be able to muster the same strength for her own sake? Somehow she didn't care.

She returned to the store to make arrangements. Her pragmatic business sense took over as she made herself tea, sat at the counter, and wrote:

a list of the furniture that needed to be sold,

a list of the items she still had to sell off,

and a list of her savings and what Claire had left her. It wasn't much, but she soon realized that she was not poor. Over the years, she saved a sizable sum, driven by the fear of not having enough to live on.

During the next few days, Jeanne spent her time visiting shops outside the palace to find buyers for her goods and furniture.

She wanted to give away Claire's clothes. She wondered if the store where she had bought the clothes could reuse the fabric. Otherwise, she could also give it to the poor.

She now stood by the large window of the Grand Mogol with a suitcase containing all the neatly packed clothes. 

***

Madeleine - Paris, 1793

Madeleine counted the fabrics and realized that there was something wrong: some parts were missing. With Marianne and Louisa's help, she found some stability and tried to think about the future. Only when she was alone and idle did memories steal their way into her mind and torment her. Then she thought of Talleyrand's tender words, his kisses, his promises, and the happy hours that they had regularly spent together over months, laughing, carefree, and in love.

She was then tormented by questions that had no answer. Why didn't he write to her? Was he afraid of getting her into trouble if his letters were intercepted? Or had he just dropped her and already found greener pastures?

When the door opened, she looked up, frowning. A young girl walked into the shop, pulling a huge trunk behind her. Madeleine came out from behind the counter. "How can I help you?"

Jeanne stared at her without uttering a word.

"Mademoiselle, how can I help you?" repeated Madeleine, now slightly irritated.

Jeanne couldn't believe it. Was it her?

"Madeleine?" she whispered shyly, setting the suitcase down.

"Do we know each other?"

"Madeleine, it's me, Jeanne."

"Jeanne? Jeanne, who ...?" And suddenly, Madeleine's face lit up. "Jeanne! Is it really you?"

Jeanne grinned.

"Oh my God," moaned Madeleine, and passed out.

Jeanne caught her at the last moment and called for help. Louisa and Marianne rushed into the salesroom.

"What happened here?" asked Louisa, but Marianne stopped dead in her tracks and stared at Jeanne.

Too worried about her unconscious sister, Jeanne didn't even notice her. Louisa sat down on the floor and placed Madeleine's head on her knees. Marianne slowly approached as if in a trance. She kept her eyes on Jeanne, fearing a hallucination that could disappear at any moment.

"Please help me. She's my sister," sobbed Jeanne.

"She's mine too," was all Marianne could add.

Jeanne didn't react as she put smelling salts under Madeleine's nose. But then she looked up at Marianne.

"How ...?" she asked.

Marianne smiled lovingly at her.

"Yes, Jeanne, she is my sister too." Jeanne's eyes filled with tears. She jumped up and ran to Marianne. The girls fell into each other's arms, sobbing and hugging each other so tightly that it was a wonder they could still breathe.

"Jeanne, you've become a woman," Marianne managed to say through her tears.

Madeleine came to in a daze.

"Is it really her?"

Jeanne broke away from one sister and ran back to the other.

"Yes, it really is me." Jeanne rushed to her and showered her with hugs and kisses.

Overcome by emotion, Louisa was also in tears when the shop door opened, and a distinguished gentleman entered.

Madeleine got up quickly and smoothed her dress.

"Oh, am I coming at an inconvenient time?"

"Not at all," said Louisa calmly and shooed the girls into the living room with a wave of her hand. Since the boss had left, quite a few workers had gone too, and customers were even more seldom. The sisters complied and withdrew, grateful for the opportunity to enjoy their reunion in peace.

"My God, how tall you are!" Madeleine couldn't believe her eyes. Her little mouse, the baby of the family, now stood before her as a young woman.

"I've remembered you all these years as a child. Whenever I looked for you, I was looking for a young girl with a doll in her arms." Tears welled up in Madeleine's eyes again.

Marianne nodded in agreement. "But I recognized you straight away."

Jeanne smiled blissfully.

The shrill cry of a baby suddenly rang out. Jeanne looked around curiously. "That's your niece, Jeanne. She even has your name."

Madeleine smiled proudly at Jeanne. The pains of birth were meanwhile forgotten, and she adored her little treasure. "Her name is Jeanne-Marie."

"Are you ... married?" Jeanne asked hesitantly. A shadow passed over Madeleine's face as she shook her head.

"But that'll be fine," said Marianne reassuringly.

The girls spent the morning sharing their experiences of the past years and pampering the baby. "I called her Jeanne because she has your blue eyes."

"But you have blue eyes too." Jeanne laughed.

"Yes, but somehow I think she looks like you."

Jeanne cradled the little bundle tenderly in her arms. She couldn't bring herself to let go of it.  A strange longing came over her.

"When I think of the number of times I've bought clothes from you ..."

"That's how I feel too," agreed Marianne.

"Marianne, do you remember the day you bumped into a salesgirl with a hawker's tray?" asked Jeanne.

"Yes," confirmed Marianne, "I do."

"That was me."

"No! But I thought you worked in a boutique?" asked Marianne in disbelief.

"Yes, but I advertised my wares with my vendor's tray. That was much better than waiting in the store for customers."

The sisters nodded admiringly. Their little Jeanne had not only become a woman but a successful businesswoman.

Alain - Paris, 1793

"I've got you now!" Alain leapt for joy. He laughed gleefully. You can no longer escape me now. He was on his way to one of his regular customers. Since the road was muddy, he chose a somewhat longer route today. That was when he came across Jeanne, who was pulling a suitcase behind her.

He could hardly believe his luck. She was trapped like a mouse in its hole with little chance of escape. He thought feverishly. His plan had to be so good that it couldn't go wrong. Since he couldn't just leave his cart on the street and go inside, he stood rooted at the corner and brooded. He watched Jeanne inside the shop. She seemed to be at ease in there and was not treated like a mere customer. He observed that she was in high spirits when she carried her bag inside.

He stood there for hours, unable to tear himself away. As he waited, he imagined the most gruesome scenes of all the things he would do to her. But for now, there were still customers and employees around her. So he would have to strike at night.

Should he get help?

He immediately dismissed the idea. They would only prevent him from enjoying his victim as he pleased. Besides, they would also want their share of the fun.

After all his years of deprivation, she would now have to pay. Three or four good ideas promptly crossed his mind. He wondered whether she could endure all of it. He grinned with pleasure at the thought of what he was going to do to her. She would finally get what she deserved.

He had to admit that she had become a hell of a beautiful woman. That almost intimidated him. But his hate came from the bottom of his soul, and nothing would deter him from his plan. He just wasn't sure whether to defile her right away or prolong her torment over a longer period. Locking her up for a few weeks and watching her suffer would be fair because he had suffered for years too. The thought of his old woman came back to him, and the terrible years he had spent with her. His eyes clouded over, and he scratched his crotch thoughtfully.

A wide grin spread on his face as a great idea occurred to him. He took a piece of chewing tobacco out of his pocket and popped it in his mouth. Chewing happily and humming to himself, he pulled his cart further down the street.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

The girls sat around the kitchen table sharing a meal. The cook had fled like so many other employees, and the four of them were now running the shop. Jeanne was overjoyed at being reunited with her sisters.

Now all that's missing is Camille, she thought sadly.

"There are fewer and fewer customers," Madeleine noted with concern.

"We'd have to advertise to attract them," Jeanne mused.

"How are we supposed to do that; we're under house arrest." Madeleine shivered at the thought. She slept poorly and had nightmares every night. The very idea of going to prison was unbearable to her.

Marianne noticed Madeleine's reaction and put a reassuring hand on her shoulder. "They have no reason to come for you. You were just an employee."

Jeanne bit into her pancake and chewed with relish. Fortunately, Madeleine and Louisa had good contacts, who provided them with the most essential foods. They traded fichus, bonnets, smocks, and cloaks for victuals.

Jeanne wiped applesauce from the corner of her mouth and licked it off her finger.

"Madame Bertin's cousin will probably take over the business soon," Louisa blurted out the news. Nonplussed, the others stared at her.

"What?"

"That's what Madame Bertin told me before she fled."

"Only now are you telling us?" Madeleine was beside herself. As is so often the case, she didn't understand Louisa's behavior, which made her furious.

"Since he didn't show up, I dismissed it as nonsense."

Jeanne calmly stuffed the rest of the delicious pancake into her mouth. She had gone through so many upheavals that she couldn't understand the present excitement.

"We'll find a solution." She licked her fingers one by one.

There was a hammering on the grille of the shop's door.

"Secret Police! Open up! Immediately!" The girls winced.

"Now they are coming, now they are coming!" Madeleine cried in panic, trembling all over.

Take her to the room upstairs, and I'll go and see. Stay calm. Nothing will happen," said Jeanne soothingly. She waited until all the girls had gone upstairs.

"Open immediately, or we'll break open the door! We have a warrant. Hurry up!"

Jeanne hurried to the shop's door. Night had already fallen, and she could only make out the outline of a man who continued to hit the bars angrily and wave a piece of paper in the air. She hastily unlocked the door as the grille was already bending.

"Okay, I'm opening up. Please don't break the grille."

Louisa gave Madeleine, who was shivering on her bed, a little laudanum to calm her nerves. "Lie down, Mady."

She obeyed.

"Jeanne," whispered Madeleine. "Jeanne." Marianne and Louisa exchanged glances.

"We are all going to prison," Madeleine whimpered, "and then to the scaffold." She sat up with an effort and said urgently: "Let's escape, immediately." She stood up swaying, pulled out her suitcase from under the bed, and frantically threw clothes into it.

"You can't leave Jeanne alone at the mercy of the policeman!" Louisa was outraged.

"I'm not as noble as you," Madeleine spat out angrily.

"Now, please calm down. No one is leaving. It's certainly just a routine check."

"In the middle of the night? "Do you think I'm stupid? You are not running any risk, but Louisa and I knew about Rose's dealings with the queen. We'll have to pay for that. And terrible things happen in the prisons, I've heard about it." she sobbed, collapsing on the bed.

"Alright, Madeleine, you're right." Marianne nodded to Louisa. "We'll help you to flee, but for heaven's sake, keep quiet, please. I'm going down now to take a look at the situation."

Madeleine nodded.

"Don't do anything rash, Madeleine, okay?"

Madeleine nodded again.

Marianne pushed the door open and disappeared into the hallway.

The grille slid to the side. What happened next took Jeanne completely by surprise. The man fell over her like an avalanche.

"Now I've got you, you slut!" Saliva sprayed onto her face as he drooled. He held her by the collar and stared at her with bloodshot eyes. Jeanne screamed in horror. Her knees went limp. Why would a police officer behave like this?

He slid his free hand into her neckline, ripped down the fabric, and groped her breasts, squeezing them.

"You whore, you hussy, now you will pay!"

Jeanne was in such shock that she could neither resist nor scream for help. She couldn't think clearly and couldn’t grasp what was happening.

The man roughly threw her to the ground, knelt, and tore open his codpiece. She tried to crawl backward, and as he grabbed her leg, she saw his erected penis laid bare. "Now you're finally going to finish what you started as a child!" he said and lunged at her.

Jeanne recovered from her stupor and shivered when she finally recognized him. Although older and thinner, it was unmistakably the shopkeeper. Her reaction was prompt; she kicked out, but his hatred for her was so blind that he didn't seem to feel the blows. She tasted blood as he slapped her face with the back of his hand. Jeanne screamed out loud while he dropped between her legs. He brutally tore her skirts, pressed her legs apart, and tried to penetrate her. Jeanne bit his ear until blood splattered. He cursed and struck her again. Everything revolved around Jeanne, and she lost consciousness.

***

Camille - Toulon, 1793

There was a smell of sweat, feces, and vomit. The September heat weighed heavily on him, the crickets chirped incessantly, and at times, the noise was deafening. They slept outdoors and woke up in the morning full of insect bites. But the air in the tents was stuffy and unbearable.

After fighting against the insurgent royalists in Marseilles, the troops had been relocated. Toulon was under siege until further notice because it opened its port to the enemy ships from the English fleet.

"The heat is killing me," said Camille. Even after so many months, he was still not used to the Mediterranean climate. "Does summer never end here?"

Bernard grinned and handed him the canteen with the homemade booze. "Here, drink and stop whining."

Camille took a few sips. The liquor burned its way down his throat.  "Aah," he groaned as he put the flask down.

"Be happy that we're still not doing too bad." Bernard jerked his head toward the hospital.

Camille looked at the poor devils in the sickbeds and had to admit that he was actually doing quite well, even though he suffered from the heat, the putrid water, the unwholesome food, the mosquitoes, and the stench. But that was nothing compared to the suffering of the half-dead men in the hospital. Suffering from dysentery, they had been lying there inert for weeks. The epidemic struck dozens every day, and many died of dehydration.

Camille slapped himself on the cheek.

"Got you." He shook the dead mosquito from his hand and wiped the blood on his dirty uniform pants.

"Have you heard? We have a new commanding officer," said Bernard, chewing on a straw.

"Oh yes?" Camille couldn't care. What would that change? “Will he bring us new shoes? Better water?"

Bernard nodded in agreement. "Well then, I don't care," muttered Camille, stretching out on the hard ground in the shade of a plane tree. He lowered his bicorn over his face and scratched his crotch, which had been infested with lice for weeks.

"Soldiers!" suddenly thundered through the camp. Startled, Camille straightened up and stood at attention. Their general waited patiently until he was sure of everyone's attention.

"I introduce you to your new Captain Buonaparte. He is here to ...," the general cleared his throat in embarrassment, "... support us in the siege of Toulon. Please go ahead, Captain."

There were whispers among the soldiers at the sight of the small man. Many grinned and winked at each other. The weedy man, barely older than Camille, stood in front of them with his legs apart, looking downright ridiculous. "Oh God, where did they pick that one up?" Bernard whispered to Camille.

"Men!" boomed from the narrow chest, and the grins and murmurs died out. "I know how much you've had to endure for the past few months and years." An impressive pause followed as his gaze wandered from one man to the other, addressing each man individually. Taken aback, the soldiers nodded and lowered their eyes. "I promise you that I will do everything in my power to put an end to these conditions." The captain looked disdainfully at the general, whose rising fury was evident. "I'll give you food, clothes, better accommodation."

The soldiers cheered, but he raised his right hand sternly to signify silence, and the soldiers complied. "In return, I demand absolute obedience and total discipline from you." Captain Buonaparte looked sternly at Camille, who was blushing with shame. "Discipline – men, this is your new best friend."

Total silence hung over the camp. Even the crickets stopped chirping. "The entire camp will be reorganized. We will rest at night. New pits must be dug further away from the camp. We will set up troops for the procurement of food as well as troops of craftsmen."

The general shook his head in dismay as if to say: "Who does the jerk think he is?"

"You there!" The captain pointed to Camille. "Come here!" Camille walked up to his new commander. "I am making you my sergeant." Camille looked into his commander's piercing gray eyes. Could he detect a smile in it? He wasn't sure.

"Yes, Captain." Camille stood at attention.

"Your first task will be to put together a proper squad of strong men and dig the new latrines half a mile from here."

"Captain," interrupted the general indignantly. "How should the sick men run half a mile?"

"General, if someone had taken care of this beforehand, so many men would never have become ill. You are sure to get sick with the stench here. The healthy will walk half a mile, the sick continue to use the old latrines, which will be newly filled with sand."

"With sand?" The general's crimson head seemed about to explode.

"General, do you lack sand here?"

The soldiers laughed.

The new captain went on unabated, and for the first time in months, Camille saw a glow in the soldiers' eyes. He thought he saw hope. This little man gives us hope.

Obeying his orders, Camille sought out the strongest from the healthy men who followed him eagerly. Buonaparte's voice continued to echo across the camp, and for the first time, Camille was really proud to be in the Army of the South.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

Slowly, she opened her eyes. It took her a moment to collect her wits. Every bone in her body ached.

"Well, there you are again, my little treasure," he said, sending a waft of his foul breath toward her. "I just wanted to wait for you to join in the fun." He grinned maliciously. "It's nicer that way. Who wants to have such a limp bride?"

Jeanne was trembling like a leaf.

"Please let me go, sir."

"Oh, are you trying the sweet way out now? How nice to hear you whine. But you don't stand a chance. You will have to pay."

Jeanne detected the insanity in his eyes. She had to buy time.

"I was just a child, sir. I didn't know right from wrong. It was certainly wrong to run away."

"Well, I like this better." The shopkeeper seemed to enjoy the situation. "I don't know what excites me more, my little dove, your helplessness, or the fact that I am about to mount a beautiful, fresh young woman," he laughed uproariously, giving Jeanne a painful cramp in the stomach. And he started to tug at her skirts again.

"I'll cooperate if that's what you want." Jeanne shuddered as she spoke. "Don't hurt me," she pleaded. She searched feverishly for a way out but soon realized that she was entirely at his mercy.

"Don't make a fool of me!" he said, holding his rigid member in his hand and rubbing it on the inside of her leg. She fought down her urge to throw up and pulled herself together. She looked for some object to defend herself, but nothing was within reach.

"Don't bother to look. I've put everything away. You won't escape me again."

Jeanne flew into a panic and again tried to free herself. He laughed spitefully and hit her once more. Jeanne's cheekbones burned like hell, and she whimpered in pain. He knew she didn't stand a chance, and the glazed look in his eyes told her that he would not stop until he had his way.

She screamed at the top of her voice: "Help, help, help me please!"

Jeanne screamed desperately as he moved forward and set himself for the thrust.

But then, he abruptly collapsed on top of her while pieces of glass flew through the air. A broken vase fell on the floor next to her. Standing in front of Jeanne was a determined Marianne.

Jeanne tried to free herself, but the shopkeeper's limp body lay on top of her like a ton of bricks. Marianne rolled the man over to one side. When she saw his penis, she turned her head away in disgust.

"My poor Jeanne." Marianne pulled her into her arms

as she struggled to stand up.

Jeanne couldn't control her trembling. Panting breathlessly, she pulled away from her sister to lean on the counter when a chair crashed down on Marianne, who groaned and fell unconscious to the floor. Dumbfounded, Jeanne turned around to her tormentor. Cold-blooded rage took over her when she saw that the man was getting ready to take another swing and finish off her sister. Jeanne found a pair of scissors on the counter, grabbed it, and stabbed him.

The shopkeeper looked at her in surprise.

Alain dropped the chair and held the scissors stuck in his body. He stared at the tailor's tool and tore it out.

Blood came out in a pulsating spray from the wound. Startled, Jeanne staggered back. In wide-eyed terror, she held back a scream, pressing her hand on her mouth.

What had she done? She would be taken to the scaffold for that.

The man sank on his knees at her feet, dropped the bloody scissors, and fell to one side, laying still with an empty look in his eyes.

He was dead.

***

Marianne - Paris, 1793

Ma chère Maria,

I was so happy to receive your letter, and that you found your sisters again.

You cannot imagine how excited I was when I finally found my beloved and he assured me of his love again. I am spending the most wonderful time of my life here in England.

It looks like the whole nobility of Paris has found its way to London – and I am holding my salon as before.

Now to your question. Yes, T. is here and has not changed. Perhaps not all of the rumors are true, but what I confided to you about him back then is still true today: He is and remains a charmer, a womanizer, an eternal lover. Your sister had better keep to herself that he put her in the family way, for security reasons, as you correctly presume, but also in case she should ever want to see him again. He shuns nothing more than a mother hen, family life, and the boredom of a conquered lady.

I miss you very much. You would have enjoyed living here. But that is not the real reason why I am writing to you. Enclosed is a letter from the Rhine Army. It's not from your husband. I hope it's not bad news. This document has traveled a lot; my father delivered it to me in London, assuming that you are still with me.

In loving friendship,

Your

Anne-Louise Germaine de Staël

Marianne folded her friend's letter and looked thoughtfully at the official military letter.

She wanted to put it away because she guessed what it contained. As a soldier's wife, you had to expect bad news every day. She had recently written to Pascal to give him her new address. Maybe he was just wounded? But deep inside, she knew what was in this letter. She sighed and tried to brace herself.

She had only slowly recovered from the terrible experiences of the past few months, and constant headaches still tormented her.

Madeleine came into the room with a box.

"I'm going to make a delivery and ... What is the matter with you; you have gone so pale?!"

Marianne nodded and showed her the envelope.

"Is it bad news?"

Marianne nodded again, feeling her throat tighten.

"You haven't even opened the envelope." Madeleine put the box down and came over to her.

"I already know what's inside."

"Do you want me to open it for you?"

Marianne handed the envelope to her sister, who took it carefully and opened it as if it were fragile porcelain.

Madeleine pulled out the official letter, postmarked by the military.

"Madame Marianne Gaspar, born Cotin," began Madeleine, "We are sorry ...", she cleared her throat, "to tell you that your husband Lieutenant Pascal Gaspar ..." Her voice failed her.

"Please, read on," said Marianne calmly. She sat sideways on the armchair, holding a lace handkerchief to her mouth.

"... that your husband, First Lieutenant Pascal Gaspar, died in Dunkirk in the exercise of his duty defending the Republic against the British Army.

We assure you of our deep respect for your husband. He was a good soldier and a dear friend to us. We, the garrison and I, would like to express our deepest condolences to you.

You are entitled to a monthly pension of 1,500 livres. Please let us know how and where the money should be transferred.

Respectfully and in mourning,

Adjutant General Lazar Hoche"

Madeleine fell silent.

Marianne nodded calmly and stared straight ahead. Not a sob escaped her throat, and she did not shed a single tear. A peculiar numbness took over her, holding all her emotions trapped inside her. She felt absolutely nothing.

"I'll have to wear black now," she said, getting up and leaving the room with her head held high.

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

Weeks went by, and the girls continued to live in Mademoiselle Bertin's shop. Jeanne was making plans for the future.

She was feeling better. The shock was still present in her mind, but the events gradually faded thanks to the fact that she was finally reunited with her sisters. They had found solutions together. The repulsive creep was lying somewhere at the bottom of the Seine. It hadn't been easy, but the four of them managed to put him in Jeanne's trunk and sink it into the river at night. The fear of being discovered had been unbearable.

Jeanne acted in self-defense. The four girls swore each other to secrecy. Even Madeleine, who was usually so fearful, accepted the situation with composure and without complaining. None of them would ever say a word about it.

Jeanne had liquidated her business and sold the furniture as profitably as she could. She now had a considerable sum at her disposal, which increased every week because Madeleine paid her for running errands and other services since she was the only one who was not under house arrest for the time being.

She did not want to spend her life in this business, even if she felt very comfortable with her sisters. Only Marianne worried her. She had been detached and lethargic since the news of her husband's death. Madeleine said it would get better with time, but Jeanne doubted it.

Marianne wore black but had still not wept since the news reached her. She spent her days in an armchair by the courtyard window, staring outside. She hardly ate anymore, and her answers, if she said anything at all, were monosyllabic.

They brought in a doctor who spoke of melancholy and said that time or a shock would cure her.

Since time did not seem to change anything, Jeanne resolved to try other means. She knew Madeleine would be against it, so she didn't raise the matter with her.

One day, she entered the room and found Marianne, as usual, sitting at the window. It was bitterly cold, but her sister didn't seem to notice. Like every day, she was staring into the courtyard. Jeanne sat down next to her, and Marianne reacted with a blink.

"How are you, Marianne?"

"I'm okay, Jeanne, I'm okay," said her sister, without moving.

"Can you tell me about your husband?"

Marianne blinked again.

"I would very much like to know what he looked like, how he was ... Was he funny? Was he serious? Was he handsome?"

"Why do you ask?"

"Well, he was my brother-in-law after all, and I would like to know more about your former life."

A long silence ensued.

"There is nothing to know," Marianne answered lethargically. "I hardly knew him ..."

"But you were expecting a child from him, weren't you?"

A sob rose into Marianne's throat, but she held it back.

That's good, Jeanne thought; I'm on the right track. She had found out a great deal about her from Madeleine, who had spent more time talking to Marianne.

"It must have been terrible to lose a child you have longed for so much." As soon as Jeanne had pronounced the sentence, Marianne's face changed. She became pale, and her breathing heavy. "I would have become an aunt, wouldn't I?" What would you have called it?"

"You stupid cow!" screamed Marianne, jumping up. "Can't you keep your mouth shut?" She now sobbed unrestrainedly. "Why are you doing this? Why?" she whimpered.

"To finally wake you up, Marianne," Jeanne exclaimed. "To bring you to fight again and come back to us." Jeanne was deeply sorry for tormenting her sister, but she saw no other way, and it seemed to work.

Marianne threw herself at her feet and wailed miserably.

"What's the use?" she said between two fits of crying. "Look how horrible and cruel life is. I've seen the way the rich live. They have only one thing in mind: having fun while we struggle for our daily bread. Nothing is fair in this world, nothing, Jeanne. Why did I lose my child while Germaine has one every year, which she doesn't want and doesn't love?"

––––––––

Jeanne sat down on the floor and hugged her sister.

"Then fight for a better life. Fight like the men and women who die every day for freedom, human rights, and for justice ..."

"Oh, Jeanne, my little Jeanne. I knew the so-called freedom fighters." Marianne almost spat the word out. "I've seen them, the people who debated ideas for hours, filling their stomachs and drinking champagne. I saw it first-hand every day and for years. But none of them knew what poverty and misery really meant, no one except me ... They don't care at all," she said, blowing her nose. It's all pure hypocrisy! Some philosophize about freedom while others starve, or kill masses of people in the name of justice. Pah!" exclaimed Marianne passionately.

"There are also people who join this struggle out of conviction," replied Jeanne, thinking of Camille.

"The worst part is, like them, I became just as jaded, just as false ..."

Jeanne didn't know what to say to that. She wondered whether she would have also slipped into this indifferent attitude had she been in Marianne's place

"The main thing," said Jeanne thoughtfully, "is that you recognize your mistake and learn from it."

Marianne stared at her sister in disbelief. "What happened to my little Jeanne? You are so grown up and smart. You are right, Jeanne. I am ashamed of my weakness. I had blamed Germaine for it. But life is often ugly and mean. It takes things and people that you love away from you. Still, there are people who are much worse off who do not lose heart." She looked at Jeanne appreciatively. "I want to be like you," she blurted out. "Strong, combative, indomitable. I want to be like Madeleine, who carries on despite her bastard and all the shame that awaits her."

Marianne gradually calmed down and stopped weeping. She sniveled for a while, lost in thought. Jeanne nestled Marianne's head on her shoulder, stroking her back.

"Come on, Jeanne, let's go look for our parents."

Jeanne stiffened. "You can do it, Marianne. But I am angry and will never forgive them for what they did to me."

Marianne nodded.

"Yes, you got the worst deal." And then she added as an afterthought: "Madeleine will want to stay with Louisa; you will have to look after her and her child. We have found each other again after all these years and we will stay in touch."

Jeanne nodded. Marianne would have to go her own way and find her purpose. Jeanne could tell that the thought of seeing her home again, her parents and the neighbors was having an invigorating influence on her.

"Close your eyes, Marianne." Jeanne said and her sister obeyed. "Do you see our countryside, Marianne? Can you hear it? Can you smell it?"

Marianne smiled. "Yes, I can hear the twittering of birds and the humming of bees, Jeanne."

Jeanne also closed her eyes and smelled the flowers and forests and saw the endless fields. She opened her eyes and looked at Marianne. Traces of dried tears ran like little paths down her cheeks, but she was beaming. She seemed infused with new zest for life.

"Yes, Jeanne, I'm going home. I have to. Maybe I can help them. And I need to be sure. I need to know what happened to them."

***

Jeanne - Paris, 1793

Jeanne was running the shop on that day because Madeleine was feeling a little weak, Louisa had a day off and Marianne was making travel arrangements. Jeanne didn't mind. She loved the fine decor and enjoyed being alone in the business. Customers were coming back more often now since she had the idea of selling army accessories. The girls happily agreed and immediately started working on the designs. Stockings, gloves, tricorns, scarves and trousers were now sold to soldiers. Most of them were poor wretches, as many of the aristocrats had left the army and fled.

Jeanne made them an excellent price, and word got around quickly. She got out the ledgers. She glanced briefly in the huge wall mirror and smiled. Marianne had given her a hairstyle that was popular in high society, and dangling green earrings to match her dress. Lots of little curls fell from her hair, which was piled on the back of her head. The blue-green silk dress à la Mademoiselle M. accentuated her slim figure. Every time she moved her head, the curls tickled her bare shoulders. She thought about all she had been through and felt that she had reason be a little proud of herself. It gave her the strength to believe in the future.

The shop bell rang. Jeanne looked up from her bills. The soldier who entered was in rags. She couldn't help thinking of Napoleon and smirked. She put her pen aside. Would she ever see Napoleon again?

"How can I help you, sir?" The young man pushed back his tricorn and scratched his head. Jeanne's heart skipped a beat. Could it be? She took a closer look at him. The long blond hair was clinging to his tanned face. With a jerk of his head, he shook his long bangs from his eyes and grinned.

"Camille?!"

"Jeanne?" came the dumbfounded exclamation from the shop door. There was no doubt; it was Camille. He was thunderstruck when he recognized her. He seemed intimidated by her appearance. His Adam's apple bounced as he gulped.

"Jeanne," he repeated pleadingly, and opened his arms. Jeanne's eyes blurred. Her heart overflowed with happiness and relief.

"Camille!" She threw herself into his arms and he hugged her gently.

"Jeanne, my dearest." He took her face in both hands and looked at her. His piercing blue eyes stabbed her heart. "You are even more beautiful than I remembered you. And at the time, you were already the most beautiful girl far and wide." he said in a trembling voice.

"Jeanne, I love you. I missed you so much." He pressed his mouth to hers, and Jeanne melted in his arms. She couldn't see anything through the blur of tears streaming down her cheeks. "Don't cry, I'm here now." Camille also had tears in his eyes. He kissed her again.

"I missed you so terribly, Camille. Don't ever leave me alone again." Jeanne felt as if she had come home. It felt so right to be in his arms. Why hadn't she realized that sooner?

"Never again!" he promised. "I was a complete idiot!" He shook his head and looked back into her eyes. He smiled.

His smile, his mouth, his eyes ... everything still had the same magical effect on her He smelled strongly, but even that felt familiar. His shoulders seemed wider and his arms were bulging with muscles. The southern sun had freckled his nose, which made his smile even perkier.

"Yes!" said Jeanne, grinning all over her face despite her tears. "You can say that again, because you certainly were, you rascal."

***

Epilogue

The fog surrounding Jeanne was so thick that she could hardly see her own hand. She heard gunshots and screams. Frightened, she took refuge in an alcove. She realized that it wasn't fog, it was smoke. The air reeked of gunpowder. She coughed. Deafening noise came from every corner of the square. People were massacred by the score, heads rolled and her torn dress was covered with blood stains ... What had happened?

Jeanne didn't want to look, but she saw a guillotine right in front of her. Lines of prisoners stood in front of it, waiting for their turn. Another head tumbled amidst blood from the scaffold.

Would she get out of here alive? She gathered up her skirts and ran for her life. As she turned the corner, she saw armed royalists coming up the street and backed away. She wanted to scream, but no sound came from her throat. She was breathless. She turned around. Through the smoke, she saw a shadow approaching across the square. She stopped short.

Was it possible?

"Bonjour, Jeanne," said Napoleon, as he calmly let himself slide from his horse.

"Bonjour, Napoleon Buonaparte," answered Jeanne gently. She couldn't have said why, but she felt safe with him.

He looked different from how she remembered him. He seemed taller and was dressed in a brand-new uniform. His wide cloak gave him a majestic stance. His long hair fluttered in the wind, which was slowly blowing the smoke away. He looked really good with the cockade around his waist.

"This is no place for you, Jeanne," he said sternly.

"I lost my way," she explained. "What are you doing here?" she asked.

"I've come to tell you something."

"Oh." Jeanne looked at him questioningly.

"I'll taking you with me as a sutler for the army in Toulon, just as you dreamed."

"Why are you doing this for me?" She could hardly believe it.

"For your eyes, Jeanne ..."

Jeanne could hear neither the shots nor the screams; she was in a daze. She spinned around in joyful frenzy. In Toulon?

Camille was there too! When she came to a stop, Napoleon was gone.

"Napoleon, Napoleon!" she called desperately through the smoke.

"Remember it, Jeanne, remember it," she heard his voice sounding from nowhere.

Then she woke up in her bed, bathed in sweat.

Madeleine leaned over her, worried. "You were dreaming, Jeanne." Jeanne fell back on her pillow.

"Are you alright now?" Marianne felt her forehead.

Jeanne nodded. It was just a dream.

But it meant much more to her. A good omen ...

***

Napoleon - Toulon, 1793

The sun was sinking on the horizon, throwing its red-yellow light on the glittering sea. Napoleon sighed and stood on the bank, his right foot propped on a knee-high rock, and admired the sunset. It smelled of pine trees. To the side was a huge pot simmering on a fire. Two washerwomen stirred it with a stick to soak soiled underwear. He would meet with his brother Joseph tonight, and think about the future of his family. In the summer, his parents and siblings had to flee Corsica and settled in Marseille. Napoleon took responsibility for them as a matter of course. They were very poor, and he would have to arrange a good marriage for Joseph as soon as possible so that the Buonaparte’s bloodline could survive.

Looking down at his clothes, he let out the breath he had unconsciously been holding. He looked shabby and was aware of it. In order to advance in his career, he too would have to seek marriage in a well-off house. Without money there could be no equipment, without equipment there was no prestige and without prestige, there was no advancement.

He tipped his worn-out boots in the ground and looked over at the camp. In just a few weeks he had made a respectable army out of what had once been a mess. Brave young men were now ready to risk their lives for him, Napoleon. It would not be long before his promotion was assured. He could feel it, even if nothing in his current situation spoke for it. The right corner of his mouth trembled slightly.

"Captain Buonaparte," a young soldier interrupted his thoughts. Napoleon growled irritably.

"Yes, what is it?"

"Sergeant Camille Duchamps, reporting from home leave and bringing you a message."

The young man handed him a sealed letter.

"From Paris?"

The soldier nodded.

"Yes, Captain, Sir."

Napoleon took the envelope hesitantly and waved the soldier away. The young man obeyed reluctantly and seemed displeased. "At your orders, Captain, Sir," he mumbled, bowed slightly and left.

Napoleon mechanically brought the envelope to his nose. It smelled like ... a woman. A slight smell of lavender tickled his nose and he sneezed. He raised his eyebrows in amazement, stared at the envelope, placed it carefully on the rock in front of him and gazed at it. He had butterflies in his stomach.

Who could the letter be from? Except for prostitutes, he knew no women in Paris. Maybe it was a message from a general's wife? A promotion perhaps? Annoyed by his own stupid thoughts, he shook his head disparagingly, picked up the letter, and broke the seal. Gently, as if the letter could crumble at the last moment and dissolve into nothing in his hands, he unfolded the soft sheet of paper.

"Mon cher ami, Napoleon" he read aloud and was taken aback. Who called him that? He felt the flutter in his stomach grow stronger. "Here in Paris, hard times continue to prevail. No one feels safe and everyone fears for his life. One is afraid of doing or saying something that could be misinterpreted. Many of our neighbors end up on the guillotine, simply because they did not wear a cockade while shopping or because someone denigrated them out of sheer malice or jealousy. There is terrible tension everywhere and overall distrust.

I have finally found my sisters and am very happy about it. We now live behind the sewing room of Madame Bertin, the queen's former seamstress. "You can probably imagine why even here we don't feel safe."

"Jeanne!" uttered the captain in surprise and looked around, startled. A warm shiver ran through him, as always when he thought of her. Nobody seemed to be watching him. Then his gaze fell on the messenger, who seemed to be eyeing him warily, but immediately turned away when Napoleon looked his way. Napoleon narrowed his eyes suspiciously, thought for a moment, but his curiosity took the better of him and he read on.

“But enough of this. Of course, that's not the reason I'm writing to you, dear Napoleon. I wanted to express my sincere and warm gratitude for saving me on the day of the storm at the Tuileries. At the time, I wasn't really aware of the danger I was in, but the more I think about it, the more I realize that I probably owe you my life. I am deeply indebted to you for this, forever.

I often think of our inspiring conversation in the attic, where time seemed to stand still, and also of your promise. In my dreams, I am already going along with you. Don't forget me, Captain!

Au revoir, dearest friend,

Your Jeanne Cotin."

While he lowered the sheet of paper, his gaze wandered over the horizon. He felt warmth in his heart. He never would have thought that a simple letter could move him that much. He hummed a melody softly to himself. He loved this little girl from the well more than he could describe, but it was a strange kind of affection. He felt like a big brother and at the same time she was his guardian angel.

He sniffed at the paper again and slipped it between the buttons of his uniform. A smile played on his lips.

"Of course, I'll keep my promise, Jeanne!" he muttered, and stomped back to his tent lost in thought, as it was time to prepare for his meeting with Joseph.

***

Postscript

Ever since I can read, I've been fascinated by the French Revolution, Napoleon, history itself and historical novels.

I read my first book when I was seven and it was about Napoleon. To this day, this subject is stays on my mind and I still devour books about this era. Actually, I didn't want to write another one, I thought to myself that there is already enough literature on this topic.

However, I have always missed a novel that not only describes the life of the rich and the nobility, but above all describes the difficult life of the common people. This was my inspiration for the story of Jeanne and her sisters, in which well-known historical figures were only given supporting roles for a change.

What has always fascinated me about history is the idea of how people lived in the different centuries - or survived- despite the harshness, brevity and ruthlessness of their existence. As a child I said to my mother: "How I would love to live two hundred years earlier."

"Not me," my mother replied deprecatingly. "As a woman, you would have had no rights and a very difficult life, unless you were from the nobility."

Although she was probably right about that, I am no less fascinated by that time. Despite hunger, disease, violence, superstition, intrigue and murder, people back then fought for their rights. Nowadays one often forgets that life was once different ...

Acknowledgements

I would like to thank my friend Magali, who always patiently listened to my stories and assured me how exciting they were. Even if it wasn't always true: Thank you, Mag! ;)

I thank my children for their patience when mom wrote obsessively for hours because she thought she had a brilliant idea, and my mother for the test reading.

I thank my agent from the bottom of my heart for her trust in me.

Finally, I would also like to thank Jörg for his suggestions for improvement and his patience with my thousands of questions by e-mail.

With warm greetings,

Sibylle Baillon

Historical figures

Napoleon

Napoleon Buonaparte (later Bonaparte), French Emperor, born in Ajaccio, Corsica in 1769, died in exile on the island of St. Helena in 1821.

Who does not know this great ruler of France, who is admired and celebrated to this day?

In this novel you get to know a facet of his personality that is not often brought out when speaking about the genius of Napoleon. Many adore and admire him, others may hate him, but he is seldom understood.

During my research, I found him to be a talented, sensitive, and often insecure man. At least at in the earlier years of his life, he was very serious and eager to learn, devoured books, loved Rousseau and Voltaire and dreamed of success. His idol, whom he admired beyond measure, was Emperor Charlemagne.

He has been described as shy and awkward, but also sentimental. Except for the first scene at the fountain, the situations described are as close to the truth as possible, and I referred to his own reports or from those of historians.

He was really present at the storming of the Tuileries, witnessed the massacre and later complained that the king was not brave enough to show himself.

Also, his fear of large crowds and mobs held him back from participating directly. Had he perhaps watched the event from an attic in the company of a girl? Who knows...

According to his own statements, his first sexual intercourse actually was with a whore from the Palais Royal. This scene is described in the novel as realistically as possible.

He loved his homeland Corsica more than anything, even if he was later just as proud to be French. While others fought for the freedom of the third phase, his heart was still with Corsica. Whether out of conviction for the values of the Enlightenment or a little out of opportunism, he was soon completely in his element and finally sensed his great opportunity.

Napoleon hated dissipation of any kind in the army. He brought order and discipline to his troops.

For all Napoleon fans: If I should have made any mistakes, I ask for your indulgence. I have stuck to texts from and about him as best as possible.

Suzanne Necker (née Curchod)

She was born in Crassier (Switzerland) in 1737 and died in Switzerland in 1794. She was a highly educated teacher, daughter of a reformed clergyman and raised in strict religious faith.

After she married Jacques Necker (see below) in 1764, her home soon became a meeting place for personalities promoting the Enlightenment. It was said that she was a strict mother, which resulted in many conflicts with her only daughter, Anne-Louise-Germaine. She wanted to marry her daughter to an Englishman, but the latter refused to put up with it, and with the help of her father, she dissuaded her mother.

Jacques Necker

The banker, born in Geneva in 1732, later became finance minister and head of government under Louis XVI. and a major figure in the revolution. The king's dismissal of the popular statesman gave the final impetus to the assault the Bastille a few days later. After 1789, he and his wife retired to their castle in Coppet, Switzerland.

He had an unusually close relationship with his daughter Anne-Louise-Germaine.

Anne-Louise-Germaine Necker

(later the famous Madame de Staël or Baroness Anne Louise Germaine de Staël-Holstein)

The Neckers' only child was shaped from an early age, and her later career was thereby predetermined. Since early childhood, she was allowed to participate in her mother's salons, where she was distinguished by her above-average intelligence and analytical talent. At the age of 17 she married the much older Baron de Staël, who had been promised to her since childhood, and bore him four children. One died after two years and the other three were rumored to be the result of their various (extramarital) affairs. She became a well-known writer and salon lady.

The trip to Geneva was described by her personally, as was the meeting with Robespierre. She also actually followed her lover to England against her parents' wishes.

Mademoiselle Rose Bertin

She went down in history as the Queen's world-famous fashion minister. Thanks to her creativity and business acumen, she rose to become a personality in all Europe in terms of fashion. In addition to thirty employees, she employed countless other people in various sideline jobs as well as 120 suppliers. Over the years, she built a fashion empire that supplied half of Europe. Her closeness and loyalty to Marie-Antoinette became dangerous for her, but  she stood by her queen until the end. She personally delivered the clothing for the queen's escape and the fichus for the execution to Marie-Antoinette. She is also said to have been involved in the exchange of messages between the queen in prison and the European royal families. Rose Bertin burned all of her papers and books so that the queen would not be burdened with excessive expenses during her trial. Rose Bertin fled to England at the last moment .

Donatien Alphonse François de Sade (Marquis de Sade)

The French aristocrat became famous for the pornographic works he wrote while in prison. He actually spent several years in prison for numerous scandals, kidnapping and mistreatment of young girls.

I have indulged in a little freedom as a writer to have him drop by Madame Gourdan's (see below) brothel. At that time, he was still in custody at the Bastille. He was released in 1790, so that he could really have turned up in Jeanne's shop.

Madame Gourdan (La Petite Comtesse)

Madame Gourdan was one of the most famous brothel owners of the 18th century. Century. She called her brothel Château, and was a notorious businesswoman. The most famous was her Salon Vulkan, a sealed torture chamber. In his remarks on Marquis de Sade, the sex researcher Iwan Bloch describes how de Sade enjoyed himself in this chamber with a special chair that was equipped with shackles and other accessories. Madame Gourdan also traded in sex toys of all kinds, especially dildos and chairs.

After her one year imprisonment, she lost many customers to the competition. She died very suddenly in 1783.

Charles-Maurice Talleyrand-Périgord

He was one of the most famous French statesmen and diplomats during the French Revolution and later under Napoleon.

As a young child, it is said that he fell off a chest of drawers so that he was later unfit for a military career because of his clubfoot. Rejected by his parents, he was denied the rights of the firstborn and provided a career in the church.

He was ordained a priest and later became Bishop of Autun. The members of the clergy elected him a member of the summoned Estates General of 1789. On the eve of the reign of terror, he left the country with the help of Danton and fled to England. He was lucky, because papers found in one of the king's boxes incriminated him as a traitor.

He had a close friendship with Germaine de Staël and was known for his many affairs and illegitimate children.

One of his famous sentences is: "Anyone who did not know the Ancien Régime will never know how sweet life was."

Historical Places

All the locations and historical events mentioned are based on research.

Palais Royal

The Palais Royal was renamed Palais Egalité after the revolution and closed after the arrest of its owner. It was a city within a city and the scene of many events during the revolution. There were around 400 shops, cafes, theaters and much more. In addition to its goods, every shop also offered its saleswomen, who  strolled between the high columns in wafer-thin robes in the evening soliciting customers.

Stohrer Confectionery

The pastry shop Patissier Stohrer has been in Paris at 51, rue Montorgueil since 1730. Even today you can taste the famous Babas aux Rhum , which King Louis XV had concocted as early as 1735.  In my novel, I made the Stohrer Confectionery Jeanne's main supplier. The current owner, Pierre Lienard, was kind enough to send me some information about the history of the house.

Historical Context

The French Revolution

Hunger, bad harvests, poverty and excessive taxes drove people in France in the 18th century  to the extreme. The people rebelled against the nobility. The decisive trigger was the dismissal of the Finance Minister Necker, who tried to convince King Ludwig XVI. to change his policy. The storming of the Bastille was only the beginning of a period that terrified all European royal families.

Horror, terror, looting and arbitrary killing became a daily part of the life of Parisians. Thousands of heads rolled. The aristocrats fled en masse, others tried to submit to the social and political change and join in. But at the latest during the reign of terror under Robespierre, they also fled - often to England.

The armies, whose officers came mostly from the nobility, had to be reorganized. Every man was welcome, and the opportunities for advancement opened up for men from the common people.

The revolution with its various civil wars claimed around 600,000 to 800,000 victims from 1789 to 1799 (the figures are estimates, many documents were destroyed in 1871).

Salt Smuggling

Due to the excessively high duties and taxes on salt, an active salt smuggling developed, particularly in the Vendée area. The farmers suffered the most from the high prices, because they needed this raw material for the curing of meat and to bake bread.

At night, poor farmers risked their lives to secretly transport salt across the Loire. But very often, they were ambushed and woe to those who got caught. Anyone who was convicted of smuggling salt or the unauthorized mining of salt had to face severe penalties. This included several years of rowing on galleys in the Mediterranean and lifelong banishment in penal camps in far away places.
